topics.title: Analysis of Steam Cycle and Steam Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Steam Cycle and Steam Engineering # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Steam Engineering** is the specialized branch of thermal engineering that deals with the generation, expansion, condensation, and recirculation of steam as a working fluid in thermodynamic power cycles. It encompasses the study of steam tables, steam generators (boilers), superheaters, economizers, and the thermodynamic performance evaluation of systems that convert the latent and sensible heat of water/steam into useful mechanical shaft work. **Analysis of Steam Cycle** refers to the rigorous thermodynamic examination of the closed-loop sequence of processes (heating, vaporization, superheating, expansion, condensation, and pumping) through which the working fluid (water/steam) passes to produce net work output in a thermal power plant. The most fundamental and widely used reference cycle is the **Ideal Rankine Cycle**, which serves as the standard against which all practical steam power plant performance is benchmarked. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Module 1 emphasizes the *ideal Rankine cycle*, its P-v, T-s, and h-s (Mollier) representations, the effect of boiler pressure, superheat, condenser pressure, and the **Carnot-versus-Rankine** comparative analysis. Numerical problems typically involve steam table look-ups and computation of thermal efficiency, work output, and steam rate. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview) Think of a steam cycle like a **water elevator in a tall building that uses a hidden heat source**: 1. **Boiler (Ground Floor Heat Source):** Water is heated at low pressure to become steam — this is the "lift" that gives the fluid high energy. 2. **Turbine (Downhill Slide):** The high-energy steam rushes through the turbine, spinning the blades and producing work — like sliding down a slide. 3. **Condenser (Cooling Tower):** After the slide, the steam needs to "reset" to liquid form. The condenser cools it back to water, throwing away waste heat to a river or cooling tower. 4. **Pump (Staircase Back Up):** A small pump pushes the condensed water back up to the boiler pressure — the input energy cost to "elevate" the fluid. The net work = (Energy gained going down the slide) − (Energy needed to climb the staircase back up). The cycle repeats endlessly, just like the elevator in continuous operation. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Standard Values Used in KTU Board Examinations:** > - **$1 \text{ kW·h} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$** (unit conversion constant for steam rate and heat rate problems) > - **$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$** (pressure conversion) > - **$1 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ bar}$** > - **Specific enthalpy ($h$), entropy ($s$), internal energy ($u$)** values must be obtained from **saturated steam tables** (for $P$ or $T$ inputs) and **superheated steam tables** (for $P$ and $T$ both above saturation). ## 1.4 Why the Rankine Cycle (and not the Carnot Cycle) for Steam Plants? The Carnot cycle, although the most efficient heat engine operating between two reservoirs, is **impractical for steam power plants** because: - It requires **isothermal heat addition** — practically impossible in a boiler where phase change occurs at constant pressure (not constant temperature). - It requires **isothermal heat rejection** — the wet mixture in the condenser has variable temperature, but practical condensers reject heat at near-constant pressure. - It demands **two-phase compression of a liquid-vapor mixture** in the compressor, which causes severe mechanical erosion. The Rankine cycle elegantly overcomes these by using a **pump (for liquid only)** and a **boiler operating at constant pressure**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Ideal Rankine Cycle The ideal Rankine cycle operates on a closed loop with **four internally reversible processes**. Let us denote the state points as **1, 2, 3, 4** (clockwise on T-s and h-s diagrams). ### Process 1→2: Isentropic Expansion in Turbine - **What:** High-pressure, high-temperature superheated steam from the boiler expands through the turbine to the condenser pressure. - **How:** The steam does work on the turbine blades, losing enthalpy. The expansion is **isentropic** (constant entropy, $s_1 = s_2$). - **Why it matters:** This is the **prime work-producing** process. The drop in enthalpy equals the turbine work. ### Process 2→3: Constant-Pressure Heat Rejection in Condenser - **What:** Wet steam at the turbine exhaust is condensed to saturated liquid at constant condenser pressure. - **How:** Heat is rejected to the cooling medium (river water, cooling tower, or air). The state exits the condenser as a **saturated liquid** (Quality $x = 0$). - **Why it matters:** Lowering the condenser pressure increases efficiency, but is limited by cooling water temperature. ### Process 3→4: Isentropic Compression in Pump - **What:** Saturated liquid from the condenser is pumped to the high boiler pressure. - **How:** The pump is a liquid pump, requiring very little work because the specific volume of liquid is small. $s_3 = s_4$. - **Why it matters:** The pump work is typically **less than 1% of the turbine work** in a real plant. ### Process 4→1: Constant-Pressure Heat Addition in Boiler - **What:** Compressed liquid enters the boiler, is heated to saturation, evaporated, and then superheated to the turbine inlet state. - **How:** Three sub-stages: **economizer** (sensible heating), **evaporator** (latent addition), **superheater** (sensible heating beyond saturation). - **Why it matters:** This is the **major heat input** process. The enthalpy rise equals the heat supplied per kg of steam. ## 2.2 The Carnot Cycle Comparison (Critical for KTU) A frequently asked KTU module question: *"Why is the Rankine cycle preferred over the Carnot cycle for steam power plants despite the Carnot cycle being theoretically more efficient?"* | Feature | Carnot Vapor Cycle | Rankine Cycle | |---|---|---| | Heat Addition | Isothermal (impossible with phase change at constant P) | Isobaric (matches real boilers) | | Heat Rejection | Isothermal | Isobaric | | Compressor | Handles two-phase mixture (cavitation/erosion) | Pump handles only liquid | | Practicality | Theoretical only | Industry standard | | Efficiency | Higher (theoretical) | Slightly lower but practical | ## 2.3 Effects of Operating Parameters on Rankine Cycle Performance > [!TIP] > **High-Yield KTU Question Type:** *"Discuss the effect of boiler pressure / superheat / condenser pressure on Rankine cycle efficiency and steam quality at turbine exhaust."* ### (a) Effect of Increasing Boiler Pressure (at constant superheat & condenser pressure) - **Thermal efficiency increases.** - **Moisture content at turbine exit increases** (quality $x$ decreases) — this is dangerous as it causes blade erosion. - Solution: introduce reheat stages. ### (b) Effect of Increasing Superheat Temperature (at constant boiler & condenser pressure) - **Thermal efficiency increases.** - **Moisture content at turbine exit decreases** (quality increases) — this is beneficial. - Limitation: Metallurgical limits of boiler tube material (typically 600°C in modern plants). ### (c) Effect of Decreasing Condenser Pressure (at constant boiler conditions) - **Thermal efficiency increases.** - **Moisture content at turbine exit increases** (quality decreases) — detrimental. - Limitation: Cooling water temperature — condenser pressure cannot drop below saturation pressure at cooling water temperature. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Use $\vert$ or \mid in tables below instead of the pipe character to preserve Markdown table integrity.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Turbine Work | $W_T = h_1 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | | 2 | Pump Work | $W_P = h_4 - h_3 = v_3 (P_4 - P_3)$ | kJ/kg (approx. $v_f$ at $P_3$) | | 3 | Net Work Output | $W_{\text{net}} = W_T - W_P$ | kJ/kg | | 4 | Heat Supplied in Boiler | $Q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | | 5 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $Q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | | 6 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Back Work Ratio | $\text{BWR} = \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | typically 0.01–0.04 | | 8 | Work Ratio | $\text{WR} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{W_T}$ | typically 0.96–0.99 | | 9 | Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | $\text{SSC} = \dfrac{3600}{W_{\text{net}}}$ | kg/kWh | | 10 | Heat Rate | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{th}}}$ | kJ/kWh | | 11 | Carnot Efficiency (Reference) | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | requires absolute temps | | 12 | Pump Work Approximation | $W_P \approx v_f (P_{\text{boiler}} - P_{\text{cond}})$ | kJ/kg | | 13 | Quality at Turbine Exit | $x_2 = \dfrac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | dimensionless | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Rankine cycle analysis underpins: - **Thermal power plant design** (coal, nuclear, biomass, solar thermal). - **Combined cycle plants** (Rankine bottoming + Brayton topping). - **Cogeneration/CHP systems** for district heating and industrial process steam. - **Waste heat recovery** systems in steel, cement, and petrochemical industries. - **Nuclear submarine propulsion** and **solar thermal power towers**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle We apply the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component, assuming **adiabatic, reversible** processes and **negligible kinetic and potential energy changes**. ### Step 1: Apply SFEE to the Boiler (Process 4 → 1) For a steady-flow control volume with no work output: $$q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4$$ **Conversion Logic:** Energy balance: heat entering the control volume equals the increase in specific enthalpy of the fluid, since the boiler has no shaft work and we neglect KE/PE changes. ### Step 2: Apply SFEE to the Turbine (Process 1 → 2) For the turbine, $q = 0$ (adiabatic ideal), and work is output: $$w_T = h_1 - h_2$$ **Conversion Logic:** The enthalpy drop of steam equals the work extracted by the turbine blades per unit mass flow. ### Step 3: Apply SFEE to the Condenser (Process 2 → 3) No work, heat is rejected at constant pressure: $$q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3$$ **Conversion Logic:** The enthalpy lost by the steam equals the heat rejected to the cooling medium per unit mass. ### Step 4: Apply SFEE to the Pump (Process 3 → 4) For the pump, $q = 0$, work is input: $$w_P = h_4 - h_3$$ **Conversion Logic:** The enthalpy rise of the liquid equals the work input to the pump. ### Step 5: Net Work and Thermal Efficiency The net work output per kg of steam is: $$W_{\text{net}} = w_T - w_P = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$$ The thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work to heat supplied: $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$$ ### Step 6: Verification via Energy Balance (Closure Check) For a cyclic process, the first law requires: $$q_{\text{in}} - q_{\text{out}} = W_{\text{net}}$$ Substituting the expressions derived above: $$(h_1 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_3) = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$$ LHS: $h_1 - h_4 - h_2 + h_3$ RHS: $h_1 - h_2 - h_4 + h_3$ Both sides are identical, confirming the cyclic closure. ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation: Pump Work Approximation (Important for KTU Problems) For an **incompressible liquid** undergoing an isentropic compression from $P_3$ to $P_4$: $$w_P = \int_{P_3}^{P_4} v \, dP = v_3 (P_4 - P_3)$$ **Conversion Logic:** Since the specific volume of saturated liquid changes very little with pressure (unlike gases), we treat $v$ as a constant equal to $v_f$ at the condenser pressure. The integral of a constant is simply the constant times the pressure difference. **Why is this important?** The pump work in steam plants is small but **non-negligible in KTU numerical problems** where 100% accuracy is required. Using $v_f$ from saturated water tables at $T_{\text{cond}}$ is the standard KTU convention. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Standard State Point Property Determination The following matrix summarizes how to determine properties at each state point using steam tables: | State | Location | Phase | Properties Known | Properties to Look Up | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Turbine Inlet | Superheated Steam | $P_1$ and $T_1$ given | $h_1$, $s_1$ from **Superheated Steam Table** at $(P_1, T_1)$ | | 2 | Turbine Exit | Wet Steam (typically) | $P_2$ known, $s_2 = s_1$ | $h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg}$, $x_2 = (s_2 - s_f)/s_{fg}$ from **Saturated Table** at $P_2$ | | 3 | Condenser Exit | Saturated Liquid | $P_3 = P_2$, $x = 0$ | $h_3 = h_f$ at $P_3$, $v_3 = v_f$ at $P_3$ from **Saturated Table** | | 4 | Pump Exit / Boiler Inlet | Compressed Liquid | $P_4 = P_1$, $s_4 = s_3$ | $h_4 = h_3 + v_3(P_4 - P_3)$ (or $h_4 \approx h_3$ for rough) | ## 3.4 Sample Numerical Solution (KTU Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at $3 \text{ MPa}$ and $350°C$, and the condenser pressure is $10 \text{ kPa}$. Determine: (a) thermal efficiency, (b) work output, (c) heat supplied, (d) quality at turbine exit. **Given:** $P_1 = 3 \text{ MPa}$, $T_1 = 350°C$, $P_2 = P_3 = 10 \text{ kPa}$. **Step 1: State 1 — Superheated Steam Table at 3 MPa, 350°C** $$h_1 = 2988.1 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 6.7428 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ (These values are standard textbook entries — students should verify with the KTU-approved steam tables.) **Step 2: State 2 — Isentropic Expansion to 10 kPa** At $P_2 = 10 \text{ kPa}$ from saturated water table: $$h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2392.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$s_f = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_{fg} = 7.4996 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ Since $s_2 = s_1 = 6.7428 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and $s_f < s_2 < s_g$, the steam is wet. Quality: $$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = 0.8127$$ Enthalpy at state 2: $$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8127 \times 2392.1 = 2136.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3: State 3 — Saturated Liquid at 10 kPa** $$h_3 = h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad v_3 = v_f = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 4: State 4 — Pump Exit** $$h_4 = h_3 + v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 191.81 + 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5: Performance Calculations** Turbine work: $$w_T = h_1 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 2136.16 = 851.94 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Pump work: $$w_P = h_4 - h_3 = 3.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Net work: $$W_{\text{net}} = 851.94 - 3.02 = 848.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Heat supplied: $$q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 194.83 = 2793.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{848.92}{2793.27} = 0.3039 = 30.39\%$$ **Answers:** (a) $\eta_{\text{th}} \approx 30.39\%$, (b) $W_{\text{net}} = 848.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$, (c) $q_{\text{in}} = 2793.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$, (d) $x_2 = 0.8127$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Layout of an Ideal Rankine Cycle Power Plant The following flowchart shows the major components and their interconnections in an ideal Rankine cycle steam power plant. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FUEL AND AIR
INPUT TO BOILER] --> B[BOILER
Heat Source] B --> C[STATE 1
Superheated Steam
High P and High T] C --> D[TURBINE
Isentropic Expansion
Work Output W sub T] D --> E[STATE 2
Wet Steam
Low P] E --> F[CONDENSER
Constant P Heat Rejection
to Cooling Water] F --> G[STATE 3
Saturated Liquid
Low P] G --> H[PUMP
Isentropic Compression
Work Input W sub P] H --> I[STATE 4
Compressed Liquid
High P] I --> B D --> J[GENERATOR
Electrical Output] F --> K[COOLING TOWER OR
COOLING WATER SOURCE] K --> F ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid T-s Diagram Representation of Rankine Cycle The following T-s (Temperature-entropy) diagram shows the four ideal Rankine processes plotted on the saturation dome. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TSDIAG[IDEAL RANKINE CYCLE ON T-s DIAGRAM] direction TB P1[STATE 1 SUPERHEATED
High T and High s1] P2[STATE 2 WET
Inside Dome s2 = s1] P3[STATE 3 SAT LIQUID
Low T s3 = sf at P low] P4[STATE 4 COMP LIQUID
Slightly Right of P3 s4 = s3] P1 -->|Isentropic Expansion 1 to 2| P2 P2 -->|Constant P Condensation 2 to 3| P3 P3 -->|Isentropic Pumping 3 to 4| P4 P4 -->|Constant P Heat Addition 4 to 1| P1 end STYLE_TS_NOTE[Note: Area Under 4 to 1 equals Q sub in. Area Under 2 to 3 equals Q sub out. Net area is W sub net.] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the T-s diagram correctly:** In the ideal Rankine cycle on a T-s diagram, the heat input equals the area under the curve from state 4 to state 1, the heat rejected equals the area under the curve from state 2 to state 3, and the enclosed area represents the **net work output**. ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: Steam Side vs. Energy Side ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WORKING_FLUID[STEAM AND WATER LOOP] BD[BOILER DRUM] --> TU[TURBINE] TU --> CD[CONDENSER] CD --> PP[PUMP] PP --> BD end subgraph ENERGY_FLOW[ENERGY AND WORK STREAMS] FQ[FUEL Q sub in] --> BD BD --> WT[TURBINE WORK W sub T] CD --> QC[CONDENSER HEAT Q sub out] PP --> WP[PUMP WORK W sub P] WT --> GEN[GENERATOR] WP --> PP end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the Rankine cycle. Why is the Rankine cycle preferred over the Carnot cycle for steam power plants?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Rankine cycle is an ideal thermodynamic cycle consisting of four internally reversible processes — isentropic compression in a pump, constant-pressure heat addition in a boiler, isentropic expansion in a turbine, and constant-pressure heat rejection in a condenser — using water/steam as the working fluid. **Reasons for preference over Carnot (2 marks):** 1. The Carnot cycle requires **isothermal heat addition**, which is impossible in a boiler where phase change occurs at constant pressure (not constant temperature). 2. The Carnot cycle requires a **compressor handling a two-phase mixture**, which causes severe mechanical erosion and is impractical. 3. The Rankine cycle uses a **pump for liquid only**, which is mechanically feasible and requires minimal work. 4. Rankine cycle is **easily realized in actual plant components** — boilers, turbines, condensers, and pumps exist as standard equipment. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is meant by "Quality" of steam? Why is high moisture content at the turbine exhaust undesirable?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Quality ($x$) of steam is the mass fraction of vapor in a liquid-vapor mixture. Mathematically, $$x = \frac{m_{\text{vapor}}}{m_{\text{vapor}} + m_{\text{liquid}}}$$ For a wet steam with quality $x$, the specific enthalpy is: $$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ **Why high moisture is undesirable (1.5 marks):** 1. The high-velocity water droplets **erode the turbine blades**, reducing blade life and increasing maintenance. 2. Excessive moisture reduces the **effective work output** of the turbine as droplets act as friction. 3. It causes **corrosion and pitting** of the metallic blading, particularly in the low-pressure stages. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **In a steam power plant operating on the ideal Rankine cycle, the steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa and 400°C, and the condenser operates at 20 kPa. Determine:** **(a)** The quality of steam at the turbine exit, thermal efficiency, and work output of the cycle. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the effect of (i) increasing boiler pressure and (ii) superheating the steam on the Rankine cycle performance. (7 marks) #### Solution (a): Numerical Analysis **Given:** $P_1 = 3 \text{ MPa}$, $T_1 = 400°C$, $P_2 = P_3 = 20 \text{ kPa}$. **State 1 — Superheated Steam at 3 MPa, 400°C (from Superheated Steam Table):** $$h_1 = 3231.7 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 6.9235 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **State 2 — Isentropic Expansion ($s_2 = s_1 = 6.9235 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$):** At $P_2 = 20 \text{ kPa}$ from saturated water table: $$h_f = 251.42 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2357.5 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2608.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$s_f = 0.8320 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_{fg} = 7.0752 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_g = 7.9072 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ Since $s_2 < s_g$ at 20 kPa, the steam is wet. Quality: $$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.9235 - 0.8320}{7.0752} = 0.8614$$ Enthalpy at state 2: $$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 251.42 + 0.8614 \times 2357.5 = 2281.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **State 3 — Saturated Liquid at 20 kPa:** $$h_3 = h_f = 251.42 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad v_3 = v_f = 0.001017 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **State 4 — Pump Exit:** $$h_4 = h_3 + v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 251.42 + 0.001017 \times (3000 - 20)$$ $$h_4 = 251.42 + 3.03 = 254.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Performance Calculations:** Turbine work: $$w_T = h_1 - h_2 = 3231.7 - 2281.93 = 949.77 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Pump work: $$w_P = h_4 - h_3 = 3.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Net work: $$W_{\text{net}} = 949.77 - 3.03 = 946.74 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Heat supplied: $$q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4 = 3231.7 - 254.45 = 2977.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{946.74}{2977.25} = 0.3180 = 31.80\%$$ **Answers (a):** $x_2 = 0.8614$, $\eta_{\text{th}} = 31.80\%$, $W_{\text{net}} = 946.74 \text{ kJ/kg}$. #### Solution (b): Discussion on Parameter Effects **(i) Effect of Increasing Boiler Pressure (3.5 marks):** With superheat temperature and condenser pressure held constant, increasing the boiler pressure: - **Increases the thermal efficiency** of the Rankine cycle because the mean temperature of heat addition rises. - **Increases the moisture content** at the turbine exhaust (quality $x_2$ decreases). This is shown in the T-s diagram as the constant-entropy expansion line crosses further into the wet region. - Practical limits: Very high pressures require thicker boiler tubes, more expensive materials, and eventually the moisture problem becomes unmanageable without reheating. - Typical range: Modern subcritical plants use 16–18 MPa; supercritical plants use $>22.1$ MPa. **(ii) Effect of Superheating the Steam (3.5 marks):** With boiler pressure and condenser pressure held constant, increasing the superheat temperature: - **Increases the thermal efficiency** by raising the average temperature of heat addition. - **Decreases the moisture content at turbine exhaust** (quality $x_2$ increases) — this is highly beneficial for blade protection. - Practical limits: Metallurgical constraints of superheater tubes, typically limited to about **600°C** in modern ultrasupercritical plants (with nickel-based alloys). - Excessive superheat without pressure increase can lead to metallurgical failures and high-temperature creep in tubes. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of an ideal Rankine cycle. A steam power plant operates on the Rankine cycle with reheat. Steam enters the high-pressure turbine at 8 MPa and 500°C and is reheated to 500°C at 1.5 MPa. The condenser pressure is 10 kPa. Determine the thermal efficiency.** **(a)** Sketch and explain the working of the ideal Rankine cycle with reheat. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the thermal efficiency of the reheat cycle. (7 marks) #### Solution (a): Sketch and Working **Sketch (T-s diagram description, 3 marks):** On a T-s diagram, the reheat cycle has **six state points**: - **State 1:** HP Turbine Inlet — high pressure (8 MPa), 500°C superheated. - **State 2:** HP Turbine Exit / Reheater Inlet — 1.5 MPa, after isentropic expansion from state 1. - **State 3:** Reheater Exit / LP Turbine Inlet — 1.5 MPa, 500°C superheated. - **State 4:** LP Turbine Exit — 10 kPa, isentropic expansion from state 3. - **State 5:** Condenser Exit — 10 kPa, saturated liquid. - **State 6:** Pump Exit — 8 MPa, isentropic compression from state 5. **Working (4 marks):** In the reheat cycle, steam is expanded in the HP turbine to an intermediate pressure, then **sent back to the boiler** where it is **reheated** to a high temperature (typically equal to the original superheat temperature) before being expanded again in the LP turbine. The reheat process: - **Avoids excessive moisture** in the LP turbine stages. - **Increases the thermal efficiency** by adding heat at a higher mean temperature. - **Reduces blade erosion**, increasing turbine life. The major components: **Boiler → HP Turbine → Reheater → LP Turbine → Condenser → Pump → (back to) Boiler.** #### Solution (b): Numerical Analysis **Given:** $P_1 = 8 \text{ MPa}$, $T_1 = 500°C$, Reheat pressure = 1.5 MPa, Reheat to $T_3 = 500°C$, $P_4 = P_5 = 10 \text{ kPa}$. **State 1 — Superheated at 8 MPa, 500°C:** $$h_1 = 3399.5 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 6.7266 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **State 2 — Isentropic Expansion to 1.5 MPa ($s_2 = s_1$):** At 1.5 MPa, from saturated water table: $$h_f = 844.84 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2792.2 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_f = 2.3147 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_g = 6.4550 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ Since $s_1 = 6.7266 > s_g$ at 1.5 MPa, the steam is superheated. From superheated steam table at 1.5 MPa and $s = 6.7266 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$: $$h_2 \approx 2870.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ (For KTU board purposes, this requires interpolation; we use a representative value here.) **State 3 — Reheat to 1.5 MPa, 500°C:** $$h_3 = 3479.0 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 7.4310 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **State 4 — Isentropic Expansion to 10 kPa ($s_4 = s_3 = 7.4310 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$):** At 10 kPa, $s_g = 8.1488 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Since $s_4 < s_g$, steam is wet. Quality: $$x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{7.4310 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = 0.9041$$ $$h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.9041 \times 2392.1 = 2354.59 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **State 5 — Saturated Liquid at 10 kPa:** $$h_5 = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad v_5 = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **State 6 — Pump Exit:** $$h_6 = h_5 + v_5 (P_6 - P_5) = 191.81 + 0.001010 \times (8000 - 10) = 191.81 + 8.07 = 199.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Performance Calculations:** Total turbine work: $$w_T = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) = (3399.5 - 2870.0) + (3479.0 - 2354.59)$$ $$w_T = 529.5 + 1124.41 = 1653.91 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Pump work: $$w_P = h_6 - h_5 = 8.07 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Net work: $$W_{\text{net}} = 1653.91 - 8.07 = 1645.84 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Total heat supplied (boiler + reheater): $$q_{\text{in}} = (h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2) = (3399.5 - 199.88) + (3479.0 - 2870.0)$$ $$q_{\text{in}} = 3199.62 + 609.0 = 3808.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Thermal efficiency: $$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{1645.84}{3808.62} = 0.4321 = 43.21\%$$ **Answer (b):** $\eta_{\text{th}} \approx 43.21\%$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the reheater heat input:** The total $q_{\text{in}}$ in a reheat cycle is the sum of boiler heat input AND reheater heat input. Many students compute only the boiler portion, leading to a falsely high efficiency and loss of 2–3 marks. > 2. **Improper interpolation in steam tables:** When $s_1$ falls between two superheated entries, students must perform **linear interpolation** for $h$. Skipping this step is a 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Sign convention errors in pump work:** Always remember $w_P = h_4 - h_3 > 0$ for the pump (work is input). The net work is $W_T - W_P$, not $W_T + W_P$. > 4. **Neglecting pump work:** Pump work is small but is **expected to be calculated** in KTU 14-mark problems. Mark deduction: 1 mark. > 5. **Using wrong steam table:** KTU allows only the specific approved steam tables. Values may differ slightly from other references; ensure the values match the KTU-prescribed tables. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Preparation:** - **Four processes of Rankine cycle:** Pump (3→4, isentropic compression), Boiler (4→1, constant-P heat addition), Turbine (1→2, isentropic expansion), Condenser (2→3, constant-P heat rejection). - **Thermal efficiency formula:** $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$ - **Pump work approximation:** $W_P = v_f (P_{\text{boiler}} - P_{\text{condenser}})$ — use $v_f$ at condenser conditions from saturated water table. - **State 1 properties** come from the **superheated steam table** using $(P_1, T_1)$. - **State 2 properties** are computed via the **isentropic relation** $s_2 = s_1$ and the **quality formula** $x_2 = (s_2 - s_f)/s_{fg}$ at the condenser pressure. - **State 3 properties** = $h_f$, $v_f$ at the condenser pressure (saturated liquid assumption). - **State 4 properties** are computed using the pump work approximation, NOT directly from the compressed liquid table. - **Carnot vs. Rankine:** Carnot is theoretical maximum but impractical. Rankine is the industry standard. - **Effects to memorize:** - ↑ Boiler pressure → ↑ efficiency but ↑ moisture at turbine exit. - ↑ Superheat → ↑ efficiency AND ↓ moisture (win-win, but metallurgically limited). - ↓ Condenser pressure → ↑ efficiency but ↑ moisture (limited by cooling water temperature). - **Reheat cycle:** Adds an extra expansion + reheat stage; increases efficiency, reduces moisture. - **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):** $SSC = 3600/W_{\text{net}}$ — lower SSC means better cycle. - **Heat Rate:** $HR = 3600/\eta_{\text{th}}$ — lower HR is better (typical modern plants: 8000–10000 kJ/kWh). - **Back Work Ratio (BWR):** $BWR = W_P/W_T$ — for steam plants, BWR is only 0.01–0.04 (very low) because liquid is incompressible. - **Practical steam plant efficiency:** 30–40% (Rankine without modifications), up to 45% with reheat-regeneration, and up to 60% in combined cycle plants. - **First-law closure check:** Always verify that $q_{\text{in}} - q_{\text{out}} = W_{\text{net}}$ for any cycle calculation. - **Common KTU mistakes:** Forgetting pump work in 14-mark problems, using $s_f$ instead of $s_g$ accidentally, omitting units in final answers, and confusing $h_{fg}$ (latent heat) with $h_f$ (sensible heat of liquid).

topics.title: Temperature-Entropy Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature-Entropy Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Temperature–Entropy (T–s) Diagram

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Temperature–Entropy (T–s) Diagram** is a thermodynamic chart on which the absolute temperature (T) of a working substance is plotted on the vertical (ordinate) axis and the specific entropy (s) on the horizontal (abscissa) axis. It graphically represents the various thermodynamic states and processes of a pure substance, particularly water/steam, and is the most powerful analytical tool in **Power Plant Engineering** because the **area under any reversible process curve on a T–s diagram directly equals the heat transfer** ($Q = \int T \, ds$) for that process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The T–s diagram is the *primary* graphical tool for analysing the Rankine cycle, vapour compression refrigeration cycle, and for evaluating the *Mean Temperature of Heat Addition* and *Mean Temperature of Heat Rejection* — both of which are central to cycle efficiency calculations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engineer's Road Map"

Imagine you are driving a car from **City A (Boiler exit)** to **City B (Condenser exit)**. On a normal road map (the P–v diagram), you only see the *pressure* your tyres exert on the road at every point — useful, but limited. Now imagine a **smart GPS map** (the T–s diagram) that not only shows you your path, but also **automatically calculates the total fuel (heat) consumed** between any two cities just by measuring the **area enclosed under your route**. That is exactly what the T–s diagram does for an engineer: the *area under the curve = heat transferred*. No other common diagram gives this direct visual readout of heat.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Metrics Used

- **Absolute Zero:** **0 K** (or **−273.15 °C**) — the theoretical lower bound of the temperature axis.
- **Triple Point of Water:** **T = 273.16 K (0.01 °C)**, **P = 0.006112 bar**.
- **Critical Point of Water:** **T = 647.1 K (374 °C)**, **P = 220.64 bar**, **s = 4.41 kJ/kg·K**.
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** (used in ideal-gas entropy relations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Absolute" Temperature?** Because entropy (s) involves $\int \frac{dQ_{rev}}{T}$, the temperature must be referenced to absolute zero for the integral to be valid. Hence the T–s diagram always uses the **Kelvin (K)** scale, not Celsius.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why T–s is Preferred Over P–v and T–v Diagrams

| Property | P–v Diagram | T–v Diagram | **T–s Diagram** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Axes | Pressure vs Specific Volume | Temperature vs Specific Volume | **Temperature vs Specific Entropy** |
| Area under curve | $\int P \, dv$ = Work (limited utility) | No direct physical quantity | **$\int T \, ds$ = Heat Transfer (direct readout)** |
| Phase regions visible | Yes (but compressed near origin) | Poor for two-phase region | **Excellent — dome is wide & clear** |
| Cycle visualisation | Moderate | Poor | **Best (Rankine, Carnot, Otto, Diesel all clear)** |
| Use in Power Plants | Secondary | Rare | **Primary analytical tool** |

## 2.2 Structural Features of the T–s Diagram (Water/Steam)

The T–s diagram for water has three distinct regions separated by a **vapour dome** (also called the *saturation curve* or *phase boundary curve*):

1. **Sub-cooled (Compressed) Liquid Region** — left of the saturated liquid line (x = 0).
2. **Wet Vapour / Two-Phase Region** — inside the dome (0 ≤ x ≤ 1).
3. **Superheated Vapour Region** — right of the saturated vapour line (x = 1).

The dome is bounded by:
- **Saturated Liquid Line** (left branch, x = 0).
- **Saturated Vapour Line** (right branch, x = 1).
- **Critical Point C** at the apex (T = 647.1 K, P = 220.64 bar).

Within the dome, **isotherms are horizontal lines** (constant T, varying s) — this is a unique feature of the T–s diagram and the visual reason why **isothermal heat transfer appears as a rectangle** on it.

## 2.3 Representation of Standard Thermodynamic Processes

| Process | Path on T–s Diagram | Direction of Travel |
|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** (T = const) | Horizontal straight line | Left → Right (heat addition) or Right → Left (rejection) |
| **Isobaric** (P = const) | Curve that is **flatter** than isotherms in superheat region | Heat addition: s ↑ |
| **Isochoric** (v = const) | Curve **steeper** than isotherms in superheat region | s ↑ but T rises sharply |
| **Isentropic** (s = const) | **Vertical straight line** | Pure work process (no heat transfer) |
| **Adiabatic Reversible** | Same as isentropic — vertical line | — |
| **Throttling** (h = const) | Curve sloping downward to the right | s ↑, T ↓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The vertical line is the signature of an ideal turbine, pump, or compressor** on the T–s diagram. Any deviation from vertical represents **irreversibility** (entropy generation), and the horizontal distance between the actual and ideal exit states is the **isentropic efficiency penalty**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} T \, ds$ | Heat transfer for a reversible process | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $Q = T \cdot \Delta s$ | Heat for constant-temperature (isothermal) process | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $ds = \frac{dQ_{rev}}{T}$ | Definition of entropy change | kJ/kg·K |
| 4 | $\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Ideal-gas entropy change | kJ/kg·K |
| 5 | $\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$ | Alternative form (using v) | kJ/kg·K |
| 6 | $\eta_{th,R} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_{L,mean}}{T_{H,mean}}$ | Rankine efficiency in terms of mean temperatures | — |
| 7 | $T_{mean} = \frac{\int T \, ds}{\Delta s}$ | Mean temperature of heat transfer | K |
| 8 | $T_{H,mean} = \frac{Q_{in}}{(s_2 - s_1)}$ | Mean temperature of heat addition | K |
| 9 | $T_{L,mean} = \frac{Q_{out}}{(s_4 - s_3)}$ | Mean temperature of heat rejection | K |
| 10 | $\Delta s_{gen} \ge 0$ | Clausius inequality (2nd Law) | kJ/kg·K |
| 11 | $s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = s$ | Entropy in wet region | kJ/kg·K |
| 12 | $s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T}{T_{ref}}\right)$ (approx.) | Liquid heating entropy change | kJ/kg·K |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse the **mean temperature of heat addition** (a fictitious constant T that gives the same Q over the same Δs) with the simple arithmetic average $\frac{T_{max} + T_{min}}{2}$. The former is defined by **Area = Rectangle** on the T–s diagram — see derivation in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Area Under T–s Curve Equals Heat Transfer

Starting from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Clausius equality for a reversible process):

$$
dQ_{rev} = T \, ds
$$

Integrating both sides between state 1 and state 2:

$$
Q_{1 \to 2} = \int_{1}^{2} T \, ds
$$

This integral is **geometrically** the area enclosed between the process curve, the s-axis, and the two vertical ordinates at $s_1$ and $s_2$ on the T–s diagram. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{Q_{1 \to 2} = \text{Area under the process curve on the T–s diagram}}
$$

This single relation is why the T–s diagram is the engineer's *graphical calculator* for heat transfer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Temperature of Heat Addition

Consider a typical boiler process in the Rankine cycle, where the working fluid is heated from state 4 (saturated liquid at $s_4$) to state 1 (superheated steam at $s_1$). The actual heat added in the boiler is:

$$
Q_{in} = \int_{4}^{1} T \, ds
$$

By definition, the **Mean Temperature of Heat Addition ($T_{H,mean}$)** is the constant temperature at which the same heat $Q_{in}$ would have to be supplied over the same entropy change $(s_1 - s_4)$:

$$
Q_{in} = T_{H,mean} \cdot (s_1 - s_4)
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
T_{H,mean} \cdot (s_1 - s_4) = \int_{4}^{1} T \, ds
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{H,mean} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{4}^{1} T \, ds}{s_1 - s_4}}
$$

**Graphical Interpretation:** $T_{H,mean}$ is the height of a rectangle whose area equals the area under the boiler curve on the T–s diagram and whose width is $(s_1 - s_4)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Rankine Cycle Efficiency from T–s Areas

For the ideal Rankine cycle (4 → 1: boiler; 1 → 2: turbine; 2 → 3: condenser; 3 → 4: pump):

**Step 1 — Heat added in boiler:**

$$
Q_{in} = \int_{4}^{1} T \, ds = \text{Area under curve 4-1} = (h_1 - h_4)
$$

**Step 2 — Heat rejected in condenser:**

$$
Q_{out} = \int_{2}^{3} T \, ds = \text{Area under curve 2-3} = (h_2 - h_3)
$$

**Step 3 — Net work output:**

$$
W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = \text{Area enclosed by cycle 1-2-3-4-1 on T–s diagram}
$$

**Step 4 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

Using mean temperatures:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_{L,mean} \cdot (s_2 - s_3)}{T_{H,mean} \cdot (s_1 - s_4)}
$$

For a cycle operating between $T_{H,mean}$ and $T_{L,mean}$ (Carnot equivalent):

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,\max} = 1 - \frac{T_{L,mean}}{T_{H,mean}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **the single most important result** in Power Plant Engineering. It tells the designer: *"To improve efficiency, raise $T_{H,mean}$ (superheat/reheat) and lower $T_{L,mean}$ (condense at lower pressure)."*

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Cycle Modifications on T–s Diagram

| Modification | Effect on T–s Diagram | Effect on $\eta_{th}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Superheating** (raise $T_1$ at same P) | Process 4-1 extends further right and up | ↑ (increases $T_{H,mean}$) |
| **Reheating** | Adds a second dome-traversal; area between high & low pressure domes | ↑ (raises mean $T_{H,mean}$) |
| **Regenerative Feedwater Heating** | Bleed steam raises T at pump exit (state 4 moves up) | ↑ (reduces irreversibility of boiler entry) |
| **Lowering Condenser Pressure** | State 3 moves left & down inside dome | ↑ (lowers $T_{L,mean}$) |
| **Increasing Boiler Pressure** | Operating point shifts right at top of dome | Depends — $T_{H,mean}$ ↑ but turbine exit wetness ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 T–s Diagram Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **logical architecture** of the T–s chart for steam, showing all key regions, lines, points, and the standard Rankine cycle overlay.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisY["Vertical Axis: TEMPERATURE T (K)"]
    axisX["Horizontal Axis: SPECIFIC ENTROPY s (kJ/kg.K)"]
    dome["VAPOUR DOME / SATURATION CURVE"]
    critPt["CRITICAL POINT C (T=647.1 K, P=220.64 bar)"]
    subCool["REGION A: SUB-COOLED LIQUID (x = 0, left of dome)"]
    wetReg["REGION B: WET VAPOUR TWO-PHASE (0 < x < 1, inside dome)"]
    superHt["REGION C: SUPERHEATED VAPOUR (x = 1, right of dome)"]
    satLiq["Saturated Liquid Line (x = 0)"]
    satVap["Saturated Vapour Line (x = 1)"]
    rankine["IDEAL RANKINE CYCLE 4-1-2-3-4"]
    boiler["Process 4 to 1: BOILER Heat Addition"]
    turbine["Process 1 to 2: TURBINE Isentropic Expansion (vertical)"]
    cond["Process 2 to 3: CONDENSER Heat Rejection"]
    pump["Process 3 to 4: PUMP Isentropic Compression (vertical)"]
    isotherm["Isotherms: HORIZONTAL lines inside dome"]
    isobar["Isobars: curves in superheat region"]
    isocho["Isochores: steep curves in superheat region"]
    isentrop["Isentropics: VERTICAL lines"]
    areaQt["Area under 4-1 = Q_in"]
    areaQc["Area under 2-3 = Q_out"]
    areaW["Enclosed area 1-2-3-4 = W_net"]

    axisY --> dome
    axisX --> dome
    dome --> subCool
    dome --> wetReg
    dome --> superHt
    dome --> critPt
    dome --> satLiq
    dome --> satVap
    rankine --> boiler
    rankine --> turbine
    rankine --> cond
    rankine --> pump
    boiler --> areaQt
    cond --> areaQc
    rankine --> areaW
    rankine --> isotherm
    rankine --> isobar
    rankine --> isocho
    rankine --> isentrop
```

## 4.2 ASCII Schematic of the T–s Diagram (Reference for Sketches)

```
   T (K)
    |
    |  *C (Critical Point)
    | /\
    |/  \   SUPERHEATED VAPOUR (Region C)
    |    \__________________________
    |   /                            \
    |  /   WET VAPOUR (Region B)      \
    | /  isotherms are horizontal      \
    |/___________________________________\______> s
    |        SUB-COOLED LIQUID (A)
    |
```

**Standard Rankine Cycle States on the Diagram:**

| State | Location on T–s | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Top-right, inside superheat region | Steam exit from superheater (turbine inlet) |
| **2** | Inside wet region (right side of dome) | Steam exit from turbine (often with quality 0.85–0.90) |
| **3** | Bottom of dome (saturated liquid) | Condenser exit (saturated liquid) |
| **4** | Just left of saturated liquid line (very close to 3) | Pump exit (compressed liquid at boiler pressure) |

**Processes drawn:**

- **4 → 1**: Boiler + Superheater (heat addition, $T$ and $s$ both increase). **Area under = $Q_{in}$**.
- **1 → 2**: Turbine (ideal = vertical line, $s$ constant). **No heat transfer.**
- **2 → 3**: Condenser (horizontal-ish line, $T$ constant if inside dome). **Area under = $Q_{out}$**.
- **3 → 4**: Pump (ideal = vertical line, very short because liquid is nearly incompressible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The enclosed area of loop 1-2-3-4 = Net Work Output.** This single graphical fact lets the engineer *see* the cycle efficiency by comparing enclosed area to the rectangle representing $Q_{in}$.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: How T–s Data Feeds Cycle Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateData["State Points from Steam Tables: T, P, h, s, x"]
    plotTS["Plot points on T-s Diagram"]
    identifyProc["Identify processes: Isobaric, Isentropic, Isothermal"]
    areaCalc["Compute area under each process curve"]
    heatIn["Q_in = Area under boiler curve"]
    heatOut["Q_out = Area under condenser curve"]
    workNet["W_net = Enclosed cycle area"]
    meanT["Mean Temperatures T_H_mean, T_L_mean"]
    eff["Thermal Efficiency eta_th = 1 - T_L_mean/T_H_mean"]

    stateData --> plotTS
    plotTS --> identifyProc
    identifyProc --> areaCalc
    areaCalc --> heatIn
    areaCalc --> heatOut
    areaCalc --> workNet
    heatIn --> meanT
    heatOut --> meanT
    meanT --> eff
    workNet --> eff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the Temperature–Entropy (T–s) diagram. Mention any two advantages of using it over the P–v diagram in power plant cycle analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The Temperature–Entropy (T–s) diagram is a thermodynamic chart in which absolute temperature (T) is plotted on the vertical axis and specific entropy (s) on the horizontal axis.

**Two Advantages over P–v diagram:**

1. **Direct heat-transfer readout:** The area under any reversible process curve on the T–s diagram equals the heat transfer ($Q = \int T \, ds$). On the P–v diagram, area equals work, which is only useful for closed systems — not for open, steady-flow devices like turbines and boilers in a power plant.
2. **Superior visualisation of two-phase region:** The vapour dome is wide and clearly resolved on the T–s diagram, making wet-region processes (boiler evaporation, condenser condensation) easy to follow. The P–v diagram compresses the two-phase region into a narrow vertical strip near the saturation line.

*(Third valid point: isotherms are horizontal, simplifying identification of constant-T processes.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**What is meant by the "Mean Temperature of Heat Addition"? How is it represented on the T–s diagram?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Mean Temperature of Heat Addition ($T_{H,mean}$)** is defined as the constant (fictitious) temperature at which an equivalent quantity of heat must be supplied to the working fluid over the same entropy change to produce the same thermodynamic effect as the actual variable-temperature heat addition process.

**Representation on T–s diagram:** It is the *height of the rectangle* whose base equals the entropy change $(s_1 - s_4)$ and whose area equals the actual area under the boiler process curve (4 → 1).

$$
T_{H,mean} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{4}^{1} T \, ds}{s_1 - s_4}
$$

It is a critical parameter in determining the Rankine cycle efficiency, since $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{L,mean}}{T_{H,mean}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Paper, 14 Marks]

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the Temperature–Entropy (T–s) diagram for water. Show the constant-pressure, constant-volume, and constant-entropy lines, and explain the significance of the critical point. (7 marks)**

**(b) For an ideal Rankine cycle operating between a boiler pressure of 100 bar and a condenser pressure of 0.1 bar, with steam superheated to 500 °C, determine the cycle thermal efficiency using the Mean Temperature of Heat Addition method. Use steam table data below. (7 marks)**

**Given Data (from steam tables):**

| State | T (°C) | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (turbine inlet, superheated) | 500 | 3373.7 | 6.5966 |
| 2 (turbine exit, at 0.1 bar) | 45.81 | 2279.2 | 6.5966 |
| 3 (condenser exit, sat. liquid @ 0.1 bar) | 45.81 | 191.81 | 0.6492 |
| 4 (pump exit) | ~46 | ~199.78 | 0.6492 |

### Solution:

#### Part (a) — T–s Diagram Description (7 marks)

The T–s diagram for water is constructed with **absolute temperature (K)** on the ordinate and **specific entropy (kJ/kg·K)** on the abscissa. The key features are:

1. **The Vapour Dome / Saturation Curve:** A bell-shaped curve consisting of:
   - **Saturated liquid line (left branch):** x = 0, from triple point to critical point.
   - **Saturated vapour line (right branch):** x = 1, from triple point to critical point.
   - **Critical Point C** at the apex: $T_c = 647.1$ K, $P_c = 220.64$ bar. Above this point, no distinction exists between liquid and vapour — the substance is a *supercritical fluid*.

2. **Three Regions:**
   - **Sub-cooled liquid region** (left of x = 0 line).
   - **Wet vapour region** (inside the dome, 0 ≤ x ≤ 1).
   - **Superheated vapour region** (right of x = 1 line).

3. **Process lines:**
   - **Constant-pressure (isobars):** Curve sloping gently upward to the right in the superheat region; within the dome they coincide with the saturation lines at the entry and exit points.
   - **Constant-volume (isochores):** Curve steeper than isobars; in superheat region, they rise more steeply as temperature increases.
   - **Constant-entropy (isentropics):** **Vertical straight lines** — the most important line for turbine and pump processes.
   - **Constant-temperature (isotherms):** **Horizontal straight lines within the dome** (unique feature of T–s diagram).

4. **Significance of Critical Point:** Above $T_c$ and $P_c$, there is no phase distinction; water can be heated continuously from liquid-like to vapour-like densities without any two-phase mixture forming. Modern **supercritical power plants** operate above this point to gain efficiency.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.2 above).**

#### Part (b) — Rankine Cycle Efficiency via Mean Temperature Method (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute entropy change in boiler and condenser:**

$$
\Delta s_{boiler} = s_1 - s_4 = 6.5966 - 0.6492 = 5.9474 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

$$
\Delta s_{cond} = s_2 - s_3 = 6.5966 - 0.6492 = 5.9474 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

(Both equal because the cycle is closed: state 1 = state 2 entropy-wise, state 3 = state 4 entropy-wise.)

**Step 2 — Compute heat added in boiler:**

$$
Q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = 3373.7 - 199.78 = 3173.92 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mean temperature of heat addition:**

$$
T_{H,mean} = \frac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s_{boiler}} = \frac{3173.92}{5.9474} = 533.65 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 260.5 \text{ °C})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat rejected in condenser:**

$$
Q_{out} = h_2 - h_3 = 2279.2 - 191.81 = 2087.39 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute mean temperature of heat rejection:**

$$
T_{L,mean} = \frac{Q_{out}}{\Delta s_{cond}} = \frac{2087.39}{5.9474} = 350.98 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 77.83 \text{ °C})
$$

**Step 6 — Compute thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_{L,mean}}{T_{H,mean}} = 1 - \frac{350.98}{533.65} = 1 - 0.6577 = 0.3423
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 34.23\%}
$$

**Verification by direct method:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{2087.39}{3173.92} = 1 - 0.6577 = 0.3423 = 34.23\% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to **convert the pump work** $(h_4 - h_3)$ into $Q_{in}$. Remember: $Q_{in} = h_1 - h_4$, **not** $h_1 - h_3$. The pump raises enthalpy from $h_3$ to $h_4$, so the boiler input is smaller by exactly the pump work. Using $h_1 - h_3$ directly is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B. [Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks]

**(a) Explain with a T–s diagram how superheating and reheating improve the thermal efficiency of a Rankine cycle. (7 marks)**

**(b) A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa, 350 °C, and is condensed at 10 kPa. Determine: (i) the thermal efficiency of the cycle, (ii) the mean temperature of heat addition, and (iii) the mean temperature of heat rejection. (7 marks)**

**Given Data (from steam tables):**

| State | T (°C) | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (turbine inlet, 3 MPa, 350 °C) | 350 | 3115.3 | 6.7428 |
| 2s (turbine exit @ 10 kPa, isentropic) | 45.81 | 2114.4 | 6.7428 |
| 3 (condenser exit, sat. liquid @ 10 kPa) | 45.81 | 191.81 | 0.6492 |
| 4 (pump exit) | ~46 | 192.86 | 0.6492 |

### Solution:

#### Part (a) — Effect of Superheating and Reheating on T–s Diagram (7 marks)

**Superheating:**
On the T–s diagram, superheating extends the boiler exit state from the saturated vapour line into the superheat region (state 1 moves right and up). Two consequences:

1. The **mean temperature of heat addition ($T_{H,mean}$) increases** because the process 4→1 is now stretched into a higher-temperature region, raising the *average* T.
2. The **enclosed cycle area** (which equals $W_{net}$) grows proportionally more than the $Q_{in}$ rectangle.

Result: **$\eta_{th}$ increases.** However, superheat is limited by metallurgical constraints of turbine blades.

**Reheating:**
A portion of the partially expanded steam is extracted from the turbine at an intermediate pressure, sent back to the boiler, reheated (typically back to the original turbine inlet temperature), and then re-expanded through a low-pressure turbine stage. On the T–s diagram:

- The boiler curve splits into **two segments** (primary + reheat).
- The combined area between the high-pressure and low-pressure isentropes is added to the cycle.
- $T_{H,mean}$ rises further because the steam spends *more time at high temperature* during heat addition.

Result: **Substantial increase in $\eta_{th}$** plus reduction of moisture content at the final turbine stage (improves blade life).

**Conclusion:** Both modifications shift the boiler heat-addition profile upward on the T–s diagram, raising $T_{H,mean}$ and thus $\eta_{th} = 1 - T_{L,mean}/T_{H,mean}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the four states:**

- State 1: $P_1 = 3$ MPa, $T_1 = 350$ °C → $h_1 = 3115.3$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K.
- State 2s: $P_2 = 10$ kPa, $s_{2s} = s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K → $h_{2s} = 2114.4$ kJ/kg.
- State 3: sat. liquid @ 10 kPa → $h_3 = 191.81$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 0.6492$ kJ/kg·K.
- State 4: pump exit → $h_4 = h_3 + v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 191.81 + 0.00101 \times (3000 - 10) = 191.81 + 3.03 = 194.84$ kJ/kg. (Using textbook value 192.86 is also accepted.)

**Step 2 — Heat added and rejected:**

$$
Q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = 3115.3 - 192.86 = 2922.44 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{out} = h_{2s} - h_3 = 2114.4 - 191.81 = 1922.59 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Entropy changes:**

$$
\Delta s_{boiler} = s_1 - s_4 = 6.7428 - 0.6492 = 6.0936 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

$$
\Delta s_{cond} = s_{2s} - s_3 = 6.7428 - 0.6492 = 6.0936 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

**Step 4 — (i) Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{1922.59}{2922.44} = 1 - 0.6580 = 0.3420
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 34.20\%}
$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Mean temperature of heat addition:**

$$
T_{H,mean} = \frac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s_{boiler}} = \frac{2922.44}{6.0936} = 479.59 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 206.4 \text{ °C})
$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Mean temperature of heat rejection:**

$$
T_{L,mean} = \frac{Q_{out}}{\Delta s_{cond}} = \frac{1922.59}{6.0936} = 315.54 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 42.4 \text{ °C})
$$

**Step 7 — Verification using Carnot equivalent:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_{L,mean}}{T_{H,mean}} = 1 - \frac{315.54}{479.59} = 1 - 0.6580 = 0.3420 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** When explaining superheating on a T–s diagram, students often draw the curve *wrong* — superheating is **beyond** the saturated vapour line (to the right of the dome), not inside it. Drawing it inside the dome will be marked wrong even if the explanation is correct. Always show the boiler curve **crossing** the saturated vapour line into the superheat region.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — T–s Diagram**

- ✅ The T–s diagram plots **absolute temperature T (K)** on the vertical axis and **specific entropy s (kJ/kg·K)** on the horizontal axis.
- ✅ **Area under any reversible process = heat transfer** ($Q = \int T \, ds$). This is the single most useful property.
- ✅ The **vapour dome** consists of the saturated liquid line (x = 0) and saturated vapour line (x = 1), meeting at the **critical point** (T = 647.1 K, P = 220.64 bar).
- ✅ **Three regions:** sub-cooled liquid, wet vapour (inside dome), superheated vapour (outside dome).
- ✅ **Isotherms are horizontal lines inside the dome** — unique to T–s diagram.
- ✅ **Isentropic processes are vertical lines** — represent ideal turbines, pumps, and compressors.
- ✅ **Enclosed area of any cycle on T–s = Net Work Output.**
- ✅ **Mean Temperature of Heat Addition:**
  $$T_{H,mean} = \frac{Q_{in}}{s_1 - s_4}$$
- ✅ **Mean Temperature of Heat Rejection:**
  $$T_{L,mean} = \frac{Q_{out}}{s_2 - s_3}$$
- ✅ **Thermal Efficiency:**
  $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_{L,mean}}{T_{H,mean}}$$
- ✅ **Superheating** raises $T_{H,mean}$ → efficiency ↑.
- ✅ **Reheating** adds high-temperature heat addition → efficiency ↑.
- ✅ **Lowering condenser pressure** lowers $T_{L,mean}$ → efficiency ↑.
- ✅ In Rankine cycle, since 1→2 and 3→4 are vertical (isentropic), $(s_1 - s_4) = (s_2 - s_3)$.
- ✅ Always use **steam-table entropy values**; never approximate using ideal-gas formulas for steam-cycle problems.
- ✅ Units must be consistent: **K** for temperature, **kJ/kg·K** for entropy, **kJ/kg** for enthalpy/heat.
- ✅ The **isentropic efficiency** of a turbine = (actual enthalpy drop) / (isentropic enthalpy drop) = (horizontal distance on T–s from 1 to actual 2) ÷ (horizontal distance from 1 to ideal 2s) — but always computed from h-values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mollier Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mollier Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Mollier Diagram** (also known as the **h–s Diagram** or **Enthalpy–Entropy Diagram**) is a thermodynamic chart in which **enthalpy (h)** is plotted as the ordinate (vertical axis) against **entropy (s)** as the abscissa (horizontal axis). It was developed by **Richard Mollier** (1904) and provides a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of working substances, most commonly **steam and refrigerants**, used for analyzing power cycles and refrigeration cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "The Mollier Diagram is a chart in which enthalpy is plotted against entropy, used to study the phase changes and thermodynamic processes of steam, particularly in the wet steam region where h-s coordinates simplify the calculation of steam quality, turbine work, pump work, and cycle efficiency."

The fundamental mathematical basis comes from the **Gibbs (T–ds) equation** in differential form:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

For an **isobaric process** (constant pressure, dP = 0), this reduces to:

$$dh = T\,ds \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

This relationship makes the **isobar (constant pressure line) slope directly proportional to absolute temperature**, which is one of the diagram's most elegant features.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are **climbing a mountain** representing the energy content of steam:

- **Horizontal movement** (left → right) represents *disorder* or *randomness* of molecular motion → this is **entropy (s)**.
- **Vertical movement** (bottom → top) represents *usable heat energy* of steam → this is **enthalpy (h)**.
- Each **contour line** on the mountain represents a path of *constant pressure* (like a contour line on a topographical map of equal elevation paths).
- The **"summit"** of the mountain is the **critical point**, beyond which steam becomes a supercritical fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Explanation:**
> Think of the Mollier diagram as a "**steam roadmap**." When water boils, vaporizes, expands through a turbine, and condenses, engineers use this map to determine the energy (enthalpy) at each stage without performing tedious calculations. The horizontal distance tells you how "spread out" the energy is, and the vertical height tells you the total heat energy per kg.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Critical Point of Water)

| Property | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Critical Pressure | **P_cr** | **22.064 MPa (≈ 220.64 bar)** |
| Critical Temperature | **T_cr** | **647.096 K (≈ 373.95 °C)** |
| Critical Specific Volume | **v_cr** | **0.003106 m³/kg** |
| Critical Enthalpy | **h_cr** | **2087.5 kJ/kg** |
| Critical Entropy | **s_cr** | **4.4120 kJ/kg·K** |

> [!NOTE]
> Above the critical point, there is **no distinction between liquid and vapor phases**. The Mollier diagram converges to a single point at this apex, and the wet steam region "closes" into a single point.

---

### 1.4 Three Principal Regions of the Mollier Diagram

1. **Sub-Cooled Liquid Region (Compressed Liquid):** Located to the left of the saturated liquid line; water at temperatures below saturation for a given pressure.

2. **Wet Steam Region (Two-Phase Dome):** The dome-shaped area bounded by the saturated liquid line (left) and saturated vapor line (right). This is where **liquid and vapor coexist in equilibrium**.

3. **Superheated Steam Region:** Located to the right of the saturated vapor line; steam at temperatures above saturation for a given pressure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the Mollier Diagram

The Mollier Diagram is constructed with the following principal lines:

#### A. Axis Orientation
- **Y-axis (Vertical):** Enthalpy, $h$ — measured in **kJ/kg**.
- **X-axis (Horizontal):** Entropy, $s$ — measured in **kJ/(kg·K)**.
- A unique feature: the **X-axis is inclined** in some Mollier diagrams (≈ 30° to 45° from horizontal) to spread out the wet steam region for better readability.

#### B. Saturation Curves
- **Saturated Liquid Line (Left Dome):** Connects the saturated liquid states from triple point to critical point. Sometimes called the **Lower Saturation Curve** or **Bubble Point Line**.
- **Saturated Vapor Line (Right Dome):** Connects the saturated vapor states from triple point to critical point. Sometimes called the **Upper Saturation Curve** or **Dew Point Line**.
- Both lines meet at the **critical point**.

#### C. Constant Property Lines
1. **Isobars (Constant Pressure Lines):** A family of curves sweeping across all three regions. **Slope of isobar** at any point = $T_{absolute}$ at that point.
2. **Isotherms (Constant Temperature Lines):** In the wet steam region, isotherms coincide with isobars (because T and P are dependent via saturation). In the superheated region, they diverge and fall more steeply.
3. **Isochores (Constant Specific Volume Lines):** Primarily in the superheated region.
4. **Lines of Constant Quality (Dryness Fraction, $x$):** Straight radial lines inside the dome, fanning out from a focal point near the critical point.

---

### 2.2 Derivation of Slope of Isobar (Tds Equation)

Starting from the **First Law for a closed system undergoing a reversible process:**

$$dq_{rev} = T\,ds \quad \text{(Clausius equality)}$$

From the **First Law in terms of enthalpy** (steady-flow energy equation simplification):

$$dq_{rev} = dh - v\,dP$$

Setting them equal:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

For an **isobaric process** ($dP = 0$):

$$T\,ds = dh$$

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

This means **the slope of any constant-pressure line at any point equals the absolute temperature at that point** — a critically important property for KTU problems.

---

### 2.3 Quality (Dryness Fraction) Determination on Mollier Diagram

For any point inside the wet steam dome, the **quality $x$** is determined by a **straight-line lever rule** on the h–s diagram:

$$x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_f - h_g} \cdot (-1) = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$$

Equivalently in terms of entropy:

$$x = \frac{s - s_f}{s_g - s_f} = \frac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$$

**Verification on the diagram:** The two values of $x$ (one from enthalpy and one from entropy) must agree for a valid thermodynamic state.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize this table — it contains 90% of the Mollier-diagram-based problems asked in KTU University Examinations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $h = u + Pv$ | Enthalpy definition | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ | Gibbs (T-ds) equation | Universal |
| 3 | $\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$ | Slope of isobar | K (Kelvin) |
| 4 | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | Quality from enthalpy | Dimensionless (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) |
| 5 | $x = \dfrac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | Quality from entropy | Dimensionless (0 ≤ x ≤ 1) |
| 6 | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Wet steam enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Wet steam entropy | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 8 | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | Wet steam internal energy | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Wet steam specific volume | m³/kg |
| 10 | $W_{turbine} = h_1 - h_2$ | Turbine work per kg | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $W_{pump} = h_4 - h_3 = v_3(P_4 - P_3)$ | Pump work per kg | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $Q_{in} = h_1 - h_4$ | Heat added in boiler | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $Q_{out} = h_2 - h_3$ | Heat rejected in condenser | kJ/kg |
| 14 | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Cycle efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 15 | $s_1 = s_2$ | Isentropic turbine process | For ideal turbine |
| 16 | $s_3 = s_4$ | Isentropic pump process | For ideal pump |

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute-value or magnitude expressions in problem solving use the standard convention $\vert h_1 - h_2 \vert$ written as $\lvert h_1 - h_2\rvert$ to avoid markdown table conflicts.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Steam Power Plant Design:** Mollier diagrams are essential for analyzing and optimizing the **Rankine cycle** — the working cycle of all thermal (coal, nuclear, solar thermal) power plants.
2. **Refrigeration and HVAC:** Refrigeration cycles (Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle) are plotted on a Mollier diagram for refrigerant R-134a, R-717 (ammonia), etc.
3. **Compressor Performance Analysis:** Multi-stage compression with intercooling is conveniently traced on h–s charts.
4. **Nozzle and Diffuser Analysis:** Used in steam turbines, rocket nozzles, and gas turbines.
5. **Desalination Plants:** Multi-effect distillation processes rely on Mollier-diagram analyses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency Using Mollier Diagram Coordinates

Consider the **ideal Rankine cycle** with the following four state points:

- **State 1:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser (P₁ = P_condenser, T₁ = T_sat at P₁)
- **State 2:** Compressed liquid leaving the pump (P₂ = P_boiler)
- **State 3:** Saturated vapor entering the turbine (P₃ = P_boiler, T₃ = T_sat at P₂)
- **State 4:** Wet steam leaving the turbine (P₄ = P_condenser)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** Throughout KTU 2024 textbooks, the Rankine cycle states are often labelled **1–2–3–4** (boiler → turbine → condenser → pump → boiler). Below, we will use **1, 2, 3, 4** corresponding to turbine inlet, turbine exit, condenser exit, and pump exit respectively.

#### Step 1 — Apply Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the Turbine

For an adiabatic, isentropic turbine:

$$h_1 + \frac{C_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{C_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + w_t$$

Neglecting kinetic energy (ΔKE ≈ 0) and potential energy (ΔPE ≈ 0) changes:

$$w_t = h_1 - h_2 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply SFEE to the Condenser

For an isobaric heat rejection process:

$$q_{out} = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply SFEE to the Pump

For an adiabatic, isentropic pump (low-grade liquid compression):

$$w_p = h_4 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

For an incompressible liquid (which is the case here since liquid water is nearly incompressible):

$$w_p = \int_{P_3}^{P_4} v\,dP \approx v_3(P_4 - P_3)$$

where $v_3$ is the specific volume of saturated liquid at the condenser pressure.

#### Step 4 — Apply SFEE to the Boiler

For an isobaric heat addition process:

$$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

#### Step 5 — Net Work Output and Thermal Efficiency

$$w_{net} = w_t - w_p = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute Typical Numerical Values (Demonstration)

Let us assume a typical subcritical power plant with the following Mollier-diagram readings:

| State Point | $P$ (bar) | $T$ (°C) | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $s$ (kJ/kg·K) | Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 100 | 500 | 3375 | 6.60 | Superheated steam |
| 2 | 0.1 | 45.8 | 2240 | 6.60 | Wet steam |
| 3 | 0.1 | 45.8 | 192 | 0.65 | Saturated liquid |
| 4 | 100 | 45.9 | 202 | 0.65 | Compressed liquid |

**Calculating each term:**

$$w_t = h_1 - h_2 = 3375 - 2240 = 1135 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_p = v_3(P_4 - P_3) = 0.00101 \times (10000 - 10) \approx 10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = 3375 - 202 = 3173 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$q_{out} = h_2 - h_3 = 2240 - 192 = 2048 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{net} = 1135 - 10 = 1125 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{1125}{3173} = 0.3545 = 35.45\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Pump work is **negligible** (≈ 1%) compared to turbine work. This is a key KTU takeaway. Therefore, in approximate calculations, many textbooks approximate $w_{net} \approx w_t$ and the efficiency simplifies to $\eta \approx \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_4}$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Superheat on Cycle Performance

| Parameter | Effect of Increased Superheat |
| :--- | :--- |
| Turbine work ($w_t = h_1 - h_2$) | **Increases** (since $h_1$ increases more than $h_2$) |
| Heat added ($q_{in}$) | **Increases** (since $h_1$ increases) |
| Thermal efficiency ($\eta_{th}$) | **Increases** initially, then plateaus |
| Steam quality at turbine exit ($x_2$) | **Increases** (less moisture, less blade erosion) |
| Boiler exit temperature | **Increases** (limited by metallurgical constraints) |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Condenser Pressure Reduction

| Parameter | Effect of Lower Condenser Pressure |
| :--- | :--- |
| Turbine work | **Increases** (larger enthalpy drop) |
| Heat rejected | **Decreases** (lower $h_2$) |
| Thermal efficiency | **Increases** |
| Steam quality at turbine exit | **Decreases** (wetter steam → blade erosion risk) |
| Cooling water requirement | **Increases** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic: Regions and Key Features of the Mollier Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart represents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Map** of the Mollier Diagram, identifying each region, boundary, and key process point.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP["MOLLIER DIAGRAM - STRUCTURAL MAP"]
        direction TB
        subgraph DOME["WET STEAM REGION TWO PHASE DOME"]
            direction LR
            subgraph DOMELEFT["LEFT BOUNDARY"]
                satL["SATURATED LIQUID LINE h_f versus s_f"]
            end
            subgraph DOMEINTERIOR["INSIDE DOME"]
                xline1["CONSTANT QUALITY LINE x EQUALS 0.2"]
                xline2["CONSTANT QUALITY LINE x EQUALS 0.5"]
                xline3["CONSTANT QUALITY LINE x EQUALS 0.8"]
                domePoint["POINT x h s INSIDE DOME"]
                domePoint -.- xline1
                domePoint -.- xline2
                domePoint -.- xline3
            end
            subgraph DOMERIGHT["RIGHT BOUNDARY"]
                satV["SATURATED VAPOR LINE h_g versus s_g"]
            end
            satL --- satV
        end
        subgraph LEFTOUTSIDE["SUB COOLED LIQUID REGION"]
            leftPoint["POINT A Compressed Liquid"]
        end
        subgraph RIGHTOUTSIDE["SUPERHEATED STEAM REGION"]
            rightPoint["POINT B Superheated Steam"]
            subgraph ISO["CONSTANT PROPERTY LINES"]
                pLine1["ISOBAR P EQUALS 10 bar"]
                pLine2["ISOBAR P EQUALS 100 bar"]
                tLine["ISOTHERM T EQUALS 500 C"]
                vLine["ISOCHORE v CONSTANT"]
            end
        end
        subgraph CRITICALPOINT["APEX OF DOME"]
            crit["CRITICAL POINT 22.064 MPa 647.1 K"]
        end
        crit --- satL
        crit --- satV
    end
    LEFTOUTSIDE --- DOME
    RIGHTOUTSIDE --- DOME
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic: Ideal Rankine Cycle Plotted on Mollier Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RK["RANKINE CYCLE PROCESSES ON h-s DIAGRAM"]
        direction TB
        state4["STATE 4 Pump Exit h_4 s_4 Compressed Liquid at Boiler Pressure"]
        state1["STATE 1 Boiler Exit h_1 s_1 Saturated or Superheated Steam at Boiler Pressure"]
        state2["STATE 2 Turbine Exit h_2 s_2 Wet Steam at Condenser Pressure"]
        state3["STATE 3 Condenser Exit h_3 s_3 Saturated Liquid at Condenser Pressure"]
        p1["PROCESS 4 to 1 BOILER Isobaric Heat Addition q_in h_1 minus h_4"]
        p2["PROCESS 1 to 2 TURBINE Isentropic Expansion w_t h_1 minus h_2"]
        p3["PROCESS 2 to 3 CONDENSER Isobaric Heat Rejection q_out h_2 minus h_3"]
        p4["PROCESS 3 to 4 PUMP Isentropic Compression w_p h_4 minus h_3"]
        state4 --> p1 --> state1
        state1 --> p2 --> state2
        state2 --> p3 --> state3
        state3 --> p4 --> state4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Vertical drop** from state 1 to state 2 represents **turbine work output** ($w_t$).
> - **Vertical rise** from state 3 to state 4 represents **pump work input** ($w_p$).
> - **Horizontal movement** represents isentropic processes (turbine, pump).
> - **Vertical climb** along an isobar represents heat addition (boiler) or heat rejection (condenser).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
Define the Mollier Diagram. Why is it preferred over the T-s diagram for analyzing steam power plant cycles?

**Model Answer:**

The Mollier Diagram is a thermodynamic chart in which **enthalpy (h)** is plotted as the ordinate against **entropy (s)** as the abscissa. It is also called the **h–s diagram**.

**Advantages over T-s diagram:**

1. **Direct read-out of work and heat:** Turbine work = vertical drop = $h_1 - h_2$; Heat added = vertical rise = $h_1 - h_4$. No need to compute area under the curve.
2. **Wet steam region is enlarged** (when the s-axis is inclined), improving readability of quality lines.
3. **Quality ($x$) is constant on straight radial lines**, making quality determination a simple proportional calculation.
4. **Heat transfer quantities are directly readable** as vertical distances on the diagram.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
What is the significance of the slope of an isobar on the Mollier Diagram? What does the slope of the isobar represent at the critical point?

**Model Answer:**

From the **Gibbs (T-ds) equation** for an isobaric process:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

Hence, the slope of any **constant pressure (isobar)** line at any point equals the **absolute temperature** at that point (in Kelvin).

**At the critical point:**
$$T = T_{cr} = 647.096 \text{ K} \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_{P_{cr}} = T_{cr}$$

So the slope of the isobar passing through the critical point equals **647.1 K**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]**

**(a)** Sketch the Mollier Diagram for steam and label all its important regions, curves, and lines. Explain the significance of constant-pressure and constant-quality lines. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Rankine cycle operating between 30 bar and 0.05 bar, steam enters the turbine at 30 bar and 350 °C. The condenser produces saturated liquid. Determine:
1. Turbine work output
2. Pump work input
3. Heat supplied in the boiler
4. Thermal efficiency of the cycle

Use Mollier diagram values: At 30 bar, 350 °C: $h_1 = 3125$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.85$ kJ/kg·K. At 0.05 bar: $h_f = 138$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2423$ kJ/kg, $s_f = 0.476$ kJ/kg·K, $s_{fg} = 7.918$ kJ/kg·K, $v_f = 0.001005$ m³/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q3:**

**(a) Mollier Diagram Sketch and Explanation (7 Marks):**

A labeled Mollier Diagram must include:

| Element | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Y-axis | Enthalpy (h) in kJ/kg |
| X-axis | Entropy (s) in kJ/(kg·K), possibly inclined |
| Saturated liquid line | Lower curve of the dome |
| Saturated vapor line | Upper curve of the dome |
| Critical point | Apex of the dome |
| Sub-cooled liquid region | To the left of saturated liquid line |
| Wet steam region | Inside the dome |
| Superheated steam region | To the right of saturated vapor line |
| Constant pressure lines (isobars) | Slope = absolute temperature |
| Constant temperature lines (isotherms) | Coincide with isobars in wet region |
| Lines of constant dryness fraction ($x$) | Straight radial lines inside the dome |

**Significance of constant-pressure lines:** The slope of an isobar at any point equals the absolute temperature at that state, derived from the Tds equation. Hence, isobars fan out upward as we move to higher temperatures.

**Significance of constant-quality lines:** These straight lines help determine steam quality ($x$) by the lever rule: $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Turbine exit state (state 2). For isentropic expansion:
$$s_2 = s_1 = 6.85 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 2:** Quality at turbine exit:
$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.85 - 0.476}{7.918} = \frac{6.374}{7.918}$$

$$x_2 = 0.8050$$

**Step 3:** Enthalpy at turbine exit:
$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 138 + (0.8050)(2423)$$

$$h_2 = 138 + 1950.5 = 2088.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4:** Pump work (state 3 = condenser exit, state 4 = pump exit):
$$w_p = v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 0.001005 \times (3000 - 5) \text{ kPa}$$

$$w_p = 0.001005 \times 2995 = 3.010 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_4 = h_3 + w_p = 138 + 3.01 = 141.01 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Turbine work:
$$w_t = h_1 - h_2 = 3125 - 2088.5 = 1036.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6:** Heat supplied in boiler:
$$q_{in} = h_1 - h_4 = 3125 - 141.01 = 2983.99 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Net work:
$$w_{net} = w_t - w_p = 1036.5 - 3.01 = 1033.49 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8:** Thermal efficiency:
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{1033.49}{2983.99} = 0.3464 = 34.64\%$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]**
