**(a)** State the T-ds equation. Using it, prove that the slope of an isobar on the Mollier Diagram equals the absolute temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Dry saturated steam at 20 bar expands isentropically in a turbine to 0.1 bar. Determine the dryness fraction and enthalpy at the turbine exit. If the turbine has an isentropic efficiency of 85%, determine the actual enthalpy at the turbine exit and the work output per kg of steam. Given: At 20 bar saturated vapor: $h_g = 2799$ kJ/kg, $s_g = 6.340$ kJ/kg·K. At 0.1 bar: $h_f = 192$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392$ kJ/kg, $s_f = 0.649$ kJ/kg·K, $s_{fg} = 7.501$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q4:**

**(a) Proof of Isobar Slope = Absolute Temperature (7 Marks):**

**Statement of T-ds (Gibbs) Equation:**

For a pure substance undergoing a reversible process, the combined first and second laws yield:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv$$

Since enthalpy is defined as $h = u + Pv$, differentiating gives $dh = du + P\,dv + v\,dP$. Substituting:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

For an **isobaric process**, $dP = 0$, hence:

$$dh = T\,ds$$

Dividing both sides by $ds$ (at constant pressure):

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

Since $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin), the slope of the constant-pressure line on the h–s diagram equals the absolute temperature at that state. **Q.E.D.**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify state 1 (turbine inlet — dry saturated steam at 20 bar):
$$h_1 = h_g = 2799 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$s_1 = s_g = 6.340 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 2:** State 2s (isentropic turbine exit at 0.1 bar). Since $s_{2s} = s_1$:
$$s_{2s} = 6.340 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 3:** Dryness fraction at state 2s:
$$x_{2s} = \frac{s_{2s} - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.340 - 0.649}{7.501} = \frac{5.691}{7.501}$$

$$x_{2s} = 0.7587$$

**Step 4:** Isentropic exit enthalpy:
$$h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 192 + (0.7587)(2392)$$

$$h_{2s} = 192 + 1814.81 = 2006.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Isentropic work output:
$$w_{s} = h_1 - h_{2s} = 2799 - 2006.81 = 792.19 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6:** Actual work output (using isentropic efficiency $\eta_t = 0.85$):
$$w_{actual} = \eta_t \cdot w_s = 0.85 \times 792.19 = 673.36 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Actual exit enthalpy:
$$h_{2,actual} = h_1 - w_{actual} = 2799 - 673.36 = 2125.64 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Isentropic dryness fraction, $x_{2s}$ | **0.7587** |
| Isentropic exit enthalpy, $h_{2s}$ | **2006.81 kJ/kg** |
| Actual exit enthalpy, $h_{2,actual}$ | **2125.64 kJ/kg** |
| Actual turbine work output | **673.36 kJ/kg** |

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the axis labels:** Students often write "T-s diagram" instead of "h-s diagram." The Mollier Diagram is **specifically** the h-s chart, NOT the T-s chart. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the dryness fraction formula:** Direct substitution of $h_2 = h_f + h_{fg}$ is **wrong** when the exit state is wet steam. Always compute $x_2$ first.
> 
> 3. **Pump work sign convention:** $w_p$ is **work input** (positive when energy is added to the fluid). The cycle work is $w_{net} = w_t - w_p$.
> 
> 4. **Units of entropy:** KTU accepts $\text{kJ/kg·K}$ or $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Do NOT use $\text{J/kg·K}$ unless explicitly converted.
> 
> 5. **Critical point values:** Some students write $374 °C$ as 374 K. Always report $T_{cr} = 647.1$ K or $373.95 °C$ — both correct forms accepted.
> 
> 6. **Inclined-axis Mollier diagrams:** When sketching, clearly indicate the inclination angle (≈ 30°–45°) and label both axes correctly.
> 
> 7. **State numbering:** KTU 2024 follows the convention **Boiler exit = 1, Turbine exit = 2, Condenser exit = 3, Pump exit = 4.** Mixing up the convention loses marks even if the math is correct.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY LAST-MINUTE REVISION CHECKLIST:**
> 
> ✅ **Mollier Diagram = h-s chart** (Enthalpy on Y-axis, Entropy on X-axis; sometimes X-axis is inclined).
> 
> ✅ **Three regions:** Sub-cooled liquid (left), Wet steam dome (middle), Superheated steam (right).
> 
> ✅ **Apex of dome = Critical point** (P_cr = 22.064 MPa, T_cr = 647.1 K).
> 
> ✅ **Tds equation:** $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$.
> 
> ✅ **Slope of isobar = T (absolute temperature)** — this is a KTU favorite derivation question.
> 
> ✅ **Wet steam properties** ($h, u, v, s$): all calculated as $y = y_f + x \cdot y_{fg}$.
> 
> ✅ **Quality x is determined by lever rule** using either enthalpy or entropy.
> 
> ✅ **Rankine cycle states on h-s diagram:** Vertical drops = work; Vertical rises on isobars = heat; Horizontal movement = isentropic process.
> 
> ✅ **Pump work approximation:** $w_p \approx v_f (P_{boiler} - P_{condenser})$, often **negligible** compared to turbine work.
> 
> ✅ **Turbine isentropic efficiency:** $\eta_t = \dfrac{h_1 - h_{2,actual}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$.
> 
> ✅ **Thermal efficiency formula:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$.
> 
> ✅ **Sketching tip:** Always label **axes, dome boundaries, critical point, isobars, isotherms, constant-x lines**, and **Rankine cycle states** (1-2-3-4) when drawing the Mollier diagram in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rankine Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Rankine Cycle** is an idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of **steam power plants** (also called Rankine engines). It is the fundamental cycle used to convert heat energy (typically from combustion of fossil fuels, nuclear reactions, or concentrated solar power) into mechanical work, which is then converted into electrical energy by a generator. The working fluid undergoes a closed loop, undergoing phase changes between liquid and vapor states.

The cycle consists of **four internally reversible processes** that operate between a high-pressure boiler and a low-pressure condenser:

| Process | Location | Thermodynamic State Change | Phase |
|---------|----------|---------------------------|-------|
| **1 → 2** | Feedwater Pump | Isentropic Compression | Subcooled Liquid |
| **2 → 3** | Steam Generator / Boiler | Isobaric Heat Addition | Liquid → Superheated Vapor |
| **3 → 4** | Steam Turbine | Isentropic Expansion | Superheated Vapor → Wet Vapor |
| **4 → 1** | Surface Condenser | Isobaric Heat Rejection | Wet Vapor → Saturated Liquid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Rankine Cycle is the **backbone of Module 1** in Power Plant Engineering. Every sub-topic in this module (reheat, regeneration, superheat, cogeneration) is built upon this cycle. Mastering it is non-negotiable for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Boiling Kettle on Wheels"

Imagine you have a closed-loop system of pipes connected to a kettle (boiler) and an old-fashioned steam locomotive (turbine):

1. You **pressurize cold water** with a hand pump (Process 1-2). This takes a little effort.
2. You send this high-pressure water into a sealed kettle over a fire. The water boils and turns into high-pressure steam (Process 2-3). Energy is *added* to the system.
3. The rushing steam hits a paddle wheel (turbine), spinning it and producing **mechanical work** (Process 3-4). Steam loses energy and pressure.
4. The exhausted, low-pressure steam enters a cooling chamber (condenser) where it meets cold pipes and **condenses back to water**, releasing waste heat (Process 4-1).

The condensed water is then re-pumped, and the cycle restarts. In a real power plant, the "paddle wheel" is a multi-stage turbine coupled to a 1000 MW generator, but the *logic* is identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from the Carnot Cycle:** The Rankine Cycle is the **practical, real-world version** of the Carnot Vapor Cycle. It avoids the impractical isothermal heat addition/rejection of the Carnot cycle (which would require moving the piston through a two-phase mixture at variable volume) by using an isobaric (constant pressure) heat exchange in the boiler and condenser, making it compatible with the design of actual industrial heat exchangers.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_1 = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- **Specific Enthalpy of Saturated Water at 100°C:** $h_f = 419.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization at 100°C:** $h_{fg} = 2257.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Specific Volume of Saturated Liquid at 100°C:** $v_f = 0.001043 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process-by-Process Operational Breakdown

### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression (Feedwater Pump)
- **Where:** Liquid water leaves the condenser (State 1) as a **saturated liquid** at low pressure $P_1$ and enters the pump.
- **What happens:** The pump raises the pressure of the liquid to the boiler pressure $P_2$ with a minimal increase in temperature.
- **Why isentropic?** The pump is modeled as a **reversible adiabatic device**. In a real plant, isentropic efficiency ($\eta_p$) accounts for friction and internal leakage (typically 70–90%).
- **Energy Cost:** Very low work input because the specific volume of liquid water is tiny.

### Process 2 → 3: Isobaric (Constant Pressure) Heat Addition (Boiler)
- **Where:** Compressed liquid enters the boiler at State 2.
- **What happens:** Heat $Q_{in}$ is added at *constant pressure* $P_2$ in three sub-stages:
  - **2 → a:** Sensible heating of subcooled liquid up to saturation temperature.
  - **a → b:** Phase change from saturated liquid to saturated vapor (latent heat absorbed).
  - **b → 3:** Superheating of vapor to turbine inlet temperature.
- **Why constant pressure?** It is the natural operating condition of a boiler shell; the fluid flows through tubes of essentially uniform cross-section.

### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (Steam Turbine)
- **Where:** Superheated steam at State 3 (high $P_3$, high $T_3$) enters the turbine and expands to condenser pressure $P_4$.
- **What happens:** The steam does **useful work** on the turbine blades, causing the rotor to spin. Pressure and temperature drop, and moisture content increases.
- **Limitation:** The expansion must stop *before* moisture content exceeds ~10–12% to avoid blade erosion (this is why superheat is used).

### Process 4 → 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser)
- **Where:** Wet vapor at State 4 enters the condenser.
- **What happens:** Heat $Q_{out}$ is rejected to a cooling medium (river water, sea water, or cooling tower) at constant pressure $P_1$. The wet vapor fully condenses back to saturated liquid.
- **Why constant pressure?** Heat is rejected at a temperature slightly above the cooling water temperature, so the condenser must operate at low pressure (often below atmospheric).

## 2.2 The Four Idealization Assumptions

For a textbook "Ideal Rankine Cycle," KTU examiners expect students to assume:

1. **Steady-flow, steady-state** operation (no mass accumulation in components).
2. **No pressure drop** in the boiler and condenser piping.
3. **Isentropic** processes in the pump and turbine (100% internal efficiency).
4. **Turbine exhaust** is a saturated vapor or low-quality mixture (no superheat at State 4).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you will need for a 14-mark Rankine Cycle problem on the KTU ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula (Ideal) | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|----------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Pump Work | $W_P = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Energy input required |
| 2 | Pump Work (Approx.) | $W_P \approx v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | $v_1 \approx v_f$ at State 1 |
| 3 | Turbine Work | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Useful energy output |
| 4 | Net Work | $W_{net} = W_T - W_P$ | kJ/kg | Drives the generator |
| 5 | Heat Added | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Energy from fuel/combustion |
| 6 | Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Energy lost to cooling water |
| 7 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | dimensionless (× 100 for %) | **Most important KTU formula** |
| 8 | Work Ratio (Back Work Ratio) | $R_{bw} = \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | Should be small (0.01–0.05) |
| 9 | Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Mass flow per unit power |
| 10 | Work Output (Total) | $\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{net}$ | kW | Multiply by mass flow rate |
| 11 | Heat Input Rate | $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m} \cdot Q_{in}$ | kW | Total fuel energy rate |
| 12 | Carnot Efficiency Reference | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | For comparison only |
| 13 | Steam Quality at Turbine Exit | $x_4 = \dfrac{h_4 - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | dimensionless | $h_f, h_{fg}$ at $P_4$ |
| 14 | Isentropic Pump Enthalpy | $h_2 = h_1 + v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | Direct calculation from liquid tables |
| 15 | Isentropic Turbine Exit | $h_4 = h_3 - (s_3 - s_f)\cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | If State 4 is wet (low $P_4$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Warning on Tables:** In the formula above for the specific steam consumption, the value **3600** represents the number of seconds in one hour. Do not confuse it with enthalpy values. Similarly, when reading superheated steam tables, the first column is *Pressure*, the second is *Temperature*, and subsequent columns are $v, h, s$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Rankine Cycle is not just an academic exercise. It is the **dominant thermodynamic cycle for global electricity generation**, accounting for approximately **80% of all thermal power generation worldwide**:

- **Coal-fired supercritical plants:** Operate at $P \approx 250 \text{ bar}$ and $T \approx 600°C$, reaching thermal efficiencies of 42–45%.
- **Nuclear power plants:** Use a slightly modified Rankine cycle called the **Indirect Rankine Cycle** (or PWR/BWR) where the reactor heats a secondary loop; the working fluid remains pure water/steam.
- **Geothermal binary plants:** Use an organic working fluid (ORC) when the heat source is too low in temperature for water.
- **Solar Thermal Power (Concentrated Solar Power - CSP):** Uses the Rankine cycle with thermal energy storage (molten salts) to generate electricity even after sunset.

The fundamental engineering "why" behind all these applications is that the Rankine Cycle is the **only practical way to convert a wide range of heat source temperatures into large-scale, dispatchable electrical power**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 1. We will derive it from the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a cyclic, steady-flow system.

### Starting Principle

By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a closed cycle (the working fluid eventually returns to its initial state), the net change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero:

$$
\Delta u_{cycle} = 0
$$

For a **cyclic heat engine**, the First Law reduces to the form:

$$
\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W
$$

This means: **Net heat transfer in the cycle = Net work done in the cycle.**

### Step 1: Express the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for each component

For any steady-flow device, the SFEE (neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes) is:

$$
\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_{out} - h_{in})
$$

For a unit mass flow ($\dot{m} = 1 \text{ kg/s}$):

$$
q - w = h_{out} - h_{in}
$$

### Step 2: Apply SFEE to the Pump (Process 1-2)

The pump is adiabatic ($Q = 0$) and isentropic:

$$
-w_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

Therefore, the **work input to the pump** is:

$$
W_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

### Step 3: Apply SFEE to the Boiler (Process 2-3)

The boiler has no work output ($W = 0$) and operates at constant pressure:

$$
q_{in} = h_3 - h_2
$$

### Step 4: Apply SFEE to the Turbine (Process 3-4)

The turbine is adiabatic ($Q = 0$) and isentropic:

$$
w_T = h_3 - h_4
$$

Therefore, the **work output from the turbine** is:

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4
$$

### Step 5: Apply SFEE to the Condenser (Process 4-1)

The condenser has no work output ($W = 0$) and operates at constant pressure:

$$
-q_{out} = h_1 - h_4
$$

Therefore, the **heat rejected to the cooling medium** is:

$$
Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1
$$

### Step 6: Compute Net Work

The net work output of the cycle is the turbine work minus the pump work (pump consumes a small portion of the turbine's output):

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 4:

$$
W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)
$$

### Step 7: Define Thermal Efficiency

The **thermal efficiency** of any heat engine is defined as the ratio of desired work output to required heat input:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Net Work Output}}{\text{Heat Input}}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 3 and 6:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

### Final Result (Aligned Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th, \text{Rankine}} &= 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \\
&= \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} \\
&= 1 - \frac{(h_4 - h_1)}{(h_3 - h_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the answer:** A student may ask, "Why is pump work subtracted, not added?" The answer: The pump is *driven by* an external motor (or by a small steam-driven auxiliary). The *net* work delivered to the generator is the work produced by the turbine minus the work consumed by the pump. This is analogous to gross income minus expenses = net profit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Approximate Pump Work

The pump work can be calculated directly from the property tables, but for a *liquid* (which is nearly incompressible), KTU examiners expect a simpler approximation.

### Step 1: General Pump Work

For an isentropic process on a liquid, the work is:

$$
W_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

### Step 2: Approximate Enthalpy Change

For an incompressible substance, the change in enthalpy is:

$$
dh = v \, dP
$$

Integrating from State 1 to State 2 (assuming $v \approx v_1$ for the liquid):

$$
h_2 - h_1 = v_1 \int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Integral

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_2 - h_1 &= v_1 \cdot [P]_1^2 \\
h_2 - h_1 &= v_1 (P_2 - P_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{W_P \approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** At $P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa}$, $v_1 = v_f \approx 0.00101 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, and $P_2 = 10 \text{ MPa} = 10{,}000 \text{ kPa}$:
>
> $W_P = 0.00101 \times (10{,}000 - 10) = 0.00101 \times 9990 \approx 10.09 \text{ kJ/kg}$.
>
> Compare this to the turbine work, typically 600–1200 kJ/kg. The pump work is less than **2%** of the turbine work — which is why it is often called the *back work ratio*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)

The **Specific Steam Consumption** tells us how many kilograms of steam are required to generate 1 kWh of electrical energy.

### Step 1: Energy Required for 1 kWh

By definition:

$$
1 \text{ kWh} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ hour} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3600 \text{ kJ}
$$

### Step 2: Net Work per kg of Steam

The net work delivered per kg of steam is $W_{net}$ in kJ/kg.

### Step 3: Form the Ratio

To produce 3600 kJ of work, we need $3600 / W_{net}$ kilograms of steam.

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{SSC = \frac{3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}}{W_{net} \text{ kJ/kg}} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}} \right]}
$$

A **lower SSC means a more efficient cycle**. For modern supercritical plants, SSC is typically around 2.0–2.5 kg/kWh.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Rankine vs. Carnot Vapor Cycle

This is a frequent KTU Part-A question. The comparison must be memorized.

| Parameter | Carnot Vapor Cycle | Ideal Rankine Cycle |
|-----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Heat Addition Process | **Isothermal** (very hard to achieve) | **Isobaric** (easy to build) |
| Heat Rejection Process | **Isothermal** (very hard to achieve) | **Isobaric** (easy to build) |
| Compression Process | Wet compression (liquid + vapor mixture) — **impractical** | **Liquid-only** compression — practical |
| Expansion Process | Wet expansion to condenser pressure | Dry/superheated expansion — practical |
| Pump Work | Large (compresses two-phase mixture) | **Small** (compresses only liquid) |
| Turbine Exhaust | Saturated vapor | Saturated vapor (ideal) / wet (real) |
| Practical Realizability | **No** | **Yes** (basis of all steam power plants) |
| Efficiency for Same $T_H, T_L$ | Higher (theoretical maximum) | Slightly lower, but **practically achievable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often erroneously claim the Rankine Cycle has the *same* efficiency as the Carnot cycle. It does **not**. The Rankine cycle's efficiency is *less than* the Carnot efficiency operating between the same $T_{max}$ and $T_{min}$ because the average temperature of heat addition in the Rankine cycle is lower.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Real Plant Component Map

The following diagram maps the **physical architecture** of a real Rankine Cycle steam power plant. The working fluid (water/steam) flows clockwise from the condenser to the boiler and back.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CONDENSER\nState 1: Saturated Liquid\nLow Pressure P1] -->|Pump Work WP| B[FEEDWATER PUMP\nState 2: Compressed Liquid\nHigh Pressure P2]
    B -->|Heat Qin Added| C[BOILER / STEAM GENERATOR\nState 3: Superheated Steam\nHigh Pressure P2, High T3]
    C -->|Turbine Work WT| D[STEAM TURBINE\nState 4: Wet Vapor or Sat Vapor\nLow Pressure P1]
    D -->|Heat Qout Rejected| A
    C -.->|Coupled to| E[Electrical Generator\nAlternator: 50 Hz AC]
    A -.->|Cooling water from| F[Cooling Tower\nor River Water]
    B -.->|Driven by| G[Auxiliary Motor\nor Steam-driven Pump]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Diagram: Four Internally Reversible Processes

This diagram isolates the **thermodynamic processes** on a T-s (Temperature-Entropy) plane representation. In an ideal Rankine cycle, the path traced is 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression\nPump: Liquid water pressurized\nEntropy s1 = s2, Pressure rises]
    P2[Process 2 to 3: Isobaric Heat Addition\nBoiler: Liquid to Superheated Vapor\nPressure P2 constant, s increases]
    P3[Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion\nTurbine: High pressure to low pressure\nEntropy s3 = s4, Pressure drops]
    P4[Process 4 to 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection\nCondenser: Wet vapor to saturated liquid\nPressure P1 constant, s decreases]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P1
```

## 4.3 State Point Table — Ideal Rankine Cycle Reference

The following table provides a **schematic snapshot** of the four key state points. This is the "signature" that KTU examiners expect students to sketch and label.

| State | Location | Pressure | Temperature | Phase Description | Typical $h$ Value |
|-------|----------|----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Pump Inlet (Condenser Exit) | Low ($P_1$) | Low ($T_1$) | Saturated Liquid | $h_1 = h_f \text{ at } P_1$ |
| 2 | Pump Outlet (Boiler Inlet) | High ($P_2$) | Slightly higher | Compressed Liquid | $h_2 = h_1 + v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ |
| 3 | Boiler Outlet (Turbine Inlet) | High ($P_2$) | Very High ($T_3$) | Superheated Vapor | Read from Superheated Tables |
| 4 | Turbine Outlet (Condenser Inlet) | Low ($P_1$) | Low ($T_4$) | Wet Vapor (or Sat. Vapor) | $h_4 = h_3 - W_T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When drawing the T-s diagram, always mark the **saturation dome** (the bell-shaped curve separating liquid and vapor regions). The line 1-2 lies to the left of the dome (compressed liquid), 2-3 cuts across the dome (entering the vapor region), 3-4 lies inside the dome (wet vapor region for ideal cycle), and 4-1 lies along the bottom of the dome (saturated liquid line).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the Rankine cycle. List its four processes with the corresponding components in a steam power plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Rankine cycle** is an idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of steam power plants, where a working fluid (water/steam) undergoes a closed loop to convert heat into work.

| Process | Thermodynamic Process | Component |
|---------|----------------------|-----------|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic compression | Feedwater Pump |
| 2 → 3 | Isobaric heat addition | Boiler |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic expansion | Steam Turbine |
| 4 → 1 | Isobaric heat rejection | Condenser |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the table)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Why is the Rankine cycle preferred over the Carnot cycle for steam power plants, even though the Carnot cycle has higher efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The **Carnot cycle** requires isothermal heat addition and rejection, which is extremely difficult to achieve in practice with a two-phase working fluid (you would need a compressor/turbine to handle a liquid-vapor mixture, which is impractical). *(1 mark)*
2. The **Carnot cycle** uses wet compression in the pump, which causes mechanical damage (cavitation) and is thermodynamically inefficient. The Rankine cycle uses a **liquid-only pump**, which is mechanically simple and efficient. *(1 mark)*
3. The Rankine cycle operates with **isobaric (constant pressure)** heat addition in the boiler and heat rejection in the condenser, which matches the design of real heat exchangers. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**In an ideal Rankine cycle, steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa and 350°C. The condenser pressure is 10 kPa. Determine:**

**(a)** The thermal efficiency of the cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The specific steam consumption (SSC) and the rate of steam flow required to produce 100 MW of net power. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**State 1: Condenser Exit (Saturated Liquid at 10 kPa)**

From saturated water tables at $P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa}$:
- $h_1 = h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $v_1 = v_f = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $s_1 = s_f = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**State 2: Pump Exit**

Pump work:
$$
W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10)
$$
$$
W_P = 0.001010 \times 2990 = 3.020 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$
*(1 mark)*

Enthalpy at State 2:
$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$
*(1 mark)*

**State 3: Turbine Inlet (Superheated Steam at 3 MPa, 350°C)**

From superheated steam tables at $P_3 = 3 \text{ MPa}$ and $T_3 = 350°C$:
- $h_3 = 2988.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_3 = 6.7420 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
*(1 mark)*

**State 4: Turbine Exit (Isentropic Expansion to 10 kPa)**

For an isentropic process: $s_4 = s_3 = 6.7420 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

At $P_4 = 10 \text{ kPa}$:
- $s_f = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $s_{fg} = 7.4996 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2392.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$

Quality of steam at exit:
$$
x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7420 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = \frac{6.0928}{7.4996} = 0.8124
$$
*(1 mark)*

Enthalpy at State 4:
$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8124 \times 2392.1
$$
$$
h_4 = 191.81 + 1943.34 = 2135.15 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$
*(1 mark)*

**Performance Calculations**

Turbine work:
$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 2135.15 = 852.95 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Net work:
$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 852.95 - 3.02 = 849.93 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Heat input:
$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 194.83 = 2793.27 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Thermal efficiency:
$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{849.93}{2793.27} = 0.3043 = 30.43\%
$$
*(2 marks for final efficiency)*

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):**
$$
SSC = \frac{3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}}{W_{net}} = \frac{3600}{849.93} = 4.235 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$
*(2 marks)*

**Mass Flow Rate for 100 MW:**

Convert power to kW: $100 \text{ MW} = 100{,}000 \text{ kW}$

Required power in kJ/s: $100{,}000 \text{ kJ/s}$

Mass flow rate:
$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ kJ/s}}{849.93 \text{ kJ/kg}}
$$
$$
\dot{m} = 117.66 \text{ kg/s}
$$
*(3 marks)*

Convert to kg/h:
$$
\dot{m} = 117.66 \times 3600 = 423{,}576 \text{ kg/h} \approx 423.6 \text{ tonnes/h}
$$
*(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A steam power plant operates on an ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 8 MPa and 500°C, and the condenser operates at 20 kPa. Determine:**

**(a)** The pump work, turbine work, net work output, and heat added per kg of steam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The thermal efficiency and the back work ratio. Compare it with the Carnot efficiency operating between the same temperature limits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution Outline to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**State 1:** $P_1 = 20 \text{ kPa}$, saturated liquid: $h_1 = 251.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $v_1 = 0.001017 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$. *(1 mark)*

**State 2 (Pump exit):**
$$
W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1) = 0.001017 \times (8000 - 20) = 8.11 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$
$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 259.53 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$
*(1 mark)*

**State 3 (Turbine inlet):** From superheated tables at 8 MPa, 500°C: $h_3 = 3399.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_3 = 6.7266 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. *(1 mark)*

**State 4 (Turbine exit):** At 20 kPa: $s_f = 0.8320$, $s_{fg} = 7.0752$, $h_{fg} = 2357.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$.
$$
x_4 = \frac{6.7266 - 0.8320}{7.0752} = 0.8329
$$
$$
h_4 = 251.42 + 0.8329 \times 2357.5 = 2214.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$
*(2 marks)*

**Work and Heat Results:**
- $W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 1185.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ *(1 mark)*
- $W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 1177.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$ *(0.5 mark)*
- $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3140.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$ *(0.5 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution Outline to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Thermal efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{1177.2}{3140.0} = 0.3750 = 37.50\%
$$
*(2 marks)*

**Back work ratio:**
$$
R_{bw} = \frac{W_P}{W_T} = \frac{8.11}{1185.3} = 0.00684 = 0.684\%
$$
*(1 mark)*

**Carnot efficiency:** $T_H = 500 + 273 = 773 \text{ K}$, $T_L = T_{sat} \text{ at } 20 \text{ kPa} = 60.06 + 273 = 333.06 \text{ K}$
$$
\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{333.06}{773} = 0.5691 = 56.91\%
$$
*(2 marks)*

**Comparison statement:** The Carnot efficiency is **higher** than the Rankine efficiency (56.91% vs 37.50%) because the Rankine cycle adds heat over a temperature range (not isothermally), making its average temperature of heat addition lower than $T_H$. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Rankine Cycle Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add pump work to $h_1$ when computing $h_2$.** A shocking number of students write $h_2 = h_1$ or omit the pump work term entirely. The examiner will deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Using dry-saturated tables for the turbine inlet when the steam is superheated.** If the problem states 350°C at 3 MPa, you **must** use the *superheated* steam table, not the saturation table at 3 MPa (which would give a much lower $h_3$).
> 3. **Calculating $x_4$ using the wrong denominator.** Some students write $x_4 = (s_4 - s_f)/s_g$, but the correct formula is $(s_4 - s_f)/s_{fg}$. This single error will cost 1 mark and propagate into $h_4$.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert MW to kW.** When using $\dot{m} = \dot{W}_{net} / W_{net}$, the power must be in kJ/s (not MW). Otherwise, the mass flow rate will be off by a factor of 1000.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions.** For a full-mark answer, the model solution must begin with the line: *"Assumptions: (1) Steady flow, steady state; (2) No pressure drop in boiler/condenser; (3) Isentropic pump and turbine."* Examiners often allocate 1 mark for this preamble.
> 6. **Confusing the sign convention.** The pump *consumes* work, so $W_P$ is sometimes called a "negative work output." Keep signs consistent: positive = work out of the system.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this as your **last-minute revision checklist** before the KTU ESE:

- ✅ The Rankine Cycle is the **theoretical model** for all steam power plants. The *real* cycle deviates due to friction, heat losses, and non-isentropic turbines.
- ✅ The **four processes** are: Pump (1-2, isentropic compression), Boiler (2-3, isobaric heat addition), Turbine (3-4, isentropic expansion), Condenser (4-1, isobaric heat rejection).
- ✅ The **master efficiency equation** is $\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$. Memorize it.
- ✅ Pump work is calculated via $W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ because liquids are nearly incompressible. It is typically **< 2%** of the turbine work.
- ✅ The **back work ratio** $R_{bw} = W_P / W_T$ is a key indicator of cycle design quality. Aim for it to be as small as possible.
- ✅ The Rankine cycle has **lower** thermal efficiency than the Carnot cycle operating between the same temperature limits, but it is *practically realizable*.
- ✅ Always specify **units** in your final answer. $W_{net}$ in kJ/kg, $\eta$ in % or decimal, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s.
- ✅ The **specific steam consumption (SSC)** = 3600 / $W_{net}$ in kg/kWh. Lower SSC = more efficient cycle.
- ✅ The turbine exhaust must be in the **wet region** (inside the saturation dome) for the ideal Rankine cycle. Quality $x_4$ must be > 0 (wet) and typically > 0.88 to avoid excessive blade erosion in real turbines.
- ✅ In the T-s diagram: lines 1-2 and 3-4 are **vertical** (isentropic, constant entropy); lines 2-3 and 4-1 are **horizontal** (isobaric, on constant pressure lines).
- ✅ For the **isentropic efficiency** of a real turbine: $\eta_T = (h_3 - h_{4,actual}) / (h_3 - h_{4,s})$.
- ✅ The Rankine cycle is a **closed loop** — the same working fluid is recirculated indefinitely. No mass is lost or added in steady operation.
- ✅ Condenser pressure must be **below atmospheric** (typically 5–15 kPa) to maximize efficiency, but cannot be too low (otherwise cooling water would boil or the turbine exit quality would drop below acceptable limits).
- ✅ Modern supercritical and ultra-supercritical plants extend the Rankine cycle to $P > 22.1 \text{ MPa}$ (above the critical point of water) to achieve efficiencies of 45%+.
- ✅ **Major limitations of the basic Rankine cycle** (to be addressed in subsequent modules): (1) High moisture content at turbine exit; (2) Low average temperature of heat addition. Solutions: **Superheating**, **Reheating**, and **Regeneration** — all covered in Modules 1 and 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

**End of Notes — Rankine Cycle | Module 1 | OEMET727**
