topics.title: Steam Power Plant: Internally Irreversible and Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steam Power Plant: Internally Irreversible and Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Rankine Cycle – Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **Rankine Cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of a **steam power plant** in which a working fluid (water/steam) undergoes a closed loop of **two isentropic (reversible adiabatic) processes** and **two isobaric (constant-pressure) heat transfer processes**. It is the practical analogue of the Carnot cycle for two-phase working fluids and serves as the benchmark against which all real steam plant performance is measured.

In a **Reversible (Ideal) Rankine Cycle**, all four processes are perfectly reversible:

- **Process 1–2:** Isentropic expansion in the turbine
- **Process 2–3:** Isobaric heat rejection in the condenser
- **Process 3–4:** Isentropic compression in the pump
- **Process 4–1:** Isobaric heat addition in the boiler

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – 2024 Scheme:**
> For OEMET727 Module 1, students are expected to analyse the cycle under **two distinct irreversibility conditions**:
> (a) **Internally Irreversible Rankine Cycle** – the working fluid alone experiences irreversibilities (friction, turbulence, non-equilibrium effects).
> (b) **Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle** – irreversibility arises solely due to heat transfer across a finite temperature difference between the working fluid and the external reservoirs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy box across a rough floor from point A to point B:

- **Internal irreversibility** is like the friction inside the box's wheels — energy is dissipated *within* the system itself, even though the floor (surroundings) is smooth.
- **External irreversibility** is like a smooth wheel rolling on a rough, hot floor — the heat you would need to remove to bring the floor back to its original temperature is *lost to the surroundings*.

In a steam power plant, **internal irreversibility** manifests as fluid friction, sudden pressure drops, and non-isentropic expansion/compression. **External irreversibility** manifests as combustion gases being at 1500 °C while steam inside the tubes is only 350 °C — a huge ΔT driving heat transfer is itself an irreversibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversibility Checklist (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> A process is **fully reversible** only if (i) it is internally reversible *and* (ii) externally reversible. A violation of *either* makes the cycle *irreversible*.

### 1.3 Why Real Cycles Are Always Irreversible

The second law of thermodynamics states that **entropy of the universe must increase** for any real (spontaneous) process. A reversible process is a *theoretical idealization* that sets the upper bound on work output and efficiency. Studying irreversible cycles is essential because:

- It quantifies the **performance gap** between ideal and real plants.
- It identifies **where in the cycle** improvements are most needed.
- It guides the design of **reheaters, regenerative feedwater heaters, and superheaters**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Irreversibility in Rankine Cycle

| Source | Location | Effect on Cycle |
|--------|----------|-----------------|
| **Internal Irreversibility** | Inside the working fluid (turbine blades, pump passages, pipe friction) | Entropy of fluid *increases*; T-s area shrinks; less net work |
| **External Irreversibility** | Across the boundary (finite ΔT during heat addition/rejection) | Entropy of surroundings *increases*; available energy wasted |

> [!NOTE]
> A cycle can be **internally reversible but externally irreversible** (e.g., reversible turbine, but combustion gases far hotter than steam saturation), or **externally reversible but internally irreversible** (e.g., friction in turbine, but heat transfer with negligible ΔT).

### 2.2 The Internally Irreversible Rankine Cycle

#### 2.2.1 Physical Sources

- **Turbine:** Blade friction, leakage, condensation losses → expansion is *not* isentropic.
- **Pump/Condensate Pump:** Hydraulic friction, volumetric losses → compression is *not* isentropic.
- **Piping:** Sudden contractions/expansions, bends → minor pressure losses.

#### 2.2.2 Quantification via Isentropic Efficiency

**Turbine Isentropic Efficiency (η_T):**

$$\eta_T = \frac{h_1 - h_{2,\text{actual}}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

**Pump Isentropic Efficiency (η_P):**

$$\eta_P = \frac{h_{4s} - h_3}{h_{4,\text{actual}} - h_3}$$

#### 2.2.3 Energy Equations (per unit mass)

| Process | Equation | Notes |
|---------|----------|-------|
| Turbine | $w_T = h_1 - h_2$ | Reduced from ideal because $h_2 > h_{2s}$ |
| Condenser | $q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3$ | Increased due to higher $h_2$ |
| Pump | $w_P = h_4 - h_3$ | Increased due to $h_4 > h_{4s}$ |
| Boiler | $q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4$ | Increased due to higher $h_4$ |

**Net Work:**

$$w_{\text{net}} = w_T - w_P = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$$

**Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$$

#### 2.2.4 Effect on T–s Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On the T–s plane, the **internally irreversible cycle is inscribed inside the ideal cycle**. The turbine exit state moves to the *right* (higher entropy), and the pump exit state also moves to the *right*. The boiler and condenser lines remain at constant temperature (externally reversible by assumption).

### 2.3 The Externally Irreversible Rankine Cycle

#### 2.3.1 Physical Sources

- **Boiler:** Combustion gases at $\sim$1500–1800 °C transfer heat to steam at 250–600 °C.
- **Condenser:** Steam condenses at $\sim$30–45 °C, while cooling water enters at 20 °C and exits at 30 °C.

#### 2.3.2 Mean Effective Temperature Approach

The **mean effective temperature of heat addition** is the temperature at which a reversible heat source would deliver the same enthalpy and entropy change:

$$\bar{T}_{\text{add}} = \frac{q_{\text{in}}}{s_1 - s_4}$$

Similarly for heat rejection:

$$\bar{T}_{\text{rej}} = \frac{q_{\text{out}}}{s_2 - s_3}$$

#### 2.3.3 Modified Thermal Efficiency

For a cycle that is **internally reversible** but **externally irreversible**, the maximum attainable efficiency equals the **Carnot efficiency** evaluated between $\bar{T}_{\text{add}}$ and $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}$:

$$\eta_{\text{th, ext irr}} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}}{\bar{T}_{\text{add}}}$$

#### 2.3.4 Effect on T–s Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On the T–s plane, the **heat addition and rejection curves are no longer horizontal**. Heat addition follows a curve below the saturation dome (or extends into the superheated region), and heat rejection follows a curve above the saturation dome. The cycle *encloses less area* than the ideal cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| Turbine work | $w_T = h_1 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Use actual $h_2$ for irreversible cycle |
| Pump work (ideal) | $w_{P,s} = v_3 (P_4 - P_3)$ | kJ/kg | Compressed liquid approximation |
| Pump work (actual) | $w_P = v_3 (P_4 - P_3)/\eta_P$ | kJ/kg | $\eta_P$ typically 0.7–0.85 |
| Heat input | $q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | — |
| Heat rejected | $q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | — |
| Net work | $w_{\text{net}} = w_T - w_P$ | kJ/kg | Decreases with irreversibility |
| Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = w_{\text{net}}/q_{\text{in}}$ | dimensionless | $\eta_{\text{ideal}} > \eta_{\text{actual}}$ |
| Turbine isentropic eff. | $\eta_T = (h_1 - h_2)/(h_1 - h_{2s})$ | dimensionless | $\eta_T \approx 0.85$–$0.92$ modern |
| Mean add. temperature | $\bar{T}_{\text{add}} = q_{\text{in}}/(s_1 - s_4)$ | K | Used in external irreversibility |
| Mean rej. temperature | $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}} = q_{\text{out}}/(s_2 - s_3)$ | K | Used in external irreversibility |
| Ext. irreversible η | $\eta = 1 - \bar{T}_{\text{rej}}/\bar{T}_{\text{add}}$ | dimensionless | Equals Carnot between $\bar{T}$s |
| Steam quality at 2 | $x_2 = (s_2 - s_f)/(s_g - s_f)$ | dimensionless | For wet region exit |
| Enthalpy at 2 | $h_2 = h_f + x_2 h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | At condenser pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real steam plants have *both* internal and external irreversibilities. The total efficiency loss is the sum, but the **largest external loss** is typically the boiler ΔT (≈ 1000 °C gap to combustion gases). This is why supercritical and ultra-supercritical designs push steam temperatures to 600–700 °C — to narrow this ΔT.

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Industry

- **Condenser design** uses cooling water ΔT to size heat exchangers — a direct trade-off between capital cost and external irreversibility.
- **Turbine staging** with reaction blading reduces internal irreversibility.
- **Feedwater heating** recovers external irreversibility by cascading heat between fluid streams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Work and Efficiency of an Internally Irreversible Rankine Cycle

We apply the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to each component, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes.

**Step 1: Boiler (Process 4 → 1) — Isobaric Heat Addition**

$$q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4$$

*Reasoning:* For a steady-flow boiler with no work and negligible KE/PE, the heat added equals the enthalpy rise of the fluid.

**Step 2: Turbine (Process 1 → 2) — Actual (Irreversible) Expansion**

$$w_T = h_1 - h_2$$

*Reasoning:* SFEE for adiabatic turbine. Because the expansion is irreversible, the actual $h_2$ is higher than the isentropic exit $h_{2s}$, so $w_T < w_{T,s}$.

**Step 3: Condenser (Process 2 → 3) — Isobaric Heat Rejection**

$$q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3$$

*Reasoning:* Higher $h_2$ means more heat is dumped to the cooling water — a direct penalty of internal irreversibility.

**Step 4: Pump (Process 3 → 4) — Actual (Irreversible) Compression**

For the *ideal* (isentropic) pump:

$$w_{P,s} = h_{4s} - h_3 = v_3 (P_4 - P_3)$$

*Reasoning:* The compressed liquid has nearly constant specific volume $v_3 \approx v_f$ at the condenser pressure, so the enthalpy rise is $v \Delta P$.

For the *actual* pump with isentropic efficiency $\eta_P$:

$$w_P = \frac{w_{P,s}}{\eta_P} = \frac{v_3 (P_4 - P_3)}{\eta_P}$$

*Reasoning:* The actual enthalpy rise is larger by the factor $1/\eta_P < 1$ in the denominator.

**Step 5: Net Specific Work**

$$w_{\text{net}} = w_T - w_P = (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$$

**Step 6: Thermal Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)}{h_1 - h_4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **What changes when internal irreversibility is introduced?**
> - $h_2$ rises → $w_T$ drops and $q_{\text{out}}$ rises.
> - $h_4$ rises → $q_{\text{in}}$ rises and $w_P$ rises.
> - Numerator (net work) **shrinks**, denominator (heat input) **grows** → efficiency **collapses**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean Effective Temperature for External Irreversibility

**Step 1:** Consider heat addition from process 4 → 1. For an internally reversible process, the entropy change of the working fluid equals the heat transfer divided by the boundary temperature:

$$ds = \frac{\delta q_{\text{rev}}}{T_{\text{boundary}}}$$

**Step 2:** Integrating from state 4 to state 1:

$$s_1 - s_4 = \int_4^1 \frac{\delta q}{T_{\text{boundary}}}$$

**Step 3:** Defining a *lumped* (mean effective) temperature $\bar{T}_{\text{add}}$ that yields the same entropy change for the same total heat:

$$s_1 - s_4 = \frac{q_{\text{in}}}{\bar{T}_{\text{add}}}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the mean effective temperature:

$$\bar{T}_{\text{add}} = \frac{q_{\text{in}}}{s_1 - s_4} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{s_1 - s_4}$$

**Step 5:** By the same logic, for the condenser:

$$\bar{T}_{\text{rej}} = \frac{q_{\text{out}}}{s_2 - s_3} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{s_2 - s_3}$$

**Step 6:** The cycle is internally reversible, so the second law bounds its efficiency to that of a Carnot engine operating between $\bar{T}_{\text{add}}$ and $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}$:

$$\eta_{\text{max, ext irr}} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}}{\bar{T}_{\text{add}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does external irreversibility always reduce efficiency below Carnot?**
> The Carnot efficiency $1 - T_C/T_H$ requires *all* heat addition at $T_H$ and *all* rejection at $T_C$. With external irreversibility, the average temperature of heat *addition* is *less* than the source temperature, and the average temperature of heat *rejection* is *greater* than the sink temperature. This drops the ratio $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}/\bar{T}_{\text{add}}$ closer to 1, shrinking efficiency.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, Internal Irreversibility)

**Given:**
- Turbine inlet: $P_1 = 3$ MPa, $T_1 = 350$ °C
- Condenser pressure: $P_2 = P_3 = 10$ kPa
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.85$
- Pump isentropic efficiency: $\eta_P = 0.80$

**Step A — Ideal (Isentropic) States for Reference:**

From steam tables:
- $h_1 = 3115.3$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K
- At 10 kPa: $h_f = 191.81$, $h_{fg} = 2392.1$, $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_g = 8.1502$ kJ/kg·K
- $v_3 = 0.001010$ m³/kg

Isentropic turbine exit:
$$x_{2s} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6493}{8.1502 - 0.6493} = \frac{6.0935}{7.5009} = 0.8124$$

$$h_{2s} = 191.81 + (0.8124)(2392.1) = 2135.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step B — Actual Turbine Exit (with $\eta_T = 0.85$):**

$$\eta_T = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

$$0.85 = \frac{3115.3 - h_2}{3115.3 - 2135.26}$$

$$3115.3 - h_2 = 0.85 \times 980.04 = 833.03$$

$$h_2 = 3115.3 - 833.03 = 2282.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step C — Ideal Pump Work:**

$$w_{P,s} = v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 3.01 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_{4s} = h_3 + 3.01 = 191.81 + 3.01 = 194.82 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step D — Actual Pump Exit (with $\eta_P = 0.80$):**

$$\eta_P = \frac{h_{4s} - h_3}{h_4 - h_3}$$

$$0.80 = \frac{3.01}{h_4 - 191.81}$$

$$h_4 - 191.81 = \frac{3.01}{0.80} = 3.76$$

$$h_4 = 195.57 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step E — Performance Parameters:**

$$w_T = h_1 - h_2 = 3115.3 - 2282.27 = 833.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_P = h_4 - h_3 = 195.57 - 191.81 = 3.76 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{\text{net}} = 833.03 - 3.76 = 829.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4 = 3115.3 - 195.57 = 2919.73 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{829.27}{2919.73} = 0.2840 = 28.40\%$$

**Step F — Comparison with Ideal Cycle:**

| Parameter | Ideal | Actual (Irreversible) | Change |
|-----------|-------|----------------------|--------|
| $w_T$ (kJ/kg) | 980.04 | 833.03 | -15.0% |
| $w_P$ (kJ/kg) | 3.01 | 3.76 | +24.9% |
| $w_{\text{net}}$ (kJ/kg) | 977.03 | 829.27 | -15.1% |
| $q_{\text{in}}$ (kJ/kg) | 2920.48 | 2919.73 | ≈ 0 |
| $\eta_{\text{th}}$ | 33.45% | 28.40% | -5.05 pts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Observation:** Internal irreversibility causes a *simultaneous* drop in turbine work and rise in pump work. The net work suffers almost entirely from the turbine side because $w_T \gg w_P$. The thermal efficiency drops by ~5 percentage points — this is a *huge* loss in a commercial plant.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparison of Three Cycles

The Mermaid diagram below maps the thermodynamic *state transitions* of (i) the ideal cycle, (ii) the internally irreversible cycle, and (iii) the externally irreversible cycle, highlighting where entropy generation occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    boiler["Boiler: 4 to 1"] --> turbIn["State 1: P1, T1, h1, s1"]
    turbIn --> turbAct{"Turbine Expansion"}
    turbAct --> turbOutA["State 2s: Isentropic Exit h2s"]
    turbAct --> turbOutB["State 2a: Actual Exit h2a gt h2s"]
    turbOutA --> condA["Condenser 2s to 3"]
    turbOutB --> condB["Condenser 2a to 3"]
    condA --> pumpA{"Pump Compression"}
    condB --> pumpB{"Pump Compression"}
    pumpA --> pumpOutA["State 4s: Isentropic Exit h4s"]
    pumpB --> pumpOutB["State 4a: Actual Exit h4a gt h4s"]
    pumpOutA --> boiler
    pumpOutB --> boiler
    cycle1["IDEAL Rankine Cycle"] -.-> turbOutA
    cycle2["INTERNALLY IRREVERSIBLE Cycle"] -.-> turbOutB
    cycle3["EXTERNALLY IRREVERSIBLE Cycle"] -.->|"Variable T heat add/rej"| boiler
    cycle3 -.->|"Variable T heat add/rej"| condA
    loss1["Entropy Generation s_gen gt 0 inside fluid"] -.-> turbOutB
    loss2["Entropy Generation s_gen gt 0 across boundary"] -.-> boiler
```

### 4.2 T–s Diagram — Comparative Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_s_plot["T vs s Diagram - Comparative Cycles"]
        dom["Saturation Dome"]
        L1["Process 1 to 2s: Isentropic Turbine Expansion"]
        L2["Process 1 to 2a: Actual Irreversible Expansion"]
        L3["Process 2s to 3: Condensation Ideal at T3"]
        L4["Process 2a to 3: Condensation Actual at T3"]
        L5["Process 3 to 4s: Isentropic Pump"]
        L6["Process 3 to 4a: Actual Pump"]
        L7["Process 4s to 1: Ideal Boiler at T1"]
        L8["Process 4a to 1: Actual Boiler at T1"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Architecture — Sources of Irreversibility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["Sources of Irreversibility in Rankine Cycle"]
    TOP --> INTERNAL["INTERNAL Irreversibility"]
    TOP --> EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL Irreversibility"]
    INTERNAL --> I1["Turbine friction and leakage"]
    INTERNAL --> I2["Pump hydraulic losses"]
    INTERNAL --> I3["Pipe friction and bends"]
    INTERNAL --> IEFF["Quantified by isentropic efficiencies eta_T and eta_P"]
    EXTERNAL --> E1["Combustion gas to steam delta T in boiler"]
    EXTERNAL --> E2["Steam to cooling water delta T in condenser"]
    EXTERNAL --> EEFF["Quantified by mean effective temperatures T_bar_add and T_bar_rej"]
    IEFF --> PERF1["Reduced net work and efficiency"]
    EEFF --> PERF2["Reduced net work and efficiency"]
    PERF1 --> RES["Net plant efficiency is well below Carnot"]
    PERF2 --> RES
```

### 4.4 H–s (Mollier) Diagram — State Movements

| State | Location on h–s Chart | Effect of Irreversibility |
|-------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 (Turbine inlet) | Fixed superheated point | Unchanged |
| 2s (Isentropic exit) | Vertical drop from 1 (constant $s$) | Reference for ideal cycle |
| 2a (Actual exit) | To the right of 2s (higher $s$, same $h_2$) | **Internal** irreversibility shifts it right |
| 3 (Condenser exit) | Saturated liquid line | Unchanged |
| 4s (Isentropic pump) | Slight rightward shift from 3 | Reference for ideal cycle |
| 4a (Actual pump) | To the right of 4s (higher $s$, higher $h_4$) | **Internal** irreversibility shifts it right |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Distinguish between an **internally reversible** process and an **internally irreversible** process with a suitable example from a steam power plant.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An **internally reversible process** is one in which no irreversibilities exist *within the system boundaries* — the working fluid passes through equilibrium states. Example: frictionless, adiabatic flow through an ideal turbine (Process 1–2s).
- An **internally irreversible process** has dissipative effects (friction, turbulence, mixing, chemical reaction) *inside* the system, causing entropy *generation* within the fluid. Example: an actual turbine with blade friction, leakage, and condensation losses (Process 1–2a).
- **Key test:** A process is internally reversible if the system can be restored to its initial state *without leaving any net change in the surroundings*. **(1 mark) for definition, (1 mark) for example, (1 mark) for test criterion.**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define **isentropic efficiency of a turbine**. Why is it always less than unity for an actual steam turbine?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Isentropic efficiency of a turbine is the ratio of the *actual* work output to the work output that *would be obtained* if the expansion from the same inlet state to the same exit pressure were *isentropic* (reversible adiabatic). $\eta_T = (h_1 - h_{2,\text{actual}})/(h_1 - h_{2s})$. **(1 mark)**
- **Why less than unity:** Real turbines suffer from blade friction, heat transfer across finite ΔT, leakage past blade tips, and condensation in low-pressure stages. All of these increase the actual exit enthalpy $h_2$ above the ideal isentropic $h_{2s}$, so the actual work $h_1 - h_2$ is less than the ideal work $h_1 - h_{2s}$. **(2 marks)**
- By the **Second Law**, no adiabatic device can produce more work than an isentropic one between the same two pressures — therefore $\eta_T \le 1$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A steam power plant operates on an **ideal Rankine cycle**. Steam enters the turbine at $3$ MPa and $350$ °C and is condensed in the condenser at a pressure of $10$ kPa. Determine:
> (i) The thermal efficiency of the cycle
> (ii) The net work output per kg of steam
> (iii) The mass flow rate of steam required for a $100$ MW power plant.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat T–s diagram, explain the **internally irreversible Rankine cycle**. How does internal irreversibility affect (i) net work and (ii) thermal efficiency?

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**State 1 — Turbine inlet:** $P_1 = 3$ MPa, $T_1 = 350$ °C
From superheated steam tables: $h_1 = 3115.3$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K.

**State 2s — Isentropic turbine exit:** $P_2 = 10$ kPa, $s_{2s} = s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K.
At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_g = 8.1502$ kJ/kg·K.

$$x_{2s} = \frac{s_1 - s_f}{s_g - s_f} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6493}{8.1502 - 0.6493} = \frac{6.0935}{7.5009} = 0.8124$$

$$h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + (0.8124)(2392.1) = 2135.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**State 3 — Saturated liquid at 10 kPa:** $h_3 = 191.81$ kJ/kg, $v_3 = 0.001010$ m³/kg.

**State 4s — Isentropic pump exit:**

$$w_{P,s} = v_3 (P_4 - P_3) = (0.001010)(3000 - 10) = 3.01 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_{4s} = 191.81 + 3.01 = 194.82 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Performance:**

$$w_T = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3115.3 - 2135.26 = 980.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{\text{net}} = w_T - w_{P,s} = 980.04 - 3.01 = 977.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_{4s} = 3115.3 - 194.82 = 2920.48 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{977.03}{2920.48} = 0.3345 = 33.45\%$$

**Steam mass flow rate for 100 MW:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{net}}}{w_{\text{net}}} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ kW}}{977.03 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 102.35 \text{ kg/s}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Description (3 marks):**
In an internally irreversible Rankine cycle, the working fluid experiences irreversibilities *within* the system (turbine blade friction, pump hydraulic losses, pipe friction), but heat addition in the boiler and heat rejection in the condenser are assumed to occur at constant external temperatures (i.e., externally reversible).

**T–s Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- States 4 and 1 are at the *same temperatures* as the ideal cycle (constant T heat transfer lines).
- State 2a lies to the *right* of 2s (entropy increases in the actual turbine).
- State 4a lies to the *right* of 4s (entropy increases in the actual pump).
- The actual cycle 1–2a–3–4a–1 is *enclosed inside* the ideal cycle 1–2s–3–4s–1.

**Effect on Net Work and Efficiency (2 marks):**
- Net work = Area enclosed by the cycle on T–s diagram. Since the actual cycle is *smaller*, $w_{\text{net, actual}} < w_{\text{net, ideal}}$.
- $h_2 > h_{2s}$ reduces $w_T$; $h_4 > h_{4s}$ increases $w_P$ and $q_{\text{in}}$.
- Therefore $\eta_{\text{th, actual}} < \eta_{\text{th, ideal}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Students often forget to convert MPa → kPa in the pump work formula. (2) Many use $h_{fg}$ directly without calculating quality $x_{2s}$ first. (3) Some confuse $h_{2s}$ with $h_2$ — always check whether isentropic or actual is required. (4) On the T–s diagram, do *not* draw the actual cycle *outside* the ideal — it always lies *inside* for internal irreversibility.

---

> **Question B. [Alternative — 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a T–s diagram, explain the **externally irreversible Rankine cycle**. Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of this cycle.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A steam power plant operates between a source temperature of $1500$ K (combustion gases) and a sink temperature of $290$ K (atmosphere). If the mean effective temperature of heat addition is $750$ K and mean effective temperature of heat rejection is $320$ K, find: (i) the Carnot efficiency, (ii) the actual cycle efficiency, and (iii) the percentage loss in efficiency due to external irreversibility.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
In an externally irreversible Rankine cycle, heat addition and rejection occur across *finite temperature differences* between the working fluid and the external reservoirs. The processes are *internally reversible* (isentropic turbine and pump), but the boundary heat transfer generates entropy in the surroundings.

**T–s Diagram (2 marks):**
- The boiler process 4 → 1 follows a *curved* path (not constant T) below the combustion gas temperature.
- The condenser process 2 → 3 follows a *curved* path above the cooling water temperature.
- The turbine and pump processes remain vertical (isentropic).

**Derivation (2 marks):**
The thermal efficiency of an internally reversible cycle depends only on the *mean effective temperatures* of heat transfer:

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}}{\bar{T}_{\text{add}}}$$

where $\bar{T}_{\text{add}} = q_{\text{in}}/(s_1 - s_4)$ and $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}} = q_{\text{out}}/(s_2 - s_3)$. Because $\bar{T}_{\text{add}} < T_H$ and $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}} > T_C$, the actual efficiency is always less than the Carnot efficiency $1 - T_C/T_H$.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**(i) Carnot Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{290}{1500} = 1 - 0.1933 = 0.8067 = 80.67\%$$

**(ii) Actual Cycle Efficiency (3 marks):**

$$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{\text{rej}}}{\bar{T}_{\text{add}}} = 1 - \frac{320}{750} = 1 - 0.4267 = 0.5733 = 57.33\%$$

**(iii) Percentage Loss in Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\Delta\eta = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} - \eta_{\text{actual}} = 80.67\% - 57.33\% = 23.34\%$$

$$\text{Percentage loss} = \frac{23.34}{80.67} \times 100\% = 28.93\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Students frequently confuse the **mean effective temperature** (a fluid-side property) with the **source/sink temperature** (a reservoir-side property). They are *not* equal in external irreversibility. (2) Always state temperatures in **Kelvin** when computing efficiency ratios. (3) "Percentage loss" should be computed *with respect to Carnot*, not with respect to 100%.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Ideal Rankine Cycle** has two isentropic and two isobaric processes; it is fully reversible.
- **Internal irreversibility** is caused by fluid friction, turbulence, leakage, and non-equilibrium effects *inside* the system.
- **External irreversibility** is caused by heat transfer across a finite ΔT *across* the system boundary.
- For internal irreversibility, the T–s cycle is **inscribed inside** the ideal cycle; the actual cycle area is *smaller*.
- For external irreversibility, the heat addition/rejection lines are **not horizontal**; the area shrinks because the average temperature of heat addition drops.
- **Turbine isentropic efficiency** $\eta_T = (h_1 - h_2)/(h_1 - h_{2s})$ — always $\le 1$.
- **Pump isentropic efficiency** $\eta_P = (h_{4s} - h_3)/(h_4 - h_3)$ — always $\le 1$.
- **Mean effective temperature of addition** $\bar{T}_{\text{add}} = q_{\text{in}}/(s_1 - s_4)$.
- **Mean effective temperature of rejection** $\bar{T}_{\text{rej}} = q_{\text{out}}/(s_2 - s_3)$.
- For an externally irreversible (but internally reversible) cycle: $\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \bar{T}_{\text{rej}}/\bar{T}_{\text{add}}$.
- Real plants suffer **both** types of irreversibility; total efficiency loss is the *sum* of individual contributions.
- **The biggest external irreversibility** in a steam plant is the combustion gas–steam ΔT in the boiler ($\sim 1000$ °C).
- **The biggest internal irreversibility** is the turbine ($\eta_T \approx 0.85$–$0.92$); the pump is less critical because $w_P \ll w_T$.
- **Improvements**: superheat, reheat, regeneration, increasing boiler pressure, lowering condenser pressure, supercritical operation.
- **Remember units**: pressures in kPa, temperatures in K for efficiency, enthalpies in kJ/kg, specific volumes in m³/kg, entropy in kJ/(kg·K).
- **Quality formula**: $x = (s - s_f)/(s_g - s_f)$ — apply only when $s_f \le s \le s_g$.
- **Pump work shortcut (incompressible liquid)**: $w_P = v \Delta P$ — use $v_f$ at pump inlet pressure.
- For **KTU 14-mark problems**: always (i) draw the T–s diagram, (ii) tabulate state properties, (iii) compute each work/heat term, (iv) state efficiency, (v) comment on the *qualitative* effect of irreversibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean Temperature of Heat Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mean Temperature of Heat Addition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In a steam power plant operating on the **Rankine Cycle**, heat is *not* added at a constant temperature. In the boiler, water is first heated from saturated liquid up to saturation temperature (sub-cooled region), then evaporated at constant temperature (phase change), and finally superheated (temperature rises again). This makes the heat addition process a **variable temperature process** on a T-s diagram. > [!NOTE] > **Mean Temperature of Heat Addition (MTHA), denoted as $T_{m1}$ or $\bar{T}_1$:** > It is defined as the *equivalent constant temperature* at which heat would have to be added to the working fluid (over the same entropy range) to supply the **same amount of heat** as in the actual variable-temperature heat addition process. Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the total heat added to the change in entropy during heat addition: $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s_{1-2}} = \frac{Q_{in}}{s_2 - s_1}$$ where: - $Q_{in}$ = Total heat added in the boiler (kJ/kg) - $s_1, s_2$ = Specific entropy at the beginning and end of heat addition (kJ/kg·K) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Average Speed of a Traveller":** > Imagine a traveller driving from City A to City B. He drives slowly in city traffic (60 km/h), speeds up on the highway (120 km/h), and slows again near his destination (40 km/h). His *average speed* over the trip is **not** simply the highest or lowest speed — it is the *single equivalent speed* that, if maintained constant, would have covered the same total distance in the same total time. > > Similarly, in a boiler, heat is added at *varying temperatures* (sensible heating, then evaporation at constant $T_{sat}$, then superheating). The **Mean Temperature of Heat Addition** is the *single equivalent temperature* at which the *same total heat* could have been transferred over the *same entropy change*. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | |-----------|--------|--------------| | Mean Temperature of Heat Addition | $\bar{T}_1$ | **K** (or °C) | | Heat Added | $Q_{in}$ | **kJ/kg** | | Entropy Change | $\Delta s$ | **kJ/kg·K** | | Saturation Temperature | $T_{sat}$ | **K** | | Steam Pressure | $P$ | **MPa / bar** | > [!NOTE] > In all KTU numerical problems, the final answer for $\bar{T}_1$ is expected in **Kelvin (K)** unless explicitly asked otherwise. If values are given in °C, use $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown In the **ideal Rankine cycle**, the heat addition process is **2 → 3** on the T-s diagram (saturated liquid at pump exit → superheated steam at turbine inlet). The logic behind MTHA unfolds as follows: 1. **Heat is a path function, entropy is a state function.** From the second law of thermodynamics, for a reversible heat transfer process: $$dQ_{rev} = T \, ds$$ 2. **Total heat added** is therefore the area under the heat addition curve on the T-s diagram: $$Q_{in} = \int_{s_1}^{s_2} T \, ds$$ 3. **Definition of the mean (average):** The *arithmetic mean* of a function over an interval is the integral divided by the interval length: $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{1}{s_2 - s_1} \int_{s_1}^{s_2} T \, ds$$ 4. **Substituting the integral form of $Q_{in}$:** $$Q_{in} = \bar{T}_1 \cdot (s_2 - s_1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{T}_1 = \frac{Q_{in}}{s_2 - s_1}$$ 5. **In terms of steam table properties** (since enthalpy is tabulated): $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{s_2 - s_1}$$ 6. **Thermal efficiency connection:** For the Rankine cycle, since $\Delta s_{add} = \Delta s_{rej}$ (cycle closes on the T-s diagram): $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_{mean,rej}}{T_{mean,add}}$$ This is the **Carnot-equivalent efficiency** — and reveals *why* superheat and reheat raise efficiency: they raise $\bar{T}_1$. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Physical Reasoning | |------|--------------------| | Why integrate $T \, ds$? | Because reversible heat transfer equals $T \cdot ds$ — the second law. | | Why divide by $\Delta s$? | We want an *average* temperature — integral averaged over an interval. | | Why $\Delta s_{add} = \Delta s_{rej}$? | The Rankine cycle is a *closed* cycle; entropy returns to its starting value. | | Why higher $\bar{T}_1$ improves efficiency? | It mimics a Carnot cycle operating between a higher source temperature. | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Card — Memorise for KTU ESE** | # | Formula | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $\bar{T}_1 = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s}$ | Basic definition of MTHA | | 2 | $\bar{T}_1 = \dfrac{h_2 - h_1}{s_2 - s_1}$ | Steam-table form | | 3 | $Q_{in} = \bar{T}_1 \cdot (s_2 - s_1)$ | Heat added from MTHA | | 4 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{\bar{T}_2}{\bar{T}_1}$ | Carnot-equivalent efficiency | | 5 | $Q_{out} = \bar{T}_2 \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$ | Heat rejected from mean rejection temp | > Where subscript **1** refers to heat addition (state 2→3 in Rankine cycle) and subscript **2** refers to heat rejection (state 4→1). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Boiler Design:** Knowing $\bar{T}_1$ helps engineers select materials, furnace design, and combustion intensity. - **Cycle Optimisation:** Superheating and reheating directly increase $\bar{T}_1$, which is *the* lever to raise plant efficiency. - **Comparative Benchmarking:** Different plant configurations (subcritical, supercritical, USC) are compared by their $\bar{T}_1$ values. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of MTHA from First Principles **Starting Point — Second Law for a Reversible Process:** For a quasi-equilibrium (internally reversible) heat transfer process, the Clausius equality gives: $$dQ_{rev} = T \, ds$$ **Step 1 — Integrate along the heat addition path (state 1 → state 2):** $$Q_{in} = \int_{s_1}^{s_2} T \, ds$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals:** The integral of a continuous function over an interval equals the function's mean value times the interval width: $$\int_{s_1}^{s_2} T \, ds = \bar{T}_1 \cdot (s_2 - s_1)$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $Q_{in}$:** $$Q_{in} = \bar{T}_1 \cdot (s_2 - s_1)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the mean temperature:** $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{Q_{in}}{s_2 - s_1}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of enthalpy** (using steam tables, since $h$ is tabulated but $Q_{in} = \Delta h$ for a steady-flow open system with negligible KE/PE changes): $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{s_2 - s_1}$$ This is the working formula used in **all KTU numerical problems**. ### 3.2 Derivation of Rankine Efficiency via MTHA **Given:** Rankine cycle on T-s diagram with states 1 (sat. liquid at condenser), 2 (compressed liquid at pump exit), 3 (superheated at turbine inlet), 4 (wet steam at turbine exit). **Step 1 — Heat added (boiler):** $$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = \bar{T}_1 (s_3 - s_2)$$ **Step 2 — Heat rejected (condenser):** $$Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = \bar{T}_2 (s_4 - s_1)$$ **Step 3 — Cycle closure on T-s diagram:** $$s_3 - s_2 = s_4 - s_1 = \Delta s_{cycle}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into efficiency expression:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_2 \cdot \Delta s_{cycle}}{\bar{T}_1 \cdot \Delta s_{cycle}}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel $\Delta s_{cycle}$:** $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_2}{\bar{T}_1}}$$ This proves that the **Rankine cycle efficiency equals a Carnot cycle efficiency operating between $\bar{T}_1$ and $\bar{T}_2$** — a powerful result for KTU exam derivations. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** In an ideal Rankine cycle, steam enters the boiler as saturated liquid at 10 MPa and leaves as superheated steam at 10 MPa, 500 °C. Turbine inlet conditions: $h_3 = 3375$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.597$ kJ/kg·K. Pump exit (state 2): $h_2 = 1010$ kJ/kg, $s_2 = 2.992$ kJ/kg·K. Determine the Mean Temperature of Heat Addition. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute heat added:** $$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3375 - 1010 = 2365 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Compute entropy change:** $$\Delta s = s_3 - s_2 = 6.597 - 2.992 = 3.605 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ **Step 3 — Compute MTHA:** $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s} = \frac{2365}{3.605}$$ $$\bar{T}_1 = 656.0 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to °C (if required):** $$T_{°C} = 656.0 - 273.15 = 382.85 \text{ °C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** $\bar{T}_1 = 656.0 \text{ K} \approx 383 \text{ °C}$. Note that this is *less than* the superheat temperature of 500 °C because MTHA is an *average* over the entire heat addition (including the sub-cooled and evaporation regions where $T < 500 \text{ °C}$). ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Cycle Configuration | $\bar{T}_1$ (approx.) | $\eta_{th}$ Effect | |---------------------|------------------------|--------------------| | No superheat, low pressure | ~ 200 °C | Lowest baseline | | Standard superheat (500 °C) | ~ 380 °C | Improved | | High superheat + reheat | ~ 450 °C | Significantly improved | | Supercritical (600 °C, 30 MPa) | ~ 500 °C | Highest efficiency | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 T-s Diagram with MTHA Visualisation The T-s diagram below shows the Rankine cycle with MTHA represented as the *height of the equivalent rectangle* whose area equals the actual heat addition area. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["State 1: Sat Liquid at Condenser Pressure
Low T, Low s"] -->|Pump 1 to 2| B["State 2: Compressed Liquid
Moderate T, Low s"] B -->|Boiler Heat Addition 2 to 3| C["State 3: Superheated Steam
High T, High s"] C -->|Turbine Expansion 3 to 4| D["State 4: Wet Steam
Moderate T, High s"] D -->|Condenser Rejection 4 to 1| A E["Mean Temperature of Heat Addition
Tm1 rectangle over s2 to s3"] -.-> B E -.-> C ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow This block diagram maps the energy and entropy interactions that govern the calculation of MTHA in a steam power plant. ```mermaid flowchart LR Feedwater["Feedwater
State 1 h1 s1"] --> Pump1["Pump
W_pump"] Pump1 --> State2["Compressed Liquid
State 2 h2 s2"] State2 --> Boiler["BOILER
Heat Source Q_in"] Boiler -->|"h3 s3 Tm1 calculation"| State3["Superheated Steam
State 3 h3 s3"] State3 --> Turbine1["Turbine
W_turbine"] Turbine1 --> State4["Exhaust Steam
State 4 h4 s4"] State4 --> Condenser["CONDENSER
Heat Sink Q_out"] Condenser -->|"Tm2 calculation"| Feedwater Calc1["MTHA FORMULA
Tm1 = h3 - h2 divided by s3 - s2"] -.-> Boiler Calc2["MTRJ FORMULA
Tm2 = h4 - h1 divided by s4 - s1"] -.-> Condenser Eff["Thermal Efficiency
eta = 1 minus Tm2 over Tm1"] -.-> Boiler Eff -.-> Condenser ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Area Equivalence on T-s Diagram > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematic:** > - The **actual heat addition** (process 2→3) traces a curve on the T-s diagram (sensible heating → evaporation → superheating). > - The **area under this curve** = $Q_{in}$. > - The **rectangle of height $\bar{T}_1$** and width $(s_3 - s_2)$ has the *same area* as the curve. > - This rectangle is the "Carnot-equivalent" representation of the heat addition process. ### 4.4 Step-by-Step Flow of MTHA Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Identify boiler inlet state
h2 and s2"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify boiler exit state
h3 and s3"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Compute Qin
Qin = h3 minus h2"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute delta s
delta s = s3 minus s2"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Apply MTHA formula
Tm1 = Qin divided by delta s"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Report Tm1 in Kelvin"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]** **Q1.** Define *Mean Temperature of Heat Addition*. State its significance in steam power plant cycle analysis. **Model Answer:** The Mean Temperature of Heat Addition ($\bar{T}_1$) is the equivalent constant temperature at which heat would have to be supplied to the working fluid over the same entropy change to deliver the same total heat as in the actual variable-temperature heat addition process in the boiler. $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{Q_{in}}{s_3 - s_2}$$ **Significance:** It allows the Rankine cycle efficiency to be expressed in Carnot-equivalent form ($\eta_{th} = 1 - \bar{T}_2 / \bar{T}_1$). Increasing $\bar{T}_1$ (via superheating, reheating, or higher boiler pressure) directly raises the cycle's thermal efficiency, making it a key design and performance metric in power plant engineering. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]** **Q2.** Why is the Mean Temperature of Heat Addition in a Rankine cycle *less than* the maximum boiler exit temperature? **Model Answer:** Because $\bar{T}_1$ is the *average* temperature over the entire heat addition process (from compressed liquid at $T_2$ to superheated steam at $T_3$), not just the peak temperature. The heat addition includes: - **Sensible heating** of sub-cooled water (where $T < T_{sat}$), - **Latent heat addition** during evaporation at constant $T_{sat}$, - **Superheating** up to the peak temperature $T_3$. The arithmetic mean of a non-constant function is always less than its maximum value. Thus $\bar{T}_1 < T_3$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive an expression for the Mean Temperature of Heat Addition in an ideal Rankine cycle. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In an ideal Rankine cycle, saturated liquid water at 0.1 MPa is pumped to a boiler pressure of 8 MPa. The steam leaves the boiler at 8 MPa and 450 °C. Compute: (i) The Mean Temperature of Heat Addition, and (ii) The cycle thermal efficiency. Given: At 8 MPa, 450 °C — $h_3 = 3273.3$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.5551$ kJ/kg·K. Pump exit (state 2): $h_2 = 1022.9$ kJ/kg, $s_2 = 2.9915$ kJ/kg·K. Turbine exhaust (state 4): $h_4 = 2244.7$ kJ/kg, $s_4 = 6.5551$ kJ/kg·K. Condenser exit (state 1): $h_1 = 191.8$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 0.6493$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):** For a reversible heat transfer process, from the second law: $$dQ_{rev} = T \, ds$$ Total heat added in the boiler (process 2→3): $$Q_{in} = \int_{s_2}^{s_3} T \, ds$$ By the Mean Value Theorem: $$Q_{in} = \bar{T}_1 (s_3 - s_2)$$ Solving for $\bar{T}_1$: $$\boxed{\bar{T}_1 = \frac{Q_{in}}{s_3 - s_2} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) MTHA Calculation:** Step 1 — Heat added: $$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3273.3 - 1022.9 = 2250.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 2 — Entropy change: $$\Delta s = s_3 - s_2 = 6.5551 - 2.9915 = 3.5636 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ Step 3 — Mean temperature: $$\bar{T}_1 = \frac{2250.4}{3.5636} = 631.5 \text{ K}$$ $$\bar{T}_1 \approx 358.3 \text{ °C}$$ **(ii) Cycle Efficiency:** Step 1 — Heat rejected: $$Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = 2244.7 - 191.8 = 2052.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 2 — Net work: $$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 2250.4 - 2052.9 = 197.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 3 — Efficiency: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{197.5}{2250.4} = 0.0878 = 8.78\%$$ **Verification via MTHA approach:** $$\bar{T}_2 = \frac{Q_{out}}{s_4 - s_1} = \frac{2052.9}{6.5551 - 0.6493} = \frac{2052.9}{5.9058} = 347.6 \text{ K}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{347.6}{631.5} = 1 - 0.5505 = 44.95\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Mismatch Alert!** The two efficiency calculations *should* give the same answer. The discrepancy arises because the given state 4 ($h_4, s_4$) is *not* consistent with an isentropic expansion from state 3 to 0.1 MPa in this problem statement (likely a typo in the question). For KTU exam writing, **present both calculations** and **state the discrepancy explicitly** — examiners reward the recognition. #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the Mean Temperature of Heat Addition is determined from the T-s diagram of a Rankine cycle. Discuss its physical significance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A steam power plant operates between 2 MPa (boiler) and 10 kPa (condenser). Steam leaves the boiler at 350 °C. Determine: (i) The Mean Temperature of Heat Addition, and (ii) The percentage improvement in $\bar{T}_1$ if the steam is superheated to 500 °C instead. Steam table data (Given): - At 2 MPa, 350 °C: $h_3 = 3137.0$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.9563$ kJ/kg·K - At 2 MPa, 500 °C: $h_3' = 3467.6$ kJ/kg, $s_3' = 7.4317$ kJ/kg·K - Pump exit: $h_2 \approx 208.4$ kJ/kg, $s_2 \approx 0.6457$ kJ/kg·K - Saturated liquid at 10 kPa: $h_1 = 191.8$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 0.6493$ kJ/kg·K **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution:** **(a) Explanation (7 Marks):** On the T-s diagram of the Rankine cycle, draw the rectangular area (height = $\bar{T}_1$, width = $s_3 - s_2$) such that its area equals the area under the actual heat addition curve (process 2→3). The height of this rectangle is the Mean Temperature of Heat Addition. **Physical Significance:** - It is the *Carnot-equivalent source temperature* for the cycle. - The efficiency formula $\eta_{th} = 1 - \bar{T}_2 / \bar{T}_1$ is identical in form to Carnot efficiency. - Superheating, reheating, and higher boiler pressure all raise $\bar{T}_1$, thereby improving efficiency. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Case 1: Steam at 350 °C** Step 1 — Heat added: $$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3137.0 - 208.4 = 2928.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 2 — Entropy change: $$\Delta s = 6.9563 - 0.6457 = 6.3106 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ Step 3 — MTHA: $$\bar{T}_{1,350} = \frac{2928.6}{6.3106} = 464.1 \text{ K}$$ **Case 2: Steam at 500 °C** Step 1 — Heat added: $$Q_{in}' = h_3' - h_2 = 3467.6 - 208.4 = 3259.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 2 — Entropy change: $$\Delta s' = 7.4317 - 0.6457 = 6.7860 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$ Step 3 — MTHA: $$\bar{T}_{1,500} = \frac{3259.2}{6.7860} = 480.3 \text{ K}$$ **Percentage Improvement:** $$\%\text{ improvement} = \frac{480.3 - 464.1}{464.1} \times 100\% = \frac{16.2}{464.1} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\%\text{ improvement} \approx 3.49\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit trap:** Many students forget to convert °C → K when computing MTHA, leading to grossly wrong answers. **Always use Kelvin in the formula.** > 2. **Formula trap:** Some students confuse MTHA with **Mean Effective Temperature** or with **Arithmetic Mean of $T_2$ and $T_3$**. MTHA is *not* $(T_2 + T_3)/2$; it is the *integral-average* weighted by $ds$. > 3. **Entropy mismatch:** Always verify that the given $s_3$ corresponds to the boiler exit state — not a different pressure point. > 4. **Cycle closure:** When applying $\eta_{th} = 1 - \bar{T}_2 / \bar{T}_1$, ensure the entropy change during heat addition equals that during heat rejection. If they differ, the formula needs adjustment. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mean Temperature of Heat Addition** - ✅ **Definition:** Equivalent constant temperature of heat addition — area-preserving rectangle on T-s diagram. - ✅ **Working Formula:** $\bar{T}_1 = (h_3 - h_2) / (s_3 - s_2)$ — always in **Kelvin**. - ✅ **Always greater than** $T_{sat}$ (boiler saturation temp) and **less than** $T_3$ (peak steam temp). - ✅ **Carnot equivalence:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \bar{T}_2 / \bar{T}_1$ — valid for any cycle (Rankine, Otto, Diesel, Brayton). - ✅ **Design levers to raise $\bar{T}_1$:** - Higher **boiler pressure** (raises $T_{sat}$) - **Superheating** the steam - **Reheating** in multi-stage turbines - Use of **supercritical / ultra-supercritical** steam parameters - ✅ **Higher $\bar{T}_1$ = higher thermal efficiency** (all else equal). - ✅ **In KTU problems:** Always show the unit clearly; convert °C → K before computation. - ✅ **MTHA ≠ arithmetic mean** of inlet/outlet temperatures — it is the *entropy-weighted* mean. - ✅ **Practical range:** For modern subcritical plants, $\bar{T}_1 \approx 350\text{–}400 \text{ °C}$; for ultra-supercritical, it can exceed **600 °C**. - ✅ **Typical 14-mark question pattern:** Derivation (7 marks) + numerical computation (7 marks). Memorise the derivation steps verbatim.

topics.title: Effect of Superheat and Inlet Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Superheat and Inlet Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Superheating — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superheating** is the process of heating **saturated (dry) steam** to a temperature **above its saturation temperature** at the **same pressure**, by adding heat in a dedicated component called a **Superheater**.

In the Rankine cycle, the superheater is placed immediately after the boiler exit. The steam leaving the superheater is called **superheated steam** or **superheated vapor**, and its state is represented on the **T-s diagram** as a point to the right of the saturation dome.

**Key Notation (KTU Standard):**
- $T_3$ → Temperature of superheated steam at turbine inlet
- $T_{sat}$ → Saturation temperature corresponding to boiler pressure
- **Degree of Superheat** = $T_3 - T_{sat}$ (in **°C** or **K**)
- **Pressure** at which superheating occurs = Boiler Pressure = $P_1$ = $P_2$ = $P_3$ (isentropic pump work is negligible, so $P_1 \approx P_2$)

## 1.2 Inlet Pressure — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inlet Pressure** ($P_1$) is the absolute pressure at which the working fluid (water) is converted into steam inside the **boiler**. It is also called **Boiler Pressure** or **Steam Generator Pressure**, since the entire Rankine cycle operates above atmospheric pressure.

In modern KTU-context thermal power stations, inlet pressure typically ranges from:
- **Subcritical Plants:** 150 bar – 220 bar
- **Supercritical Units:** > 221.2 bar (Critical Pressure of water = **220.64 bar**)
- **Ultra-Supercritical:** 250 bar – 350 bar
- **Advanced Ultra-Supercritical (AUSC):** Up to 700 bar

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Pot vs. Pressure Cooker" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for Superheat:** Imagine boiling water in an open vessel. Steam bubbles out at 100 °C — that is *saturated* steam. Now imagine a chef passes that steam through a hot pipe (a *superheater*) before it reaches the turbine. The steam is now "drier, hotter, and carries more punch" — just like a cricket ball bowled at higher speed carries more energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for Inlet Pressure:** Now replace the open pot with a **pressure cooker**. As you lock the lid and increase the pressure inside, the boiling point of water rises (e.g., to 121 °C at 1 bar gauge). The higher the pressure, the **higher the temperature at which heat is added** to the working fluid. Higher temperature of heat addition = **higher cycle efficiency** (Carnot logic).

> [!TIP]
> **Combined Intuition:** Superheat raises the *upper limit* of temperature, while increased pressure raises the *saturation floor* (and the entire heat-addition curve). Both push the cycle's "average heat-addition temperature" upward — which is the heart of why modern power plants go to extreme pressures and temperatures.

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used in This Module

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Critical Pressure of Water | $P_{cr}$ | **22.064 MPa (220.64 bar)** |
| Critical Temperature of Water | $T_{cr}$ | **374.15 °C (647.3 K)** |
| Triple Point of Water | — | **0.006112 bar, 0.01 °C** |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** |
| Specific Heat of Superheated Steam (avg.) | $C_p$ | **2.1 kJ/(kg·K)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rankine Cycle — Re-baseline

The Rankine cycle has four ideal processes:
1. **1→2:** Isentropic compression in **Pump** (water compressed from condenser pressure $P_4$ to boiler pressure $P_1$).
2. **2→3:** Isobaric heat addition in **Boiler + Superheater** (water → saturated steam → superheated steam).
3. **3→4:** Isentropic expansion in **Turbine** (superheated steam → wet steam at condenser pressure).
4. **4→1:** Isobaric heat rejection in **Condenser** (wet steam → saturated liquid at $P_4$).

## 2.2 Why Superheat and Inlet Pressure Matter — The Core "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental principle:** The thermal efficiency of any heat engine depends on the **mean temperature of heat addition** ($\bar{T}_{add}$) and the **mean temperature of heat rejection** ($\bar{T}_{rej}$). The Carnot relation:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$

shows that **raising $\bar{T}_{add}$** (and **lowering $\bar{T}_{rej}$**) increases efficiency. Superheating and raising inlet pressure **both increase $\bar{T}_{add}$**, but by different mechanisms.

## 2.3 Effect of Superheating — Step-by-Step Logic

> **Step 1:** At a given boiler pressure $P_1$, saturation temperature is fixed at $T_{sat}(P_1)$.
> **Step 2:** Without superheater, the cycle heat addition occurs along the **saturated vapor line** — limited to $T_{sat}$.
> **Step 3:** Adding a superheater extends heat addition **beyond $T_{sat}$** to a higher temperature $T_3$.
> **Step 4:** The mean temperature of heat addition $\bar{T}_{add}$ **increases** because the heat is now added across a wider (and higher) temperature range.
> **Step 5:** From the Carnot logic, $\eta_{th}$ **increases**.
> **Step 6:** Additionally, superheating **reduces moisture content** at turbine exhaust, preventing blade erosion.

### Quantitative Effect of Superheat:
- For every **~10 °C rise** in superheat, the Rankine cycle efficiency rises by approximately **0.5 – 1%** (rule of thumb in KTU-standard problems).
- Modern plants use superheats of **~540 °C – 600 °C**.

## 2.4 Effect of Inlet Pressure (Boiler Pressure) — Step-by-Step Logic

> **Step 1:** As $P_1$ increases, the saturation temperature $T_{sat}(P_1)$ of water increases.
> **Step 2:** The whole heat addition process is shifted to a **higher temperature region** on the T-s diagram.
> **Step 3:** $\bar{T}_{add}$ rises, so $\eta_{th}$ rises.
> **Step 4:** However, the **moisture content at turbine exhaust increases** with pressure (because expansion starts from a higher point on the saturation dome and reaches the same low condenser pressure).
> **Step 5:** To counter this, plants either:
>   - Use **superheat** (covered above)
>   - Use **reheat** (covered in subsequent modules)
>   - **Reheat + regenerative feedwater heating**

### Practical Trade-off:
- Pressure too low → poor efficiency
- Pressure too high → excessive moisture, costly thick-walled boiler, metallurgy limits
- **Optimum pressure** in real plants: 150–250 bar (subcritical) or supercritical range

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the complete set of formulas required for any KTU exam question on this topic. **Memorize these.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal Efficiency (Rankine) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | dimensionless | Most-used KTU formula |
| 2 | Turbine Work | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Specific work output |
| 3 | Pump Work | $W_P = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Usually small, $\approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$ |
| 4 | Heat Added | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Includes sensible + latent + superheat |
| 5 | Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | In condenser |
| 6 | Steam Quality at Turbine Exit | $x_4 = \dfrac{h_4 - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\geq 0.88$ to avoid blade erosion |
| 7 | Specific Volume (Saturated Liquid) | $h_2 \approx h_1 + v_1(P_1 - P_4)$ | kJ/kg | Pump work approximation |
| 8 | Degree of Superheat | $\Delta T_{sh} = T_3 - T_{sat}(P_1)$ | K or °C | Direct superheat measure |
| 9 | Specific Heat of Superheated Steam | $h_3 = h_g + C_p(T_3 - T_{sat})$ | kJ/kg | Approx. at constant $P$ |
| 10 | Mean Temperature of Heat Addition | $\bar{T}_{add} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s_{total}}$ | K | $\Delta s_{total} = s_3 - s_2$ |
| 11 | Work Ratio | $WR = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | Indicates pump penalty |
| 12 | Back Work Ratio | $BWR = \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | Should be small |
| 13 | Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 14 | Carnot Efficiency (Reference) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{rej}}{T_{add}}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical upper bound |
| 15 | Relative Efficiency | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta_{Rankine}}{\eta_{Carnot}}$ | dimensionless | Realistic performance index |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> - Modern 800 MW supercritical units operate at **250 bar / 600 °C** with **double reheat**, achieving cycle efficiencies of **~45–47%**.
> - **USC plants** (700 bar / 700 °C) target **~50%+** efficiency.
> - **A-USC research plants** (AUSC) target efficiencies of **> 55%** with reduced CO₂ emissions.
> - These gains are **directly traceable** to the superheat and inlet pressure principles studied in this module.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for Thermal Efficiency of Rankine Cycle

**Statement:** Derive the thermal efficiency of the Rankine cycle in terms of enthalpy at the four cardinal states.

> **Step 1: Define net work output.**
> The net work per kg of working fluid is the algebraic sum of turbine work and pump work.

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P
$$

> **Step 2: Express turbine and pump work as enthalpy differences** (steady-flow energy equation, SFEE, with negligible KE and PE changes).

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4
$$

$$
W_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

> **Step 3: Substitute back into the net work equation.**

$$
W_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)
$$

> **Step 4: Define heat input** (heat added in boiler + superheater, at constant pressure).

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2
$$

> **Step 5: Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

> **Step 6: Re-express using $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$** where $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$.

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}
$$

This is the **master KTU equation** for Rankine cycle efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Superheat on Mean Temperature of Heat Addition

**Statement:** Prove mathematically that increasing degree of superheat raises $\bar{T}_{add}$.

> **Step 1:** The mean temperature of heat addition is defined as:

$$
\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s_{add}} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}
$$

> **Step 2:** For an ideal cycle, the heat addition in the boiler occurs at constant pressure. If we split the process into **sensible heating (2→2'), evaporation (2'→2''), and superheating (2''→3)**, we have:

$$
h_3 - h_2 = (h_{2'} - h_2) + h_{fg} + (h_3 - h_{2''})
$$

> **Step 3:** The corresponding entropy change is:

$$
s_3 - s_2 = (s_{2'} - s_2) + \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}} + \int_{T_{sat}}^{T_3} \frac{C_p}{T} dT
$$

> **Step 4:** Solving the integral:

$$
s_3 - s_2 = (s_{2'} - s_2) + \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}} + C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_3}{T_{sat}}\right)
$$

> **Step 5:** Substitute back into the mean temperature expression:

$$
\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{(h_{2'} - h_2) + h_{fg} + C_p (T_3 - T_{sat})}{(s_{2'} - s_2) + \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}} + C_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_3}{T_{sat}}\right)}
$$

> **Step 6:** As $T_3$ increases, both numerator and denominator grow, but the **numerator grows faster** (linear in $T_3$) than the denominator (logarithmic in $T_3$). Hence $\bar{T}_{add}$ **increases monotonically with $T_3$**.

> **Step 7:** Therefore, $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$ **increases** with superheat. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Inlet Pressure on $\bar{T}_{add}$

> **Step 1:** The mean temperature of heat addition, written generally as:

$$
\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{\int_{2}^{3} T\,ds}{s_3 - s_2}
$$

> **Step 2:** As $P_1$ increases, $T_{sat}$ increases (e.g., at 100 bar, $T_{sat} = 311 °C$; at 200 bar, $T_{sat} = 365 °C$).

> **Step 3:** The whole T-s curve is shifted **upward and to the right** along the saturation dome. The lower limit of integration moves from $T_{sat,low}$ to $T_{sat,high}$.

> **Step 4:** Numerically, $\bar{T}_{add}$ is found to scale with the Clausius relation:

$$
\bar{T}_{add} \approx T_{sat}(P_1) + \Delta T_{sh}/2
$$

> **Step 5:** Thus, $d\bar{T}_{add}/dP_1 > 0$ for subcritical range, confirming efficiency improvement with pressure. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem:** A Rankine cycle operates between a boiler pressure of **100 bar** and a condenser pressure of **0.1 bar**. Steam is superheated to **500 °C** at the turbine inlet. Calculate:
> (a) The thermal efficiency
> (b) The work ratio
> (c) The steam quality at turbine exit
> (d) Comment on the effect of raising superheat to 600 °C and inlet pressure to 150 bar.

### Property Extraction (from steam tables):

| State | $P$ (bar) | $T$ (°C) | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $s$ (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (sat. liquid at 0.1 bar) | 0.1 | 45.81 | $h_1 = 191.8$ | $s_1 = 0.6493$ |
| 2 (compressed liquid at 100 bar) | 100 | — | $h_2 = 211.5$ | $s_2 = 0.6493$ |
| 3 (superheated, 100 bar, 500 °C) | 100 | 500 | $h_3 = 3373.7$ | $s_3 = 6.5966$ |
| 4 (wet steam at 0.1 bar) | 0.1 | — | $h_4 = ?$ | $s_4 = s_3 = 6.5966$ |

> **Step 1 (Pump Work):** Using incompressible liquid approximation,

$$
W_P = h_2 - h_1 = v_1 (P_1 - P_4)
$$

$$
W_P = 0.00101 \times (100 - 0.1) \times 100 = 10.09 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

> **Step 2 (Turbine Inlet Condition):** From superheated steam tables at 100 bar, 500 °C:

$$
h_3 = 3373.7 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_3 = 6.5966 \text{ kJ/kg·K}
$$

> **Step 3 (Turbine Exit — Quality):** At 0.1 bar: $h_f = 191.8$, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$, $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$.

$$
x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.5966 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.7935
$$

> **Step 4 (Turbine Exit Enthalpy):**

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.8 + 0.7935 \times 2392.8 = 2090.7 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

> **Step 5 (Turbine Work and Net Work):**

$$
W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3373.7 - 2090.7 = 1283.0 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 1283.0 - 10.09 = 1272.9 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

> **Step 6 (Heat Input):**

$$
Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3373.7 - 211.5 = 3162.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

> **Step 7 (Thermal Efficiency):**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{1272.9}{3162.2} = 0.4026 = 40.26\%
$$

> **Step 8 (Work Ratio):**

$$
WR = \frac{W_{net}}{W_T} = \frac{1272.9}{1283.0} = 0.9921
$$

> **Step 9 (Comment on Superheat ↑ to 600 °C and P ↑ to 150 bar):**
> - Raising $T_3$ to 600 °C increases $h_3$ (≈ 3625 kJ/kg) and $s_3$. Turbine work grows; $x_4$ becomes **drier** (closer to 0.88–0.92), and $\eta_{th}$ rises by ~1.5–2%.
> - Raising $P_1$ to 150 bar increases $T_{sat}$ to ≈ 342 °C. $\bar{T}_{add}$ rises. However, $x_4$ **decreases** (more moisture at exhaust), so **reheat is recommended**.
> - Combined: efficiency improves to **~42–44%**, but blade erosion risk demands reheat or moisture separation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Qualitative Summary

| Parameter Change | $\eta_{th}$ | $W_{net}$ | $x_4$ (dryness) | Boiler Cost | Turbine Life |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Superheat ↑ (P fixed) | ↑↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Slightly | ↑↑ Better |
| Inlet Pressure ↑ (T₃ fixed) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases (wetter) | ↑↑↑ Heavily | ↓↓ Worse (erosion) |
| Both ↑ together | ↑↑↑ Best | ↑↑ | ↑ (if balanced) | ↑↑↑↑ | Depends |
| Condenser Pressure ↓ | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | Same | Better |

```mermaid
        nodeA2 -->|Boiler: Saturated Vapor Line| nodeA3["State 2s: Saturated Vapor at P1"]
        nodeA3 -->|Superheater: Heat Addition beyond Tsat| nodeA4["State 3: SUPERHEATED STEAM at Higher T3"]
        nodeA4 -->|Turbine: Isentropic Expansion| nodeA5["State 4: Wet Steam at Condenser Pressure"]
        nodeA5 -->|Condenser: Heat Rejection| nodeA1
    end
    style nodeA4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeA3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The path **2 → 2' → 3** represents water → saturated steam → superheated steam. The **horizontal jump beyond the dome** to state 3 is the *superheat region*. Higher $T_3$ moves state 3 further right on the T-s plot.

```mermaid

    end
    subgraph pathC["Cycle Path at P1 = 200 bar, T3 = 500 C"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define the term *degree of superheat*. How does it differ from *superheat temperature*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Degree of Superheat:** It is the numerical difference between the temperature of superheated steam and the saturation temperature at the same pressure.
>
> $$\Delta T_{sh} = T_3 - T_{sat}(P_1)$$
>
> **Superheat temperature** is a general qualitative term referring to the *condition* of steam being heated above its saturation point, while the **degree of superheat** is the *quantitative measure* (in °C or K) of how far above the saturation point the steam has been heated.
>
> Example: At 100 bar, $T_{sat} = 311 °C$. If steam is at 500 °C, then degree of superheat = **189 °C**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** State the effect of increasing the boiler pressure (inlet pressure) on the dryness fraction at the turbine exhaust in a Rankine cycle.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> As the boiler pressure $P_1$ is **increased** (at constant turbine inlet temperature $T_3$), the steam expands across a larger pressure ratio. The state at the turbine exit lies **deeper inside the wet region** of the saturation dome.
>
> This causes the **dryness fraction $x_4$ to decrease**, leading to **higher moisture content** in the exhaust steam. If $x_4$ falls below **0.88**, the moisture droplets cause **erosion of turbine blades**.
>
> **Remedies:** Use reheat cycle, regenerative feedwater heating, or moisture separators.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** With the help of a T-s diagram, explain the effect of superheat and inlet pressure on the performance of a Rankine cycle. Discuss the practical limitations in adopting very high superheat and very high boiler pressure.

**OR**

#### **(a)** Discuss how superheat and inlet pressure affect the **mean temperature of heat addition** in a Rankine cycle. Show the effect on a T-s diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> The thermal efficiency of the Rankine cycle is governed by:
>
> $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$
>
> where $\bar{T}_{add}$ is the mean temperature at which heat is added in the boiler and superheater.
>
> **Effect of Superheat:** At a given boiler pressure $P_1$, increasing the superheat from $T_{sat}$ to a higher $T_3$ shifts the upper limit of heat addition upward on the T-s diagram. The mean temperature of heat addition rises because:
>
> $$\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{\int_{2}^{3} T\,ds}{\Delta s_{add}}$$
>
> Numerically, $\bar{T}_{add}$ increases linearly with degree of superheat, leading to **higher thermal efficiency**.
>
> **Effect of Inlet Pressure:** As $P_1$ increases, the saturation temperature $T_{sat}(P_1)$ increases, and the entire heat-addition process occurs at a higher temperature range. The T-s curve is **shifted upward and to the right**, increasing $\bar{T}_{add}$ and therefore $\eta_{th}$.
>
> **T-s Diagram:** The figure (in Section 4.2) shows two cycles — one at $P_1 = 100$ bar and another at $P_1 = 200$ bar. The second cycle has a higher mean heat-addition temperature, validating the analysis.
>
> **Conclusion:** Both superheat and inlet pressure raise $\bar{T}_{add}$, but they have **opposing effects on exhaust moisture**, requiring a balanced design.

#### **(b)** What are the **practical limitations** in adopting very high superheat and very high boiler pressure? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Limitations on Superheat:**
> 1. **Metallurgical limits** — boiler tubes, superheater tubes, and turbine blade materials lose strength above **~620 °C** (for standard ferritic steels) and require exotic alloys (Inconel, austenitic steels) for higher temperatures.
> 2. **Cost escalation** — high-temperature alloys are expensive and difficult to fabricate.
> 3. **Creep and oxidation** of tubes at sustained high temperatures reduce component life.
> 4. **Higher thermal stresses** during start-up and load variations.
>
> **Limitations on Inlet Pressure:**
> 1. **Thicker boiler drums and tubes** are required to withstand high pressure, drastically increasing capital cost.
> 2. **Higher moisture content** at turbine exhaust leads to blade erosion. If $x_4 < 0.88$, **reheat or moisture separators** become mandatory.
> 3. **Pump work** $W_P$ increases with pressure, slightly offsetting the efficiency gain.
> 4. **Critical and supercritical regions** require **once-through boilers** instead of drum-type boilers, increasing complexity.
> 5. **Safety considerations** — high-pressure systems need stricter safety protocols.
>
> **Conclusion:** Modern plants balance superheat and pressure at **~540–600 °C / 150–250 bar (subcritical)** or **~600 °C / 250 bar+ (supercritical)** to optimize cost, efficiency, and reliability.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** A steam power plant operates on an ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa, 350 °C** and is condensed in the condenser at **10 kPa**. Determine:
- **(a)** The thermal efficiency of the cycle, work ratio, and steam quality at turbine exit. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** If the turbine inlet pressure is increased to **5 MPa** (with the same superheat of 350 °C) and condenser pressure remains 10 kPa, comment on the change in thermal efficiency, work ratio, and exhaust steam quality. **(7 marks)**

#### **(a) Solution (7 marks):**

> **State 1: Saturated liquid at 10 kPa** ($T_{sat} = 45.81 °C$):
> $h_1 = 191.81$ kJ/kg, $v_1 = 0.001010$ m³/kg, $s_1 = 0.6492$ kJ/kg·K
>
> **State 2: After pump:**
> $h_2 = h_1 + v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = 191.81 + 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 194.83$ kJ/kg
>
> **State 3: Superheated steam at 3 MPa, 350 °C** (from superheated steam tables):
> $h_3 = 3115.3$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K
>
> **State 4: After isentropic expansion to 10 kPa:**
> $s_4 = s_3 = 6.7428$ kJ/kg·K
> At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_{fg} = 7.4996$
>
> $$x_4 = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = 0.8125$$
>
> $h_4 = 191.81 + 0.8125 \times 2392.1 = 2135.0$ kJ/kg
>
> **Turbine work:** $W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3115.3 - 2135.0 = 980.3$ kJ/kg
>
> **Net work:** $W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 980.3 - 3.02 = 977.3$ kJ/kg
>
> **Heat input:** $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3115.3 - 194.83 = 2920.5$ kJ/kg
>
> **Thermal Efficiency:**
>
> $$\eta_{th} = \frac{977.3}{2920.5} = 0.3346 = 33.46\%$$
>
> **Work Ratio:**
>
> $$WR = \frac{977.3}{980.3} = 0.9969$$
>
> **Steam Quality at Exit:** $x_4 = 0.8125$

#### **(b) Solution (7 marks):**

> At **5 MPa, 350 °C** (from superheated tables):
> $h_3' = 3068.4$ kJ/kg, $s_3' = 6.4571$ kJ/kg·K
>
> **State 2':** $h_2' = 191.81 + 0.001010 \times (5000 - 10) = 196.85$ kJ/kg
>
> **State 4':** $s_4' = s_3' = 6.4571$ kJ/kg·K
> $x_4' = \dfrac{6.4571 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = 0.7744$
> $h_4' = 191.81 + 0.7744 \times 2392.1 = 2044.2$ kJ/kg
>
> **Turbine work:** $W_T' = 3068.4 - 2044.2 = 1024.2$ kJ/kg
>
> **Net work:** $W_{net}' = 1024.2 - 5.04 = 1019.2$ kJ/kg
>
> **Heat input:** $Q_{in}' = 3068.4 - 196.85 = 2871.6$ kJ/kg
>
> **New thermal efficiency:**
>
> $$\eta_{th}' = \frac{1019.2}{2871.6} = 0.3549 = 35.49\%$$
>
> **New work ratio:**
>
> $$WR' = \frac{1019.2}{1024.2} = 0.9951$$
>
> **New exhaust quality:** $x_4' = 0.7744$
>
> **Comments:**
> - Thermal efficiency increased from **33.46% to 35.49%** (≈ **+2%**) due to higher mean temperature of heat addition.
> - Net work increased from 977.3 to 1019.2 kJ/kg.
> - Exhaust quality **decreased from 0.8125 to 0.7744** — moisture content increased, indicating **need for reheat cycle**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks for):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting pump work** — Many students compute $W_{net} = W_T$ only. While pump work is small (≈1%), KTU examiners **explicitly deduct 1–2 marks** for neglecting it. Always include $W_P = v_1(P_1 - P_4)$.
>
> 2. **Using wrong steam table columns** — At 3 MPa, 350 °C is **superheated** (since $T_{sat} = 233.9 °C$). At 5 MPa, 350 °C is **also superheated** ($T_{sat} = 263.9 °C$). Misreading the table is the **#1 source of calculation errors**.
>
> 3. **Confusing degree of superheat with superheat temperature** — see Part A Q1 above.
>
> 4. **Ignoring moisture constraint** — If the calculated $x_4 < 0.88$, you **must** state that the design is not feasible and recommend reheat. Examiners look for this critical comment.
>
> 5. **Unit errors** — Pressures in steam tables are in **MPa** or **bar**, but specific volumes in **m³/kg**. Always check the unit consistency. Pump work formula: $W_P = v_1 (P_1 - P_4) \times 100$ if $P$ is in bar and $v$ in m³/kg.
>
> 6. **Skipping the T-s diagram** — In "Discuss" questions, the T-s diagram carries **2–3 marks** by itself. Always draw it, even if rough.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**
>
> - [x] **Superheat** raises $T_3$ above $T_{sat}$ at the same $P_1$, increasing $\bar{T}_{add}$ and $\eta_{th}$.
> - [x] **Inlet pressure** raises $T_{sat}$ and shifts the entire heat-addition curve to higher T, increasing $\eta_{th}$.
> - [x] **Both** raise $\bar{T}_{add}$ but have **opposing effects** on exhaust dryness: superheat *dries* the exhaust; high pressure *wets* it.
> - [x] **Master formula:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}$ (Kumar-style Rankine efficiency).
> - [x] **Pump work approximation:** $h_2 \approx h_1 + v_1(P_1 - P_4)$.
> - [x] **Critical limits:** $P_{cr} = 220.64$ bar, $T_{cr} = 374.15 °C$.
> - [x] **Moisture constraint:** $x_4 \geq 0.88$ for safe turbine operation.
> - [x] **Practical superheat limit:** ~600–620 °C (metallurgical), advanced plants push to 700 °C (AUSC).
> - [x] **Work ratio** $WR = 1 - W_P/W_T$ is typically **> 0.99** in modern plants.
> - [x] **Back work ratio** $BWR = W_P/W_T$ is typically **< 0.01** (negligible in Rankine, unlike gas turbines).
> - [x] **Net work per kg** of steam is the most important practical design parameter.
> - [x] **Reheat** is the standard solution for high-pressure Rankine cycles with low $x_4$.
> - [x] **Carnot comparison** — Rankine cycle approaches Carnot when superheat and pressure are high.
> - [x] Always draw the **T-s diagram** with the saturation dome, isentropes, and labeled states.
> - [x] **Exam Tip:** Practice at least 3 numerical problems covering different combinations of $P_1$, $T_3$, and $P_{cond}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Generator Classifications: Cochran Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Generator Classifications: Cochran Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Steam Generator — The Formal Definition

A **Steam Generator (Boiler)** is a closed pressure vessel in which water is converted into steam by transferring the heat released from the combustion of fuel to the water contained within the vessel. According to the **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)**, a boiler is defined as "any closed vessel exceeding **1 m³ in capacity** which is used exclusively for the generation of steam and is subjected to internal pressures exceeding **1 atmosphere (≈ 101.325 kPa)**."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Steam Generator" is preferred over "Boiler" in modern nomenclature to distinguish water-tube designs where steam is *generated* in tubes rather than around them.

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Steam Generators (Boilers)

Boilers can be classified on several criteria, and the KTU 2024 module primarily follows the structural classification:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Classification Table (Most Frequently Asked):**
>
> | Basis | Type | Example |
> |---|---|---|
> | Tube–Water Contact | **Fire-Tube** (hot gases inside tube, water outside) | Cochran, Lancashire, Locomotive |
> | | **Water-Tube** (water inside tube, hot gases outside) | Babcock–Wilcox, La-Mont |
> | Axis of Shell | **Vertical** | Cochran |
> | | **Horizontal** | Lancashire, Locomotive |
> | Pressure | Low (< 20 bar), Medium (20–40 bar), High (> 40 bar) | — |
> | Circulation | Natural, Forced, Assisted | — |
> | Mobility | Stationary, Portable, Locomotive, Marine | — |

### 1.3 Cochran Boiler — Formal Academic Definition

The **Cochran Boiler** is a **vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, fire-tube boiler** with a **hemispherical (dished) crown** at both the top and bottom, designed for **small-to-medium capacity steam generation**. It was patented by **William Cochran** in **1899** and is one of the most efficient vertical boilers available in the low-pressure range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specifications (KTU Standard Values to Memorize):**
> - **Capacity Range:** **1500 – 4000 kg of steam per hour**
> - **Working Pressure:** **up to 15 bar** (modern variants up to 20 bar)
> - **Efficiency:** **70 % – 75 %**
> - **Fuel:** Coal, wood, or bagasse
> - **Shell Diameter:** 1.25 – 2.75 m
> - **Shell Height:** 2.5 – 6.0 m

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **"Think of a Pressure Cooker Sitting Upside-Down on a Stove..."**
>
