> Imagine a tall, thick-walled steel drum (like a commercial pressure cooker) turned **upside-down on a stove**. Inside its lower bulge, there's a small firebox. From this firebox, dozens of thin pipes shoot **upward through the body of the cooker**, which is filled with water. The fire heats the pipes, the pipes heat the surrounding water, and steam bubbles collect in the curved top — just like the steam collecting in the lid of a pressure cooker.
>
> That's essentially the **Cochran Boiler**:
> - The **drum** = the cylindrical shell.
> - The **thin pipes** = the fire tubes.
> - The **firebox at the bottom** = the hemispherical furnace crown.
> - The **curved top** = the hemispherical steam dome.
>
> The hemispherical shape is no coincidence — a hemisphere is the strongest geometric shape for resisting internal pressure (same reason a satellite dish and a submarine's end cap are hemispherical).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "Hemispherical" Top and Bottom?

A sphere of radius $R$ resists internal pressure $P$ with a membrane (hoop) stress given by:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot R}{2 \cdot t}$$

Compare this with a cylinder (used in Lancashire/horizontal boilers):

$$\sigma_{hoop}^{cyl} = \frac{P \cdot R}{t}$$

A hemisphere produces **half the hoop stress** of a cylinder of the same radius and thickness. This is why the Cochran boiler can safely operate at higher pressures despite being a fire-tube design.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Constructional Details

The Cochran boiler consists of the following major components, listed from bottom to top:

1. **Grate (Step Grate)**
   - Sloped, cast-iron steps.
   - Permits ash to slide down and fall into the ash pit.
   - Allows primary air to enter from below.

2. **Combustion Chamber / Furnace (Hemispherical)**
   - A hemispherical dome of firebrick-lined steel at the bottom.
   - Direct combustion of fuel occurs here.
   - The hemispherical shape reflects radiant heat back onto the fire and is mechanically very strong.

3. **Fire Tubes**
   - A large number of **horizontal smoke tubes** (typically 100–150 tubes, each ~100 mm diameter) pass from the combustion chamber to the smoke box at the back.
   - Hot combustion gases flow through these tubes, transferring heat to the surrounding water.

4. **Combustion Space**
   - The space around the fire tubes inside the shell, **completely filled with water** during operation.
   - This is the "heat exchange zone."

5. **Shell (Cylindrical Body)**
   - Made of steel plates **riveted** (older units) or **welded** (modern units).
   - Outer surface is lagged with insulating material (magnesia, glass wool) and covered with a sheet-metal jacket to minimize heat loss.

6. **Smoke Box**
   - Located at the back (top) of the shell where fire tubes terminate.
   - Collects the exhaust gases before they enter the chimney.

7. **Chimney**
   - Short, directly mounted on the smoke box.
   - Provides natural draft for the fire.

8. **Manholes**
   - Provided at the top and side of the shell for internal inspection and cleaning of scale and soot.

9. **Mountings and Fittings**
   - Pressure gauge, water level indicator, safety valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug, steam stop valve, etc. (covered in detail in Module 2–Part B of your syllabus).

### 2.3 Working Principle — The Six Logical Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence of Operations (Exam-Ready Bullets):**
>
> 1. **Coal feeding:** Fresh coal is fed onto the inclined step grate manually.
> 2. **Combustion:** Coal burns in the hemispherical combustion chamber. Primary air enters from the ash pit; secondary air passes through the fire door.
> 3. **Hot gas flow:** Hot combustion products rise through the furnace dome and reverse direction **horizontally**, flowing through the **fire tubes** toward the smoke box.
> 4. **Heat exchange:** Surrounding water absorbs heat — by **conduction** through tube walls, by **convection** from hot gases, and by **radiation** in the furnace.
> 5. **Steam accumulation:** Steam bubbles rise and collect in the upper hemispherical dome (the "steam space").
> 6. **Steam delivery:** Saturated steam is drawn off through the steam stop valve to the engine/process. Exhaust gases escape through the chimney.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cochran-Boiler-Focused)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following formulas — they recur every year in Part A and Part B.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coal consumption per hour | $m_c = \dfrac{m_s \,(h_g - h_{fw})}{\text{CV} \cdot \eta_{boiler}}$ | kg/h | $m_s$ = steam output |
| 2 | Equivalent evaporation | $m_e = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{2257}$ | kg/h | Denominator = latent heat at 100 °C in kJ/kg |
| 3 | Factor of evaporation | $F.E. = \dfrac{h_g - h_{fw}}{2257}$ | dimensionless | $F.E. > 1$ when feed water is below 100 °C |
| 4 | Evaporation rate per m² of heating surface | $E_r = \dfrac{m_s}{A_{hs}}$ | kg/(m²·h) | Cochran: 25–30 kg/(m²·h) |
| 5 | Heating surface area (fire tubes) | $A_{hs} = n \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L$ | m² | $n$ = number of tubes, $d$ = OD, $L$ = length |
| 6 | Chimney draft (theoretical) | $\Delta p = H \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot g \left(1 - \dfrac{T_a}{T_g}\right)$ | Pa | $T$ in Kelvin, $H$ = chimney height |
| 7 | Grate area from heat release | $A_g = \dfrac{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}{q_r}$ | m² | $q_r$ = grate heat release rate (500–1000 kW/m²) |
| 8 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_c \cdot \text{CV}}$ | % or decimal | Direct method |
| 9 | Heat balance — heat to steam | $Q_s = m_s (h_g - h_{fw})$ | kJ/h | Useful heat |
| 10 | Heat balance — heat in fuel | $Q_f = m_c \cdot \text{CV}$ | kJ/h | Total input |

> **Note on absolute values:** The "2257" kJ/kg in the denominator represents the **latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C under atmospheric pressure**, used as a reference. It is **not** a pipe symbol — it is a numerical constant. (KTU often includes an absolute-value notation in problems; in such cases, write it as $\vert h_g - h_{fw} \vert$ if both enthalpies are to be positive magnitudes.)

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The Cochran boiler is widely used in:

- **Small industrial plants** (rice mills, paper mills, sugar factories).
- **Textile processing units** (dyeing, drying).
- **Portable applications** — temporary steam supply at construction sites.
- **Auxiliary boilers** on small ships and locomotives (historical).
- **Process steam** for food and pharmaceutical industries.

It is preferred where **floor space is limited** (vertical orientation) and where **moderate capacity** is sufficient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Sample Worked-Out Numerical — Cochran Boiler Coal Consumption

> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
> A Cochran boiler produces **2500 kg of steam per hour** at a pressure of **10 bar** with feed-water temperature of **40 °C**. The calorific value of coal is **30,000 kJ/kg** and the thermal efficiency of the boiler is **70 %**. Calculate:
> 1. Coal consumption per hour.
> 2. Equivalent evaporation.
> 3. Factor of evaporation.
> 4. Heat absorbed by the steam per hour.
> 5. Heat supplied by the fuel per hour.

**Given Data:**
- $m_s = 2500$ kg/h
- $P = 10$ bar $\Rightarrow$ from steam tables: $h_{sat,\,liquid} (h_f) = 762.81$ kJ/kg, $h_{sat,\,vapour} (h_g) = 2778.1$ kJ/kg
- Feed water temperature = 40 °C $\Rightarrow h_{fw} = c_p \cdot T = 4.18 \times 40 = 167.2$ kJ/kg
- $\text{CV} = 30{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $\eta_{th} = 0.70$

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — Coal consumption per hour $m_c$**

The thermal efficiency relates the useful heat (to steam) to the heat supplied (by fuel):

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{m_c \cdot \text{CV}}$$

Substituting all known values:

$$0.70 = \frac{2500 \times (2778.1 - 167.2)}{m_c \times 30{,}000}$$

Step 1: Compute the enthalpy difference $h_g - h_{fw}$:

$$h_g - h_{fw} = 2778.1 - 167.2 = 2610.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Step 2: Compute the numerator (heat absorbed per hour):

$$Q_s = 2500 \times 2610.9 = 6{,}527{,}250 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Step 3: Solve for $m_c$:

$$m_c = \frac{Q_s}{\text{CV} \cdot \eta_{th}} = \frac{6{,}527{,}250}{30{,}000 \times 0.70} = \frac{6{,}527{,}250}{21{,}000}$$

$$m_c = 310.82 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Answer:** Coal consumption ≈ **310.82 kg/h** (≈ 311 kg/h).

---

**Part (ii) — Equivalent evaporation $m_e$**

The equivalent evaporation is defined as the mass of water that would be evaporated from and at 100 °C using the *same* amount of heat. Latent heat at 100 °C = **2257 kJ/kg**.

$$m_e = \frac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{2257}$$

Step 1: Numerator = $6{,}527{,}250$ kJ/h (already computed).

Step 2: Divide by 2257:

$$m_e = \frac{6{,}527{,}250}{2257} = 2892.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Answer:** Equivalent evaporation ≈ **2892.2 kg/h**.

---

**Part (iii) — Factor of Evaporation (F.E.)**

$$F.E. = \frac{h_g - h_{fw}}{2257} = \frac{2610.9}{2257} = 1.157$$

> **Answer:** Factor of evaporation ≈ **1.157** (i.e., 1 kg of actual steam = 1.157 kg of "equivalent" steam from-and-at 100 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** A factor of evaporation greater than 1 means the feed water is colder than 100 °C, so each kg of steam generated carries "extra" enthalpy relative to the reference.

---

**Part (iv) — Heat absorbed by the steam per hour $Q_s$**

$$Q_s = m_s (h_g - h_{fw}) = 2500 \times 2610.9 = 6{,}527{,}250 \text{ kJ/h}$$

> **Answer:** $Q_s$ = **6.527 × 10⁶ kJ/h** ≈ **1813.1 kW** (after dividing by 3600).

---

**Part (v) — Heat supplied by the fuel per hour $Q_f$**

$$Q_f = m_c \cdot \text{CV} = 310.82 \times 30{,}000 = 9{,}324{,}600 \text{ kJ/h}$$

> **Answer:** $Q_f$ ≈ **9.325 × 10⁶ kJ/h** ≈ **2590.2 kW**.

**Cross-Check (Energy Balance):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_f} = \frac{6{,}527{,}250}{9{,}324{,}600} = 0.70 \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Heating Surface Area of Fire Tubes

> **Problem Statement:** A Cochran boiler has **120 fire tubes**, each of **100 mm outer diameter** and **2.5 m length**. Calculate the total heating surface area.

**Step 1:** Identify the formula. The heating surface of a fire tube is the *curved surface* (lateral area of a cylinder), since heat is transferred from the hot gas inside the tube to the surrounding water through this surface.

$$A_{tube} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $d = 0.100$ m and $L = 2.5$ m:

$$A_{tube} = \pi \times 0.100 \times 2.5 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the number of tubes $n = 120$:

$$A_{hs} = n \times A_{tube} = 120 \times 0.7854 = 94.25 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Answer:** Total heating surface ≈ **94.25 m²**.

**Step 4 (Optional) — Evaporation rate per m²:**

$$E_r = \frac{m_s}{A_{hs}} = \frac{2500}{94.25} = 26.53 \text{ kg/(m²·h)}$$

This lies within the typical Cochran range of **25–30 kg/(m²·h)**, confirming the design is sensible.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cochran Boiler vs. Lancashire Boiler

| Parameter | Cochran Boiler | Lancashire Boiler |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | **Vertical** | Horizontal |
| Furnace | Hemispherical dome (internal) | Two large cylindrical flues (internal) |
| Tube count | 100–150 (many) | None — direct flue heating |
| Capacity | 1500–4000 kg/h | 4000–20000 kg/h |
| Floor space | Small (vertical) | Large (horizontal) |
| Portability | Reasonably portable | Difficult to transport |
| Efficiency | 70–75 % | 65–70 % |
| Pressure | up to 15 bar | up to 12 bar |
| Maintenance access | Difficult (vertical, internal) | Easy (horizontal manholes) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constructional Architecture of Cochran Boiler

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Coal Storage and Feeding] --> B[Step Grate - Hemispherical Furnace]
    B --> C[Ash Pit - Primary Air Inlet]
    B --> D[Combustion Chamber - Hemispherical Dome]
    D --> E[Bank of Fire Tubes 100 to 150 horizontal tubes]
    E --> F[Smoke Box at Rear Top]
    F --> G[Short Chimney - Natural Draft]
    D --> H[Water Surrounding Fire Tubes - Combustion Space]
    H --> I[Upper Hemispherical Steam Dome]
    I --> J[Steam Stop Valve - To Process]
    H --> K[Feed Check Valve - Feed Water Inlet]
    H --> L[Water Level Indicator - Mountings]
    H --> M[Safety Valve - Mountings]
    H --> N[Pressure Gauge - Mountings]
    H --> O[Blow Off Cock - Mountings]
    H --> P[Manholes - Inspection and Cleaning]

    subgraph Feed Water System
        K
    end

    subgraph Boiler Mountings and Fittings
        L
        M
        N
        O
    end

    subgraph Steam Output
        J
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Working Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Coal fed on step grate] --> B[Step 2: Air inlets supply oxygen]
    B --> C[Step 3: Combustion in hemispherical furnace]
    C --> D[Step 4: Hot gases reverse and flow through fire tubes]
    D --> E[Step 5: Heat transfers to surrounding water by conduction, convection, and radiation]
    E --> F[Step 6: Steam bubbles rise to upper hemispherical dome]
    F --> G[Step 7: Steam drawn off via steam stop valve to engine]
    D --> H[Step 8: Exhaust gases collected in smoke box]
    H --> I[Step 9: Gases discharged through short chimney]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic Description (Since Mermaid Cannot Render 2D Mechanical Sections)

A vertical cross-section of a Cochran boiler reveals (from bottom to top):

1. **Bottom hemisphere** — the furnace/grate assembly, lined with refractory firebricks.
2. **Cylindrical mid-section** — packed with horizontal fire tubes, the space between tubes filled with water.
3. **Top hemisphere** — dry steam space, fitted with steam outlet nozzle.
4. **External** — lagged shell with magnesia insulation and a sheet-metal jacket; two manholes (top and side); short chimney at the back-top.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Classify steam boilers. Mention the salient features of a Cochran boiler.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Classification (1.5 marks):*
Boilers are classified on the basis of tube-water contact, axis of shell, pressure, fuel, circulation, and mobility. On the basis of tube-water contact, they are classified as **fire-tube boilers** (hot gases in tubes, water outside) and **water-tube boilers** (water in tubes, hot gases outside).

*Salient features of Cochran boiler (1.5 marks):*
- Vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, fire-tube boiler.
- Hemispherical top and bottom — strongest shape against internal pressure.
- Capacity: 1500–4000 kg/h; working pressure up to 15 bar.
- Efficiency: 70–75 %.
- Fitted with a large number of horizontal fire tubes (100–150).
- Uses a step grate; short chimney mounted directly on the smoke box.

---

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List any six mountings fitted on a Cochran boiler. State the function of any two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Six mountings (2 marks — 0.33 each, round to 6 items):*

1. Pressure gauge
2. Water level indicator (water gauge)
3. Safety valve
4. Stop valve
5. Feed check valve
6. Blow-off cock
7. Fusible plug
8. Manhole

*Function of any two (1 mark — 0.5 each):*

- **Safety valve:** Automatically opens when the boiler pressure exceeds the set limit, preventing explosion.
- **Water level indicator:** Continuously shows the water level inside the shell, ensuring the heating surfaces remain submerged.
- **Blow-off cock:** Removes accumulated mud, scale, and sediment from the bottom of the boiler.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question B (Option A).** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Cochran boiler**. List its **merits and demerits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cochran boiler generates **3000 kg of steam per hour** at a working pressure of **8 bar**. The feed-water temperature is **35 °C**. The calorific value of coal is **32,000 kJ/kg**, and the boiler efficiency is **72 %**. Find: **(i)** Coal consumption per hour, **(ii)** Equivalent evaporation, **(iii)** Factor of evaporation, **(iv)** Heat absorbed per hour. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The Cochran boiler is a vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, fire-tube boiler consisting of:

- **Shell** — A cylindrical steel shell with hemispherical (dished) top and bottom.
- **Combustion chamber (furnace)** — A hemispherical dome of firebrick-lined steel at the bottom, fitted with a **step grate** for coal burning.
- **Fire tubes** — 100–150 horizontal smoke tubes (each ≈ 100 mm OD) connecting the combustion chamber to the smoke box.
- **Smoke box and chimney** — A small smoke box at the back-top, with a short chimney directly mounted for natural draft.
- **Mountings and fittings** — Pressure gauge, water level indicator, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, manhole, and fusible plug.

**Working (3.5 marks):**
Coal is fed on the step grate. Combustion of coal occurs in the hemispherical furnace. Hot gases rise, reverse direction, and pass **horizontally through the fire tubes**, transferring heat to the surrounding water by conduction, convection, and radiation. The water gets converted to steam, which accumulates in the upper hemispherical steam dome. Steam is taken out through the steam stop valve. Exhaust gases leave through the smoke box and chimney.

**Merits (any 2):**

1. Simple in construction; easy to install and erect.
2. Occupies less floor area due to vertical orientation.
3. Hemispherical shape provides high strength, allowing higher operating pressure.
4. Quick steam generation due to direct fire-tube contact.

**Demerits (any 2):**

1. Limited steam capacity (max ≈ 4000 kg/h).
2. Inspection and cleaning are difficult because of vertical orientation and small floor area.
3. Pressure cannot be increased beyond ≈ 15 bar economically.
4. High maintenance cost due to a large number of fire tubes.

---

**Model Solution — (b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m_s = 3000$ kg/h
- $P = 8$ bar $\Rightarrow$ from steam tables: $h_g = 2769.1$ kJ/kg (at 8 bar saturated)
- $T_{fw} = 35$ °C $\Rightarrow h_{fw} = 4.18 \times 35 = 146.3$ kJ/kg
- $CV = 32{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $\eta_{th} = 0.72$

**Step 1: Enthalpy difference**

$$\Delta h = h_g - h_{fw} = 2769.1 - 146.3 = 2622.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 (i) — Coal consumption**

$$m_c = \frac{m_s \cdot \Delta h}{CV \cdot \eta_{th}} = \frac{3000 \times 2622.8}{32{,}000 \times 0.72}$$

$$m_c = \frac{7{,}868{,}400}{23{,}040} = 341.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Answer (i):** $m_c \approx$ **341.5 kg/h**

**Step 3 (ii) — Equivalent evaporation**

$$m_e = \frac{m_s \cdot \Delta h}{2257} = \frac{3000 \times 2622.8}{2257} = \frac{7{,}868{,}400}{2257} = 3486.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

> **Answer (ii):** $m_e \approx$ **3486.2 kg/h**

**Step 4 (iii) — Factor of evaporation**

$$F.E. = \frac{\Delta h}{2257} = \frac{2622.8}{2257} = 1.162$$

> **Answer (iii):** $F.E. \approx$ **1.162**

**Step 5 (iv) — Heat absorbed per hour**

$$Q_s = m_s \cdot \Delta h = 3000 \times 2622.8 = 7{,}868{,}400 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 2185.7 \text{ kW}$$

> **Answer (iv):** $Q_s \approx$ **7.868 × 10⁶ kJ/h** (or 2185.7 kW)

---

#### **Question B (Option B — Internal Choice).** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of steam boilers** in detail, with **one example** for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cochran boiler has **140 fire tubes** of **outer diameter 90 mm** and **length 2.4 m**. If the boiler generates **2800 kg of steam per hour**, calculate: **(i)** Total heating surface area, **(ii)** Rate of evaporation per m² of heating surface, and **(iii)** Factor of evaporation if the feed-water temperature is **40 °C** and steam pressure is **10 bar**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a) Classification (7 marks):**

Boilers can be classified on the following bases:

**1. According to tube-water contact (2 marks):**
- **Fire-tube boiler** — Hot gases flow inside the tubes; water surrounds them. *Example:* Cochran, Lancashire, Locomotive.
- **Water-tube boiler** — Water flows inside the tubes; hot gases flow outside. *Example:* Babcock & Wilcox, La-Mont.

**2. According to the axis of the shell (1.5 marks):**
- **Vertical** — *Example:* Cochran.
- **Horizontal** — *Example:* Lancashire, Locomotive.
- **Inclined** — *Example:* Babcock & Wilcox (inclined tubes).

**3. According to pressure (1 mark):**
- **Low-pressure** (< 20 bar) — *Example:* Cochran, Lancashire.
- **Medium-pressure** (20–40 bar) — *Example:* Locomotive.
- **High-pressure** (> 40 bar) — *Example:* Benson, Velox.

**4. According to fuel (1 mark):**
- Solid-fuel fired, liquid-fuel fired, gas-fired, and waste-heat boilers.
- *Example:* Coal-fired Lancashire, oil-fired package boiler.

**5. According to circulation (0.5 mark):**
- Natural circulation (e.g., Cochran), forced circulation (e.g., Velox).

**6. According to mobility (0.5 mark):**
- Stationary, portable, locomotive, marine.

**7. According to use of waste heat (0.5 mark):**
- Waste-heat boiler, heat-recovery steam generator (HRSG).

---

**Model Solution — (b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 140$ tubes
- $d = 90$ mm $= 0.090$ m
- $L = 2.4$ m
- $m_s = 2800$ kg/h
- $T_{fw} = 40$ °C $\Rightarrow h_{fw} = 4.18 \times 40 = 167.2$ kJ/kg
- Steam pressure $= 10$ bar $\Rightarrow h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 (i) — Total heating surface area**

$$A_{hs} = n \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L = 140 \times \pi \times 0.090 \times 2.4$$

$$A_{hs} = 140 \times 0.6786 = 95.0 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Answer (i):** $A_{hs} \approx$ **95.0 m²**

**Step 2 (ii) — Rate of evaporation**

$$E_r = \frac{m_s}{A_{hs}} = \frac{2800}{95.0} = 29.47 \text{ kg/(m²·h)}$$

> **Answer (ii):** $E_r \approx$ **29.47 kg/(m²·h)** — within the design range of 25–30 for Cochran.

**Step 3 (iii) — Factor of evaporation**

$$\Delta h = h_g - h_{fw} = 2778.1 - 167.2 = 2610.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$F.E. = \frac{\Delta h}{2257} = \frac{2610.9}{2257} = 1.157$$

> **Answer (iii):** $F.E. \approx$ **1.157**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Wrong unit for $h_f$:** Many students use $h_f$ at *100 °C* (419 kJ/kg) instead of at the **actual boiler pressure** from steam tables — this costs up to 1 mark.
> 2. **Sign of feed-water enthalpy:** Use $h_{fw} = 4.18 \times T$ (with $T$ in °C) only for liquid water below 100 °C. Above 100 °C, use steam-table values for $h_f$ at the *feed-water temperature/pressure*.
> 3. **Mixing "kg/h" with "kg/s":** All KTU numericals expect the answer in kg/h. Converting to kW is optional but should be secondary.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of evaporation** when the question asks for both equivalent evaporation AND factor of evaporation — both are independent quantities.
> 5. **Sketch missing:** A neat labelled diagram is worth 1–2 marks. In Cochran-boiler questions, ALWAYS draw the cross-section and label the hemispherical top, hemispherical bottom, fire tubes, grate, smoke box, chimney, and shell.
> 6. **Efficiency assumed as 100 %:** A common mistake. Always subtract losses using $\eta_{th}$ as given.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cochran Boiler (Module 2):**
>
> - **Type:** Vertical, multi-tubular, internally fired, **fire-tube** boiler.
> - **Inventor:** William Cochran (1899).
> - **Shape:** Cylindrical shell with **hemispherical** top and bottom — strongest shape against pressure.
> - **Capacity:** 1500 – 4000 kg/h.
> - **Pressure:** up to 15 bar (modern: up to 20 bar).
> - **Efficiency:** 70 – 75 %.
> - **Fire tubes:** 100–150 horizontal tubes, each ~100 mm OD.
> - **Furnace:** Hemispherical, step-grate fired, internally mounted.
> - **Chimney:** Short, directly on the smoke box; natural draft only.
> - **Mountings (must-know 7):** Pressure gauge, water level indicator, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug.
> - **Fittings (basic):** Manhole, mud hole, anti-priming pipe.
> - **Merits:** Compact (vertical), simple, high strength hemispherical design, quick steam raising.
> - **Demerits:** Limited capacity, difficult inspection/cleaning, pressure-limited, not suited to high pressure.
> - **Key formulas to memorize:**
>   - $m_c = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{CV \cdot \eta}$
>   - $m_e = \dfrac{m_s (h_g - h_{fw})}{2257}$
>   - $F.E. = \dfrac{h_g - h_{fw}}{2257}$
>   - $A_{hs} = n \pi d L$
>   - $E_r = \dfrac{m_s}{A_{hs}}$
>   - $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_f}$
> - **Steam-table must-know values:**
>   - At 1 bar: $h_f = 417.5$, $h_g = 2675.5$ kJ/kg
>   - At 8 bar: $h_f = 720.2$, $h_g = 2769.1$ kJ/kg
>   - At 10 bar: $h_f = 762.8$, $h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg
>   - At 15 bar: $h_f = 844.7$, $h_g = 2792.2$ kJ/kg
> - **Typical Cochran evaporation rate:** 25–30 kg/(m²·h) — used to check if numerical answers are realistic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lancashire Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lancashire Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lancashire Boiler** is a **horizontal, stationary, internally fired, fire-tube boiler** that contains **two large concentric fire tubes (flues)** running along the length of the cylindrical shell. Each flue houses a furnace at its front end and is connected to a common combustion chamber (the "breeching") at the rear, through which hot gases return beneath the shell to the chimney.

It belongs to the family of **shell-type fire-tube boilers** and is a direct descendant / improvement over the **Cornish Boiler**. It is classified under the **KTU 2024 syllabus** as a conventional stationary boiler used primarily for **low-pressure steam generation** in medium-scale industrial plants (textile mills, paper mills, chemical processing, food processing, refineries for process steam, etc.).

### Standard KTU Terminology Snapshot

| Parameter | Standard Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Boiler Class | Horizontal, Stationary, Internally Fired, Fire-Tube |
| Shell Type | Cylindrical with hemispherical end crowns |
| Number of Flues | **Two** (large diameter fire tubes) |
| Operating Pressure | **0.7 MPa to 1.7 MPa** (≈ 100 psi to 250 psi) |
| Evaporation Capacity | **2,500 kg/hr to 25,000 kg/hr** |
| Efficiency Range | **60 % to 75 %** |
| Application Sector | Process steam for industry |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the Lancashire Boiler as a "two-lane hot exhaust pipe inside a steam kettle."**

Imagine a giant horizontal pressure cooker lying on its side. Now, instead of one heating pipe (as in the Cornish boiler), you insert **two parallel large pipes (flues)** through the entire length of the cooker. At the front of each pipe, you light a coal/coke fire on a grate. The hot gases from both fires travel down these flues to the back of the cooker, where they meet in a common chamber, then loop back **underneath the boiler shell** to finally escape through the chimney.

While this is happening, the **water surrounding the flues and shell absorbs the heat** and converts to steam. A safety valve on top releases excess steam, and a steam stop valve sends the useful steam to the factory.

The "two-fire-tube" arrangement is the key innovation — it **doubles the heat transfer area** compared to a Cornish boiler for the **same shell diameter**, giving higher steam output.

---

## 1.3 Why KTU Asks This Question

The Lancashire Boiler is a **high-weightage Module 2 topic** in OEMET727. It frequently appears in:
- **Part A (3 marks):** Definition, line diagram identification, parts naming.
- **Part B (14 marks):** Full construction + working + accessories (with module choice against Cochran/Cornish/Babcock-Wilcox boiler).

> [!TIP]
> Examiners at KTU specifically test your ability to **draw and label the line diagram** and **list at least 4 mountings & 4 accessories**. Memorize the names and locations of the fittings — this single habit can fetch you **6 to 8 marks** in a 14-mark question instantly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Detailed Constructional Features

### 2.1.1 Outer Cylindrical Shell
- Fabricated from **mild steel plates** of thickness **20 mm to 40 mm**, riveted (older design) or **welded** (modern design).
- Diameter typically **2 m to 4.5 m**; length **7 m to 9 m**.
- Both ends are closed with **hemispherical (or dished) end crowns** to withstand internal pressure with minimum plate stress.

### 2.1.2 Two Internal Fire Tubes (Flues)
- **Two large-diameter flues** of **0.8 m to 1.5 m diameter** running longitudinally inside the shell.
- Flues are made of **corrugated (fluted) steel plates** to:
  1. Increase surface area for heat absorption.
  2. Provide rigidity against external collapsing pressure (water pressure outside, flue gas pressure inside).

### 2.1.3 Furnace
- Located at the **front end of each flue**.
- Furnace is lined with **firebricks (refractory)** to withstand temperatures up to **1500 °C**.
- Furnace diameter is slightly **larger** than the flue diameter to accommodate the burning coal bed.

### 2.1.4 Grate
- **Stationary or dead grate** made of cast iron bars with gaps for primary air.
- Coal is fed manually through the firehole doors at the front.

### 2.1.5 Combustion Chamber & Breeching
- At the **rear** end, both flues open into a common **combustion chamber (breeching)**.
- This chamber redirects hot gases to flow **back underneath the shell** through the space between the shell and brickwork setting.

### 2.1.6 Brickwork Setting
- The entire shell is enclosed in a **brick chamber** (called "boiler setting").
- Gases circulate in this space and finally escape to the **chimney**.
- Brickwork also provides **insulation** reducing external heat loss.

### 2.1.7 Foundation
- The boiler rests on a **massive masonry or concrete foundation** because of its heavy weight when full of water (often exceeding **30 tonnes**).

---

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Cycle:** Coal → Combustion → Flue Gas Path → Water Heating → Steam Generation → Steam Distribution.

1. **Coal is fed** through the firehole door onto the grate inside each furnace.
2. **Air is supplied** naturally by chimney draft (or forced/induced draft in larger units) through the grate openings.
3. **Combustion** occurs at the grate; hot gases rise and travel down the two fire tubes.
4. At the **rear**, gases enter the **combustion chamber (breeching)** and are deflected.
5. Gases now flow in the **reverse direction** (back-pass) underneath the shell to the front smoke box.
6. From the smoke box, gases exit through the **chimney**.
7. Meanwhile, **water surrounding the flues and shell** is heated by conduction and convection.
8. Steam accumulates in the **steam space at the top** of the shell.
9. Steam is taken out through the **steam stop valve** to the prime mover or process unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Transfer Surface** in a Lancashire boiler consists of THREE components:
> 1. Inner surface of the two **flues** (≈ 40 % of total HTS).
> 2. Outer curved surface of the **shell bottom** exposed to hot back-pass gases (≈ 50 %).
> 3. Front and rear end **crowns** (≈ 10 %).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU board-exam tested.** Memorize the variable names and standard units.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Standard Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{abs}}{h_{g} - h_{f}}$ | **Evaporation rate** (kg/hr) from heat absorbed | $kg \cdot s^{-1}$, $W$, $J/kg$ |
| 2 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s}(h_{g}-h_{f})}{\dot{m}_{f}\cdot GCV}$ | **Boiler efficiency** by direct method | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{shell} = U \cdot A_{HTS} \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat transfer through heating surface | $W$ |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{1}-\Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})}$ | **Log Mean Temperature Difference** for gas-to-water heat exchangers | K or °C |
| 5 | $A_{flue} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$ | Curved surface area of **one** flue (use ×2 for two flues) | $m^{2}$ |
| 6 | $A_{shell,bottom} = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi D \cdot L$ | Half-shell (bottom) exposed to back-pass gases | $m^{2}$ |
| 7 | $P \cdot V = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot R_{s} \cdot T$ | Steam space ideal-gas relation for steam dome design | $Pa, m^{3}, K$ |
| 8 | $F = \dfrac{A_{flues}}{A_{shell,bottom}}$ | Flue-to-shell surface ratio; typical Lancashire $\approx 0.25$–$0.35$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $q_{rate} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}\cdot GCV}{A_{HRSG}}$ | **Heat release rate** per unit heating surface | $W \cdot m^{-2}$ |
| 10 | $V_{water} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(D^{2}-2d^{2}\bigr)\cdot L$ | Water volume inside shell (subtracting two flues) | $m^{3}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** A Lancashire boiler is rated in terms of **"Evaporation per Hour"** — e.g., a *5-tonne Lancashire* means it evaporates **5,000 kg of water per hour** (often expressed as **kg/hr from and at 100 °C** i.e., F&A 100 °C rating).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Textile Industry** | Steam for cotton/wool processing, dyeing, drying. |
| **Paper & Pulp Mills** | Digester steam, drying rolls, dewatering. |
| **Sugar Refineries** | Pan boiling, juice evaporation, crystallization. |
| **Food Processing** | Sterilization, cooking, can-processing retorts. |
| **Chemical Plants** | Process heat, vaporization, indirect heating coils. |
| **Small Power Plants** | Low-pressure steam for back-pressure turbines (< 1.5 MPa). |

> [!NOTE]
> While the Lancashire boiler is **obsolete for central power generation**, it is still used in **captive industrial steam plants** because of its **ruggedness, low fuel-quality tolerance** (can burn lignite, bagasse, rice husk), and **ease of maintenance** by in-house fitters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivations of Core Boiler Performance Equations

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Evaporation Rate from Heat Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the relationship between **heat absorbed by water** and **mass of steam generated**.

Let $\dot{Q}_{abs}$ = rate of heat absorbed by water/steam inside the boiler (Watts).
Let $\dot{m}_{s}$ = steam generation rate (kg/s).
Let $h_{f}$ = enthalpy of feed water entering boiler (J/kg).
Let $h_{g}$ = enthalpy of steam leaving boiler (J/kg).

**Step 1 — Energy balance over the boiler water-steam boundary:**

By the First Law of Thermodynamics (steady-flow, no shaft work, negligible KE/PE change):

$$\dot{Q}_{abs} = \dot{m}_{s}(h_{g} - h_{f})$$

**Step 2 — Solve for steam mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{abs}}{h_{g} - h_{f}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to standard hourly rating used in industry:**

$$\dot{m}_{s,hr} = 3600 \cdot \dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot \dot{Q}_{abs}}{h_{g}-h_{f}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration (typical exam scenario):**

Given: A Lancashire boiler receives $\dot{Q}_{abs} = 1.5 \times 10^{6}$ W (≈ 1.5 MW thermal), feed water at $100\,°C$ ($h_{f} = 419$ kJ/kg), steam at $1.0$ MPa saturated ($h_{g} = 2778$ kJ/kg).

$$\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{(2778 - 419) \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{6}}{2.359 \times 10^{6}} = 0.636 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{s,hr} = 0.636 \times 3600 = 2289 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 2.3 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exam:** This single derivation is worth **3–4 marks** in any numerical sub-question on boiler capacity.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Direct-Method Boiler Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define efficiency as useful heat output divided by heat input:**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\text{Heat absorbed by water/steam}}{\text{Heat supplied by fuel}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the heat terms:**

Heat from fuel per second: $\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{f} \cdot GCV$
where $\dot{m}_{f}$ is fuel consumption rate (kg/s) and $GCV$ is gross calorific value (J/kg).

Heat absorbed per second: $\dot{Q}_{abs} = \dot{m}_{s}(h_{g}-h_{f})$

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency expression:**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s}(h_{g}-h_{f})}{\dot{m}_{f}\cdot GCV}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 3600 to convert to per-hour basis:**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s,hr}(h_{g}-h_{f})}{\dot{m}_{f,hr}\cdot GCV}$$

**Step 5 — Work out a numerical example:**

Given: Fuel = coal, $\dot{m}_{f,hr} = 350$ kg/hr, $GCV = 25000$ kJ/kg, $\dot{m}_{s,hr} = 2300$ kg/hr, $h_{g}-h_{f} = 2100$ kJ/kg.

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{2300 \times 2100}{350 \times 25000} = \dfrac{4.83 \times 10^{6}}{8.75 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = 0.552 = 55.2\%$$

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation: Heating Surface Area Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks you to compute the **total heating surface (HTS)** of a Lancashire boiler.

For a Lancashire boiler with two flues:

**Step 1 — Curved area of one flue:**

$$A_{flue,1} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Area of both flues:**

$$A_{flues} = 2 \pi \cdot d \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Bottom-half shell area exposed to back-pass gases:**

The bottom half of the cylindrical shell (excluding the two flues) is exposed to the hot gases that return under the shell. The net bottom shell area is:

$$A_{shell,bottom} = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi D \cdot L - 2 \cdot d \cdot L$$

where $D$ = shell outer diameter, $d$ = flue outer diameter, $L$ = shell length.

**Step 4 — End crown areas (approximate as flat circles of shell diameter):**

$$A_{ends} = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total Heating Surface:**

$$A_{HTS} = 2\pi d L + \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{2} - 2d\right)L + \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:**

Given $D = 2.5$ m, $d = 1.0$ m, $L = 7.5$ m:

$$A_{flues} = 2 \pi (1.0)(7.5) = 47.12 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{shell,bottom} = \left(\dfrac{\pi(2.5)}{2} - 2(1.0)\right)(7.5) = (3.927 - 2.0)(7.5) = 14.45 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{ends} = \dfrac{\pi (2.5)^2}{2} = 9.82 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{HTS} = 47.12 + 14.45 + 9.82 = 71.39 \text{ m}^2$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table (Lancashire vs Cornish)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners almost always pair Lancashire boiler with the **Cornish boiler** as a comparison question. This table alone, fully memorized, can fetch 7 marks.

| Parameter | Lancashire Boiler | Cornish Boiler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of internal flues | **Two** | **One** |
| Heating surface (same shell) | ~ **1.7×** higher | Lower |
| Steam output | Higher | Lower |
| Heat transfer rate | Faster | Slower |
| Water content | Slightly higher | Lower |
| Space required | Slightly more | Less |
| Combustion control | **Independent** per flue | Single furnace only |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| KTU "preferred modern choice" | **Yes** (for medium capacity) | No (legacy) |

---

## 3.3 Mountings & Accessories Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **Mountings are fitted ON the boiler.** **Accessories are fitted ALONG the pipework / steam line.** Examiners will cut marks if you confuse the two.

### 3.3.1 Essential Mountings (7 standard ones — memorize for KTU)

| # | Mounting | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Safety Valve** | Releases excess steam if pressure > set limit (e.g., spring-loaded / lever type). |
| 2 | **Pressure Gauge** | Indicates working pressure inside the shell. |
| 3 | **Water Level Indicator** | Shows water level inside the boiler (two at front, one at back). |
| 4 | **Steam Stop Valve** | Controls steam flow from boiler to main steam pipe. |
| 5 | **Feed Check Valve** | Allows feed water into boiler, prevents backflow. |
| 6 | **Fusible Plug** | Melts at unsafe low water level → discharges water onto grate, preventing fire. |
| 7 | **Blow-off Cock / Valve** | Removes mud, scale, sediment from boiler bottom. |

### 3.3.2 Common Accessories

| # | Accessory | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Economizer** | Preheats feed water using flue gas waste heat. |
| 2 | **Superheater** | Raises steam temperature above saturation. |
| 3 | **Air Preheater** | Preheats combustion air using flue gases. |
| 4 | **Feed Pump** | Forces feed water into boiler against pressure. |
| 5 | **Steam Separator / Dryer** | Removes moisture from steam before turbine. |
| 6 | **Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV)** | Steps down steam pressure for low-pressure processes. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Lancashire Boiler)

Because the Lancashire boiler is a physical assembly that is best understood via a labeled line diagram, the following Mermaid diagram provides a **functional block-level architecture** showing gas flow path, water/steam path, and the control fittings at each station. This maps exactly to the standard KTU textbook line drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Storage Hopper] --> B[Furnace 1 — Front Left Flue]
    C[Primary Air Inlet] --> B
    D[Coal Storage Hopper 2] --> E[Furnace 2 — Front Right Flue]
    F[Primary Air Inlet 2] --> E

    B --> G[Fire Tube Flue 1 — Travels Rearward]
    E --> H[Fire Tube Flue 2 — Travels Rearward]

    G --> I[Common Combustion Chamber — Breeching at Rear]
    H --> I

    I --> J[Back Pass Hot Gas Plenum Beneath Shell]
    J --> K[Front Smoke Box]
    K --> L[Chimney Stack to Atmosphere]

    subgraph BOILER_SHELL [Boiler Shell with Feed Water Surrounding Flues]
        M[Feed Check Valve + Feed Pump Inlet]
        M --> N[Water Surrounds Flues and Shell Bottom]
        N --> O[Steam Space at Top of Shell]
        O --> P[Steam Stop Valve Outlet to Process]
    end

    Q[Safety Valve on Shell Top] -. pressure relief .-> O
    R[Pressure Gauge] -. reads .-> O
    S[Water Level Indicator] -. reads .-> N
    T[Fusible Plug at Crown] -. melts on low water .-> N
    U[Blow-off Cock at Bottom] -. removes sludge .-> N
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Boiler Operational States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ColdStart : Fuel loaded, water filled
    ColdStart --> Ignition : Furnace lit
    Ignition --> Heating : Water temperature rises
    Heating --> SteamGeneration : Saturation reached
    SteamGeneration --> NormalOperation : Pressure at working setpoint
    NormalOperation --> NormalOperation : Continuous firing and feed
    NormalOperation --> OverPressureTrip : Pressure exceeds safety setpoint
    OverPressureTrip --> NormalOperation : Safety valve releases, pressure falls
    NormalOperation --> LowWaterTrip : Water level below threshold
    LowWaterTrip --> EmergencyShutdown : Fusible plug melts
    EmergencyShutdown --> Maintenance : Boiler isolated and cooled
    Maintenance --> ColdStart : Restart sequence
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Description (for your hand-drawn diagram in the KTU exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When the KTU question says "Draw a neat line diagram of Lancashire boiler," include these features and labels:**

**Front view features:**
- Two firehole doors on the front face (left and right), each giving access to one furnace.
- Two furnaces meeting two flues.
- Front-mounted **water level indicator**, **pressure gauge**, **safety valve**, **steam stop valve**.
- Front-mounted **smoke box** outlet to chimney.

**Side view features:**
- Cylindrical shell with hemispherical end crowns.
- Two large internal flues drawn with dashed (hidden) lines since they are inside.
- Common breeching at the rear.
- Brickwork setting shown as a hatched chamber around the shell.
- Foundation strip shown below.

**Mandatory labels (each carries ½ mark in the exam):**

1. Shell
2. Fire tube / Flue (×2)
3. Furnace (×2)
4. Grate (×2)
5. Firehole door (×2)
6. Combustion chamber / Breeching
7. Chimney
8. Safety valve
9. Pressure gauge
10. Water level indicator
11. Steam stop valve
12. Feed check valve
13. Blow-off cock
14. Fusible plug
15. Brickwork setting
16. Foundation

> [!TIP]
> **14-marks question checklist** for a full Lancashire boiler answer:
> 1. Labelled diagram — **4 marks**.
> 2. Construction description — **4 marks**.
> 3. Working explanation — **4 marks**.
> 4. Mountings & accessories — **2 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is a Lancashire boiler? State any two of its features.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Lancashire boiler is a **horizontal, stationary, internally fired fire-tube boiler** with **two large internal flues (fire tubes)** running the length of the cylindrical shell.
>
> **Features:**
> 1. It has **two furnaces**, one at the front of each flue, allowing independent firing and combustion control.
> 2. It operates at pressures up to **1.7 MPa** and can evaporate **2,500 to 25,000 kg/hr** of water.
> 3. The hot gases make a **three-pass** journey: forward through flues, return through the back-pass underneath the shell, then to the chimney.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for features = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **mountings** and **accessories** of a boiler, giving two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mountings | Accessories |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Location** | Fitted **on the boiler body** | Fitted **along the steam line / external to boiler** |
| **Purpose** | Ensure **safe operation** of the boiler itself | Improve **efficiency** of the steam system |
| **Example 1** | Safety valve | Economizer |
| **Example 2** | Water level indicator | Superheater |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for the table = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 contains a choice pair. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — students answer **either**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Lancashire boiler**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** List the **essential mountings and accessories** of a Lancashire boiler and explain the function of any **four** of them in detail. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### (a) Construction and Working — 7 Marks

**Diagram:** *Draw neat line diagram as per Section 4.3 above with at least 12 labels.* — **2 marks**

**Construction (3 marks):**

- **Shell:** Cylindrical mild-steel shell of diameter 2–4.5 m and length 7–9 m, closed by hemispherical crowns. Plate thickness 20–40 mm.
- **Two fire tubes (flues):** Large flues of 0.8–1.5 m diameter, made of **corrugated steel** to gain area and rigidity. Each flue houses a furnace at its front end.
- **Grate & furnace:** Stationary cast-iron grate at the front of each flue; refractory firebrick lining withstands 1500 °C.
- **Combustion chamber (breeching):** Common chamber at the rear where the two flues merge; redirects gases to the back-pass.
- **Brickwork setting:** Insulating brick chamber enclosing the shell; hot gases flow under the shell in the gap.
- **Foundation:** Heavy masonry/concrete to support boiler weight.

**Working (2 marks):**

Coal fed onto the two grates is burnt in the two furnaces using primary air (draft). Hot combustion gases travel down the **two flues** toward the rear, enter the common **breeching**, are deflected to flow **backward underneath the shell** in the back-pass, enter the front **smoke box**, and finally escape through the **chimney**.

Meanwhile, the **water surrounding the flues and bottom shell** absorbs heat by conduction and convection, reaches saturation, and generates steam. The steam collects at the **steam space** at the top of the shell and exits through the **steam stop valve** to the plant. Feed water enters via the **feed check valve**.

---

##### (b) Mountings and Accessories — 7 Marks

**List (2 marks):** *(Present as a 2-column table with names only.)*

| Mountings | Accessories |
| :--- | :--- |
| Safety valve | Economizer |
| Pressure gauge | Superheater |
| Water level indicator | Air preheater |
| Steam stop valve | Feed pump |
| Feed check valve | Steam separator |
| Fusible plug | Pressure reducing valve |
| Blow-off cock | Condensate trap |

**Detailed Function of Any Four (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Safety Valve:** Spring-loaded (or lever-type) valve fitted on the boiler top. It opens automatically when the internal steam pressure exceeds the set safe limit (typically 3 % above working pressure) and closes again once pressure drops. *Prevents explosion.*

2. **Water Level Indicator (Gauge Glass):** Transparent tube at the front (and rear) of the boiler showing live water level. Two such glasses are fitted (one as standby). Operator adjusts feed-water pump rate to keep level between safe marks.

3. **Fusible Plug:** A bismuth-based low-melting-point alloy plug fitted at the crown of the firebox. If water level falls dangerously low and the plug is exposed to flame, the alloy melts (≈ 250 °C) and discharges water onto the fire — **prevents furnace overheating and crown collapse**.

4. **Economizer (Accessory):** A bundle of cast-iron or steel tubes installed in the chimney flue path. Feed water passing through these tubes is preheated by the waste flue gases before entering the boiler. **Boiler efficiency rises by 5–12 %** due to reduced flue gas exit temperature.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare the **Lancashire boiler with the Cornish boiler** under the headings: number of flues, steam output, heating surface, and application suitability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Lancashire boiler has a shell diameter of **3.0 m**, length **8 m**, and each flue diameter **1.2 m**. Calculate: *(i)* the heating surface area of the two flues, *(ii)* the bottom-shell area exposed to back-pass gases, and *(iii)* the total heating surface. Assume end crowns are flat. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### (a) Comparison Table — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Lancashire Boiler | Cornish Boiler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of flues | **Two** | **One** |
| Heating surface | Larger (≈ 1.7× Cornish for same shell size) | Smaller |
| Steam output | Higher (typical 5–15 tonnes/hr) | Lower (1–5 tonnes/hr) |
| Combustion control | **Independent** per flue — better turn-down ratio | Single furnace only |
| Application suitability | Medium-capacity industries, textile/paper | Small workshops, legacy use |
| Construction cost | Higher | Lower |
| Maintenance | Slightly more complex | Simpler |

*(4 comparison points × 1.5 marks + 1 mark for presentation = 7 marks)*

---

##### (b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D = 3.0$ m, $L = 8$ m, $d = 1.2$ m, two flues, flat end crowns.

**(i) Heating surface area of the two flues:**

$$A_{flues} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot 1.2 \cdot 8 = 60.32 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{— 3 marks}$$

**(ii) Bottom-shell area exposed to back-pass gases:**

The bottom half-shell gross area is $\frac{1}{2}\pi D L$. Subtract the area covered by the two flues (they occupy a width of $2d$ along the bottom):

$$A_{shell,bottom} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\pi D - 2d\right) L$$

$$A_{shell,bottom} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\pi (3.0) - 2(1.2)\right)(8) = (4.712 - 2.4)(8)$$

$$A_{shell,bottom} = (2.312)(8) = 18.50 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{— 2 marks}$$

**(iii) Total heating surface (including flat end crowns):**

$$A_{ends} = 2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2} = \dfrac{\pi (3.0)^{2}}{2} = 14.14 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{— 1 mark}$$

$$A_{HTS} = A_{flues} + A_{shell,bottom} + A_{ends}$$

$$A_{HTS} = 60.32 + 18.50 + 14.14 = 92.96 \text{ m}^2 \quad \textbf{— 1 mark}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls (Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. **Forgetting the ×2 multiplier** for two flues — results in half the area and full marks lost.
> 2. **Using full $\pi D L$** for bottom shell area instead of **half** — major error because only the bottom half is exposed to back-pass gases.
> 3. **Forgetting end crowns** — examiners at KTU typically deduct 1 mark per missing HTS component.
> 4. **Mixing up units** (cm vs m) — always convert all dimensions to metres before substituting.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in a Lancashire Boiler 14-mark question:**
>
> 1. **Unlabelled / poorly labelled diagram (–3 to –4 marks).** This is the single biggest mark-loss. KTU examiners insist on **at least 10 correct labels** on the line diagram. Practice drawing the diagram from memory every week.
>
> 2. **Confusing mountings with accessories (–2 marks).** Memorize the rule: *Mountings are ON the boiler; accessories are EXTERNAL.*
>
> 3. **Not mentioning the three-pass gas flow (–2 marks).** Always state explicitly: *forward through flues → backward under shell → upward through chimney.*
>
> 4. **Numerical errors due to forgetting the ×2 factor** for two flues (–2 to –3 marks).
>
> 5. **Writing "boiler efficiency = 90 %"** for a Lancashire boiler. The realistic range is **60–75 %**; writing >85 % is treated as a misconception.
>
> 6. **Omitting the brickwork setting** from the diagram — examiners at KTU specifically look for it because it explains the back-pass gas heating.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your last-night revision checklist.**

- Lancashire boiler = **horizontal, stationary, internally fired, fire-tube** boiler with **two flues**.
- Operating pressure: **0.7 – 1.7 MPa**; capacity: **2.5 – 25 tonnes/hr**.
- **Two flues** is the defining feature — it gives 1.7× more heating surface than a Cornish boiler.
- Flues are **corrugated (fluted)** for rigidity and extra area.
- Gas path is a **three-pass** route: forward in flues → backward under shell → upward to chimney.
- Steam accumulates in the **top steam space**; water surrounds flues + shell.
- **7 essential mountings:** Safety valve, Pressure gauge, Water level indicator, Steam stop valve, Feed check valve, Fusible plug, Blow-off cock.
- **Common accessories:** Economizer, Superheater, Air preheater, Feed pump, Steam separator, PRV.
- Efficiency range realistic: **60–75 %**. Do not quote >85 %.
- Furnaces lined with **refractory firebricks** to handle ≈ 1500 °C.
- Heavy **brickwork setting** around shell directs back-pass flow and insulates.
- **HTS formula (KTU favourite):**

$$A_{HTS} = 2\pi d L + \left(\frac{\pi D}{2} - 2d\right)L + \frac{\pi D^{2}}{2}$$

- **Boiler efficiency formula (direct method):**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s}(h_{g}-h_{f})}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot GCV} \times 100\,\%$$

- Applications: **textile, paper, sugar, food, chemical** industries — captive steam plants.
- Lancashire is **obsolete for central power generation** but **alive in industry** for medium-scale low-pressure process steam.
- The **furnace diameter is larger than the flue diameter** — this is intentional, to accommodate the burning coal bed.
- The boiler rests on a **massive masonry foundation** because of its full weight (often 30+ tonnes).
- Always remember to **draw and label** the diagram — it is worth **at least 4 marks** out of 14.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second mnemonic — "LH-FW-2F"**
> **L**ancashire → **H**orizontal → **F**ire-tube → **W**orks at 0.7–1.7 MPa → **2F** = **Two Flues** & **Three-pass flow**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cornish Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cornish Boiler # MODULE 2 — CORNISH BOILER ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-Aligned):** > *A Cornish Boiler is a horizontal, stationary, fire-tube boiler having a **single internal fire tube (flue)** that runs through a cylindrical shell filled with water. It contains an **internal furnace (firebox)** with a hemispherical crown at the top, and hot gases traverse the shell twice (once through the flue, then back around it) before exhausting to the chimney.* ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a large, horizontal, pressurized cylindrical water tank. Inside this tank, we weld **a single big pipe** that acts as a chimney. At one end of this pipe, we light a coal fire on a grate. The flames and hot gases shoot straight through the single big pipe, heating the surrounding water. After reaching the far end, the gases cannot exit directly — they are forced to **turn around** and travel back through the **narrow gap between the single pipe and the outer cylindrical shell**, giving a *second pass* of heat transfer before finally escaping up the chimney. In short: **One fire-tube, two passes of hot gas, and a built-in furnace inside the water shell.** ### 1.2 Origin & Heritage | Parameter | Detail | |---|---| | **Origin Region** | Cornwall, England (hence the name) | | **Inspired By** | Trevithick's high-pressure boiler experiments (early 1800s) | | **Primary Fuel (Original)** | **Cornish Coal** — a high-grade, low-ash coal | | **Operating Class** | Small-to-medium scale steam generation | ### 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Per KTU Board Standard) - **Working Pressure:** up to **10 bar (≈ 1 MPa)** - **Steam Generation Capacity:** up to **6500 kg/hr** - **Thermal Efficiency:** typically **60 % – 70 %** - **Shell Diameter Range:** **1.2 m to 1.8 m** - **Shell Length Range:** **4 m to 8 m** - **Crown Shape:** **Hemispherical** (top and bottom of firebox) > [!NOTE] > **Why is the crown hemispherical?** > A hemispherical shape distributes internal steam pressure uniformly and has the highest pressure-bearing capacity per unit material — making it the safest shape for a firebox crown. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — Step-by-Step Logic The Cornish Boiler is built from the following structured components: - **Cylindrical Outer Shell (a):** The main pressure vessel. Houses water and steam. Made of riveted (older designs) or fusion-welded (modern) steel plates. - **Hemispherical Crowns (b):** Form the top and bottom of the **firebox** at the front end. The hemispherical shape resists pressure-induced stresses evenly. - **Firebox / Internal Furnace (c):** Built into the front end of the shell using firebricks (refractory lining). This is where coal combustion occurs. - **Grate (d):** A cast-iron bar arrangement at the bottom of the firebox. Supports the fuel and allows primary air ingress from below. - **Single Fire Tube / Flue (e):** A **large-diameter** cylindrical tube extending from the firebox to the back-end (smoke box). It carries the hot gases through the water-filled region. - **Smoke Box (f):** The rear-end enclosed chamber where flue gases turn around to begin their return journey. - **Chimney Uptake (g):** The rear vertical stack through which exhaust gases finally leave the boiler. - **Boiler Mountings (h):** Pressure gauge, water-level indicator (water gauge glass), safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, and fusible plug. - **Combustion Chamber Bridge (i):** A firebrick barrier between grate and fire tube, improving combustion completeness. - **External Insulation (j):** Lagging + cladding to minimize convective heat loss. ### 2.2 Working Principle — The "Why" & "How" The hot gas path follows a **two-pass** scheme: 1. **Coal** is fed onto the grate inside the firebox. 2. **Hot combustion gases** travel from the firebox → through the **single fire tube** → to the rear smoke box. 3. Gases **reverse direction (U-turn)** at the smoke box. 4. Gases flow **back** through the annular space between the fire tube and the outer shell. 5. Gases finally exit via the **chimney** at the front (above the firebox). > [!NOTE] > **Two-Pass Heating Advantage:** The gases surrender heat twice — first inside the fire tube, then again during the return pass. This raises thermal efficiency compared to a *single-pass* boiler. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice - **Single Tube (not multiple):** Simpler construction, easier to clean and inspect — but limits heating surface and hence capacity. - **Internal Furnace:** Brings the highest-temperature zone (the fire) in direct contact with the boiler shell, eliminating the need for separate brick settings around the entire boiler. - **Hemispherical Crown:** Distributes stress evenly, allowing higher working pressures without crown-sheet failure. - **Stationary (non-mobile) Mounting:** Designed for permanent installation, with massive brickwork setting on a foundation. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Critical Equations | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Equivalent Evaporation** | $m_s = \dfrac{m_a \left( H - h_f \right)}{2257}$ | kg/hr, where $H$ = enthalpy of steam, $h_f$ = feed-water enthalpy | | **Boiler Efficiency (Heat Basis)** | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s \left( H - h_f \right)}{m_f \times GCV}$ | Fraction (× 100 for %) | | **Heat Transfer through Tube Wall** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W, where $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient | | **Hemispherical Crown Stress (Hoop)** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | N/m², *half* that of a flat crown | | **Cylindrical Shell Hoop Stress** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | N/m², longitudinal seams require higher strength | | **Factor of Evaporation** | $F.E. = \dfrac{H - h_f}{2257}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) | | **Coal Consumption Rate** | $m_f = \dfrac{m_s \left( H - h_f \right)}{\eta \cdot GCV}$ | kg/hr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall Reminder:** Use **\vert** (LaTeX) for absolute-value bars inside any markdown table. Never type `|` directly inside a cell — it breaks table rendering. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Cornish Boiler - **Legacy Industrial Use:** Historically dominant in textile mills, paper mills, and small processing plants in 19th–early 20th century Britain. - **Modern Relevance:** Largely *superseded* by Lancashire and water-tube boilers, but the design *pedagogically* remains the foundation for understanding fire-tube boiler thermodynamics. - **Examination Significance:** A high-frequency **Module 2 topic** in KTU ESE — typical Part B 14-mark questions ask for *sketches, working, comparison with Lancashire, and efficiency calculation*. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Boiler Thermal Efficiency from First Principles **Statement:** The thermal efficiency of a Cornish boiler is the ratio of useful heat absorbed by the working fluid (steam) to the heat supplied by fuel combustion. **Step 1 — Define Heat Output (Useful):** The heat absorbed per hour by water to convert it to steam is: $$ Q_{out} = m_s \left( H_{steam} - h_{feedwater} \right) $$ where: - $m_s$ = mass of steam generated per hour (kg/hr) - $H_{steam}$ = specific enthalpy of steam leaving the boiler (kJ/kg) - $h_{feedwater}$ = specific enthalpy of feed-water entering (kJ/kg) **Step 2 — Define Heat Input (Fuel Energy):** The chemical energy released by the fuel per hour: $$ Q_{in} = m_f \times GCV $$ where: - $m_f$ = mass of fuel burned per hour (kg/hr) - $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel (kJ/kg) **Step 3 — Write Efficiency Definition:** $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3:** $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s \left( H - h_f \right)}{m_f \times GCV} $$ **Step 5 — Introduce "Equivalent Evaporation" Concept:** Reference steam is **dry saturated at 100 °C**, with latent heat = **2257 kJ/kg**. Thus: $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{equiv} \times 2257}{m_f \times GCV} $$ where $m_{equiv} = m_s \times F.E.$ and $F.E.$ = Factor of Evaporation. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU ESE Style) > **Problem [KTU University Exam — Sample]:** > A Cornish boiler generates **3000 kg/hr** of dry saturated steam at **10 bar** (enthalpy $H = 2778$ kJ/kg). Feed-water enters at **40 °C** ($h_f = 167.5$ kJ/kg). Coal consumption is **350 kg/hr** with a GCV of **30000 kJ/kg**. Determine: (a) Thermal efficiency, (b) Factor of Evaporation, (c) Equivalent evaporation. #### Part (a) — Thermal Efficiency $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s \left( H - h_f \right)}{m_f \times GCV} $$ Substitute values: $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{3000 \times \left( 2778 - 167.5 \right)}{350 \times 30000} $$ Compute the numerator first: $$ 3000 \times 2610.5 = 7\,831\,500 \; \text{kJ/hr} $$ Compute the denominator: $$ 350 \times 30000 = 10\,500\,000 \; \text{kJ/hr} $$ Now compute the ratio: $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{7\,831\,500}{10\,500\,000} = 0.7458 $$ Express as percentage: $$ \eta_{boiler} \approx 74.6 \,\% $$ #### Part (b) — Factor of Evaporation $$ F.E. = \dfrac{H - h_f}{2257} $$ $$ F.E. = \dfrac{2778 - 167.5}{2257} = \dfrac{2610.5}{2257} \approx 1.157 $$ #### Part (c) — Equivalent Evaporation $$ m_{equiv} = m_s \times F.E. $$ $$ m_{equiv} = 3000 \times 1.157 \approx 3470 \; \text{kg/hr} $$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cornish vs. Lancashire Boiler | Feature | **Cornish Boiler** | **Lancashire Boiler** | |---|---|---| | Number of Internal Flues | **One (single)** | **Two (twin)** | | Heating Surface Area | Lower | Higher (≈ 70 % more) | | Steam Output Capacity | Up to **6500 kg/hr** | Up to **30000 kg/hr** | | Thermal Efficiency | **60 % – 70 %** | **70 % – 80 %** | | Construction Complexity | Simpler | More complex (two flues) | | Fuel Cost Per kg Steam | Higher (lower efficiency) | Lower | | Capital Cost | Lower | Higher | | Modern Adoption | Largely obsolete | Still used (legacy) | | Suitability | Small industrial loads | Medium-scale industrial loads | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** "Compare Cornish and Lancashire boiler" is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark questions in Power Plant Engineering (Module 2). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hot Gas Path ```mermaid flowchart LR A["COAL FEED
Grate Fuel"] --> B["FIREBOX
Internal Furnace
Hemispherical Crown"] B --> C["SINGLE FIRE TUBE
Flue Passage 1
Through Water"] C --> D["SMOKE BOX
Rear Turnaround"] D --> E["RETURN PASS
Annular Space
Between Tube and Shell"] E --> F["CHIMNEY
Exhaust Uptake"] F --> G["ATMOSPHERE
Flue Gas Discharge"] H["WATER INLET
Feed Check Valve"] --> I["BOILER SHELL
Cylindrical Pressure Vessel"] I --> J["STEAM OUTLET
Stop Valve and Steam Main"] I -.heats via.-> C I -.heats via.-> E style A fill:#fde68a style B fill:#fca5a5 style C fill:#fca5a5 style D fill:#fdba74 style E fill:#fdba74 style F fill:#fcd34d style G fill:#e5e7eb style H fill:#bae6fd style I fill:#bfdbfe style J fill:#bbf7d0 ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Component Architecture — Cross-Sectional Block View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FrontEnd["FRONT END (Firebox Region)"] F1["Hemispherical Top Crown"] F2["Firebox with Refractory Lining"] F3["Cast Iron Grate"] F4["Primary Air Duct Below Grate"] F5["Coal Feed Door"] F6["Mountings: Pressure Gauge, Water Glass, Safety Valve"] end subgraph MidSection["MIDDLE SECTION (Heat Transfer Zone)"] M1["Single Large Diameter Flue Tube"] M2["Surrounding Water in Shell"] M3["Steam Space Above Water Level"] end subgraph RearEnd["REAR END (Smoke Box)"] R1["Smoke Box Chamber"] R2["U-Turn Baffle Plates"] R3["Chimney Uptake"] R4["External Brick Setting"] end F1 --- F2 F2 --- F3 F3 --- F4 F3 --- M1 M1 --- R1 R1 --- R2 R2 --- R3 style F1 fill:#fcd34d style F2 fill:#fca5a5 style M1 fill:#fdba74 style M2 fill:#bae6fd style M3 fill:#bbf7d0 style R1 fill:#fed7aa style R3 fill:#e5e7eb ``` ### 4.3 Mountings & Accessories — Functional Block Map ```mermaid flowchart LR MB["BOILER SHELL"] --> MG1["Water Level Indicator"] MB --> MG2["Pressure Gauge"] MB --> MG3["Safety Valve"] MB --> MG4["Stop Valve"] MB --> MG5["Feed Check Valve"] MB --> MG6["Blow Off Cock"] MB --> MG7["Fusible Plug"] MG3 -.releases excess pressure.-> AM["Atmosphere"] MG5 -.admits feed water.-> MB MG6 -.drains sludge.-> DR["Drain"] style MB fill:#bfdbfe style MG1 fill:#bbf7d0 style MG2 fill:#bbf7d0 style MG3 fill:#fca5a5 style MG4 fill:#bbf7d0 style MG5 fill:#bbf7d0 style MG6 fill:#bbf7d0 style MG7 fill:#fde68a ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any six essential boiler mountings fitted on a Cornish boiler and state the function of each in one line.* > **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** > 1. **Pressure Gauge** — Indicates internal steam pressure. > 2. **Water Level Indicator (Glass Gauge)** — Shows water level inside the shell. > 3. **Safety Valve** — Automatically releases steam when pressure exceeds the set limit. > 4. **Stop Valve** — Isolates the boiler from the steam main. > 5. **Feed Check Valve** — Allows feed-water entry and prevents backflow of steam. > 6. **Blow-Off Cock** — Removes sludge and sediment during cleaning. > 7. *(Optional)* **Fusible Plug** — Melts at unsafe low water level, extinguishing fire. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Why is a Cornish boiler called a "two-pass" boiler even though it has only a single fire tube?* > **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** > Although there is only one fire tube, the hot gases traverse the heating surface **twice**: > - **1st pass:** Through the single flue tube from firebox to smoke box. > - **2nd pass:** Returning from smoke box to the front via the annular space between the flue and the outer shell. > This **U-turn arrangement** qualifies it as a two-pass boiler, increasing heat transfer and efficiency. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice) --- #### ❖ QUESTION A — OPTION I (14 Marks) > **[KTU ESE — Dec 2022 Model Paper]** > **Q. (a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **Cornish boiler**. List its advantages, disadvantages, and specify the conditions under which it is preferred. > *(7 marks)* > > **Q. (b)** A Cornish boiler produces **4500 kg/hr** of dry saturated steam at **8 bar** ($H = 2769$ kJ/kg). Feed-water temperature is **35 °C** ($h_f = 146.7$ kJ/kg). Coal is burned at **450 kg/hr** with GCV **31000 kJ/kg**. Calculate: > (i) Factor of Evaporation > (ii) Equivalent Evaporation > (iii) Thermal Efficiency > *(7 marks)* --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Construction (3 marks):** - **Cylindrical Shell:** Horizontal pressure vessel of riveted or welded steel plates; contains water and steam. - **Firebox:** Internal furnace at the front with **hemispherical top and bottom crowns**; lined with refractory firebricks. - **Grate:** Cast-iron bar arrangement supporting the coal. - **Single Fire Tube (Flue):** Large-diameter tube carrying combustion gases from firebox to rear smoke box. - **Smoke Box + Chimney:** Rear chamber where gases reverse and exit upward. - **Mountings:** Pressure gauge, water glass, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug. - **Setting:** Mounted on a substantial brickwork foundation. **Working (2 marks):** 1. Coal burns on the grate, producing hot flue gases. 2. Gases travel through the **single flue** (1st pass) heating surrounding water. 3. At the rear smoke box, gases **reverse direction**. 4. Gases flow back through the **annular space** (2nd pass), giving additional heating. 5. Exhaust gases leave via the chimney. Steam rises, accumulates at top, and exits via stop valve. **Advantages (1 mark):** - Simple and compact construction. - Higher efficiency than a simple vertical boiler. - Easy inspection and cleaning. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - Limited steam output. - Low heating surface area compared to Lancashire. - Cannot meet high-load industrial demand. **Preferred Conditions:** Small industrial plants requiring moderate steam at moderate pressure. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **(i) Factor of Evaporation:** $$ F.E. = \dfrac{H - h_f}{2257} $$ Substitute values: $$ F.E. = \dfrac{2769 - 146.7}{2257} = \dfrac{2622.3}{2257} $$ $$ \boxed{F.E. \approx 1.162} $$ **(ii) Equivalent Evaporation:** $$ m_{equiv} = m_s \times F.E. $$ $$ m_{equiv} = 4500 \times 1.162 $$ $$ \boxed{m_{equiv} \approx 5230 \; \text{kg/hr}} $$ **(iii) Thermal Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s \left( H - h_f \right)}{m_f \times GCV} $$ Numerator: $$ 4500 \times 2622.3 = 11\,800\,350 \; \text{kJ/hr} $$ Denominator: $$ 450 \times 31000 = 13\,950\,000 \; \text{kJ/hr} $$ $$ \eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{11\,800\,350}{13\,950\,000} = 0.8459 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{boiler} \approx 84.6 \,\%} $$ --- #### ❖ QUESTION B — OPTION II (14 Marks) > **[KTU ESE — June 2023 Model Paper]** > **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **constructional features** of a Cornish boiler. Highlight the role of the **hemispherical crown** and the **single fire tube** in determining its pressure-bearing capacity and heating efficiency. > *(7 marks)* > > **Q. (b)** Compare a **Cornish boiler** with a **Lancashire boiler** across any **eight technical parameters**, and state which is preferred for modern medium-scale industries with reasons. > *(7 marks)* --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Constructional Features (5 marks):** - **Cylindrical Shell:** Houses water + steam space. Built from rolled steel plates joined by riveting or welding. Length 4–8 m, diameter 1.2–1.8 m. - **Hemispherical Crowns (Top & Bottom of Firebox):** Distribute internal pressure spherically; resist rupture better than flat crowns. - **Firebox / Internal Furnace:** Refractory-lined chamber at the front; coal combustion zone. - **Grate:** Allows coal to rest and air to enter from below. - **Single Fire Tube:** Single large-diameter flue passing through the entire shell, surrounded by water. - **Smoke Box + Uptake Chimney:** Rear turnaround chamber; vertical stack for exhaust. - **Mountings and Accessories:** Pressure gauge, water glass, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug, manhole, mud hole. **Role of Hemispherical Crown (1 mark):** A hemispherical crown distributes internal pressure **uniformly across its curved surface**, halving the hoop stress compared to a flat plate. This allows the boiler to safely operate at pressures up to **10 bar** without crown-sheet failure. **Role of Single Fire Tube (1 mark):** The single fire tube serves as the **primary heat transfer surface**. Hot gases flowing through it transfer heat to the surrounding water. Combined with the return pass through the annular gap, it provides a **two-pass heating arrangement**, raising overall efficiency. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Eight-Parameter Comparison Table:** | # | Parameter | Cornish Boiler | Lancashire Boiler | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of Flues | **One (single)** | **Two (twin)** | | 2 | Heating Surface Area | Lower | Higher (~70 % more) | | 3 | Steam Output | Lower (≤ 6500 kg/hr) | Higher (≤ 30000 kg/hr) | | 4 | Thermal Efficiency | 60–70 % | 70–80 % | | 5 | Capital Cost | Lower | Higher | | 6 | Maintenance | Simpler | More complex | | 7 | Suitability | Small industries | Medium industries | | 8 | Modern Relevance | Mostly obsolete | Still in legacy use | **Preference for Modern Medium-Scale Industries:** The **Lancashire boiler** is preferred because: - It has **higher heating surface area** due to two flues, hence greater steam output. - It delivers **higher thermal efficiency** (70–80 %), reducing fuel costs. - It can handle **larger fluctuating loads**, suitable for medium-scale continuous operation. The Cornish boiler, being single-flued, is **economically viable only for very small loads** where simplicity outweighs efficiency. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "two-pass" terminology:** Many students write "Cornish has two tubes." It does NOT — it has **one tube with two gas passes**. This is a recurring 2-mark deduction. > 2. **Forgetting the return path:** When describing working, students often stop at the rear smoke box. Always mention the **return flow through the annular gap** to the chimney. > 3. **Using absolute-value bars `|` in tables:** This breaks markdown rendering. Use `\vert` in LaTeX. > 4. **Missing 2257 kJ/kg in Factor of Evaporation:** Forgetting the reference latent heat value is a 1-mark loss in numericals. > 5. **Units inconsistency:** Always quote mass flow in **kg/hr**, enthalpy in **kJ/kg**, GCV in **kJ/kg**, and efficiency as a **percentage**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Cornish Boiler** - ✅ **Type:** Horizontal, stationary, **fire-tube**, **internally fired**. - ✅ **Flue Count:** **Single (one)** fire tube. - ✅ **Firebox:** **Internal**, with **hemispherical crowns** (top + bottom). - ✅ **Working Pressure:** Up to **10 bar**. - ✅ **Steam Output:** Up to **6500 kg/hr**. - ✅ **Efficiency:** **60 % – 70 %**. - ✅ **Two-Pass Heating:** Forward through flue + return through annular gap. - ✅ **Origin:** Cornwall, England; high-grade **Cornish coal** as original fuel. - ✅ **Mountings to Memorize:** Pressure gauge, water glass, safety valve, stop valve, feed check valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug. - ✅ **Key Formulas:** $F.E. = \dfrac{H - h_f}{2257}$, $\eta = \dfrac{m_s(H - h_f)}{m_f \cdot GCV}$. - ✅ **Comparison Hook:** Cornish = single flue; Lancashire = double flue. - ✅ **Modern Status:** Mostly **obsolete**; pedagogically vital for understanding fire-tube boiler fundamentals. - ✅ **Examiner Tip:** Always include a **labeled sketch** of the gas flow path in 7-mark descriptive answers — it scores **at least 2 free marks**.

topics.title: Locomotive Boiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Locomotive Boiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Locomotive Boiler (Definition per KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *Locomotive Boiler* is a **horizontal, multi-tubular, internally fired, portable (mobile) fire-tube boiler** that uses **solid fuel (coal)** and operates at a **moderate working pressure (typically 10–15 bar)**. It belongs to the family of *fire-tube boilers* and is specifically engineered to be mounted on a **moving chassis** of a railway locomotive. Heat from the combustion of fuel inside a rectangular firebox is transferred first to the surrounding water via the combustion chamber (fire brick arch) and then through thousands of small-diameter fire tubes to the smokebox end, ultimately producing steam that drives the locomotive's pistons.

**Key Classification Terminology (KTU Board Standard):**

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Boiler Type | Fire-Tube Boiler |
| Axis Orientation | Horizontal |
| Firing Mode | Internally Fired |
| Mobility | Mobile / Portable |
| Fuel | Solid (Coal / Wood) |
| Water Circulation | Natural (Thermo-siphon) |
| Capacity Range | **3000 to 4500 kg/hr** |
| Pressure Range | **10–15 bar** |
