# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Degree of Supersaturation" in steam nozzles. Mention its typical critical value.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Degree of Supersaturation** is defined as the ratio of the actual steam pressure at the nozzle exit to the saturation pressure corresponding to the actual (sub-cooled) exit temperature of steam:

$$\eta_d = \frac{P_2}{P_{s2}(T_2)}$$

Where:
- $P_2$ = actual exit pressure (Pa or bar)
- $P_{s2}$ = saturation pressure at the actual exit temperature $T_2$

**Typical Critical Value:** For dry saturated steam, the critical value at which spontaneous condensation begins is:

$$\eta_{d,critical} = 1.58$$

Below this value, the steam remains in a metastable supersaturated state.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention that $P_{s2}$ corresponds to the **actual** sub-cooled temperature, not the saturation temperature at $P_2$. This distinction is the **key marker** of a top-scoring answer.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Impulse and Reaction steam turbines in any four points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Pressure drop occurs **only in stationary nozzles** | Pressure drop occurs in **both stationary nozzles and moving blades** |
| 2 | **Pressure constant** across rotor blades | Pressure **decreases** across rotor blades |
| 3 | Blade shape is **symmetrical** (equal inlet and outlet angles) | Blade shape is **asymmetrical / convergent** (acts as a nozzle) |
| 4 | Relative velocity $V_{r2} < V_{r1}$ (steam decelerates in rotor) | Relative velocity $V_{r2} > V_{r1}$ (steam accelerates in rotor) |
| 5 | Lower blade efficiency (~70–80% simple) | Higher blade efficiency (~85–92% multi-stage) |
| 6 | Examples: De Laval, Curtis, Rateau | Examples: Parsons, Ljungstrom |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "reaction turbine has pressure drop in moving blades only" — this is **WRONG**. Pressure drops in **both** stator AND rotor.

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question 1A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done per kg of steam in a single-stage impulse turbine. **7 Marks**

**(b)** A single-stage impulse turbine has a blade speed of 175 m/s. Steam leaves the nozzle at 450 m/s at an angle of 20°. The blades are symmetrical. Determine: (i) Relative velocity at inlet, (ii) Relative velocity at outlet, (iii) Work developed per kg of steam per second, (iv) Blade efficiency. **7 Marks**

### Model Solution — Question 1A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Work Done

**Given Setup:**
- $V_1$ = absolute velocity at blade inlet, angle $\alpha_1$ with blade velocity $u$
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity at blade outlet, angle $\alpha_2$ on opposite side of $u$
- $V_{r1}, V_{r2}$ = relative velocities at inlet and outlet

**Step 1:** From inlet velocity triangle (using cosine rule):

$$V_{r1}^2 = V_1^2 + u^2 - 2 V_1 u \cos\alpha_1$$

**Step 2:** From outlet velocity triangle (with $\alpha_2$ measured such that $V_{w2}$ adds to $V_{w1}$):

$$V_{r2}^2 = V_2^2 + u^2 - 2 V_2 u \cos\alpha_2$$

**Step 3:** Newton's tangential force equation on the blade (per kg/s):

$$F_x = V_{w1} + V_{w2} = V_1 \cos\alpha_1 + V_2 \cos\alpha_2$$

**Step 4:** Work done per kg of steam:

$$\boxed{W = F_x \cdot u = u(V_1 \cos\alpha_1 + V_2 \cos\alpha_2) = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $u = 175$ m/s
- $V_1 = 450$ m/s
- $\alpha_1 = 20°$
- Symmetrical blades: $\beta_1 = \beta_2$ and $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$

**Step 1: Compute $V_{r1}$ (Relative velocity at inlet)**

$$V_{r1}^2 = V_1^2 + u^2 - 2 V_1 u \cos\alpha_1$$

$$V_{r1}^2 = 450^2 + 175^2 - 2(450)(175)\cos 20°$$

Compute each term:
- $450^2 = 202{,}500$
- $175^2 = 30{,}625$
- $\cos 20° = 0.9397$
- $2(450)(175)(0.9397) = 147{,}985.5$

$$V_{r1}^2 = 202{,}500 + 30{,}625 - 147{,}985.5 = 85{,}139.5$$

$$V_{r1} = \sqrt{85{,}139.5} = 291.79 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute $V_{r2}$**

For symmetrical blades, $V_{r2} = V_{r1}$:

$$V_{r2} = 291.79 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute exit angle $\beta_2$**

For symmetrical blades, $\beta_2 = \beta_1$.

From inlet triangle:

$$\sin\beta_1 = \frac{V_1 \sin\alpha_1}{V_{r1}} = \frac{450 \times \sin 20°}{291.79} = \frac{450 \times 0.342}{291.79} = \frac{153.9}{291.79}$$

$$\sin\beta_1 = 0.5274$$

$$\beta_1 = 31.83°$$

So $\beta_2 = 31.83°$.

**Step 4: Compute $V_2$ (Absolute exit velocity)**

From exit velocity triangle:

$$V_2 \cos\alpha_2 = \text{whirl component}$$

We can find $V_2$ from $V_{r2}$ geometry. With $\alpha_2$ measured on opposite side:

$$\cos\alpha_2 = \frac{V_{r2} \cos\beta_2 - u}{V_2}$$

Alternative — using the fact that for symmetrical blades, $V_{w2} = V_{w1} - $ ... let me solve directly:

Using velocity addition:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{V_{r2}^2 + u^2 - 2 V_{r2} u \cos\beta_2}$$

$$V_2 = \sqrt{291.79^2 + 175^2 - 2(291.79)(175)\cos 31.83°}$$

Compute:
- $291.79^2 = 85{,}141.5$
- $175^2 = 30{,}625$
- $\cos 31.83° = 0.8494$
- $2(291.79)(175)(0.8494) = 86{,}760.4$

$$V_2^2 = 85{,}141.5 + 30{,}625 - 86{,}760.4 = 29{,}006.1$$

$$V_2 = 170.31 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Compute Work Done**

First find $\alpha_2$:

$$\cos\alpha_2 = \frac{V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - u}{V_2} = \frac{291.79 \times 0.8494 - 175}{170.31}$$

$$\cos\alpha_2 = \frac{247.85 - 175}{170.31} = \frac{72.85}{170.31} = 0.4278$$

$$\alpha_2 = 64.66°$$

Now:

$$W = u(V_1 \cos\alpha_1 + V_2 \cos\alpha_2)$$

$$W = 175 \times (450 \times 0.9397 + 170.31 \times 0.4278)$$

$$W = 175 \times (422.87 + 72.85)$$

$$W = 175 \times 495.72 = 86{,}751 \text{ W/kg} = 86.75 \text{ kW per kg/s}$$

**Step 6: Compute Blade Efficiency**

$$\eta_B = \frac{W}{\frac{1}{2} V_1^2} = \frac{86{,}751}{\frac{1}{2}(450)^2} = \frac{86{,}751}{101{,}250}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_B = 0.8568 = 85.68\%}$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $V_{r1}$ | 291.79 m/s |
| $V_{r2}$ | 291.79 m/s |
| $V_2$ | 170.31 m/s |
| $\alpha_2$ | 64.66° |
| Work done | **86.75 kW/kg·s** |
| Blade efficiency | **85.68%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Loss-of-Marks):**
> 1. Forgetting to **square and take square root** in cosine-rule steps (sign errors).
> 2. Using $\alpha_2$ measured on the **same side** as $\alpha_1$ — this gives **negative work** (wrong sign).
> 3. Blade efficiency formula is $W / (V_1^2/2)$, NOT $W / (V_1^2/2 \times 1000)$. Unit conversion to **W/kg·s** matters.
> 4. KTU expects **degree**, not radian, for angles.

---

### Question 1B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of supersaturated flow of steam through a nozzle with the help of a neat sketch on the h-s diagram. What is Wilson's line? **7 Marks**

**(b)** Dry saturated steam at 10 bar expands isentropically to 0.1 bar in a nozzle. Determine: (i) Final condition of steam (supersaturated or wet), (ii) Exit velocity from the nozzle assuming no condensation. **7 Marks**

### Model Solution — Question 1B

#### Part (a) — Explanation of Supersaturated Flow

**Definition:**
When dry saturated steam expands rapidly through a convergent-divergent nozzle, the time spent in the divergent section is extremely small (**order of microseconds, ≈10⁻⁶ s**). Condensation requires:
1. Formation of nuclei (droplet initiation)
2. Growth of droplets

Both processes need time. Since the steam transits too quickly, **condensation does not occur** even after crossing the saturation curve. The steam remains as **vapor at a temperature below the saturation temperature** corresponding to its pressure.

**Diagram Description:**
On the Mollier (h-s) diagram:
- Point 1: Initial state (high pressure dry/saturated steam)
- Point 2: Equilibrium final state (on saturation line, $h_2$)
- Point 2': Actual final state (below saturation line, $h_{2'}$, where $h_{2'} < h_2$)
- The path 1→2' is **isentropic** (constant entropy)
- The path 1→2 is the **equilibrium expansion**

**Wilson's Line:**
It is the locus of points on the Mollier diagram where condensation **actually begins** in a rapidly expanding steam flow. It lies slightly below the saturation line in the wet region. Below Wilson's line, steam is in a metastable supersaturated state.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 10$ bar, dry saturated
- $P_2 = 0.1$ bar
- Isentropic expansion

**Step 1: Read steam tables**

At $P_1 = 10$ bar (dry saturated):
- $h_1 = h_g = 2778.1$ kJ/kg
- $s_1 = s_g = 6.5865$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 2: Find equilibrium exit state at $P_2 = 0.1$ bar**

At $P_2 = 0.1$ bar (saturation):
- $T_{sat} = 45.81°C$
- $s_f = 0.6493$ kJ/kg·K, $s_g = 8.1502$ kJ/kg·K
- $h_f = 191.83$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg

Since $s_1 = 6.5865 < s_g = 8.1502$ at 0.1 bar, equilibrium state would be **wet steam**.

Dryness fraction at equilibrium:
$$x_2 = \frac{s_1 - s_f}{s_g - s_f} = \frac{6.5865 - 0.6493}{8.1502 - 0.6493} = \frac{5.9372}{7.5009} = 0.7916$$

Equilibrium enthalpy:
$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 h_{fg} = 191.83 + 0.7916 \times 2392.8 = 191.83 + 1894.0 = 2085.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: Find supersaturated exit state**

If steam remains as vapor (no condensation) at $P_2 = 0.1$ bar:

For superheated steam at $P_2 = 0.1$ bar with $s = 6.5865$ kJ/kg·K:
- At $T = 45.81°C$ (sat): $s_g = 8.1502$
- At $T = 100°C$: $s = 7.6947$
- At $T = 150°C$: $s = 7.6134$
- At $T = 200°C$: $s = 7.8343$ — wait, need to check superheated tables

Re-checking: At $P = 0.1$ bar, superheated steam:
- $T = 100°C$: $h = 2676.2$ kJ/kg, $s = 7.3614$ kJ/kg·K
- $T = 150°C$: $h = 2776.4$ kJ/kg, $s = 7.6134$ kJ/kg·K
- $T = 200°C$: $h = 2875.3$ kJ/kg, $s = 7.8343$ kJ/kg·K

We need $s = 6.5865$ — but at $P_2 = 0.1$ bar, even saturation $s_g = 8.1502$ which is greater. So $s = 6.5865$ corresponds to a **temperature BELOW the saturation temperature** at 0.1 bar.

**Check: Supersaturation condition**

At supersaturated state, $T_{2'} < T_{sat}(P_2) = 45.81°C$.

We need $h_{2'}$ for the **vapor at pressure 0.1 bar and entropy 6.5865 kJ/kg·K**.

From steam tables at lower pressures (metastable vapor):
- At $P_{s2}$ corresponding to $T_{2'}$ (where $T_{2'}$ is the actual exit temperature):

Estimate: The vapor follows an isentropic line $s = 6.5865$ from $h_1, s_1$.

Using the superheated steam approximation: $h_{2'} \approx h_g(P_{s2}) + c_p (T_{2'} - T_{sat}(P_{s2}))$.

Practical approach — interpolate at 0.1 bar superheated table to find $T_{2'}$:
- We have $s_g$ at lower pressure points where the value reaches 6.5865.
- At $P = 0.05$ bar, $s_g = 8.3963$ (still > 6.5865)
- Actually, since entropy decreases as pressure decreases, we need very low pressure.

Let me re-examine: At the **saturation line**, going down in pressure:
- $P = 1$ bar: $s_g = 7.3594$
- $P = 0.5$ bar: $s_g = 7.5939$
- $P = 0.1$ bar: $s_g = 8.1502$

Wait — $s_g$ INCREASES as pressure decreases (because $T_{sat}$ decreases, lowering molecular order). So $s = 6.5865$ is **below** even the saturation entropy at very low pressures — this corresponds to **incomplete expansion**.

**More careful approach:**
At $P_2 = 0.1$ bar, vapor state with $s = 6.5865$ requires finding the metastable vapor continuation.

Standard KTU approach: Use **metastable vapor steam tables** or assume that in the metastable region, $h_{2'}$ is approximated by the isentropic line evaluated at the metastable vapor temperature.

Approximate: From Mollier chart or thermodynamic property software, for $P_2 = 0.1$ bar and $s = 6.5865$ kJ/kg·K:

$h_{2'} \approx 2230$ kJ/kg (approximate value from Mollier diagram)

This value is **less than** $h_1 = 2778.1$ kJ/kg but **more than** $h_2 = 2085.83$ kJ/kg.

Heat drop in supersaturated case:
$$\Delta h_{sup} = h_1 - h_{2'} = 2778.1 - 2230 = 548 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat drop in equilibrium case:
$$\Delta h_{eq} = h_1 - h_2 = 2778.1 - 2085.83 = 692.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Supersaturation loss:**
$$\Delta h_{loss} = 692.27 - 548 = 144.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Check supersaturation condition**

The exit pressure $P_2 = 0.1$ bar = 10 kPa. The corresponding saturation pressure at $T_{2'}$ (the actual exit temperature, which is below 45.81°C) is $P_{s2}$.

Since the steam is in supersaturated vapor state:

$$P_{s2} \approx 0.063 \text{ bar (estimated at } T_{2'} \approx 36°C\text{)}$$

$$\eta_d = \frac{P_2}{P_{s2}} = \frac{0.1}{0.063} = 1.587$$

Since $\eta_d \approx 1.58$, the steam is **at the threshold of supersaturation** — Wilson's line applies.

**Step 5: Compute exit velocity (assuming no condensation)**

$$V_2 = \sqrt{2000 \cdot \Delta h_{sup}} = \sqrt{2000 \times 548}$$

$$V_2 = \sqrt{1{,}096{,}000} = 1046.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Equilibrium exit enthalpy $h_2$ | 2085.83 kJ/kg |
| Supersaturated exit enthalpy $h_{2'}$ | ≈ 2230 kJ/kg (from Mollier) |
| Supersaturation loss $\Delta h_{loss}$ | 144.27 kJ/kg |
| Degree of supersaturation $\eta_d$ | ≈ 1.58 |
| Exit velocity $V_2$ | **≈ 1047 m/s** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. **Critical Error:** Reading the wrong column of steam tables (saturation vs superheated). For supersaturated expansion, you must use **superheated steam tables** at pressure $P_2$ but with the **isentropic entropy**.
> 2. **Common Mistake:** Using equilibrium expansion (1→2) instead of supersaturated expansion (1→2'). This gives a **higher exit velocity** (≈1175 m/s) which is **wrong** — the KTU expects the **lower** supersaturated velocity.
> 3. Students often forget to **verify supersaturation** by computing $\eta_d$ — losing 1 mark on the question.
> 4. Unit conversion: $\Delta h$ in kJ/kg → velocity formula uses $V = \sqrt{2000 \cdot \Delta h}$ (NOT 2×).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Bullet List**

### Supersaturated Flow (Steam Nozzles)
- ☐ Steam expanding rapidly may **fail to condense** below saturation temperature
- ☐ Time of flight in nozzle is **~10⁻⁶ s** — too short for droplet nucleation
- ☐ **Degree of supersaturation**: $\eta_d = P_2 / P_{s2}$
- ☐ **Critical value**: $\eta_d \approx 1.58$ for dry saturated steam
- ☐ **Wilson's Line** = locus on Mollier diagram where condensation finally begins
- ☐ Supersaturated expansion line lies **below** equilibrium saturation line
- ☐ **Loss of available heat drop**: $\Delta h_{loss} = \Delta h_{eq} - \Delta h_{sup}$
- ☐ Exit velocity is **lower** than equilibrium-predicted velocity
- ☐ Superheated steam rarely supersaturates
- ☐ Practically important in **last stages of LP turbines**

### Impulse Turbine
- ☐ Pressure drop **only in stationary nozzles**
- ☐ Blade shape: **symmetrical** (equal inlet and outlet angles)
- ☐ Relative velocity: $V_{r2} \leq V_{r1}$ (no expansion in rotor)
- ☐ Work done: $W = u(V_1 \cos\alpha_1 + V_2 \cos\alpha_2) = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$
- ☐ Optimum blade-speed ratio: $\rho = u/V_1 = \cos\alpha_1 / 2$
- ☐ Maximum blade efficiency: $\eta_{B,max} = (\cos^2\alpha_1 / 2) \cdot (\text{factor})$
- ☐ Examples: **De Laval** (single stage), **Curtis** (velocity compounded), **Rateau** (pressure compounded)
- ☐ Blade efficiency simple stage: $\eta_B \approx 70\text{–}80\%$

### Reaction Turbine
- ☐ Pressure drop occurs in **both stator AND rotor**
- ☐ Blade shape: **asymmetrical / convergent** (rotor is also a nozzle)
- ☐ Relative velocity: $V_{r2} > V_{r1}$ (expansion in rotor)
- ☐ **50% reaction (Parsons)**: $P_m = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$
- ☐ Work done: $W = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ (with similar form, different triangle geometry)
- ☐ Stage efficiency max: $\eta_{st,max} = 2\cos^2\alpha_1 - \cos^4\alpha_1$
- ☐ Examples: **Parsons**, **Ljungstrom**
- ☐ Multi-stage efficiency: 85–92%

### Velocity Triangles — Key Formulas
- ☐ Cosine rule: $V_r^2 = V^2 + u^2 - 2 V u \cos\alpha$
- ☐ Whirl component: $V_w = V \cos\alpha$
- ☐ Flow component: $V_f = V \sin\alpha$
- ☐ Sine rule: $\dfrac{V}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{V_r}{\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{u}{\sin(180° - \alpha - \beta)}$

### Compounding Strategies
- ☐ **Pressure compounding** (Rateau): multiple pressure drops across multiple stages
- ☐ **Velocity compounding** (Curtis): one pressure drop, multiple velocity stages
- ☐ **Combined compounding**: both — used in large thermal power turbines
- ☐ **Reheat factor** = Total actual work / Isentropic work per stage (always > 1)

### KTU Board Tips
- ☐ Always draw the velocity triangle — even if not asked
- ☐ Show all steam-table lookups explicitly — KTU awards step marks
- ☐ Use **units** consistently (kJ/kg for enthalpy, m/s for velocity, W or kW for power)
- ☐ Specify **whether supersaturation is considered** when calculating nozzle exit velocity
- ☐ For reaction turbines, mention **degree of reaction** explicitly
- ☐ Always mention **Wilson's Line** when discussing nozzle expansions

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Velocity Diagram** (also called a **Velocity Triangle**) is a graphical vector representation of the absolute, relative, and blade velocities of a working fluid (steam, gas, water, or combustion gases) as it enters and exits the rotor blades of a turbo-machine. It is the foundational analytical tool used to determine the energy transfer between the fluid and the rotor in steam turbines, gas turbines, hydraulic turbines, compressors, and pumps.

In the context of **Power Plant Engineering (OEMET727, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, velocity diagrams are used to:
- Quantify the **work done per unit mass** (or per unit weight) of fluid.
- Determine the **blade efficiency** of impulse and reaction blading.
- Design the **blade angles** (α, β, θ) at inlet and outlet.
- Evaluate the **degree of reaction (R)** for reaction turbines.
- Analyze **compounding and reheat factors** in multi-stage turbines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Velocity triangles form the analytical backbone of Module 2 — *Steam Turbines, Gas Turbines, and Hydraulic Turbines*. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for solving numerical problems in ESE (End Semester Examination) and is a high-weightage area.

## 1.2 Nomenclature of the Velocity Triangle

A velocity triangle consists of **three principal vectors** at every station (inlet and outlet):

| Symbol | Vector Name | Notation | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **C** | Absolute velocity of the fluid | $\vec{C}$ | Velocity of fluid w.r.t. stationary casing |
| **W** | Relative velocity of the fluid | $\vec{W}$ | Velocity of fluid w.r.t. moving rotor |
| **U** | Blade velocity (Peripheral velocity) | $\vec{U}$ | Velocity of the rotor blade at that radius |

These three vectors are related by the **fundamental kinematic equation of turbo-machinery**:

$$\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$$

This is a direct application of the classical mechanics principle — *velocity of a particle w.r.t. a stationary frame = velocity w.r.t. a moving frame + velocity of the moving frame*.

## 1.3 Components of Velocity

The absolute velocity **C** is resolved into two orthogonal components:

- **Whirl component (Cw)** — Tangential to the direction of blade motion. Responsible for work transfer.
- **Flow component (Cf)** — Axial (or radial, depending on machine type). Does **no work** but governs flow rate.

The relative velocity **W** is resolved into:
- **Relative whirl component (Ww)**
- **Relative flow component (Wf)**

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you are standing on a moving escalator holding an open umbrella against the rain. The rain falls vertically (like the **absolute velocity C** of fluid falling on the blade). However, from *your* perspective standing on the moving escalator (the **moving rotor**), the rain appears to strike the umbrella at an angle — this is the **relative velocity W**. The escalator's own motion is the **blade velocity U**. The velocity triangle simply tells you, "If I know two sides of a triangle, I can find the third." That's why it's drawn as a triangle!

**Real-world analogy for work transfer:** Think of a person throwing water balloons at a moving bicycle basket. The balloon's velocity in the air is **C** (absolute). The basket is moving with velocity **U** (blade). To the biker, the balloon appears to approach at velocity **W** (relative). The biker wants the *tangential component* of the balloon's velocity to be in the *same direction* as the basket's motion to catch it — this tangential component is the **whirl velocity Cw** that does the work.

## 1.5 Types of Velocity Triangles in Power Plant Engineering

The configuration of the velocity triangle changes based on the type of turbo-machine:

1. **Impulse Turbine Blade Triangles** (e.g., De Laval, Curtis, Rateau stages of steam turbines)
   - Pressure drop occurs entirely in the **nozzle**; rotor blades only redirect the flow.
   - Inlet pressure = Outlet pressure across the rotor (ideally).
   - Relative velocity magnitude remains **constant** ($W_1 = W_2$) for simple impulse blading.

2. **Reaction Turbine Blade Triangles** (e.g., Parsons, Ljungström, Francis, Kaplan)
   - Pressure drop occurs **partially in the nozzle** and **partially in the rotor**.
   - Relative velocity **changes** across the rotor ($W_1 \neq W_2$).
   - Defined by **Degree of Reaction (R)**, where $0 \leq R \leq 1$.

3. **Gas Turbine Blade Triangles** (Axial flow compressors & turbines)
   - Both stator (nozzle) and rotor act as aerodynamic profiles.
   - Triangles computed stage-by-stage.

## 1.6 Standard Reference Quantities

Three fundamental non-dimensional ratios govern every velocity triangle:

| Ratio | Definition | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Speed Ratio ($\rho$)** | $U/C_1$ or $U/C_0$ (for hydraulic) | 0.4 – 0.5 (Pelton), 0.6 – 0.9 (Francis) |
| **Flow Ratio ($\psi$)** | $C_{f1}/U$ or $C_{f2}/U$ | 0.05 – 0.30 |
| **Blade Ratio (k)** | $W_2/W_1$ (impulse) | 0.4 – 0.9 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Velocity Triangle

A velocity triangle at any station is constructed using the following sequential logic:

1. **Draw the blade velocity vector $\vec{U}$** along the horizontal axis (tangential direction of rotor motion). This is always **tangential** to the rotor circle.
2. **Locate the absolute velocity $\vec{C}$** starting from the same origin (for inlet) or the tip of the U vector (for outlet, depending on convention).
3. **Compute the relative velocity $\vec{W}$** as the vector closing the triangle, using $\vec{W} = \vec{C} - \vec{U}$.
4. **Resolve $\vec{C}$ and $\vec{W}$** into their whirl (tangential) and flow (axial) components using the respective blade angles.

## 2.2 Key Blade Angles

The blade angles are measured from the **plane of blade motion** (i.e., the tangential direction):

| Angle | Symbol | Definition | Measured From |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nozzle angle** | $\alpha_1$ | Angle made by $C_1$ with $U_1$ direction | Tangential direction |
| **Blade inlet angle** | $\beta_1$ | Angle made by $W_1$ with $U_1$ direction (opposite sense) | Tangential direction |
| **Blade outlet angle** | $\beta_2$ | Angle made by $W_2$ with $U_2$ direction | Tangential direction |
| **Exit angle of absolute velocity** | $\alpha_2$ | Angle made by $C_2$ with $U_2$ direction | Tangential direction |

## 2.3 Euler's Equation for Turbines (The Heart of Velocity Triangle Analysis)

Euler's equation relates the **work done per unit mass** (or per unit weight) to the change in whirl velocity:

$$\boxed{W_{turbine} = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}$$

For **unit weight basis** (commonly used in steam/hydraulic turbines):

$$\boxed{W_{turbine} = \frac{1}{g}(U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}) \;\; \text{m·kgf/kg}}$$

For **unit mass basis** (commonly used in gas turbines):

$$\boxed{w_{turbine} = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2} \;\; \text{J/kg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Euler's equation matters:** This is the **single most important equation** in KTU Power Plant Engineering Module 2. It is the theoretical foundation of every work-output calculation, blade efficiency derivation, and stage efficiency problem. Most 14-mark problems in ESE will use this equation in some form.

## 2.4 Degree of Reaction (R)

The **Degree of Reaction** quantifies what fraction of the total enthalpy drop occurs in the **rotor** (as opposed to the stator/nozzle):

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\text{Enthalpy drop in rotor}}{\text{Total enthalpy drop (stator + rotor)}} = \frac{\Delta h_{rotor}}{\Delta h_{total}}}$$

In terms of velocity components:

$$\boxed{R = 1 - \frac{C_{w1} + C_{w2}}{2U}}$$

For **symmetric blading** (where $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $C_{f1} = C_{f2}$), the relation simplifies to:

$$\boxed{R = 1 - \frac{C_w}{U}}$$

## 2.5 Blade Efficiency Formulae

For an **Impulse Turbine** (with $W_1 = W_2$ and zero exit whirl for max efficiency, $C_{w2} = 0$):

$$\boxed{\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})}{C_1^2 - C_2^2}}$$

For maximum efficiency condition, when $\alpha_2 = 90°$ (i.e., $C_{w2} = 0$):

$$\eta_{blade,max} = \frac{2\rho\cos\alpha_1 - 2\rho^2}{1 - \rho^2(K^2 + \psi^2 \tan^2\alpha_1)}$$

where $\rho = U/C_1$, $K = W_2/W_1$ (blade friction factor), and $\psi = C_{f1}/U$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **master formula reference** for all velocity diagram problems in KTU ESE. Memorize these.

| # | Formula | Application / Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ | Fundamental velocity vector relation |
| 2 | $C_w = C \cos\alpha$ | Whirl component of absolute velocity |
| 3 | $C_f = C \sin\alpha$ | Flow (axial) component of absolute velocity |
| 4 | $W_w = W \cos\beta$ | Whirl component of relative velocity |
| 5 | $W_f = W \sin\beta$ | Flow component of relative velocity |
| 6 | $W = U^2 + C^2 - 2UC\cos\alpha$ | Magnitude of relative velocity (Cosine rule) |
| 7 | $W/U = \sin\alpha / \sin\beta$ | Sine rule application |
| 8 | $W/U = \sin(180° - \alpha) / \sin\beta$ | Alternate sine-rule form |
| 9 | $w = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}$ | Euler's equation (work per unit mass, J/kg) |
| 10 | $W = (U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2})/g$ | Work per unit weight (m·kgf/kg) |
| 11 | $R = 1 - (C_{w1} + C_{w2})/(2U)$ | Degree of reaction (general) |
| 12 | $R = 1 - C_w/U$ | Degree of reaction (symmetric blading) |
| 13 | $\eta_{blade} = 2U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})/(C_1^2 - C_2^2)$ | Blade efficiency (impulse) |
| 14 | $\eta_{blade,max} = (1+K\cos\beta_2)/2$ | Max efficiency at $\alpha_2 = 90°$, impulse |
| 15 | $U = \pi D N / 60$ | Blade velocity from rotor diameter and RPM |
| 16 | $C_1 = \sqrt{2gH_n}$ or $C_1 = \sqrt{2 \Delta h}$ | Jet velocity from nozzle (head or enthalpy) |
| 17 | $\rho = U/C_1$ | Speed ratio |
| 18 | $\psi = C_{f1}/U$ | Flow ratio |
| 19 | $K = W_2/W_1$ | Blade ratio (impulse) |
| 20 | $C_{f1} = C_{f2} = C_f$ (assumed) | Flow continuity for axial machines |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Velocity diagrams are not just academic exercises — they are **daily tools** used in industry by:

- **Steam Turbine Design Engineers (BHEL, Siemens, GE):** To optimize stage efficiency, reduce blade erosion, and design last-stage blades for low-pressure (LP) turbines.
- **Aircraft Jet Engine Designers (Pratt & Whitney, Rolls-Royce):** To design compressor and turbine blade profiles for maximum specific work and minimum loss.
- **Hydroelectric Plant Engineers (NHPC, KSEB):** To design Francis, Kaplan, and Pelton runners for optimum head utilization and cavitation avoidance.
- **Refrigeration & Cryogenic Industries:** To design expanders and cryogenic turbines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Connection:** Every numerical problem in Module 2 essentially reduces to constructing a velocity triangle and applying one of the 20 formulas above. The mark distribution typically follows: (a) Diagram construction: 4 marks, (b) Component calculation: 4 marks, (c) Efficiency / Work done: 6 marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Euler's Equation for Turbines (Comprehensive)

### Starting Principle
Consider a turbo-machine rotor. A fluid particle enters at station 1 with absolute velocity $C_1$ and leaves at station 2 with absolute velocity $C_2$. The rotor moves with blade velocity $U$ at the mean radius.

### Step 1: Tangential Momentum Balance
By Newton's second law, the **tangential force** exerted by the rotor on the fluid equals the rate of change of tangential momentum of the fluid:

$$F_t = \dot{m}(C_{w2} - C_{w1})$$

where $C_{w1}$ and $C_{w2}$ are the whirl components (tangential components) of absolute velocity at inlet and outlet respectively.

### Step 2: Equal and Opposite Reaction
By Newton's third law, the force exerted **by the fluid on the rotor** is:

$$F_{t,rotor} = -\dot{m}(C_{w2} - C_{w1}) = \dot{m}(C_{w1} - C_{w2})$$

### Step 3: Work Done by the Rotor
The work done per unit time (Power) is the force times the blade velocity:

$$P = F_{t,rotor} \times U = \dot{m}(C_{w1} - C_{w2})U$$

### Step 4: Work Done per Unit Mass
Dividing both sides by the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$:

$$\boxed{w = U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) \;\; \text{J/kg}}$$

For unequal blade velocities at inlet and outlet (e.g., mean-line vs. tip-line), the generalized form is:

$$\boxed{w = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}$$

### Step 5: Per Unit Weight Form
Dividing by $g$ (acceleration due to gravity, **9.81 m/s²**):

$$\boxed{W = \frac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g} \;\; \text{m·kgf/kg}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Magnitude of Relative Velocity using Cosine Rule

### Step 1: Vector Setup
The relative velocity is $\vec{W} = \vec{C} - \vec{U}$, where $\vec{U}$ is along the positive horizontal axis and $\vec{C}$ makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal.

### Step 2: Apply Cosine Rule
In the velocity triangle, the angle between $C$ and $U$ is $(180° - \alpha)$, because $W$ is the closing side and $\alpha$ is measured from $U$.

$$W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos(180° - \alpha)$$

### Step 3: Simplify
Since $\cos(180° - \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$W^2 = C^2 + U^2 + 2CU\cos\alpha$$

### Step 4: Take Square Root

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{C^2 + U^2 + 2CU\cos\alpha}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Many textbooks write this as $W^2 = C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos\alpha$ with the angle measured differently. Always verify the angle convention (whether $\alpha$ is measured from $U$ or from the extension of $U$) before plugging values.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Degree of Reaction (Symmetric Blading)

### Step 1: Total Enthalpy Drop
The total enthalpy drop across a stage (nozzle + rotor) equals the kinetic energy at nozzle exit (for impulse) or distributed (for reaction):

$$\Delta h_{total} = \frac{C_1^2}{2}$$

### Step 2: Enthalpy Drop in Rotor
By Euler's equation, the work done equals the kinetic energy change in the **whirl** component, but for a reaction turbine, the rotor also has a **relative velocity change**:

$$\Delta h_{rotor} = \frac{W_1^2 - W_2^2}{2}$$

### Step 3: Degree of Reaction Definition
$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{rotor}}{\Delta h_{total}} = \frac{W_1^2 - W_2^2}{C_1^2}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Sine Rule Relations
For a velocity triangle, $W_1 / \sin\alpha_1 = C_1 / \sin\beta_1$, hence:

$$W_1 = C_1 \cdot \frac{\sin\alpha_1}{\sin\beta_1}$$

Similarly, for symmetric blading ($\alpha_1 = \beta_2$):

$$W_2 = C_1 \cdot \frac{\sin\alpha_2}{\sin\beta_2}$$

### Step 5: Final Expression
After algebraic manipulation using $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1$ and $C_{w2} = C_2 \cos\alpha_2$:

$$\boxed{R = 1 - \frac{C_{w1} + C_{w2}}{2U}}$$

For **50% reaction** (Parsons turbine), the condition $R = 0.5$ gives:

$$\boxed{U = C_w = \frac{C_1 + C_2}{2}\cos\alpha_1 \;\; \text{(for symmetric blading)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Blade Efficiency Maximum Condition

### Step 1: Start with Blade Efficiency
For an impulse stage with $W_1 = W_2$ (no friction assumed for ideal case) and $C_{f1} = C_{f2}$:

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})}{C_1^2 - C_2^2}$$

### Step 2: Express in Terms of Speed Ratio $\rho = U/C_1$
$$C_1^2 - C_2^2 = C_1^2 - (C_{f2}^2 + C_{w2}^2) = C_1^2 - C_{f1}^2 - C_{w2}^2$$

Since $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1$, $C_{w2} = C_2 \cos\alpha_2$, and the flow components are conserved:

$$C_1^2 - C_2^2 = C_1^2 \sin^2\alpha_1 + C_1^2 \cos^2\alpha_1 - C_2^2 \cos^2\alpha_2 - C_2^2 \sin^2\alpha_2$$
$$= C_{w1}^2 - C_{w2}^2 + C_{f}^2 - C_{f}^2 = C_{w1}^2 - C_{w2}^2$$

### Step 3: Substitute Speed Ratio
After dividing numerator and denominator by $C_1^2$:

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2\rho(\cos\alpha_1 - (C_2/C_1)\cos\alpha_2)}{1 - (C_2/C_1)^2}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Condition
Differentiating $\eta_{blade}$ with respect to $\alpha_2$ and setting $d\eta/d\alpha_2 = 0$ gives the **optimum exit condition**: $\alpha_2 = 90°$, which means $C_{w2} = 0$ (axial exit).

Under this condition, the simplified maximum efficiency is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{blade,max} = \frac{2\rho(\cos\alpha_1 - \rho)}{1 - \rho^2(K^2 + \psi^2\tan^2\alpha_1)}}$$

where $K$ is the blade friction coefficient and $\psi$ is the flow ratio.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Impulse vs Reaction Turbines

| Parameter | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure drop in nozzle | **100%** | Part (50–60%) |
| Pressure drop in rotor | **0%** (ideal) | Part (40–50%) |
| $W_1$ vs $W_2$ | $W_1 = W_2$ (ideal) | $W_1 \neq W_2$ (W decreases) |
| Degree of Reaction (R) | 0 | 0.5 (Parsons) or any 0–1 |
| Blade shape | Symmetric / spoon-shaped | Asymmetric airfoil |
| Relative velocity | Constant magnitude | Decreases across rotor |
| Compounding needed? | Yes (Velocity/Pressure) | No (multi-stage suffices) |
| Example | De Laval, Curtis, Rateau | Parsons, Ljungström |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Single-Stage Impulse Turbine

**Given:**
- Nozzle exit velocity $C_1 = 600 \text{ m/s}$
- Nozzle angle $\alpha_1 = 20°$
- Blade speed $U = 250 \text{ m/s}$
- Blade ratio $K = W_2/W_1 = 0.9$
- Blade outlet angle $\beta_2 = 30°$
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 5 \text{ kg/s}$

**Find:** (a) Relative velocity at inlet $W_1$, (b) Relative velocity at outlet $W_2$, (c) Work done per kg, (d) Blade efficiency.

### Solution

**Step (a): Find $W_1$ using cosine rule**

$$W_1 = \sqrt{C_1^2 + U^2 + 2 C_1 U \cos\alpha_1}$$

$$W_1 = \sqrt{600^2 + 250^2 + 2(600)(250)\cos 20°}$$

$$W_1 = \sqrt{360000 + 62500 + 300000 \times 0.9397}$$

$$W_1 = \sqrt{360000 + 62500 + 281910} = \sqrt{704410}$$

$$\boxed{W_1 \approx 839.3 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step (b): Find $W_2$**

$$W_2 = K \times W_1 = 0.9 \times 839.3 = 755.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (c): Work done per kg (Euler's equation)**

First, find whirl components:
- $C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 600 \cos 20° = 600 \times 0.9397 = 563.8 \text{ m/s}$

For outlet, we need $\alpha_2$. Since $W_2$ is known, and from velocity triangle at outlet:

The whirl component at outlet is $C_{w2} = U - W_2 \cos\beta_2$ (when the whirl component of $W_2$ is in opposite sense to $U$, as is typical for impulse blades):

$$C_{w2} = 250 - 755.4 \cos 30° = 250 - 755.4 \times 0.8660 = 250 - 654.2 = -404.2 \text{ m/s}$$

Wait, this needs careful sign convention. Let me redo: For an impulse blade, $W_2$ has a tangential component in the same direction as $U$ (so it actually subtracts from $C_1$'s whirl contribution to $C_2$'s whirl). The correct expression is:

$$C_{w2} = W_2 \cos\beta_2 - U$$

$$C_{w2} = 755.4 \cos 30° - 250 = 654.2 - 250 = 404.2 \text{ m/s}$$

So $C_2 = \sqrt{C_{f2}^2 + C_{w2}^2}$ where $C_{f2} = W_2 \sin\beta_2 = 755.4 \sin 30° = 377.7 \text{ m/s}$.

Now apply Euler's equation:

$$w = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2} = 250(563.8) - 250(404.2)$$

$$w = 250(563.8 - 404.2) = 250 \times 159.6$$

$$\boxed{w = 39900 \text{ J/kg} = 39.9 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step (d): Blade Efficiency**

First, find $C_2$:
$$C_2 = \sqrt{C_{w2}^2 + C_{f2}^2} = \sqrt{404.2^2 + 377.7^2} = \sqrt{163378 + 142657} = \sqrt{306035}$$
$$C_2 \approx 553.2 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})}{C_1^2 - C_2^2} = \frac{2(250)(563.8 - 404.2)}{600^2 - 553.2^2}$$

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{500 \times 159.6}{360000 - 306035} = \frac{79800}{53965}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{blade} \approx 1.479 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 147.9\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is a common KTU examination trap!** Efficiency greater than 100% is a **red flag**. It means either: (i) the assumption of $C_2$ direction is wrong, (ii) the sign convention of $C_{w2}$ is reversed, or (iii) the problem is ill-posed. The correct physical interpretation is that **kinetic energy at exit is being recovered** (axial exit principle), but the formula as derived assumes a different convention. In KTU exams, always recheck the sign of $C_{w2}$ before finalizing your answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrected interpretation:** The negative sign of $C_{w2}$ (in the convention where $C_{w1} > 0$ in $U$-direction) means the whirl is now **opposite** to the blade motion. So $C_{w2} = -404.2$ m/s, and:
> $$w = 250(563.8 - (-404.2)) = 250 \times 968 = 242000 \text{ J/kg}$$
> $$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2(250)(968)}{600^2 - 553.2^2} = \frac{484000}{53965} \approx 8.97$$
> This is also > 1, indicating the formula needs to use $C_{w2}$ **magnitude** when $C_{w2}$ is in the opposite direction. The KTU-correct formulation is:
> $$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} + \vert C_{w2} \vert)}{C_1^2 - C_2^2}$$
> yielding $\eta_{blade} = \frac{2(250)(563.8 + 404.2)}{53965} = \frac{484000}{53965} \approx 8.97$... which still exceeds 1 due to the unrealistic input data. In KTU exams, the input values are designed to give physically realistic efficiencies between 0.5 and 0.95.

---

## 3.7 KTU Examination Pattern — Worked Template

For any velocity diagram problem, KTU expects the following **sequence** (this is the valuation key pattern):

1. **State the given data** with units (1 mark).
2. **Draw the velocity triangle** clearly with labeled vectors and angles (2 marks).
3. **Apply the cosine/sine rule** to compute unknown velocity components (3 marks).
4. **Apply Euler's equation** for work done (3 marks).
5. **Compute efficiency or degree of reaction** (3 marks).
6. **State final answer with units** and physical interpretation (2 marks).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Velocity Triangle. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **velocity triangle** is a vector diagram representing the velocities of a fluid particle — absolute velocity (**C**), relative velocity (**W**), and blade velocity (**U**) — as it flows through the rotor of a turbo-machine. It consists of two triangles, one at the **inlet** and one at the **outlet** of the blade row.

The three vectors are related by the fundamental kinematic equation:
$$\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$$

The absolute velocity **C** is resolved into two components: the **whirl component ($C_w$)** in the tangential direction (responsible for work transfer) and the **flow component ($C_f$)** in the axial direction (governing mass flow). Velocity triangles are essential for determining **work done (Euler's equation)**, **blade efficiency**, and **degree of reaction** in steam, gas, and hydraulic turbines.

---

### Question 2: What is Degree of Reaction? State its value for Impulse and 50% Reaction turbines. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **degree of reaction (R)** is defined as the ratio of enthalpy drop occurring in the **rotor** to the total enthalpy drop across the stage:

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{rotor}}{\Delta h_{total}}$$

In terms of velocity components:
$$R = 1 - \frac{C_{w1} + C_{w2}}{2U}$$

**Values:**
- **Impulse Turbine:** $R = 0$ (entire pressure drop in nozzle, none in rotor)
- **50% Reaction Turbine (Parsons):** $R = 0.5$ (pressure drop equally distributed)

The degree of reaction is a critical design parameter that determines the blade shape, blade angles, and the pressure distribution across the rotor.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive Euler's equation for the work done per unit mass of fluid in a turbo-machine. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-stage impulse turbine has the following specifications:
- Nozzle angle $\alpha_1 = 22°$
- Blade speed $U = 175$ m/s
- Blade inlet angle $\beta_1 = 30°$
- Blade outlet angle $\beta_2 = 25°$
- Blade friction factor $K = W_2/W_1 = 0.85$

Calculate: (i) Absolute velocity at inlet $C_1$, (ii) Relative velocity at inlet $W_1$, (iii) Work done per kg, (iv) Blade efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Derivation of Euler's Equation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. The flow is steady and continuous.
2. The fluid is incompressible (or follows ideal gas with no heat transfer).
3. Velocity is uniform across the blade passage (one-dimensional flow).
4. No friction losses at the blade surface (ideal blading).
5. The wheel rotates at constant angular velocity.

**Derivation:**

Consider a mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ entering the rotor at station 1 with absolute velocity $C_1$ and leaving at station 2 with absolute velocity $C_2$. The blade velocity is $U$ at the mean radius.

**Step 1:** Apply the angular momentum theorem (moment of momentum equation) to the control volume of the rotor:

$$\text{Torque} = \dot{m}(r_2 C_{w2} - r_1 C_{w1})$$

**Step 2:** Multiply torque by angular velocity $\omega$ to get power, and use $U = \omega r$:

$$P = \dot{m}(U_2 C_{w2} - U_1 C_{w1})$$

**Step 3:** Work done by fluid on rotor is negative of power input to fluid, hence:

$$P_{rotor} = \dot{m}(U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2})$$

**Step 4:** Work done per unit mass:

$$\boxed{w = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2} \;\; \text{J/kg}}$$

For per unit weight (dividing by $g$):

$$W = \frac{U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2}}{g} \;\; \text{m·kgf/kg}$$

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\alpha_1 = 22°$, $U = 175$ m/s, $\beta_1 = 30°$, $\beta_2 = 25°$, $K = 0.85$

**Step (i): Find $C_1$ using the inlet velocity triangle**

From the sine rule in the inlet triangle:
$$\frac{C_1}{\sin\beta_1} = \frac{U}{\sin(180° - \alpha_1 - \beta_1)} = \frac{U}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$$

The angle $\angle(180° - \alpha_1 - \beta_1) = \sin(180° - 52°) = \sin 52° = 0.7880$

$$C_1 = \frac{U \sin\beta_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} = \frac{175 \times \sin 30°}{\sin 52°}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{175 \times 0.5}{0.7880} = \frac{87.5}{0.7880}$$

$$\boxed{C_1 \approx 111.04 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step (ii): Find $W_1$ using cosine rule**

$$W_1 = \sqrt{C_1^2 + U^2 - 2C_1 U \cos\alpha_1}$$

$$W_1 = \sqrt{111.04^2 + 175^2 - 2(111.04)(175)\cos 22°}$$

$$W_1 = \sqrt{12330 + 30625 - 38864 \times 0.9272}$$

$$W_1 = \sqrt{42955 - 36039} = \sqrt{6916}$$

$$\boxed{W_1 \approx 83.16 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step (iii): Find $W_2$ and work done**

$$W_2 = K \times W_1 = 0.85 \times 83.16 = 70.69 \text{ m/s}$$

Find whirl component at inlet:
$$C_{w1} = C_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 111.04 \times \cos 22° = 111.04 \times 0.9272 = 102.96 \text{ m/s}$$

Find exit whirl component. For an impulse blade, $C_{w2}$ is found from the outlet velocity triangle:
$$C_{w2} = W_2 \cos\beta_2 - U = 70.69 \times \cos 25° - 175$$
$$C_{w2} = 70.69 \times 0.9063 - 175 = 64.07 - 175 = -110.93 \text{ m/s}$$

The negative sign indicates $C_{w2}$ is in the **opposite** direction to $U$. Taking the magnitude for work calculation:
$$|C_{w2}| = 110.93 \text{ m/s}$$

**Work done per kg:**
$$w = U(C_{w1} + |C_{w2}|) = 175 \times (102.96 + 110.93)$$

$$w = 175 \times 213.89$$

$$\boxed{w = 37431 \text{ J/kg} \approx 37.43 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step (iv): Find $C_2$ and blade efficiency**

Flow component (constant through stage):
$$C_{f1} = C_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 111.04 \times \sin 22° = 111.04 \times 0.3746 = 41.60 \text{ m/s}$$

$$C_2 = \sqrt{C_{w2}^2 + C_{f2}^2} = \sqrt{110.93^2 + 41.60^2} = \sqrt{12305 + 1730} = \sqrt{14035}$$

$$C_2 \approx 118.47 \text{ m/s}$$

**Blade efficiency:**
$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} + |C_{w2}|)}{C_1^2 - C_2^2} = \frac{2 \times 175 \times 213.89}{111.04^2 - 118.47^2}$$

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{74862}{12330 - 14035} = \frac{74862}{-1705}$$

Since the denominator is negative, the formula needs reinterpretation. The physically correct form, accounting for the reversed direction of $C_{w2}$, is:

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} - (-C_{w2}))}{C_1^2 - C_2^2} \text{ with sign convention}$$

**Alternative direct formulation:** $\eta_{blade} = \frac{w}{(C_1^2 - C_2^2)/2}$:

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{37431}{(12330 - 14035)/2} = \frac{37431}{-852.5} \approx -43.9$$

The negative sign indicates the **kinetic energy at exit is greater than at inlet**, which physically means the flow is **accelerating** through the stage — a sign of an **incorrectly designed** impulse stage (this happens when $U$ is too small for the given blade angles). For a properly designed impulse stage, $C_2 < C_1$ always.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** This negative-efficiency scenario is a **classic KTU trap**! When you encounter a negative denominator or efficiency > 1, do **not** blindly apply formulas. Recheck: (i) the sign of $C_{w2}$, (ii) whether the data is consistent with an impulse stage ($C_1 > C_2$ requirement), (iii) whether the correct formula form (with $C_{w1} - C_{w2}$ for the case where $C_{w2}$ is positive, or $C_{w1} + |C_{w2}|$ when $C_{w2}$ is negative in $U$-frame) is being used. In the KTU exam, you should **explicitly state the sign convention adopted** to gain full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a velocity triangle, explain the concept of **Degree of Reaction** in a Parsons reaction turbine. Show that for a 50% reaction stage, the blade velocity is half the mean of inlet and outlet absolute velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a single-stage impulse turbine, the isentropic heat drop is **120 kJ/kg**, nozzle efficiency is **0.92**, and the ratio of blade speed to jet speed is **0.45**. The nozzle angle is $20°$ and the blade outlet angle is $25°$ with a blade friction factor of 0.85. Determine: (i) Blade velocity, (ii) Work done per kg, (iii) Blade efficiency, (iv) Power output if mass flow rate is 10 kg/s. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution Outline to Question B

#### Part (a): Degree of Reaction (7 marks)

**Definition:** $R = \Delta h_{rotor} / \Delta h_{total}$

**Velocity Triangle Derivation (Parsons Turbine):**

For a 50% reaction stage with symmetric blading ($\alpha_1 = \beta_2$, $C_{f1} = C_{f2}$):

The enthalpy drop in the rotor is:
$$\Delta h_{rotor} = \frac{W_1^2 - W_2^2}{2}$$

Total enthalpy drop:
$$\Delta h_{total} = \frac{C_1^2 - C_2^2}{2}$$

For 50% reaction: $\Delta h_{rotor} = \Delta h_{total} / 2$

Using velocity triangle relations and $C_{f1} = C_{f2}$:

$$C_1^2 - C_2^2 = 2(W_1^2 - W_2^2)$$

After trigonometric manipulation using $W_1 = \sqrt{C_1^2 + U^2 - 2C_1 U \cos\alpha_1}$ and $W_2 = \sqrt{C_2^2 + U^2 - 2C_2 U \cos\alpha_2}$:

For $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ (symmetric), the condition $R = 0.5$ yields:

$$\boxed{U = \frac{C_{w1} + C_{w2}}{2} = \frac{C_1\cos\alpha_1 + C_2\cos\alpha_2}{2}}$$

This is the desired result — **blade velocity equals half the mean of the whirl components**, which for symmetric blading becomes half the mean of the absolute velocity components.

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution (7 marks) — Brief Solution

**Step 1: Find $C_1$ (jet velocity)**
$$\Delta h_{nozzle} = \eta_N \times \Delta h_s = 0.92 \times 120 = 110.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$C_1 = \sqrt{2 \times 110400} = \sqrt{220800} \approx 469.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Find $U$**
$$U = 0.45 \times 469.9 = 211.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Find $C_{w1}$ and $C_{w2}$**
$$C_{w1} = C_1 \cos 20° = 469.9 \times 0.9397 = 441.6 \text{ m/s}$$

For an impulse stage with $W_2 = 0.85 W_1$ and $\beta_2 = 25°$:
$$W_1 = \sqrt{C_1^2 + U^2 - 2C_1 U \cos 20°}$$
$$W_1 = \sqrt{220800 + 44732 - 2(469.9)(211.5)(0.9397)}$$
$$W_1 = \sqrt{220800 + 44732 - 186743} = \sqrt{78789} = 280.7 \text{ m/s}$$

$$W_2 = 0.85 \times 280.7 = 238.6 \text{ m/s}$$

$$C_{w2} = W_2 \cos 25° - U = 238.6 \times 0.9063 - 211.5 = 216.2 - 211.5 = 4.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Work done per kg**
$$w = U(C_{w1} - C_{w2}) = 211.5 \times (441.6 - 4.7) = 211.5 \times 436.9$$
$$\boxed{w \approx 92,405 \text{ J/kg} = 92.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Step 5: Find $C_2$ and efficiency**
$$C_{f1} = C_1 \sin 20° = 469.9 \times 0.342 = 160.7 \text{ m/s} = C_{f2}$$
$$C_2 = \sqrt{C_{w2}^2 + C_{f2}^2} = \sqrt{4.7^2 + 160.7^2} \approx 160.8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})}{C_1^2 - C_2^2} = \frac{2 \times 211.5 \times 436.9}{220800 - 25857} = \frac{184810}{194943}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{blade} \approx 0.948 \text{ or } 94.8\%}$$

**Step 6: Power output**
$$P = \dot{m} \times w = 10 \times 92.4 = 924 \text{ kW}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Sign Convention (4 marks lost commonly):** Mixing positive and negative whirl components without stating convention. Always state: *"I take the direction of blade motion as positive."*
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert g in Euler's Equation (2 marks):** For unit weight, divide by $g = 9.81$ m/s². For unit mass, do **not** divide by $g$.
> 3. **Inlet vs Outlet Confusion in Sine Rule (3 marks):** Remember, in the inlet triangle, the angle opposite to $C_1$ is $\beta_1$ (NOT $\alpha_1$). Many students write $C_1/\sin\alpha_1$, which is **wrong**.
> 4. **Not Drawing the Diagram (2-3 marks):** KTU allocates explicit marks for a labeled velocity triangle. Skipping it = direct loss.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kJ/kg with J/kg, or km/h with m/s, is a **favourite deduction** by KTU examiners.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Summary**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember

- **Velocity triangle** = vector diagram of $C$, $W$, $U$ at inlet and outlet of rotor.
- **Three vectors:** Absolute velocity **C**, Relative velocity **W**, Blade velocity **U**.
- **Vector relation:** $\vec{C} = \vec{W} + \vec{U}$ (always, at every station).
- **Components:** $C_w = C\cos\alpha$ (whirl, does work), $C_f = C\sin\alpha$ (flow, axial/radial).
- **Cosine rule:** $W = \sqrt{C^2 + U^2 - 2CU\cos\alpha}$
- **Sine rule:** $C/\sin\beta = W/\sin\alpha = U/\sin(180° - \alpha - \beta)$

### ✅ Euler's Equation — The Most Important Formula

$$w = U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2} \;\; [\text{J/kg}]$$

$$W = (U_1 C_{w1} - U_2 C_{w2})/g \;\; [\text{m·kgf/kg}]$$

- For **equal U** at inlet/outlet: $w = U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})$.
- For **maximum efficiency** in impulse stage: $C_{w2} = 0$ (i.e., $\alpha_2 = 90°$, axial exit).

### ✅ Degree of Reaction

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_{w1} + C_{w2}}{2U}$$

- Impulse turbine: **$R = 0$**
- 50% Reaction (Parsons): **$R = 0.5$**, and $U = C_w$ for symmetric blading.

### ✅ Blade Efficiency

$$\eta_{blade} = \frac{2U(C_{w1} - C_{w2})}{C_1^2 - C_2^2}$$

Maximum (for $\alpha_2 = 90°$): $\eta_{max} = (1 + K\cos\beta_2)/2$ where $K = W_2/W_1$.

### ✅ Key Ratios to Memorize

| Ratio | Formula | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed ratio | $\rho = U/C_1$ | 0.4–0.5 typical |
| Flow ratio | $\psi = C_{f1}/U$ | Small, 0.05–0.30 |
| Blade ratio | $K = W_2/W_1$ | Friction effect, 0.4–0.9 |

### ✅ Impulse vs Reaction (Quick Recall)

| Feature | Impulse | Reaction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| R | 0 | 0 to 1 |
| $W_1, W_2$ | $W_1 = W_2$ | $W_1 > W_2$ |
| Blade shape | Symmetric spoon | Asymmetric airfoil |
| Pressure drop | 100% in nozzle | Split |
| Compounding | Often needed | Multi-stage |

### ✅ Quick-Reference Angles

- $\alpha_1$: Nozzle angle (inlet absolute velocity direction from $U$).
- $\beta_1$: Blade inlet angle (inlet relative velocity direction).
- $\beta_2$: Blade outlet angle (outlet relative velocity direction).
- $\alpha_2$: Absolute exit angle (for max efficiency in impulse: $\alpha_2 = 90°$).

### ✅ Examiner's Golden Rules (Lose ZERO Marks)

1. **Always draw the diagram** — labeled, to scale, with all angles.
2. **State your sign convention** for $C_{w1}$ and $C_{w2}$ explicitly.
3. **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** for unit-weight problems, omit for unit-mass.
4. **Show all intermediate steps** — KTU rewards process, not just the final number.
5. **Check physical sanity:** $0 < \eta_{blade} < 1$, $0 \leq R \leq 1$, $C_2 < C_1$ for impulse.
6. **Use the sine rule correctly:** Angle opposite to $C_1$ is $\beta_1$, not $\alpha_1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condition for Maximum Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Condition for Maximum Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Steam Power Plant Engineering (OEMET727)**, the **Condition for Maximum Efficiency** refers to the specific thermodynamic operating parameters (boiler pressure, turbine inlet temperature, and condenser pressure) at which the **thermal efficiency (η_th)** of a steam power cycle (typically the Rankine cycle) attains its peak value for a given set of source and sink temperatures.

Mathematically, it is obtained by setting the **partial derivative of thermal efficiency with respect to the variable parameter (e.g., boiler pressure P_1)** equal to zero, subject to the constraints of fixed turbine inlet temperature (T_3) and fixed condenser pressure (P_2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The condition is most rigorously expressed as: *"The thermal efficiency of a Rankine cycle is maximum when the mean temperature of heat addition (T̄_add) approaches the maximum cycle temperature (T_max), and the mean temperature of heat rejection (T̄_rej) approaches the minimum cycle temperature (T_min)."* This is the engineering ideal of a **Carnot-equivalent cycle**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **brewing tea** to extract maximum flavor:
- The **water temperature (T_3)** is like the boiler steam temperature — hotter water extracts more "work" (flavor) per unit of energy.
- The **boiling intensity (P_1)** is like the boiler pressure — high pressure forces more energy transfer.
- The **cold milk you pour in at the end (T_1)** is like the condenser — the colder the rejection temperature, the less energy is "wasted."

**The golden rule:** To get the strongest tea (maximum efficiency), you must use the **hottest water**, the **strongest boil**, and the **coldest milk**, while keeping the steam in contact with the leaf for the **shortest, hottest duration** possible. In thermodynamic terms, this means: **maximize the average temperature at which heat is added** and **minimize the average temperature at which heat is rejected**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standards:**
> - **Carnot Efficiency Limit:** η_Carnot = 1 − T_min/T_max (theoretical absolute maximum)
> - **Standard Steam Conditions:** 540 °C / 200 bar (modern supercritical plants)
> - **Condenser Reference:** ~0.04 bar (≈28 °C saturation)

## 1.3 Why Maximum Efficiency Matters in Power Plants

A **1% improvement** in thermal efficiency of a 500 MW coal-fired plant saves approximately **15,000 tonnes of coal per year** and reduces **CO₂ emissions by ~37,500 tonnes/year**. Hence, every percentage point of efficiency is worth crores of rupees over the plant's lifetime. This is why the "condition for maximum efficiency" is a cornerstone of power plant design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Maximum Efficiency

For any heat engine operating between a source (T_H) and sink (T_L), the thermal efficiency is governed by the **Carnot relationship**:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$

where **T̄_add** and **T̄_rej** are the **mean (mass-averaged) temperatures of heat addition and heat rejection** respectively.

### Pillar 1: Maximize T̄_add (Mean Temperature of Heat Addition)
In a Rankine cycle, heat is added across two processes:
- **Feedwater heating** (compressed liquid from state 2 to saturation at state 3')
- **Evaporation + Superheating** (states 3' to 3)

The mean temperature of heat addition is the area under the heat addition curve on a T-s diagram divided by the entropy change.

### Pillar 2: Minimize T̄_rej (Mean Temperature of Heat Rejection)
Heat is rejected in the condenser at a temperature close to the saturation temperature corresponding to the condenser pressure. Lowering condenser pressure lowers T_rej, but excessive lowering raises **moisture content** in the low-pressure turbine, causing blade erosion.

## 2.2 Why Efficiency Has a Maximum (and not monotonic increase with pressure)

For a fixed turbine inlet temperature T_3, increasing boiler pressure P_1 has **two opposing effects**:

| Effect of ↑ P_1 | Resulting Change in η |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| ↑ Mean temperature of heat addition (T̄_add) | η ↑ (improves) |
| ↑ Moisture content in turbine exhaust | W_T ↓, η ↓ (degrades) |
| ↑ Saturation temperature in boiler | T̄_add ↑↑ (initially) |
| ↑ Latent heat of vaporization ↓ | Q_in changes |

> Initially, the first effect dominates (η increases), but beyond an optimum pressure P_opt, the second effect dominates, and η **decreases**. This is why an **optimum pressure exists** for maximum efficiency.

## 2.3 The Mathematical Condition

For a Rankine cycle with superheat, neglecting pump work (W_P ≈ 0):

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_1}$$

For an isentropic turbine expansion, s_4 = s_3.

Applying the condition for maximum efficiency:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \eta_{th}}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3, P_2} = 0$$

This yields the **governing condition** (derived fully in Section 3):

$$\frac{h_{f1} - h_1 + v_1(P_1 - P_2)}{s_{f1} - s_1} = T_{sat}(P_1)$$

In words: **The efficiency is maximum when the slope of the line joining state 1 and state 3' on a T-s diagram equals the slope of the constant-pressure line at the saturation point in the boiler.**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Universal thermal efficiency |
| 2 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{min}}{T_{max}}$ | Theoretical absolute maximum (K) |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | Rankine cycle efficiency (full) |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} \approx \dfrac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_1}$ | Rankine cycle (neglecting pump work) |
| 5 | $\bar{T}_{add} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s} = \dfrac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}$ | Mean temp. of heat addition |
| 6 | $\bar{T}_{rej} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{\Delta s} = \dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{s_4 - s_1}$ | Mean temp. of heat rejection |
| 7 | $h_2 - h_1 = v_1 (P_1 - P_2)$ | Pump work (incompressible liquid) |
| 8 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$ | Carnot-equivalent form |
| 9 | $T_3^{opt} = T_{sat}(P_1^{opt})$ | Optimum relation for max η |
| 10 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \eta}{\partial P_1}\right) = 0$ | Optimization condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Units:** All temperatures in Kelvin (K) for Carnot formula. Enthalpies in kJ/kg, pressures in bar or kPa, specific entropy in kJ/kg·K.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Condition for Maximum Efficiency" is the **design compass** for:
- **Supercritical & Ultra-Supercritical Boilers** (operating at 250–300 bar, 600–700 °C)
- **Combined Cycle Power Plants** (gas turbine TIT + steam bottoming cycle)
- **Cogeneration Plants** (optimizing for both power and process heat)
- **Thermal Power Plant Performance Audits** (CAMPS — Computer Aided Plant Performance Studies)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Boiler Pressure for Maximum Efficiency of an Ideal Rankine Cycle

**Given:**
- Simple Rankine cycle with superheat
- Fixed turbine inlet temperature: T_3 (constant)
- Fixed condenser pressure: P_2 (constant)
- Variable: Boiler pressure P_1

**Assumptions:**
- Isentropic turbine expansion: s_4 = s_3
- Isentropic pump compression: s_2 = s_1
- Steady-state, steady-flow operation
- Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes

### Step 1: State Point Definitions

| State | Description | Pressure | Temperature |
|-------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Saturated liquid at condenser inlet | P_2 | T_1 = T_sat(P_2) |
| 2 | Compressed liquid at pump exit | P_1 | T_2 (slight rise) |
| 3 | Superheated steam at turbine inlet | P_1 | T_3 (given, fixed) |
| 4 | Wet vapor at turbine exit | P_2 | T_4 = T_sat(P_2) |

### Step 2: Express Thermal Efficiency

Thermal efficiency of the Rankine cycle:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Pump Work Approximation

For a liquid (incompressible), the pump work is small:

$$h_2 - h_1 = v_1 (P_1 - P_2)$$

where v_1 is the specific volume of saturated liquid at T_1.

Substituting:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - v_1(P_1 - P_2)}{h_3 - h_2}$$

### Step 4: Identify the Variables

For an isentropic expansion in the turbine (s_4 = s_3):
- h_4 depends on P_1 and T_3 (via h_3 and s_3 = s_4 at P_2)
- h_3 depends on P_1 and T_3
- h_1 depends only on P_2 (saturated liquid, fixed)
- v_1 depends only on T_1 (fixed)

**Only P_1 is variable.**

### Step 5: Set Up the Optimization Condition

For maximum efficiency:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \eta_{th}}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3, P_2} = 0$$

### Step 6: Compute the Partial Derivatives

Let N = (h_3 − h_4) − v_1(P_1 − P_2) (numerator)
Let D = h_3 − h_2 (denominator)

Using the quotient rule:

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial P_1} = \frac{(\partial N / \partial P_1) \cdot D - N \cdot (\partial D / \partial P_1)}{D^2}$$

Since D > 0 (heat is added), the condition ∂η/∂P_1 = 0 implies:

$$\left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial P_1}\right) \cdot D = N \cdot \left(\frac{\partial D}{\partial P_1}\right)$$

### Step 7: Compute the Partial Derivatives of N and D

**For N:**
- ∂h_3/∂P_1 at constant T_3 (from superheated steam tables)
- ∂h_4/∂P_1 at constant T_3 (since s_4 = s_3, h_4 = h(P_2, s_3))
- ∂[v_1(P_1 − P_2)]/∂P_1 = v_1

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial P_1} = \left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} - \left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} - v_1$$

**For D:**
- ∂h_3/∂P_1 at constant T_3
- ∂h_2/∂P_1 = ∂[h_1 + v_1(P_1 − P_2)]/∂P_1 = v_1

$$\frac{\partial D}{\partial P_1} = \left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} - v_1$$

### Step 8: Use the Maxwell Relation from Thermodynamics

From the fundamental relation: dh = T ds + v dP

At constant entropy (s = constant):

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_s = v$$

Applying this at state 3 (isentropic expansion to state 4):

$$\left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{s_3} = v_3$$

But h_4 = h(P_2, s_3), so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = \left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial h_3}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3}$$

Since s_4 = s_3 (fixed s) and P_2 is fixed, h_4 is a function of h_3 alone. Differentiating h_4 with respect to h_3 along constant s_3:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial h_3}\right)_{s_3} = 1$$

(This is because the same isentropic line passes through both 3 and 4.)

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = \left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = v_3$$

### Step 9: Simplify the Derivatives

Substituting into the expressions:

$$\frac{\partial N}{\partial P_1} = v_3 - v_3 - v_1 = -v_1$$

$$\frac{\partial D}{\partial P_1} = v_3 - v_1$$

### Step 10: Apply the Maximum Condition

From the condition (∂N/∂P_1) · D = N · (∂D/∂P_1):

$$(-v_1)(h_3 - h_2) = [(h_3 - h_4) - v_1(P_1 - P_2)] \cdot (v_3 - v_1)$$

### Step 11: Rearrange and Interpret

Dividing throughout and rearranging:

$$v_1 = \left[\frac{(h_3 - h_4) - v_1(P_1 - P_2)}{(h_3 - h_2)}\right] \cdot (v_3 - v_1)$$

Recognizing the bracketed term as η_th (since pump work is included), and using N = η_th · D:

$$v_1 = \eta_{th}(v_3 - v_1)$$

Solving for η_th:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{v_1}{v_3 - v_1}$$

### Step 12: Final Result — The Condition for Maximum Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{th}^{max} = \frac{v_1}{v_3 - v_1}}$$

This is the **maximum efficiency** of the Rankine cycle, attained when the boiler pressure is at its optimum value. Note that this efficiency depends **only on the specific volumes** at the pump inlet and turbine inlet — a remarkably elegant result.

### Step 13: Equivalent T-s Diagrammatic Form

The same condition can be expressed graphically on a T-s diagram. The maximum efficiency occurs when:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_{P=P_1} \bigg|_{s = s_3} = \frac{T_3 - T_1}{s_3 - s_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The efficiency is maximum when the **tangent to the saturation curve at the boiler pressure (P_1) and entropy s_3** is parallel to the **straight line joining state 1 to state 3** on the T-s diagram. This means the average temperature of heat addition coincides with the saturation temperature at the optimum pressure.

## 3.2 Special Case: Neglecting Pump Work (h_2 ≈ h_1)

If we neglect pump work entirely (valid for low pressures), the condition simplifies to:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = 0 \implies \left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = \left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial P_1}\right)_{s}$$

Using dh = T ds + v dP at constant s:

$$v_3 = v_4$$

This gives the **approximate condition**: at maximum efficiency, the specific volume of steam at turbine inlet equals the specific volume at turbine exit (for the isentropic case). This is a useful engineering approximation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Cycle Parameters on Efficiency

| Parameter | Direction of Change | Effect on η_th | Practical Limit |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------:|-----------------|
| ↑ Boiler Pressure (P_1) | Initial ↑ then ↓ | Maximum at P_opt | Metallurgical limit of boiler tubes |
| ↑ Turbine Inlet Temp (T_3) | Monotonic ↑ | Always ↑ | Creep limit of turbine blades (~620 °C) |
| ↓ Condenser Pressure (P_2) | Monotonic ↑ | Always ↑ | Cooling water temp (T_min) |
| ↑ Feedwater Heating | ↑ | ↑ (up to ~15%) | Pinch point in heater |
| ↑ Reheat Pressure | Has optimum | Maximum at P_opt_rh | Turbine metallurgy |
| ↑ No. of Reheat Stages | ↑ (diminishing) | ↑ (each stage ~2–4%) | Economic limit (~2–3 stages) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rankine Cycle Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid

    turbine -->|Exhaust 4| cond

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: State and explain the condition for maximum efficiency of a steam power plant cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The condition for maximum efficiency of a Rankine cycle (with fixed turbine inlet temperature T_3 and fixed condenser pressure P_2) is obtained by setting the partial derivative of thermal efficiency with respect to boiler pressure equal to zero:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \eta_{th}}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3, P_2} = 0$$

This yields the condition that the **mean temperature of heat addition (T̄_add) must be as close to the maximum cycle temperature (T_3) as possible**, while the **mean temperature of heat rejection (T̄_rej) must be as close to the minimum cycle temperature (T_1) as possible**.

Geometrically, on a T-s diagram, the condition states that the **slope of the isobar at the boiler pressure equals the slope of the line joining state 1 to state 3**. Mathematically, this corresponds to:

$$\eta_{th}^{max} = \frac{v_1}{v_3 - v_1}$$

(where v_1 is the specific volume at pump inlet and v_3 is the specific volume at turbine inlet).

The efficiency is thus bounded by the **Carnot efficiency** between the mean temperatures of heat addition and rejection, and can be improved by **superheating, reheating, regenerative feedwater heating, and operating at supercritical pressures**.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Why does the thermal efficiency of a Rankine cycle exhibit a maximum (rather than a monotonic increase) with increasing boiler pressure? Explain in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

As boiler pressure P_1 increases at constant turbine inlet temperature T_3:

1. **Initial Effect (Beneficial):** The mean temperature of heat addition (T̄_add) increases because a larger portion of heat is added at higher temperatures. This **increases** thermal efficiency.

2. **Opposing Effect (Detrimental):** Higher boiler pressure reduces the specific enthalpy drop available in the turbine (h_3 − h_4) due to:
   - Increased moisture content in the exhaust steam
   - Lower latent heat of vaporization at high pressures
   - Reduced specific volume v_3, which limits work output

3. **Optimum Point:** Initially, effect (1) dominates and efficiency rises. Beyond an **optimum pressure P_opt**, effect (2) dominates and efficiency **decreases**. Hence, a true maximum exists.

The condition is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \eta_{th}}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3, P_2} = 0 \quad \text{at} \quad P_1 = P_{opt}$$

This is why modern plants use **supercritical conditions** with **reheat** to push the optimum pressure higher and recover lost work from excessive moisture.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**Q: (a)** Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a steam power plant operating on an ideal Rankine cycle, with respect to boiler pressure. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa and 350 °C, and is condensed in the condenser at 10 kPa. Determine:
   (i) The pump work, turbine work, and heat supplied per kg of steam.
   (ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
   (iii) The maximum possible (Carnot) efficiency between the same temperature limits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Derivation of the Condition for Maximum Efficiency (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state, steady-flow operation.
2. Isentropic processes in turbine and pump.
3. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
4. Turbine inlet temperature T_3 is constant; condenser pressure P_2 is constant; boiler pressure P_1 is the variable.
5. Steam behaves as an ideal substance (steam tables used).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Consider the Rankine cycle with the following states:
- State 1: Saturated liquid at condenser pressure P_2
- State 2: Compressed liquid at boiler pressure P_1 (after pump)
- State 3: Superheated steam at P_1 and T_3 (turbine inlet)
- State 4: Wet vapor at P_2 (after isentropic expansion)

Thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - v_1(P_1 - P_2)}{h_3 - h_2}$$

The pump work is small, so neglecting it (or treating it separately):

$$\eta_{th} \approx \frac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_1} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

For an isentropic turbine (s_4 = s_3) and s_2 ≈ s_1, applying the condition for maximum efficiency:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \eta_{th}}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3, P_2} = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

From the fundamental relation dh = T ds + v dP, at constant s:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_s = v \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Differentiating equation (i) with respect to P_1 at constant T_3 and P_2, and applying (iii) along the isentropic line from 3 to 4:

$$\left(\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = \left(\frac{\partial h_4}{\partial P_1}\right)_{T_3} = v_3$$

This gives the simplified condition:

$$\eta_{th}^{max} = \frac{v_1}{v_3 - v_1} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Equivalent geometric form:** On a T-s diagram, the maximum efficiency occurs when the **tangent to the saturation dome at the optimum boiler pressure (point of intersection with line 1-3)** is parallel to the **straight line joining state 1 and state 3**.

This is the condition for maximum efficiency.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- P_1 = 3 MPa, T_3 = 350 °C
- P_2 = 10 kPa

**Step 1: Determine enthalpies at each state (2 marks)**

**State 1: Saturated liquid at P_2 = 10 kPa**
- T_1 = T_sat(10 kPa) = 45.81 °C
- h_1 = h_f @ 10 kPa = 191.81 kJ/kg
- v_1 = v_f @ 10 kPa = 0.001010 m³/kg
- s_1 = s_f @ 10 kPa = 0.6492 kJ/kg·K

**State 2: After isentropic pump to P_1 = 3 MPa**
- Pump work: w_P = v_1 (P_1 − P_2)
- w_P = 0.001010 × (3000 − 10) = 0.001010 × 2990
- w_P = 3.020 kJ/kg
- h_2 = h_1 + w_P = 191.81 + 3.020 = **194.83 kJ/kg**

**State 3: Superheated steam at 3 MPa, 350 °C** (from superheated steam tables)
- h_3 = 3115.3 kJ/kg
- s_3 = 6.7428 kJ/kg·K

**State 4: After isentropic expansion to 10 kPa (s_4 = s_3 = 6.7428 kJ/kg·K)**

At P_2 = 10 kPa:
- s_f = 0.6492 kJ/kg·K
- s_fg = 7.5010 kJ/kg·K
- s_g = 8.1502 kJ/kg·K
- h_f = 191.81 kJ/kg
- h_fg = 2392.8 kJ/kg
- h_g = 2584.6 kJ/kg

Quality of steam at state 4:

$$x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6492}{7.5010} = \frac{6.0936}{7.5010} = 0.8124$$

Enthalpy at state 4:

$$h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8124 \times 2392.8 = 191.81 + 1943.91 = 2135.72 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2: Compute work and heat terms (2 marks)**

**Pump work:**
$$W_P = h_2 - h_1 = 194.83 - 191.81 = 3.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Turbine work:**
$$W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3115.3 - 2135.72 = 979.58 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Net work:**
$$W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 979.58 - 3.02 = 976.56 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Heat supplied:**
$$Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3115.3 - 194.83 = 2920.47 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: Compute thermal efficiency (1.5 marks)**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{976.56}{2920.47} = 0.3344 = 33.44\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Carnot efficiency (1.5 marks)**

- T_max = 350 + 273 = 623 K
- T_min = 45.81 + 273 = 318.81 K

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{min}}{T_{max}} = 1 - \frac{318.81}{623} = 1 - 0.5117 = 0.4883 = 48.83\%$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Pump Work, W_P | 3.02 kJ/kg |
| Turbine Work, W_T | 979.58 kJ/kg |
| Net Work, W_net | 976.56 kJ/kg |
| Heat Supplied, Q_in | 2920.47 kJ/kg |
| Rankine Efficiency | **33.44%** |
| Carnot Efficiency | **48.83%** |
| Relative Efficiency (η/η_Carnot) | 68.5% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students frequently forget to **convert temperatures to Kelvin** when computing Carnot efficiency, leading to wildly incorrect values (e.g., 33% instead of 49%). **Always use absolute temperatures (K) in Carnot's formula.**
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to compute the quality x_4 from entropy, leading to an **incorrect h_4** (using s_g instead of computing x_4). This error can shift the efficiency by ±3%.
>
> **Pitfall #3:** Mixing up states — state 1 is at the **condenser**, not the boiler. State 3 is at the **turbine inlet**, not the turbine exhaust.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat T-s diagram why the mean temperature of heat addition must be maximized and the mean temperature of heat rejection must be minimized to obtain the maximum efficiency of a Rankine cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam power plant operates with an ideal reheat cycle. Steam enters the high-pressure turbine at 15 MPa and 600 °C. After expansion to 3 MPa, it is reheated to 600 °C and then expanded in the low-pressure turbine to 10 kPa. Determine:
   (i) The quality of steam at the exit of each turbine stage.
   (ii) The total turbine work output per kg of steam.
   (iii) The thermal efficiency, and compare it with the Carnot efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution Outline (For Self-Study):

### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)

**Key Points to Cover:**

1. **Definition of Mean Temperature:**
   The mean temperature of heat addition is defined as:
   
   $$\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{Q_{in}}{\Delta s}$$
   
   It represents the equivalent constant temperature at which heat Q_in would have to be added to produce the same entropy change Δs.

2. **Carnot Relation:**
   For any reversible cycle:
   
   $$\eta = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$

3. **T-s Diagram Argument:**
   - Draw the saturation dome, isobars, and the Rankine cycle.
   - Show that T̄_add is the height of the rectangle on the T-s diagram having area equal to the area under the heat addition curve.
   - To maximize T̄_add, push the heat addition curve **upward and to the right** (higher T_3, higher P_1, superheat).
   - To minimize T̄_rej, lower the condenser pressure (T_1 decreases).

4. **Carnot Limit:**
   The maximum possible efficiency is when T̄_add = T_max and T̄_rej = T_min, giving the Carnot cycle. Practical Rankine cycles approach this with reheat, regeneration, and supercritical conditions.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution Outline (7 marks)

**Key Steam States:**

| State | P (MPa) | T (°C) | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) |
|-------|---------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| 3 (HPT inlet) | 15 | 600 | ~3583 | ~6.679 |
| a (HPT exit / RH inlet) | 3 | — | (compute x_a) | s_a = s_3 |
| b (RH exit / LPT inlet) | 3 | 600 | ~3683 | ~7.508 |
| 4 (LPT exit) | 0.01 | — | (compute x_4) | s_4 = s_b |

**Efficiency computation steps:**
- Compute isentropic expansion h_3 → h_a (use x_a from s_3 at 3 MPa)
- Compute isentropic expansion h_b → h_4 (use x_4 from s_b at 10 kPa)
- W_T = (h_3 − h_a) + (h_b − h_4)
- W_P = v_1(P_1 − P_2) ≈ 3 kJ/kg
- Q_in = (h_3 − h_2) + (h_b − h_a)
- η_th = (W_T − W_P) / Q_in ≈ 41–43%
- η_Carnot = 1 − (T_min / T_max) = 1 − 318.81/873 ≈ 63.5%

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Reheat Cycle Problems:**
> 1. **Reheat entropy trap:** After reheat, s_b > s_3 (entropy increases due to heat addition in reheater). Students often erroneously set s_b = s_3, which is wrong.
> 2. **Moisture content:** Forgetting to check x_4 > 0.88 (KTU board specifically asks for moisture limits).
> 3. **Double counting work:** Computing h_b − h_a as part of work, when it is actually part of Q_in (heat supplied in reheater).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Condition for Maximum Efficiency**

### 🎯 Core Concepts
- [x] The thermal efficiency of a Rankine cycle is **maximum** at an **optimum boiler pressure** (P_opt), not at the highest possible pressure.
- [x] Maximum efficiency is achieved when **T̄_add → T_max** and **T̄_rej → T_min** (Carnot limit).
- [x] The mathematical condition is: **(∂η_th/∂P_1) = 0** at constant T_3 and P_2.
- [x] The result: **η_th^max = v_1 / (v_3 − v_1)**.
- [x] The equivalent T-s condition: slope of isobar at P_1 = slope of line 1-3.

### 🎯 Key Parameters Affecting Maximum Efficiency
- [x] ↑ **Boiler pressure** → increases η (up to P_opt) then decreases η.
- [x] ↑ **Turbine inlet temperature (T_3)** → always increases η (limited by metallurgy).
- [x] ↓ **Condenser pressure** → always increases η (limited by cooling water T).
- [x] **Superheat** → raises T̄_add, increases η.
- [x] **Reheat** → raises T̄_add, increases η, reduces exhaust moisture.
