- ✅ **Centrifugal force on fly-ball:** $F_c = \dfrac{m \pi^2 N^2 r}{900}$ N.
- ✅ **Throttling loss = reduction in available heat drop** (isenthalpic, $\Delta s > 0$).
- ✅ **Governor types:** Watt (simple, used for low speeds) → Hartnell (precise, compressed spring, for high speeds) → Porter (high-speed variant).
- ✅ **Hunting:** Continuous oscillation around the setpoint; solved by **dashpot** / damper.
- ✅ **Indian Grid Code:** Droop setting of **3% to 6%** is mandatory for all grid-connected units.
- ✅ **Isochronous governor:** Speed remains *exactly* constant; used in special applications (parallel operation with load sharing).
- ✅ **Governor linkage** → **Servo-motor** (hydraulic amplifier) → **Throttle valve** is the universal control chain.
- ✅ **Nozzle control at full load = throttle governing performance** (when all groups are open).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Examiner Emphasis:** Numerical problems on droop & sensitivity are **frequently asked** (3–4 marks inside a 14-mark question).

---
*End of Notes — Governing of Steam Turbines | OEMET727 Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cogeneration and Combined Cycle Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cogeneration and Combined Cycle Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power – CHP)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cogeneration (CHP)** is the sequential or simultaneous generation of two useful forms of energy (mechanical/electrical energy and thermal/process heat energy) from a single primary fuel source in an integrated system, achieving significantly higher overall fuel utilization efficiency compared to separate heat-only and power-only systems.

Mathematically, the principle is expressed through the **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)**:

$$\text{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{useful}}{\text{Heat Input from Fuel}}$$

where:
- $W_{net}$ = Net electrical/mechanical work output (kW)
- $Q_{useful}$ = Useful thermal energy recovered (kW)
- Heat input is measured at the LHV (Lower Heating Value) of the fuel.

A standalone Rankine power plant has an efficiency of only **30%–40%**, while the remaining **60%–70%** of fuel energy is wasted as low-grade condenser heat. Cogeneration recovers this waste for industrial heating, district heating, or process steam, pushing the overall fuel utilization to **70%–90%**.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Multi-Tasking Stove:** Imagine cooking rice on a gas stove. A normal stove burns LPG, makes rice, and the rest of the flame heat simply warms the kitchen uselessly. A *cogeneration stove* would route the hot exhaust gases around a small boiler to simultaneously heat water for a bath *while* cooking rice — same fuel, two useful outputs. The gas turbine exhaust in a power plant is the "extra flame heat," and the heat recovery boiler is the "bath water heater."

---

## 1.2 Types of Cogeneration Cycles

### A. Topping Cycle Cogeneration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **Topping Cycle**, the fuel is first used to generate **high-grade energy** (electricity in a gas turbine or high-pressure steam turbine), and the *waste heat* from that process is then recovered at a lower temperature to supply process thermal energy.

**Typical sequence:** Fuel → Gas/Steam Turbine (Electricity) → Waste Heat → Boiler Feed or Process Steam

**Industries:** Sugar mills, paper mills (with low-pressure steam requirement around 1.5–3 bar), textile processing.

### B. Bottoming Cycle Cogeneration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **Bottoming Cycle**, fuel is first used to supply **high-temperature process heat** (e.g., for a furnace or kiln), and the *residual heat in the flue gases* is then routed to a waste heat boiler to generate electricity via a steam turbine.

**Typical sequence:** Fuel → High-Temperature Furnace/Kiln → Waste Heat Boiler → Steam Turbine → Electricity

**Industries:** Steel plants (blast furnaces), cement rotary kilns, glass melting furnaces, metallurgical smelters.

### C. Combined / Parallel Cogeneration

Used when the process heat demand closely matches the back-pressure or extraction steam flow. The turbine can be **back-pressure**, **extraction-condensing**, or **pass-out type**.

---

## 1.3 Combined Cycle Power Generation (CCPG)

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Cycle Power Generation** is the integration of two (or more) thermodynamic cycles where the waste heat rejected by the *topping cycle* (typically a Brayton/Gas Turbine cycle) is utilized as the heat input for the *bottoming cycle* (typically a Rankine/Steam Turbine cycle), thereby maximizing the overall thermal efficiency of the plant.

The two cycles operate at different temperature ranges:

| Cycle | Working Fluid | Heat Addition Source | Heat Rejection Sink |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topping — Brayton | Air + Combustion Gases | Fuel Combustion ($1500^\circ C$ turbine inlet) | Exhaust at $500$–$600^\circ C$ |
| Bottoming — Rankine | Steam | Gas Turbine Exhaust via **HRSG** | Condenser at $30$–$40^\circ C$ |

The HRSG (**Heat Recovery Steam Generator**) is the heart of the combined cycle plant — it converts hot gas turbine exhaust into superheated steam without any additional fuel consumption.

---

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hybrid Car Engine:** A traditional car burns petrol and wastes ~70% of the fuel's energy as exhaust heat and coolant loss. A *combined cycle power plant* is the engineering equivalent of a hybrid car: the gas turbine is the petrol engine (high power density), and the steam turbine is the electric motor (recovers otherwise wasted energy). Together, they squeeze out maximum useful work from the same tank of fuel.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three metrics dominate KTU board examinations on this topic:
> 1. **Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)** — measures total useful energy (electrical + thermal).
> 2. **Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)** — $Q_{u}/W_{net}$ — used to size the system against the process heat demand.
> 3. **Overall Combined Cycle Efficiency** — $\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} - 1$ (without supplementary firing).

A modern combined cycle plant achieves **55%–62% efficiency**, compared to **35%–40%** for a standalone Rankine or **38%–42%** for a standalone Brayton plant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cogeneration Thermodynamics — Energy Analysis

In any cogeneration scheme, the first law analysis splits the fuel input $Q_f$ into three streams:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_f &= W_{net} + Q_{useful} + Q_{rejected}
\end{aligned}$$

The **fuel energy NOT converted to useful work or useful heat is the rejected loss** — through flue gas, condenser, radiation, and blowdown.

### 2.1.1 Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{u}}{Q_{in}}$$

A conventional Rankine plant using all fuel for electricity:

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} \approx 0.35$$

A cogeneration plant:

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_{u}}{Q_{in}} \approx 0.80 \text{ to } 0.90$$

### 2.1.2 Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_{u}}{W_{net}}$$

- **Topping cycle (industrial process):** HPR typically ranges from **1 to 10** depending on whether the application is electricity-dominant or heat-dominant.
- A **back-pressure turbine** inherently has a fixed HPR (since $Q_u$ = exhaust steam enthalpy $\times$ mass flow).

### 2.1.3 Cogeneration Efficiency (Electrical-only Basis)

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in} - Q_{u}}$$

This tells us: *of the fuel that was NOT used for useful heat, what fraction became electricity?*

---

## 2.2 Combined Cycle — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Gas Turbine (Brayton) Cycle Exhaust Energy

The exhaust gas leaves the gas turbine at temperature $T_{ex}$ (typically $500^\circ C$ to $600^\circ C$) and contains enormous recoverable heat.

The recoverable heat from gas turbine exhaust (per kg of exhaust gas):

$$Q_{ex} = m_{ex} \cdot c_{p,ex} \cdot (T_{ex} - T_{stack})$$

where:
- $m_{ex}$ = exhaust mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $c_{p,ex}$ = specific heat of exhaust gas $\approx \mathbf{1.15 \text{ kJ/kg·K}}$
- $T_{ex}$ = exhaust temperature at HRSG inlet (K)
- $T_{stack}$ = minimum permissible stack temperature (typically $95^\circ C$ to $120^\circ C$)

### Step 2: Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) Operation

> [!NOTE]
> The HRSG contains **Economizer → Evaporator → Superheater**, and may include a reheat section. Pinch point analysis determines the approach temperature difference ($\Delta T_{pinch}$, typically $10$–$20^\circ C$).

Heat transferred to steam:

$$Q_{HRSG} = m_{ex} \cdot c_{p,ex} \cdot (T_{ex} - T_{stack})$$

### Step 3: Steam Cycle (Rankine) Work Output

Steam generated in HRSG flows through a multi-stage steam turbine. Net specific work:

$$w_{st} = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)$$

(for reheat) — Standard Rankine cycle analysis.

### Step 4: Overall Combined Cycle Efficiency

The **elegant cascade formula** (without supplementary firing):

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} - (\eta_{GT} \times \eta_{ST})$$

Or equivalently:

$$\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{GT})(1 - \eta_{ST})$$

With **supplementary firing** (burning extra fuel in HRSG):

$$\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT} + W_{ST}}{Q_{fuel,GT} + Q_{fuel,supp}}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU exam questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Utilization Factor | $\eta_{EUF} = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{u}}{Q_{in}}$ | $W_{net}$ (kW), $Q_u$ (kW), $Q_{in}$ (kW) | Very High |
| 2 | Heat-to-Power Ratio | $\text{HPR} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless | Very High |
| 3 | Cogeneration (electrical-only) Efficiency | $\eta_{e} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in} - Q_{u}}$ | — | High |
| 4 | Combined Cycle Efficiency (no supplemental firing) | $\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{GT})(1 - \eta_{ST})$ | — | Very High |
| 5 | Combined Cycle Efficiency (with supplemental firing) | $\eta_{CC} = \dfrac{W_{GT} + W_{ST}}{Q_{GT,fuel} + Q_{supp,fuel}}$ | — | High |
| 6 | Gas Turbine Exhaust Heat | $Q_{ex} = m_{ex} \cdot c_{p,ex} \cdot (T_{ex} - T_{stack})$ | $m_{ex}$ (kg/s), $c_{p,ex}$ (kJ/kg·K), $T$ (K) | High |
| 7 | Steam mass flow from HRSG | $m_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{HRSG}}{h_{steam,out} - h_{feedwater,in}}$ | $m_s$ (kg/s) | High |
| 8 | Cogeneration Fuel Savings | $\text{Savings} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{EUF}} - \dfrac{1}{\eta_{sep,th} + \eta_{sep,el}}$ | — | Medium |
| 9 | Topping Cycle Range | $T_{process} < 0.5 \cdot T_{top,max}$ | — | Low |
| 10 | Utilization Factor (Total Useful Energy Basis) | $\epsilon = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_{u}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV}$ | LHV in kJ/kg | High |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sugar Industry (India, Brazil):** Bagasse-fired cogeneration plants produce process steam for crystallization and electricity for grid export — top performers exceed **80% EUF**.
- **District Heating (Scandinavia, Northern Europe):** Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants supply hot water to entire cities via insulated underground pipelines — Helsinki, Copenhagen, Vienna.
- **Combined Cycle Megaplants (Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE):** The **Ras Laffan CCGT plant** delivers **>60% efficiency** — the current world benchmark for large-scale power generation.
- **Paper & Pulp Mills:** Black liquor recovery boilers paired with back-pressure steam turbines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU/Kerala industry:** The Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) actively promotes cogeneration in coconut-based and coir industries, and the **NTPC Kayamkulam Combined Cycle Plant** (359.58 MW) is a landmark KTU-relevant regional installation.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Cycle Efficiency Formula (Without Supplementary Firing)

**Starting Premise:** A combined cycle plant has two cycles. Each takes a portion of the fuel energy and rejects a portion as waste. The waste of the topping cycle becomes the input of the bottoming cycle.

### Step 1: Define Gas Turbine Efficiency

For the Brayton (gas turbine) cycle alone:

$$\eta_{GT} = \frac{W_{GT}}{Q_{GT,fuel}}$$

Therefore the rejected (waste) heat fraction:

$$Q_{GT,rej} = Q_{GT,fuel} - W_{GT} = Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT})$$

### Step 2: HRSG Captures the Rejected Heat

In a combined cycle, the waste heat from the GT exhaust is fed to the HRSG as the heat input for the steam cycle. So:

$$Q_{ST,in} = Q_{GT,rej} = Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT})$$

### Step 3: Steam Turbine Output

The steam turbine converts this input to work:

$$W_{ST} = \eta_{ST} \cdot Q_{ST,in} = \eta_{ST} \cdot Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT})$$

### Step 4: Total Plant Output

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= W_{GT} + W_{ST} \\
&= \eta_{GT} \cdot Q_{GT,fuel} + \eta_{ST} \cdot Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT}) \\
&= Q_{GT,fuel} \cdot [\eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST}(1 - \eta_{GT})]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Overall Combined Cycle Efficiency

Since the *only* fuel input to the entire plant is $Q_{GT,fuel}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{CC} &= \frac{W_{total}}{Q_{GT,fuel}} \\
&= \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST}(1 - \eta_{GT}) \\
&= \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{GT}\eta_{ST}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{GT}\,\eta_{ST} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{GT})(1 - \eta_{ST})}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Highly Probable for KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem Statement:**
A cogeneration plant supplies **3000 kW** of electrical power and **4500 kW** of process heat. The fuel input rate is **10,000 kW** (based on LHV of fuel). Compute:
(a) Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)
(b) Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)
(c) Electrical efficiency based on net fuel input (excluding useful heat)
(d) Percentage fuel savings over a separate heat-only boiler ($\eta_{boiler}=0.85$) and separate electricity generation ($\eta_{el}=0.35$).

### Part (a): Energy Utilization Factor

Given:
- $W_{net} = 3000$ kW
- $Q_u = 4500$ kW
- $Q_{in} = 10000$ kW

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_u}{Q_{in}} = \frac{3000 + 4500}{10000}$$

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{7500}{10000} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

### Part (b): Heat-to-Power Ratio

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_u}{W_{net}} = \frac{4500}{3000} = 1.5$$

This means for every 1 kW of electricity, 1.5 kW of process heat is produced — typical for a paper/pulp or sugar process.

### Part (c): Electrical Efficiency (on net fuel)

$$\eta_{elec} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in} - Q_u} = \frac{3000}{10000 - 4500} = \frac{3000}{5500}$$

$$\eta_{elec} = 0.5455 = 54.55\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the *plant* uses 10,000 kW of fuel, only 5,500 kW of it was "available" for electrical work (the rest went to process heat). Compared to that effective input, the plant produced electricity at 54.55% efficiency — a respectable figure for an extraction/back-pressure unit.

### Part (d): Fuel Savings over Separate Generation

**Fuel required for separate systems:**

For electricity:

$$F_{el} = \frac{W_{net}}{\eta_{el}} = \frac{3000}{0.35} = 8571.43 \text{ kW of fuel}$$

For process heat:

$$F_{th} = \frac{Q_u}{\eta_{boiler}} = \frac{4500}{0.85} = 5294.12 \text{ kW of fuel}$$

Total separate fuel:

$$F_{sep} = 8571.43 + 5294.12 = 13865.55 \text{ kW}$$

Cogeneration fuel:

$$F_{cogen} = 10000 \text{ kW}$$

**Fuel Savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{F_{sep} - F_{cogen}}{F_{sep}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{13865.55 - 10000}{13865.55} \times 100\% = \frac{3865.55}{13865.55} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Fuel Savings} \approx 27.88\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Combined Cycle Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A combined cycle power plant has:
- Gas Turbine (Brayton) electrical output: **120 MW**
- Steam Turbine (Rankine) electrical output: **80 MW**
- Gas Turbine fuel input: **300 MW (LHV basis)**

Compute:
(a) Combined cycle efficiency (no supplementary firing)
(b) Steam turbine efficiency if the gas turbine efficiency is **40%**

### Part (a): Combined Cycle Efficiency

$$\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{GT} + W_{ST}}{Q_{GT,fuel}} = \frac{120 + 80}{300} = \frac{200}{300}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Check:** A 66.67% combined cycle efficiency is high but achievable in modern F-class and H-class turbines (GE 9HA, Siemens HL-class, MHPS J-series). Most practical installations report **55%–62%**.

### Part (b): Back-Calculating Steam Turbine Efficiency

Given $\eta_{GT} = 40\% = 0.40$:

$$\eta_{GT} = \frac{W_{GT}}{Q_{GT,fuel}} = \frac{120}{300} = 0.40 \checkmark$$

GT exhaust heat input to HRSG:

$$Q_{ST,in} = Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT}) = 300 \times (1 - 0.40) = 180 \text{ MW}$$

Steam turbine efficiency:

$$\eta_{ST} = \frac{W_{ST}}{Q_{ST,in}} = \frac{80}{180} = 0.4444$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ST} = 44.44\%}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Cogeneration vs. Combined Cycle)

| Parameter | Cogeneration | Combined Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Generate electricity + useful thermal energy | Maximize electricity from same fuel |
| **Typical Topping Cycle** | Back-pressure steam turbine / Gas turbine | Gas turbine (Brayton) |
| **Typical Bottoming Cycle** | Process heat boiler / Steam cycle | HRSG + Steam Turbine |
| **Useful Outputs** | Electricity AND Process Heat (dual) | Only Electricity |
| **Energy Utilization Factor** | 70%–90% | 55%–62% (electrical only) |
| **Application** | Industries (sugar, paper, refineries), District Heating | Utility power generation |
| **Heat Sink** | Industrial process / Heating load | Condenser (waste) |
| **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus** | High (defines the technology) | High (defines the optimization) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction for KTU Exams:** Cogeneration values *heat* as a useful output; combined cycle treats *all* waste heat as unrecovered. A **cogeneration combined cycle** (with district heating from condenser cooling water) is the ultimate synergy, but is not commonly tested at Module 2 level.

---

## 3.5 Pinch Point Analysis (HRSG — Supplementary Concept)

The pinch point $\Delta T_{pinch}$ is the minimum temperature difference between the hot exhaust gas and the working steam/water at the HRSG evaporator's saturation zone:

$$\Delta T_{pinch} = T_{ex,sat} - T_{steam,sat}$$

- Lower $\Delta T_{pinch}$ → higher steam generation, but larger HRSG.
- Typical $\Delta T_{pinch} = 10^\circ C$ to $20^\circ C$.
- This affects steam turbine inlet conditions and thus $\eta_{ST}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The pinch point directly controls steam mass flow $m_s$ generated per kg of exhaust gas. KTU problems occasionally ask students to compute $m_s$ given exhaust conditions and steam conditions.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cogeneration System — Topping Cycle (Steam-Turbine Based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Coal or Biomass] --> B[High Pressure Boiler]
    B --> C[Superheated Steam 500 to 540 deg C]
    C --> D[Back Pressure Steam Turbine]
    D --> E[Exhaust Steam 1.5 to 4 bar for Process]
    D --> F[Generator Electrical Output]
    E --> G[Industrial Process Heat or District Heating]
    B --> H[Flue Gas to Chimney with Heat Recovery]
    H --> I[Economizer Preheats Feedwater]

    style A fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Fuel combusts in the HP boiler, generates superheated steam which expands through a **back-pressure turbine** (no condenser). The exhaust steam directly enters the industrial process. The generator taps the shaft for electricity. This is the **classic KTU-asked topping cycle**.

---

## 4.2 Cogeneration System — Bottoming Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Temperature Process Furnace 1000 to 1500 deg C] --> B[Process Output Steel or Cement]
    A --> C[Hot Flue Gas 600 to 900 deg C]
    C --> D[Waste Heat Recovery Boiler WHRB]
    D --> E[Steam Generation]
    E --> F[Steam Turbine with Condenser]
    F --> G[Condensate Return Pump]
    F --> H[Generator Electrical Output]
    D --> I[Cooled Flue Gas to Stack 130 to 150 deg C]

    style A fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Fuel first produces *high-temperature process heat* (e.g., melting scrap in a steel plant or heating a cement kiln). The flue gas, still very hot, is routed through a **Waste Heat Recovery Boiler (WHRB)** to generate steam that drives a turbine-generator set. This is **bottoming cogeneration**.

---

## 4.3 Combined Cycle Power Plant (Gas Turbine + Steam Turbine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air] --> B[Air Compressor]
    B --> C[High Pressure Air 15 to 30 bar]
    C --> D[Combustion Chamber]
    E[Fuel Natural Gas or HSD] --> D
    D --> F[Hot Combustion Gas 1500 deg C]
    F --> G[Gas Turbine Expander]
    G --> H[Generator 1 Electrical Output]
    G --> I[Exhaust Gas 500 to 600 deg C]
    I --> J[Heat Recovery Steam Generator HRSG]
    K[Feedwater Pump] --> J
    J --> L[Superheated Steam 540 deg C, 90 bar]
    L --> M[Steam Turbine]
    M --> N[Generator 2 Electrical Output]
    M --> O[Condenser at 30 to 40 deg C]
    O --> P[Cooling Water Loop]
    O --> K
    I --> Q[Stack Gas 95 to 120 deg C]

    style A fill:#cff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#fbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Brayton loop** (blue/green nodes) and the **Rankine loop** (yellow/purple nodes) are thermally coupled only at the HRSG — there is no direct mixing of working fluids. This is the **integrated gasification combined cycle's downstream architecture**, applied in natural gas CCGT plants worldwide.

---

## 4.4 Energy Flow Sankey — Cogeneration vs Separate Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SeparateGeneration["Separate Generation 13865 kW fuel input"]
        A1[Fuel 13865 kW] --> B1[Power Plant eta 0.35]
        A1 --> C1[Boiler eta 0.85]
        B1 --> D1[Electricity 3000 kW]
        B1 --> E1[Condenser Loss 5571 kW]
        C1 --> F1[Process Steam 4500 kW]
        C1 --> G1[Stack Loss 794 kW]
    end

    subgraph Cogeneration["Cogeneration 10000 kW fuel input"]
        A2[Fuel 10000 kW] --> B2[CHP Plant eta EUF 0.75]
        B2 --> C2[Electricity 3000 kW]
        B2 --> D2[Process Heat 4500 kW]
        B2 --> E2[Rejection Loss 2500 kW]
    end

    style SeparateGeneration fill:#fee,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style Cogeneration fill:#efe,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#faa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A2 fill:#afa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Same useful outputs (3000 kW electricity + 4500 kW heat), but **cogeneration requires ~27.88% less fuel** (as computed in Section 3.2). This is the single most important advantage KTU examiners test.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Supplementary Firing in HRSG

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas Turbine Exhaust 540 deg C] --> B[Supplementary Firing Duct Burner]
    C[Additional Fuel Optional] --> B
    B --> D[Elevated Exhaust Temp up to 700 deg C]
    D --> E[HRSG with Higher Steam Output]
    E --> F[Steam Turbine Higher Capacity]
    F --> G[Generator]
    E --> H[Stack]

    style A fill:#ffd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#bde,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** When more steam is needed than the GT exhaust can provide, a **duct burner** adds fuel *upstream* of the HRSG. This **lowers** overall plant efficiency (because the new fuel is at relatively low exhaust temperature) but **increases** total output — a strategic trade-off during peak demand.
