# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define cogeneration. Differentiate between topping and bottoming cycles with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Cogeneration is the simultaneous production of electrical (or mechanical) energy and useful thermal energy from a single fuel source in an integrated system.

- **Topping Cycle:** Electricity is generated first; waste heat is then recovered for process use. *Example:* Natural gas-fired gas turbine in a paper mill, with exhaust heat producing process steam.
- **Bottoming Cycle:** High-temperature process heat is generated first; residual flue gas heat is then used in a waste heat boiler for electricity. *Example:* Steel plant blast furnace flue gas powering a WHRB and steam turbine.

**[3 marks breakdown: Definition 1 mark + Topping 1 mark + Bottoming 1 mark]**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a combined cycle power plant? Write the formula for combined cycle efficiency in the absence of supplementary firing.**

**Model Answer:**
A combined cycle power plant integrates two thermodynamic cycles — typically a Brayton (gas turbine) cycle as the topping cycle and a Rankine (steam turbine) cycle as the bottoming cycle. The waste heat from the gas turbine exhaust is recovered by a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) to generate steam, which drives the steam turbine.

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{GT}\eta_{ST} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{GT})(1 - \eta_{ST})$$

**[3 marks breakdown: Definition 2 marks + Formula 1 mark]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – Past Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a combined cycle power plant using a gas turbine (Brayton cycle) as the topping cycle and a steam turbine (Rankine cycle) as the bottoming cycle. What is the role of the HRSG in such a plant?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A combined cycle power plant has a gas turbine efficiency of 38% and a steam turbine efficiency (based on heat input to HRSG) of 42%. The fuel input to the gas turbine combustor is 500 MW (LHV basis). Determine:
1. The combined cycle efficiency.
2. The electrical output of the gas turbine.
3. The electrical output of the steam turbine.
4. The total plant output.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:**

Refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.3** above. Key narrative points to write:

1. **Air Circuit:** Atmospheric air is drawn into the axial/radial flow compressor, compressed to high pressure (15–30 bar), and sent to the combustion chamber.

2. **Combustion Chamber:** Fuel (natural gas, HSD, or syngas) is burned continuously in the combustion chamber, raising the air temperature to approximately 1500°C. This is the highest temperature in the cycle.

3. **Gas Turbine:** The hot, high-pressure gas expands through the gas turbine, producing shaft work. A portion of this work drives the compressor (typically 50%–60% of gross output), while the remaining drives the **Generator 1**.

4. **Exhaust Heat Recovery:** Gas turbine exhaust at 500–600°C is routed through the **HRSG** (Heat Recovery Steam Generator), which contains economizer, evaporator, and superheater sections. This is **waste heat from cycle 1 becoming useful heat input for cycle 2**.

5. **Steam Circuit:** Feedwater enters the HRSG economizer, is heated to saturation, evaporated, and superheated. The superheated steam (typically 540°C, 90–100 bar) enters the steam turbine.

6. **Steam Turbine + Condenser:** Steam expands through multi-stage turbine, drives **Generator 2**, and is condensed in a surface condenser using cooling water. Condensate is pumped back to the HRSG as feedwater.

7. **HRSG Role:** The HRSG is the **thermal coupling device** between the two cycles. It transfers heat from the GT exhaust to the working fluid of the Rankine cycle without any additional fuel (in unsupplemented mode). HRSG efficiency is critical — pinch point analysis determines performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (a):** Many students forget to label the **compressor work consumption** in the gas turbine and incorrectly calculate net output as equal to gross. KTU expects you to state: "A portion of GT shaft work drives the compressor; only the *net* output goes to Generator 1." Also, **always label temperatures and pressures** — partial credit is lost without numerical anchors.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\eta_{GT} = 0.38$
- $\eta_{ST} = 0.42$
- $Q_{GT,fuel} = 500$ MW

**Step 1: Gas Turbine Output**

$$W_{GT} = \eta_{GT} \times Q_{GT,fuel} = 0.38 \times 500 = 190 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 2: Heat Available for HRSG (= GT Exhaust Heat)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{HRSG} &= Q_{GT,fuel} - W_{GT} \\
&= 500 - 190 = 310 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently: $Q_{HRSG} = Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT}) = 500 \times 0.62 = 310$ MW ✓

**Step 3: Steam Turbine Output**

$$W_{ST} = \eta_{ST} \times Q_{HRSG} = 0.42 \times 310 = 130.2 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4: Combined Cycle Efficiency**

$$\eta_{CC} = \eta_{GT} + \eta_{ST} - \eta_{GT}\eta_{ST}$$

$$\eta_{CC} = 0.38 + 0.42 - (0.38 \times 0.42)$$

$$\eta_{CC} = 0.80 - 0.1596 = 0.6404$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{CC} = 64.04\%}$$

**Step 5: Total Plant Output**

$$W_{total} = W_{GT} + W_{ST} = 190 + 130.2 = 320.2 \text{ MW}$$

**Verification using combined cycle formula:**

$$W_{total} = \eta_{CC} \times Q_{GT,fuel} = 0.6404 \times 500 = 320.2 \text{ MW} \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Past Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with reference to cogeneration systems:
1. Energy Utilization Factor (EUF)
2. Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)
3. Cogeneration efficiency on electrical-only basis
4. Topping cycle
5. Bottoming cycle
6. Fuel savings
7. Utilization factor

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cogeneration plant supplies 5000 kW of electrical power and 8000 kW of process heat to a sugar mill. The fuel input rate is 14,000 kW (LHV basis). If a separate-generation system requires boiler efficiency of 88% and electrical generation efficiency of 32% to produce the same useful outputs, calculate:
1. Energy Utilization Factor
2. Heat-to-Power Ratio
3. Fuel consumption of the separate system
4. Percentage fuel savings achieved by cogeneration

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Definitions (1 mark each):**

1. **EUF:** Ratio of total useful energy output (electrical + thermal) to the fuel energy input.

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{W_{net} + Q_u}{Q_{in}}$$

2. **HPR:** Ratio of useful thermal energy output to net electrical work output.

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_u}{W_{net}}$$

3. **Cogeneration Efficiency (Electrical-only Basis):** Ratio of electrical output to the fuel *not* used for process heat.

$$\eta_{e,cog} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in} - Q_u}$$

4. **Topping Cycle:** Cogeneration configuration in which electricity is generated first; subsequent waste heat supplies process thermal energy.

5. **Bottoming Cycle:** Cogeneration configuration in which high-temperature process heat is generated first; waste flue gas heat is recovered in a waste heat boiler for electricity generation.

6. **Fuel Savings:** Reduction in fuel consumption achieved by cogeneration compared to producing the same useful outputs via separate heat-only and power-only systems.

$$\text{Savings} (\%) = \frac{F_{sep} - F_{cog}}{F_{sep}} \times 100\%$$

7. **Utilization Factor:** A generalized term synonymous with EUF — fraction of fuel input converted to useful forms of energy (mechanical + thermal).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_{net} = 5000$ kW
- $Q_u = 8000$ kW
- $Q_{in} = 14{,}000$ kW
- $\eta_{boiler} = 0.88$
- $\eta_{el} = 0.32$

**Step 1: Energy Utilization Factor**

$$\eta_{EUF} = \frac{5000 + 8000}{14000} = \frac{13000}{14000}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{EUF} = 0.9286 = 92.86\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A EUF of 92.86% is exceptionally high — achievable in **sugar mill cogeneration** with bagasse firing and multi-pressure boilers. Conventional systems operate at 75%–85%.

**Step 2: Heat-to-Power Ratio**

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{8000}{5000} = 1.6$$

This means heat demand is 1.6 times electrical demand — characteristic of a sugar mill where crystallization and evaporation dominate energy use.

**Step 3: Separate-Generation Fuel Consumption**

For electricity in separate system:

$$F_{el,sep} = \frac{W_{net}}{\eta_{el}} = \frac{5000}{0.32} = 15{,}625 \text{ kW}$$

For process heat in separate system:

$$F_{th,sep} = \frac{Q_u}{\eta_{boiler}} = \frac{8000}{0.88} = 9090.91 \text{ kW}$$

Total separate-system fuel:

$$F_{sep} = 15{,}625 + 9090.91 = 24{,}715.91 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Fuel Savings**

Cogeneration fuel: $F_{cog} = 14{,}000$ kW

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{24715.91 - 14000}{24715.91} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{10715.91}{24715.91} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Fuel Savings} \approx 43.35\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Two common mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting to add** $F_{el,sep}$ and $F_{th,sep}$ — students often compute only one branch and forget the total.
> 2. **Mixing EUF with electrical efficiency** — remember EUF uses *total* fuel input including the heat-producing portion; electrical-only efficiency excludes that.
> 3. **Always carry units** (kW or MW) until the final percentage — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for ambiguous unitless ratios.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the morning of the exam.**

### Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **Cogeneration / CHP:** Simultaneous generation of electricity + useful heat from one fuel source.
- ✅ **Topping Cycle:** Electricity first → heat recovered.
- ✅ **Bottoming Cycle:** High-temp process heat first → residual heat generates electricity.
- ✅ **Combined Cycle:** Brayton (topping) + Rankine (bottoming); HRSG is the bridge.
- ✅ **HRSG:** Heat Recovery Steam Generator — recovers GT exhaust heat to raise steam.
- ✅ **Pinch Point:** Minimum temperature gap ($\Delta T_{pinch}$) between exhaust gas and steam in HRSG.

### Must-Know Formulas (boxed in your brain)
- ✅ $\eta_{EUF} = \dfrac{W_{net} + Q_u}{Q_{in}}$
- ✅ $\text{HPR} = \dfrac{Q_u}{W_{net}}$
- ✅ $\eta_{e} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in} - Q_u}$
- ✅ $\eta_{CC} = 1 - (1 - \eta_{GT})(1 - \eta_{ST})$
- ✅ $\text{Fuel Savings} = \dfrac{F_{sep} - F_{cog}}{F_{sep}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ $Q_{HRSG} = Q_{GT,fuel}(1 - \eta_{GT})$

### Must-Remember Numerical Values
- ✅ Modern combined cycle efficiency: **55%–62%** (state-of-the-art up to **64%**).
- ✅ Cogeneration EUF: typically **75%–90%**.
- ✅ Gas turbine exhaust temperature: **500°C–600°C**.
- ✅ HRSG stack temperature: **95°C–120°C** (acid dew point constraint).
- ✅ Pinch point in HRSG: **10°C–20°C**.

### Must-Draw Diagrams (Practice on Graph Sheet)
- ✅ Back-pressure turbine topping cycle (sugar/paper mill).
- ✅ Waste heat boiler bottoming cycle (steel/cement).
- ✅ Full combined cycle with HRSG, including both generator outputs.
- ✅ Energy flow Sankey for cogeneration vs separate generation.

### Frequently Tested Edge Cases
- ✅ "Why is supplementary firing used?" → Answer: To increase steam output during peak demand, but it lowers overall plant efficiency.
- ✅ "Why is cogeneration not used in standalone utility plants?" → Answer: Utility plants have no local heat sink; condenser cooling is rejected to environment.
- ✅ "What limits combined cycle efficiency?" → Answer: Gas turbine inlet temperature (material limit ~1600°C), pinch point, and minimum stack temperature.

### Kerala/KTU Context to Mention in Long Answers
- ✅ **NTPC Kayamkulam Combined Cycle Plant** — 359.58 MW, KSEB landmark.
- ✅ **Cochin Shipyard / FACT** cogeneration potential.
- ✅ **Coir and coconut-based industries** as future cogeneration microgrid hubs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning:** In every KTU 14-mark question, **always** include: (1) a labeled diagram, (2) the formula *before* the numerical substitution, (3) units at every step, and (4) a one-sentence **interpretation** of your numerical answer. Students who skip the interpretation routinely lose 1–2 marks even with correct math.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Power Plants: General Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Power Plants: General Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Thermal Power Plant (TPP)** is an integrated electromechanical installation that converts the chemical energy locked in fossil fuels (coal, lignite, fuel oil, or natural gas) — or in some modern variants, nuclear fission energy — into electrical energy by sequentially transforming it through **thermal → mechanical → electrical** energy stages. The conversion chain is governed by the **Rankine Cycle** (or a modified Rankine with reheat and regeneration) operating between a high-temperature source (the boiler furnace, ~1500 °C flame zone) and a low-temperature sink (the condenser, ~30–45 °C).

The **General Layout** of a thermal power plant refers to the systematic spatial and functional arrangement of all major and auxiliary systems — from fuel reception and storage, through the combustion–steam generation train, to the turbo-generator, condensing system, water–steam cycle auxiliaries, and the switchyard — ensuring optimal thermodynamic efficiency, operational safety, ash disposal continuity, and minimum environmental footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The general layout must be studied in conjunction with **auxiliary systems** such as coal handling, ash handling, water treatment, cooling systems, and pollution control equipment (ESP, FGD). Layout decisions directly govern plant **availability, heat rate, and auxiliary power consumption** — a recurring KTU theory question.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Tea Kettle Powering a Spinning Top"

Imagine a giant pressure cooker (the **boiler**) heating water on a huge stove. The steam rushing out of the cooker spins a wheel (**turbine**). The wheel turns a coil of wire inside magnets (**generator**), lighting up a city. Once the steam has done its job spinning the wheel, we don't throw it away — we cool it back into water (**condenser**) and pump it (**feedwater pump**) back into the cooker. A small portion of the electricity generated is used to keep the stove lit, the pumps running, and the coal moving (**auxiliaries**).

That single visual loop — *boil → push → spin → cool → return → boil* — is, in essence, the entire general layout of a thermal power plant, scaled up by a factor of roughly **a million**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Mandatory Recall)

The following engineering constants and benchmark metrics are universally applied in TPP performance evaluation and must be memorized verbatim for KTU examinations:

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of water | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** | Energy to raise 1 kg of water by 1 K |
| Latent heat of vaporization | **2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C | Energy to convert 1 kg water → steam |
| Net calorific value of Indian coal | **12,000 – 25,000 kJ/kg** | Heat released on combustion (excluding latent heat of water vapor) |
| Rankine cycle peak steam temp | **540 – 600 °C** (modern subcritical) | Main steam temperature at turbine inlet |
| Boiler drum pressure | **150 – 250 bar** (subcritical) | Working pressure of steam generator |
| Condenser vacuum | **0.05 – 0.1 bar abs** | Back pressure on turbine exhaust |
| Typical net plant efficiency | **33 – 42 %** (subcritical) | Overall thermal → electrical conversion |
| Supercritical plant efficiency | **≥ 45 %** | At > 221 bar and 600 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** Every layout decision — whether to install a regenerative feedwater heater, an air preheater, or an electrostatic precipitator — is justified by quantitatively improving one or more of these baseline metrics. KTU questions frequently test whether you can **justify** a layout component in terms of efficiency/availability.

---

## 1.4 Site Selection Criteria (Foundational Layout Driver)

Before the engineer places a single block on the general arrangement drawing, the **plant site** must satisfy the following non-negotiable criteria. These are the *physical* inputs to the layout:

- **Proximity to fuel source:** Minimize coal transport cost (≈ 60–70 % of operating expense).
- **Availability of cooling water:** Within 5 km of a river/large reservoir (≈ 70,000 m³/hr per 500 MW unit).
- **Distance from load centre:** To limit transmission losses and right-of-way costs.
- **Land availability & topography:** Flat, firm ground; low water table; foundation-bearing capacity > 2 t/m².
- **Approach & connectivity:** Rail siding for coal rakes, road for heavy equipment.
- **Environmental clearance & ash disposal area:** A 25-year ash pond must be planned.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Functional Blocks of a Thermal Power Plant

The general layout is best understood by classifying every equipment into **six functional blocks**. This block taxonomy is the *standard reference framework* KTU examiners use when framing 14-mark questions.

### Block A — Fuel & Ash Handling System
- **Coal handling plant (CHP):** Wagon tipplers → conveyors → crushers → bunker storage.
- **Ash handling plant (AHP):** Bottom ash via water/sea-ash conveying; fly ash via ESP hoppers → silos → pneumatic/lean-phase conveying to ash dyke or cement plant.

### Block B — Combustion & Steam Generation Unit (Boiler)
This is the thermodynamic heart of the plant. A modern utility boiler contains, in sequence along the flue-gas path and the water-steam path, the following heat-transfer surfaces:

- **Economizer** (recovers residual flue-gas heat to preheat feedwater)
- **Drum** (steam-water separator)
- **Evaporator / Water walls** (radiant heat absorption)
- **Primary Superheater** (raises steam to 540 °C+)
- **Secondary Superheater / Reheater** (if reheat cycle is used)
- **Platen Superheater / Final Superheater**
- **Air Preheater (APH)** — Ljungström rotary type or tubular recuperative
- **Burners** (tangential corner-fired or opposed-wall-fired)

### Block C — Turbo-Generator Block
- **High-Pressure (HP) Turbine** → **Intermediate-Pressure (IP) Turbine** → **Low-Pressure (LP) Turbine**
- **Generator** (synchronous, hydrogen-cooled inner stator/rotor, water-cooled stator windings)
- **Exciter** (rotating rectifier or static)
- **Step-up Transformer** (21 kV → 400 kV / 220 kV grid voltage)

### Block D — Condensate & Feedwater System
- **Condenser** (surface-type, shell-and-tube, water-cooled)
- **Condensate Extraction Pumps (CEP)**
- **Gland Steam System**
- **Low-Pressure (LP) Feedwater Heaters** (closed regenerative)
- **Deaerator** (removes dissolved O₂ and CO₂ to ≤ 7 ppb)
- **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP)** — centrifugal, multi-stage
- **High-Pressure (HP) Feedwater Heaters**

### Block E — Cooling System
- **Once-through cooling** (river or sea) — high efficiency, low ΔT
- **Cooling tower** (natural/induced-draft) — closed loop, requires 1.5–2 % makeup water
- **Cooling water pumps**

### Block F — Electrical, Control & Switchyard
- **Unit Auxiliary Transformer (UAT)**
- **Station Transformer (ST)**
- **Switchyard** (with circuit breakers, isolators, CTs, PTs, lightning arresters)
- **Distributed Control System (DCS)** + **SCADA** for remote grid dispatch
- **Battery rooms, fire-fighting, HVAC**

---

## 2.2 Why Each Block Exists — The Engineering "Why"

| Block | Without it, what fails? | Real-world consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Economizer | Feedwater enters drum at low T | 5–7 % fuel penalty |
| Air Preheater | Cold combustion air → poor ignition | 3–5 % fuel penalty + incomplete combustion |
| Deaerator | O₂ corrodes boiler tubes | Tube failure within months |
| Condenser | Exhaust steam pressure rises | Specific steam consumption ↑ 40 % |
| Reheater | Last-stage blades see wet steam | Blade erosion, efficiency drop |
| ESP | Particulate matter to atmosphere | ₹ 5–10 lakh/day fine + public health hazard |
| Cooling tower | Once-through water unavailable | Plant cannot be built inland |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol inside table cells.** Absolute values and "or" relations are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Typical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{\text{out}}}{q_{\text{in}}}$ | Ideal Rankine / modified Rankine efficiency | dimensionless | 0.33 – 0.45 |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{gross}} - P_{\text{aux}}}{m_f \times \text{GCV}}$ | Net plant thermal efficiency | dimensionless | 0.30 – 0.42 |
| 3 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{plant}}}$ | Heat rate | kJ/kWh | 8600 – 11000 |
| 4 | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{\text{HR}}{\text{GCV}} = \dfrac{3600}{\text{GCV} \cdot \eta_{\text{plant}}}$ | Specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh | 0.45 – 0.75 (coal) |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{sink}}}{T_{\text{source}}}$ | Theoretical max efficiency | dimensionless | 0.62 (1500 K → 300 K) |
| 6 | $P_{\text{aux}} \approx (6 - 9)\,\% \text{ of } P_{\text{gross}}$ | Auxiliary power consumption fraction | dimensionless | 0.06 – 0.09 |
| 7 | $W_{\text{turbine}} = \sum_i \left( h_i - h_{i+1} \right) \dot{m}$ | Enthalpy-drop work extraction | kW | Rankine analysis |
| 8 | $A_{\text{condenser}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{reject}}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}}$ | Heat exchanger sizing | m² | 10,000 – 25,000 for 500 MW |
| 9 | $\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{reject}}}{\rho c_p \Delta T_{\text{cw}}}$ | Cooling water flow | m³/s | ΔT ≈ 8 – 10 °C |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{net}}}{0.8 \cdot \eta_{\text{boiler}} \cdot \eta_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \text{GCV}}$ | Coal feed rate per hour | kg/s | ≈ 200 kg/s for 500 MW |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The general layout of a thermal power plant is the **engineering blueprint** that bridges thermodynamics, mechanical design, electrical systems, civil works, and environmental management. Mastering it enables the engineer to:

- **Optimize capital cost** by co-locating auxiliaries to minimize pipe/cable lengths.
- **Reduce auxiliary power** — every 1 % aux power saved ≈ ₹ 25 crore/year for a 500 MW unit.
- **Plan for redundancy** — N+1 boiler feed pump, twin induced-draft fans.
- **Achieve grid compliance** — switchyard must match CEA (Central Electricity Authority) and CTU (Central Transmission Utility) norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, KTU-exam-grade derivations. Every algebraic step is written out — **no skipped lines**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Plant Thermal Efficiency from First Principles

**Given:** A thermal power plant burns coal at mass flow rate $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/s) with gross calorific value **GCV** (kJ/kg). The gross electrical output is $P_{\text{gross}}$ (kW), and the auxiliary consumption is $P_{\text{aux}}$ (kW). Determine the **net plant thermal efficiency** $\eta_{\text{plant}}$.

**Step 1 — Heat input rate from fuel combustion:**

The chemical energy released per second is the product of mass flow rate and calorific value:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

**Step 2 — Net electrical power output:**

The plant delivers to the grid only the gross power minus what its own auxiliaries consume (pumps, fans, coal mills, lighting, controls):

$$
P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{gross}} - P_{\text{aux}} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of net thermal efficiency:**

By definition, efficiency is the ratio of useful output to energy input:

$$
\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{net}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (Step 1) and (Step 2) into (Step 3):**

$$
\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{gross}} - P_{\text{aux}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as percentage (KTU convention):**

$$
\eta_{\text{plant}}\,\% = \dfrac{P_{\text{gross}} - P_{\text{aux}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV}} \times 100
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)

**Given:** A 210 MW thermal unit operates at net plant efficiency 35 % with Indian coal of GCV = 20,000 kJ/kg. Compute SFC.

**Step 1 — Start from the heat rate equation:**

Heat rate is the inverse of efficiency, multiplied by 3600 (kJ per kWh):

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{plant}}} = \dfrac{3600}{0.35}
$$

$$
\text{HR} = 10{,}285.71 \; \text{kJ/kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert heat rate to SFC using GCV:**

By definition, SFC is heat rate divided by calorific value of fuel:

$$
\text{SFC} = \dfrac{\text{HR}}{\text{GCV}} = \dfrac{10{,}285.71}{20{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{SFC} = 0.5143 \; \text{kg/kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily coal consumption for 210 MW unit operating at 85 % PLF:**

Average power delivered $= 210 \times 0.85 = 178.5$ MW = 178,500 kW.

Daily energy $= 178{,}500 \times 24 = 4{,}284{,}000$ kWh.

Daily coal required:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{daily}} = \text{SFC} \times E_{\text{daily}} = 0.5143 \times 4{,}284{,}000
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{daily}} \approx 2{,}203{,}200 \; \text{kg/day} = 2203.2 \; \text{tonnes/day}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Modified Rankine Cycle with Reheat (Layout Justification)

To justify why **reheaters** appear in the general layout between HP and IP turbines, we compare two cycles:

### Without Reheat
$$
\eta_{\text{cycle,1}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cond}}}{T_{\text{boiler,avg}}}
$$

### With Reheat (single reheat at 50 % pressure drop)

Steam expands in HP turbine from state 1 (T₁, P₁) to state 2 (T₂, P₂ = P₁/2), is then **reheated** in the boiler back to T₁ at pressure P₂, then expands in IP/LP turbine to condenser.

$$
\eta_{\text{cycle,reheat}} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{heat rejected}}{\text{heat supplied in boiler} + \text{heat supplied in reheater}}
$$

**Numerical demonstration for a subcritical plant:**

Let $T_1 = 838$ K, $T_{\text{cond}} = 318$ K.

Without reheat:

$$
\eta_{\text{cycle,1}} = 1 - \dfrac{318}{838} = 0.6205 \; (62.05\%)
$$

With reheat, the average temperature of heat addition rises. Suppose average addition temperature rises from $T_1$ to $T_1' = 900$ K effectively:

$$
\eta_{\text{cycle,reheat}} = 1 - \dfrac{318}{900} = 0.6467 \; (64.67\%)
$$

$$
\Delta \eta = 0.6467 - 0.6205 = 0.0262 = 2.62 \%
$$

**Engineering implication:** A 2–3 % cycle efficiency gain on a 500 MW unit operating 8000 hr/year saves ≈ ₹ 35 crore/year in fuel cost. **This is why reheaters are mandatory in the layout of any modern subcritical/supercritical unit.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Condenser Cooling Water Requirement

**Given:** A 500 MW unit rejects 60 % of its input heat to the condenser. Cooling water enters at 27 °C and leaves at 37 °C. Compute the cooling water flow rate.

**Step 1 — Heat rejected by condenser:**

Assume plant efficiency = 38 % and coal GCV = 20,000 kJ/kg, fuel flow $\dot{m}_f \approx 200$ kg/s.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 200 \times 20{,}000 = 4{,}000{,}000 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{reject}} = (1 - 0.38) \times 4{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}480{,}000 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Cooling water equation:**

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{reject}}}{\rho c_p \Delta T_{\text{cw}}}
$$

Where $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K, $\Delta T = 10$ K.

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{2{,}480{,}000}{1000 \times 4.186 \times 10}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{2{,}480{,}000}{41{,}860}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} \approx 59.25 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 2{,}13{,}300 \; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 500 MW unit needs about **2.13 lakh m³ of cooling water per hour** — which is exactly why thermal plants are sited adjacent to large rivers, reservoirs, or coastal belts with hypersaline-tolerant cooling systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Layout Options

| Layout Choice | Option A | Option B | Net Δ Efficiency | Cost Impact | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling system | Once-through (river) | Induced-draft cooling tower | +1.5 to 3 % A | High A, low B | A if water available |
| Steam cycle | Subcritical 540 °C | Supercritical 600 °C | +4 to 6 % B | +30 % B | B for new installations |
| Reheat | None | Single reheat | +2.5 to 3 % B | +12 % B | B is standard |
| Regenerative FWH | 1 LP + 1 HP | 2 LP + 2 HP | +1 to 1.5 % B | Marginal | B is industry standard |
| Coal drying | None | Pre-drying mill | +0.5 to 1 % B | Low | B for lignite plants |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block-Level Architecture of a Thermal Power Plant (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the major equipment, their functional role, and the energy/material pathways between them. This is the **standard KTU layout diagram** expected in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CoalStock["COAL STOCK YARD"] --> WagonTip["WAGON TIPPLER"]
    WagonTip --> Crusher["CRUSHER HOUSE"]
    Crusher --> Convey1["BELT CONVEYOR 1"]
    Convey1 --> Bunker["COAL BUNKER"]
    Bunker --> Mill["PULVERISER MILL"]
    Mill --> Furnace["BOILER FURNACE"]

    Furnace -->|"Flue gas path"| APH["AIR PREHEATER"]
    APH -->|"Hot flue gas"| ESP["ELECTROSTATIC PRECIPITATOR"]
    ESP -->|"Cleaned gas"| IDFan["INDUCED DRAFT FAN"]
    IDFan --> Stack["STACK CHIMNEY"]

    APH -->|"Preheated air"| Furnace

    Furnace -->|"Steam 540C 250 bar"| HPTurb["HP TURBINE"]
    HPTurb -->|"Cold reheat 320C"| Reheater["REHEATER BOILER"]
    Reheater -->|"Reheat 540C"| IPTurb["IP TURBINE"]
    IPTurb --> LPTurb["LP TURBINE"]
    LPTurb --> Condenser["CONDENSER"]

    Condenser -->|"Hot well 45C"| CEP["CONDENSATE EXTRACTION PUMP"]
    CEP --> LPH1["LP FEEDWATER HEATER 1"]
    LPH1 --> LPH2["LP FEEDWATER HEATER 2"]
    LPH2 --> Deaerator["DEAERATOR 165C"]
    Deaerator --> BFP["BOILER FEED PUMP"]
    BFP --> HPH1["HP FEEDWATER HEATER 1"]
    HPH1 --> HPH2["HP FEEDWATER HEATER 2"]
    HPH2 --> Economizer["ECONOMIZER"]
    Economizer --> Furnace

    HPTurb -->|"Extraction steam"| LPH1
    HPTurb -->|"Extraction steam"| LPH2
    IPTurb -->|"Extraction steam"| HPH1
    IPTurb -->|"Extraction steam"| HPH2
    IPTurb -->|"Crossover steam"| HPTurb

    HPTurb --> Generator["SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR"]
    IPTurb --> Generator
    LPTurb --> Generator
    Generator --> UAT["UNIT AUX TRANSFORMER"]
    Generator --> MainXmer["STEP UP TRANSFORMER"]
    MainXmer --> Switchyard["SWITCHYARD 400 KV"]
    UAT --> AuxBoard["UNIT AUX BOARD 6 6 KV"]

    Condenser --> CWPump["COOLING WATER PUMP"]
    CWPump --> CoolTower["COOLING TOWER"]
    CoolTower --> CWPump

    Bunker --> AshPit["BOTTOM ASH HOPPER"]
    AshPit --> AshDyke["ASH DYKE DISPOSAL"]
    ESP --> FlyAshSilo["FLY ASH SILO"]
    FlyAshSilo --> CementPlant["CEMENT INDUSTRY OFFTAKE"]
```

---

## 4.2 Companion — Steam–Water Path vs. Flue-Gas Path (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SteamWater["STEAM WATER CYCLE PATH"]
        A1["Feedwater 240C"] --> A2["Economizer"]
        A2 --> A3["Boiler Drum"]
        A3 --> A4["Evaporator Tubes"]
        A4 --> A5["Primary Superheater"]
        A5 --> A6["Final Superheater 540C"]
        A6 --> A7["HP Turbine"]
        A7 --> A8["Reheater Boiler"]
        A8 --> A9["IP Turbine"]
        A9 --> A10["LP Turbine"]
        A10 --> A11["Condenser 0 05 bar"]
        A11 --> A1
    end

    subgraph FlueGas["FLUE GAS PATH"]
        B1["Pulverised Coal plus Primary Air"] --> B2["Furnace 1500C"]
        B2 --> B3["Reheater and Superheater Section"]
        B3 --> B4["Economizer Section"]
        B4 --> B5["Air Preheater 350C"]
        B5 --> B6["ESP 99 9 percent efficiency"]
        B6 --> B7["ID Fan"]
        B7 --> B8["Stack 200m"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Interpretation Guide for the Student

- **Steam–Water path** (left/inner cycle) is the *working fluid loop*. Steam changes state but never leaves the cycle.
- **Flue-Gas path** (outer loop) is *one-time-through*. Hot combustion gases transfer heat to the steam–water cycle and are exhausted.
- **Condenser–cooling tower loop** is a *tertiary* loop that only carries heat out to the atmosphere — it does not mix with the working fluid.
- The **three loops are thermally coupled but hydraulically isolated** — this is the central design principle of every thermal power plant layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drawing Tip for KTU:** When asked to draw the layout in the answer sheet, draw the boiler as the central block with steam going right (to the turbine) and flue gas going up (to the APH, ESP, ID fan, stack). This is the **conventional KTU/Board layout convention**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
*With the help of a neat sketch, describe the general layout of a modern coal-fired thermal power plant. List any six major auxiliaries.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A modern coal-fired thermal power plant is functionally divided into six major blocks: (1) **Coal handling plant** (wagon tippler, conveyors, crushers, bunkers), (2) **Boiler** with economizer, superheater, reheater and air preheater, (3) **Turbo-generator** (HP–IP–LP turbines coupled to a synchronous generator), (4) **Condensate-feedwater system** (condenser, CEP, LP/HP feedwater heaters, deaerator, BFP), (5) **Cooling system** (cooling towers or once-through pumps), and (6) **Switchyard and control** (UAT, step-up transformer, switchyard, DCS).

**Six major auxiliaries:** (i) Induced Draft (ID) fan, (ii) Forced Draft (FD) fan, (iii) Pulveriser mill, (iv) Boiler Feed Pump, (v) Condensate Extraction Pump, (vi) Cooling water pump.

*(Marking key: Layout sketch with labelling = 1.5 marks; six correct auxiliaries = 1.5 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
*Define "Net Plant Heat Rate" and "Specific Fuel Consumption" of a thermal power plant. State their typical ranges for an Indian coal-based plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Net Plant Heat Rate (HR)** is the amount of fuel energy (in kJ) required to generate one kWh of electrical energy exported to the grid. $\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{plant}}}$ kJ/kWh. Typical range: **8600 – 11,000 kJ/kWh** for subcritical Indian plants.

- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** is the mass of fuel (in kg) required per kWh of net electrical energy exported. $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{\text{HR}}{\text{GCV}}$ kg/kWh. Typical range for Indian coal (GCV ≈ 20,000 kJ/kg): **0.45 – 0.60 kg/kWh**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Students must answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled line diagram, describe the **general layout of a 250 MW coal-fired thermal power plant**. Explain the function of each of the following: Economizer, Deaerator, Air Preheater, Electrostatic Precipitator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 MW thermal power plant operates at 38 % net efficiency with coal of GCV 22,000 kJ/kg. The auxiliary power consumption is 7 % of gross output. Determine (i) the net heat rate, (ii) the specific fuel consumption, and (iii) the daily coal requirement at 85 % plant load factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Layout + Equipment Function (7 marks)**

**Layout:** Draw a single-line block diagram showing the six functional blocks in proper sequence (as per the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1). Labelled components must include coal stockyard → bunker → mill → boiler (with economizer, APH, superheater) → HP/IP/LP turbines → condenser → feedwater train (CEP, LPHs, deaerator, BFP, HPHs) → cooling tower/cooling water → generator → switchyard.

**Functions (½ mark each):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Economizer** | Recovers residual heat from flue gas (after the evaporator) to preheat feedwater before it enters the boiler drum, improving boiler efficiency by 5–7 %. |
| **Deaerator** | Removes dissolved oxygen and free CO₂ from feedwater by heating to saturation temperature (~165 °C); prevents corrosion of boiler tubes and HP heaters. |
| **Air Preheater** | Transfers heat from flue gas leaving the economizer to combustion air entering the furnace; raises furnace efficiency and supports better fuel ignition. |
| **ESP** | Electrostatic Precipitator removes > 99.9 % of fly-ash particulates from flue gas before it is exhausted through the chimney, ensuring particulate emissions < 50 mg/Nm³ (compliance with CPCB norms). |

*(Mark split: 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for four correct functional descriptions.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $P_{\text{gross}} = 250$ MW, $\eta_{\text{plant,net}} = 0.38$, GCV = 22,000 kJ/kg, $P_{\text{aux}} = 0.07 \cdot P_{\text{gross}}$, PLF = 0.85.

**Step 1 — Compute net heat rate:**

$$
\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{plant}}} = \dfrac{3600}{0.38}
$$

$$
\text{HR} = 9473.68 \; \text{kJ/kWh}
$$

*(½ mark for formula, 1 mark for answer.)*

**Step 2 — Compute SFC:**

$$
\text{SFC} = \dfrac{\text{HR}}{\text{GCV}} = \dfrac{9473.68}{22{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{SFC} = 0.4306 \; \text{kg/kWh}
$$

*(½ mark for formula, 1 mark for answer.)*

**Step 3 — Daily energy generated:**

$$
P_{\text{net delivered}} = 250 \times 0.85 = 212.5 \; \text{MW}
$$

$$
E_{\text{daily}} = 212.5 \times 10^3 \times 24 = 5.1 \times 10^6 \; \text{kWh}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 4 — Daily coal requirement:**

$$
m_{\text{daily}} = \text{SFC} \times E_{\text{daily}} = 0.4306 \times 5.1 \times 10^6
$$

$$
m_{\text{daily}} = 2{,}196{,}060 \; \text{kg} = 2196.06 \; \text{tonnes/day}
$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer.)*

**Final Answers:**
- HR = **9473.68 kJ/kWh**
- SFC = **0.4306 kg/kWh**
- Daily coal = **≈ 2196 tonnes/day**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** Discuss the **site-selection criteria** for a thermal power plant. Explain why a **cooling water source** is the single most decisive factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supercritical thermal plant of 800 MW net capacity has a Rankine cycle efficiency of 45 %. The coal used has GCV = 25,000 kJ/kg and the auxiliary power consumption is 6 % of gross output. Calculate (i) the mass flow rate of coal, (ii) the mass flow rate of cooling water, if the cooling water enters at 25 °C and leaves at 33 °C (assume 60 % of input heat is rejected via condenser, $c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/kg·K). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Site Selection (7 marks)**

A thermal power plant of large capacity requires careful site selection for techno-economic viability. The major criteria are:

1. **Availability of fuel source nearby** — Coal transportation constitutes the largest operating cost (~60 %). Plant should be within 50–100 km of the coal mine (pit-head plant preferred). *(1 mark)*
2. **Large quantity of cooling water** — Single most decisive factor because condenser must reject ~60 % of input heat; ≈ 70,000 m³/hr for a 500 MW unit. Inland plants need cooling towers; coastal plants use sea water. *(1.5 marks — including emphasis on why it is decisive)*
3. **Distance from load centre** — Reduces transmission losses (≈ 5–8 % per 1000 km) and right-of-way cost. *(1 mark)*
4. **Availability of suitable land** — Flat, firm foundation (bearing > 2 t/m²), ~50–60 acres for 2×500 MW, low water table. *(0.75 mark)*
5. **Connectivity** — Rail siding for coal rakes, road for heavy equipment. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Environmental and social** — Distance from habitations (≥ 1 km), availability of ash dyke land for 25 years, water–air clearance. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Approach to grid infrastructure** — 400 kV switchyard accessibility. *(0.5 mark)*

**Why cooling water is decisive:** If water is unavailable, the plant cannot be built. Once-through river cooling is the cheapest option but environmentally constrained. Cooling towers add 1.5–3 % efficiency penalty. Sea-water cooling requires titanium condenser tubes (corrosion-resistant) and chlorination plants. Hence, location often becomes synonymous with water access. *(1.25 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $P_{\text{net}} = 800$ MW, $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 0.45$, GCV = 25,000 kJ/kg, $P_{\text{aux}} = 0.06$, $\dot{Q}_{\text{reject fraction}} = 0.60$, $\Delta T_{\text{cw}} = 8$ °C, $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Gross output and fuel input:**

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{net}}}{1 - 0.06} = \dfrac{800}{0.94} = 851.06 \; \text{MW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{gross}}}{\eta_{\text{cycle}}} = \dfrac{851{,}064}{0.45} = 1{,}891{,}253 \; \text{kW}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 2 — Coal mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}{\text{GCV}} = \dfrac{1{,}891{,}253}{25{,}000}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = 75.65 \; \text{kg/s} \approx 272{,}340 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer.)*

**Step 3 — Heat rejected:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{reject}} = 0.60 \times 1{,}891{,}253 = 1{,}134{,}752 \; \text{kW}
$$

*(0.5 mark.)*

**Step 4 — Cooling water mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{reject}}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{1{,}134{,}752}{4.186 \times 8}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cw}} = \dfrac{1{,}134{,}752}{33.488}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cw}} \approx 33{,}887 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

Converting to volumetric flow at $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} \approx 33.9 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1{,}22{,}040 \; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer, 0.5 mark for unit conversion.)*

**Final Answers:**
- $\dot{m}_f \approx$ **75.65 kg/s** (≈ 272 tonnes/hr)
- $\dot{V}_{\text{cw}} \approx$ **33.9 m³/s** (≈ 1.22 lakh m³/hr)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Gross vs. Net efficiency:** Many students use net plant efficiency when computing coal mass flow from gross output, or vice versa. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting auxiliary power in long problems:** When the question gives both gross and net outputs, treat the aux % as a deduction **before** dividing by efficiency.
> 3. **Missing ΔT in cooling water:** Using ΔT = 33 − 0 = 33 °C instead of 33 − 25 = 8 °C. Always subtract.
> 4. **Drawing the layout sketch without labels:** A neat diagram without labels scores 0 marks for "function of X". Always label economizer, APH, ESP, condenser, deaerator, BFP, cooling tower.
> 5. **Not converting kJ to kWh or vice versa:** 1 kWh = 3600 kJ. Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks per numerical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Six functional blocks** of any thermal power plant: Fuel & Ash Handling, Boiler, Turbo-Generator, Condensate-Feedwater, Cooling, Electrical/Switchyard.
- The **steam–water cycle** is a closed loop; the **flue-gas cycle** is open-loop (one pass to atmosphere); the **cooling-water loop** is a tertiary open loop.
- **Rankine cycle** is the basic working cycle; **Modified Rankine with reheat + regeneration** is the industry standard.
- **Net Plant Efficiency** $\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{gross}} - P_{\text{aux}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{GCV}}$ (typical 33–42 %).
- **Heat Rate** HR = $3600 / \eta_{\text{plant}}$ (typical 8600 – 11,000 kJ/kWh).
- **Specific Fuel Consumption** SFC = HR / GCV (typical 0.45 – 0.6 kg/kWh for Indian coal).
- **Cooling water demand** ≈ 2 m³ per MWh for closed cooling tower or 60 m³/MWh for once-through.
- **Boiler auxiliaries**: FD fan, ID fan, PA fan, pulveriser mills, soot blowers, ESP.
- **Steam cycle auxiliaries**: BFP, CEP, LPHs, HPHs, deaerator, gland steam system.
- **Layout hierarchy:** Coal receiving → coal preparation → boiler island → turbine island → switchyard → ash & cooling auxiliaries.
- **Standard steam conditions for subcritical plants:** 540 °C / 250 bar with single reheat.
- **Condenser back pressure:** 0.05 – 0.1 bar abs (i.e., 95 % vacuum).
- **Ash generation:** ≈ 30–40 % of coal mass becomes ash (must be disposed or utilized).
- **Modern trend:** Indian supercritical plants (e.g., NTPC Sipat, Kudgi) use **600 °C / 250 bar** with double reheat achieving **> 42 % net efficiency**.
- **Switchyard standard voltage:** 220 kV or 400 kV (evacuated via step-up transformer).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Site Selection** in Power Plant Engineering refers to the systematic, multi-criteria engineering decision-making process used to identify, evaluate, and finalize an optimal geographical location for the installation of a power generation facility. The objective is to minimize the levelized cost of electricity (LCOE), ensure long-term operational reliability, comply with statutory environmental and safety regulations, and guarantee uninterrupted fuel, water, and load accessibility throughout the plant's operational lifespan (typically **25–40 years** for thermal plants and **50–100 years** for hydroelectric stations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of OEMET727 mandates a comparative study of site selection criteria for **Steam Power Plants, Hydroelectric Power Plants, Diesel Power Plants, and Nuclear Power Plants**. Students must distinguish between *primary* (must-have) and *secondary* (preferred) selection parameters for each type.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a location to build your house. You wouldn't build it in the middle of a flood-prone swamp, far from the market, on weak soil, with no road access. Instead, you'd want:
- **Solid, dry, elevated ground** (foundational stability),
- **Proximity to your workplace/school** (load center proximity),
- **Easy road/highway access** (transportation of materials),
- **Access to municipal water and electricity lines** (utilities),
- And **legal permission** to build (statutory clearance).

A power plant is essentially a *giant house for energy conversion*. The selection of its site follows the same logic but at a massive industrial scale. The site must be geotechnically stable, close to the consumers (cities/industries), near fuel and water sources, well-connected by transport (rail/road/water), and legally permissible under pollution control and zoning norms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a house, once a power plant is commissioned, *shifting the plant is economically impractical*. Hence, site selection is a **once-in-a-lifetime irreversible decision** — mistakes are extremely costly (often running into thousands of crores). This is why engineering boards like KTU emphasize this topic heavily.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Standard Parameter | Typical Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** | **60%–85%** (thermal), **40%–60%** (solar/wind) | Determines optimal plant size for site |
| **Specific Water Consumption** | **3.5–4.5 m³/MWh** (thermal) | Cooling water availability check |
| **Coal Transportation Distance** | Ideally **<500 km** | Beyond this, pit-head plants are preferred |
| **Ash Generation Rate** | **0.4–0.5 kg/kWh** of coal | Land requirement for ash disposal |
| **Minimum Land for 1000 MW Thermal Plant** | **~200–400 acres** | Footprint calculation |
| **Distance from City (Fence Line)** | **>2 km** (modern norms) | Pollution buffer zone |
| **Hydro Plant Capacity Factor** | **0.4–0.6** | Hydrological site assessment |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Site Selection Factors

The site selection parameters can be broadly classified into **four categories**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for answering KTU's comparative questions.

### A. Primary (Compulsory) Factors — *The "Must-Haves"*
These factors are non-negotiable. Absence of any one makes the site **completely unsuitable**.

1. **Availability of Fuel** (Coal/Gas/Uranium/Water-head)
2. **Availability of Cooling Water** (in required quantity and quality)
3. **Geotechnical Stability** (soil bearing capacity, seismic zone, flood level)
4. **Load Center Proximity** (distance from demand area)
5. **Statutory/Environmental Clearances** (Consent to Establish from State Pollution Control Board)

### B. Secondary (Preferred) Factors — *The "Should-Haves"*
These factors differentiate between *acceptable* and *ideal* sites.

1. Transportation infrastructure (rail siding, road, port)
2. Land availability and cost
3. Manpower/Local skill availability
4. Climate (ambient temperature, humidity affect condenser performance)
5. Expansion potential

### C. Tertiary (Strategic) Factors — *Long-term Considerations*
1. Regional industrial development plans
2. Political stability and government incentives
3. Future fuel logistics
4. Disaster resilience (cyclone, earthquake, tsunami)

## 2.2 Comparative Table: Site Selection Factors for Different Power Plants

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table:** This is one of the most frequently asked comparison questions in KTU ESE. Memorize the *contrasts*, not just the contents.

| Selection Factor | Steam (Coal) Plant | Hydroelectric Plant | Nuclear Power Plant | Diesel Plant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Fuel Source** | Coal (millions of tonnes/yr) | Water head (river/dam) | Uranium (kg/yr — tiny mass) | Diesel/HSD (small stockpile) |
| **Water Requirement** | **Very High** (cooling) | Head & flow rate | Moderate (cooling) | Low |
| **Site Topography** | Flat, near river/sea | Hilly, with dam site | Flat, geologically stable | Flat, accessible |
| **Distance from Load** | <500–800 km ideal | Anywhere (transmission OK) | **Far from population (>10–25 km)** | Near load (small, distributed) |
| **Geological Stability** | Good soil bearing | Bedrock foundation needed | **Most stringent** (seismic, fault-free) | Standard |
| **Transportation** | Heavy (rail for coal) | Roads for equipment | Heavy (special shielding for fuel) | Road tankers |
| **Land Area** | **Large** (ash ponds, coal yard) | **Largest** (reservoir submergence) | Moderate (exclusion zone large) | Small |
| **Cooling Medium** | Water (or cooling tower) | Reservoir water | Water (sea often) | Air (radiator) |
| **Pollution Risk** | Air (SO₂, PM, ash) | Minimal (but displacement) | **Radiological (strictest)** | Moderate (NOx, noise) |
| **Capital Cost Influencer** | Coal logistics | Civil works (dam) | **Reactor + safety systems** | Lowest |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = V \times I \times \cos\phi \times t$ | Electrical energy delivered (kWh) | Sizing transmission losses |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{plant}} = \dfrac{\text{MWh generated}}{\text{Fuel heat input (MWh equivalent)}}$ | Plant thermal efficiency | Cost-benefit at site |
| 3 | $Q = m \times C_p \times \Delta T$ | Heat absorbed by cooling water | Condenser cooling water mass flow |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{w} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{C_p \times \Delta T}$ | Required cooling water mass flow rate (kg/s) | Site water resource sizing |
| 5 | $C_{\text{transport}} = D \times R \times M$ | Annual transport cost (₹) | Coal transport cost estimation |
| 6 | $C_{\text{transmission}} = k \cdot \left(\dfrac{\text{MW} \times \text{km}}{1000}\right)^{0.65}$ | Empirical transmission cost (approximate) | Distance-to-load penalty |
| 7 | $P = \eta \times \rho \times g \times Q \times H_{\text{net}}$ | Hydroelectric power (W) | Hydro site evaluation |
| 8 | $H_{\text{net}} = H_{\text{gross}} - h_f - h_{\text{tail}}$ | Net head (m) | Hydraulic losses at site |
| 9 | $\text{Cooling water required} \approx 4 \text{ m}^3/\text{MWh}$ | Rule of thumb (rule of **4 cubic metres**) | Quick site screening |
| 10 | $\text{Ash yard area} = \dfrac{\text{Ash/day} \times \text{Stack life (days)}}{\text{Ash density} \times \text{Stack height}}$ | Ash pond sizing | Land requirement for thermal site |

**Notation Key:**
- $E$ = Electrical energy, $V$ = Voltage, $I$ = Current, $\cos\phi$ = Power factor, $t$ = Time
- $\dot{Q}$ = Heat rejected to condenser (W), $C_p$ = Specific heat of water (**4.186 kJ/kg·K**), $\Delta T$ = Temperature rise
- $D$ = Distance (km), $R$ = Rate (₹/tonne-km), $M$ = Mass transported (tonnes/year)
- $\eta$ = Hydro turbine-generator efficiency (~**0.85–0.92**), $\rho$ = Water density (**1000 kg/m³**), $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $Q$ = Flow rate (m³/s), $H_{\text{net}}$ = Net head

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The site selection principles studied here are directly applied by:
- **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)** of India during National Electricity Plan formulation
- **NTPC, NHPC, NPCIL** for greenfield project appraisal
- **State GENCOs and IPPs** for project feasibility reports (PFR/DPR)
- **Environmental consultants** preparing EIA/EMP reports

For example, India's policy of preferring **pit-head thermal plants** (e.g., Vindhyachal, Rihand, Sipat) versus **coastal plants** (e.g., Mundra, Krishnapatnam) is a direct application of *transportation economics + cooling water availability + land reclamation* tradeoffs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cooling Water Requirement for a Thermal Plant Site

The single most important site-specific calculation in thermal plant engineering is determining **whether the available water body can supply enough cooling water**.

### Step 1: Define the Plant Heat Balance

A thermal power plant converts fuel chemical energy → thermal energy → mechanical energy → electrical energy. By the First Law of Thermodynamics, the rejected heat must be absorbed by cooling water (or dissipated via cooling towers).

Let:
- $\dot{W}_{\text{elec}}$ = Net electrical power output (W)
- $\eta_{\text{net}}$ = Net plant efficiency (typically **35%–42%** for subcritical, **42%–45%** for supercritical)
- $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$ = Heat input from fuel (W)

Therefore:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{\text{elec}}}{\eta_{\text{net}}}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Heat Rejected to Condenser

The dominant heat rejection (about **45%–55%** of $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$) occurs at the condenser.

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{cycle}})$$

where $\eta_{\text{cycle}}$ is the Carnot/Rankine cycle efficiency of the steam side.

A simpler, industry-standard approximation:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} \approx \dot{W}_{\text{elec}} \times \dfrac{(1 - \eta_{\text{net}})}{\eta_{\text{net}}}$$

### Step 3: Apply Energy Balance Across Condenser

The cooling water absorbs the rejected heat:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{m}_w \times C_p \times \Delta T_w$$

Solving for mass flow rate of cooling water:
$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}}}{C_p \times \Delta T_w}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate

Since water resource assessments use volumetric units:
$$\dot{V}_w = \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}}}{\rho_w \times C_p \times \Delta T_w}$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** A 500 MW thermal plant operates at $\eta_{\text{net}} = 38\%$. Cooling water temperature rise $\Delta T_w = 8^\circ C$. Use $C_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K and $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

**Step A:** Heat rejected to condenser:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = (500 \times 10^6) \times \dfrac{(1 - 0.38)}{0.38}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = 500 \times 10^6 \times \dfrac{0.62}{0.38} = 815.79 \text{ MW}_{\text{th}}$$

**Step B:** Mass flow rate of cooling water:
$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{815.79 \times 10^6 \text{ W}}{4186 \text{ J/kg·K} \times 8 \text{ K}}$$
$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{8.158 \times 10^8}{3.349 \times 10^4} = 2.436 \times 10^4 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step C:** Volumetric flow rate:
$$\dot{V}_w = \dfrac{2.436 \times 10^4}{1000} = 24.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step D:** Convert to cubic metres per hour:
$$\dot{V}_w = 24.36 \times 3600 = 87{,}696 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step E:** Convert to million litres per day (MLD):
$$\dot{V}_w = 87{,}696 \times 24 / 1000 = 2104.7 \text{ MLD}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Site Selection:** If the local river/discharge channel cannot reliably supply ~2100 MLD even during lean season (90% dependability), **the site is rejected** as a candidate for an oncethrough cooling plant. This single calculation dominates thermal site feasibility.

## 3.2 Derivation: Coal Transport vs. Transmission Cost Trade-off

A classic KTU 14-mark question involves comparing *pit-head* vs. *load-center* siting.

### Step 1: Define Cost Components

- **Coal transport cost** (₹/year): $C_T = D \times R \times M_{\text{coal}}$
- **Transmission cost** (₹/year): $C_{tx} = f(\text{MW}, \text{km})$ — usually modeled as proportional to MW·km

### Step 2: Derive Break-Even Distance

Setting coal transport cost equal to transmission cost:
$$D \times R \times M_{\text{coal}} = k \times (\text{MW} \times D)$$

Solving for $D$:
$$D_{\text{break-even}} = \dfrac{k \times \text{MW}}{R \times M_{\text{coal}}}$$

### Step 3: Typical Industry Values

- $k \approx \text{₹ 1500–2500 per MW·km·yr}$ (EHV transmission)
- $R \approx \text{₹ 1.5–3.0 per tonne·km}$ (rail)
- $M_{\text{coal}} \approx \text{0.5 kg/kWh} \times \text{PLF} \times 8760 \text{ hr} \times \text{MW}$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** 1000 MW plant, PLF = 80%, $k = 2000$ ₹/MW·km·yr, $R = 2.5$ ₹/t·km, coal consumption = 0.5 kg/kWh.

**Solution:**

Annual coal mass:
$$M_{\text{coal}} = 0.5 \times 0.80 \times 8760 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ tonnes/year}$$
$$M_{\text{coal}} = 3.504 \times 10^6 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

Break-even distance:
$$D_{\text{break-even}} = \dfrac{2000 \times 1000}{2.5 \times 3.504 \times 10^6}$$
$$D_{\text{break-even}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{8.76 \times 10^6} = 0.228 \text{ km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-intuitive Result:** With modern high-capacity EHV transmission, the break-even distance becomes *very small* — suggesting plants should always be at the load center. However, this ignores (a) **transmission losses** (~5%–7% for 1000 km lines), (b) **right-of-way costs**, and (c) **grid stability constraints**. Real-world decisions balance *all* of these. CEA's general rule: pit-head preferred when coal source is **>500 km** away.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flowchart Logic

| Decision Criterion | Question to Ask | If Yes | If No |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is required fuel quantity available within viable distance? | Proceed | **Reject site** |
| 2 | Is cooling water source adequate (year-round)? | Proceed | **Reject site** |
| 3 | Is geotechnical/seismic suitability confirmed? | Proceed | **Reject site** |
| 4 | Is environmental clearance obtainable? | Proceed | **Reject site** |
| 5 | Is distance to load center acceptable? | Proceed | Add transmission penalty |
| 6 | Is transport infrastructure adequate? | Proceed | Add capex for infra |
| 7 | Is expansion land available? | Proceed | Constrain future growth |
| 8 | Are local incentives/taxes favorable? | Proceed | Add to operating cost |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Site Selection Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Site Selection Initiated] --> B[Define Plant Type and Capacity]
    B --> C[Fuel Source Survey]
    C --> D{Fuel Available Within Viable Distance?}
    D -- No --> Z1[Reject Site]
    D -- Yes --> E[Water Resource Assessment]
    E --> F{Cooling Water Adequate Year Round?}
    F -- No --> Z2[Reject Site or Use Cooling Towers]
    F -- Yes --> G[Geotechnical and Seismic Survey]
    G --> H{Site Geologically Stable?}
    H -- No --> Z3[Reject Site]
    H -- Yes --> I[Environmental Impact Assessment]
    I --> J{Clearance Obtainable?}
    J -- No --> Z4[Reject Site]
    J -- Yes --> K[Distance to Load Center Analysis]
    K --> L{Distance Within 500 km?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Load Center Preferred]
    L -- No --> N[Pit Head or Coastal Preferred]
    M --> O[Transport Infrastructure Check]
    N --> O
    O --> P{All Transport Links Available?}
    P -- No --> Q[Add Infrastructure Capex]
    P -- Yes --> R[Final Economic Feasibility]
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Site Selected or Rejected]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Site Selection Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRIM[Primary Must Have Factors]
        A1[Fuel Availability]
        A2[Water Availability]
        A3[Geotechnical Stability]
        A4[Load Proximity]
        A5[Environmental Clearance]
    end
    subgraph SEC[Secondary Preferred Factors]
        B1[Transport Links]
        B2[Land and Cost]
        B3[Manpower]
        B4[Climate Suitability]
    end
    subgraph TERT[Tertiary Strategic Factors]
        C1[Regional Policy]
        C2[Disaster Resilience]
        C3[Expansion Potential]
        C4[Future Fuel Logistics]
    end
    PRIM --> DEC[Integrated Site Decision Matrix]
    SEC --> DEC
    TERT --> DEC
    DEC --> OUT{Final Site Selection Decision}
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Block: Plant-Type Specific Priorities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Power Plant Site Selection] --> Type{Plant Type}
    Type -- Steam --> S1[Prioritize: Coal, Cooling Water, Ash Disposal]
    Type -- Hydro --> H1[Prioritize: Head, Flow, Geology, Reservoir]
    Type -- Nuclear --> N1[Prioritize: Seismic Stability, Exclusion Zone, Cooling]
    Type -- Diesel --> D1[Prioritize: Load Proximity, Fuel Logistics, Noise]
    S1 --> S2[Flat Land, Near River or Coast]
    H1 --> H2[Mountainous, Strong Bedrock, River Catchment]
    N1 --> N2[Remote, Seismically Quiet, Coastal Cooling]
    D1 --> D2[Distributed, Urban Periphery, Modular]
    S2 --> Final[Final Comparative Scoring]
    H2 --> Final
    N2 --> Final
    D2 --> Final
```

## 4.4 Mermaid: Pit-Head vs. Load-Center Trade-off Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PH[Pit Head Plant]
        A1[Zero Coal Transport] --> A2[Lower Fuel Cost]
        A1 --> A3[High Transmission Cost]
        A1 --> A4[Transmission Losses]
    end
    subgraph LC[Load Center Plant]
        B1[Coal Transport Required] --> B2[High Fuel Cost]
        B1 --> B3[No Transmission Cost]
        B1 --> B4[Lower Distribution Cost]
    end
    A2 --> E[Economic Balance]
    A3 --> E
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    E --> R{Break Even Distance Achieved?}
    R -- Yes --> SEL[Plant Configuration Selected]
    R -- No --> REJ[Reconsider Site Choice]
```
