# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any **six** factors that influence the site selection of a steam power plant.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Availability and proximity of coal** (fuel logistics)
2. **Adequate cooling water supply** year-round (~4 m³/MWh)
3. **Geotechnical stability** — soil bearing capacity, flood level
4. **Proximity to load center** to reduce transmission losses
5. **Transportation facilities** — rail siding for coal and ash
6. **Environmental clearance** — air, water, noise pollution norms
7. Availability of land for ash pond and future expansion *(optional 7th)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only 4–5 factors. KTU strictly awards **0.5 marks per valid point**. Writing more than 6 is fine, but missing one costs 0.5 marks.

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between **pit-head** and **load-center** thermal power plants with respect to site selection.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pit-Head Plant | Load-Center Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Adjacent to coal mine | Near demand/load area |
| **Coal Transport** | Minimal (conveyor/short rail) | Long-distance (rail/roro) |
| **Fuel Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Transmission** | EHV lines to distant load | Short distribution lines |
| **Water Source** | Often far (river/sea) | Often difficult in cities |
| **Land Cost** | Lower (rural) | Higher (urban) |
| **Examples** | Vindhyachal, Rihand, Korba | Dahanu, Faridabad (old) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (typically 7 marks each). Students must answer *either* Option A *or* Option B completely.

---

### **Option A (14 Marks) — Detailed Analytical Question**

**Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]:**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various factors that govern the site selection of a **steam power plant**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 250 MW thermal power plant operates with a net plant efficiency of **36%** and uses coal with a calorific value of **3500 kcal/kg**. The cooling water temperature rise in the condenser is **9°C**. Calculate:
  - (i) The hourly coal consumption in tonnes
  - (ii) The cooling water mass flow rate required in kg/s
  - (iii) Comment on the suitability of a river with **15000 m³/hr** dependable flow. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Site Selection Factors for Steam Power Plant (7 marks)**

The site selection of a steam (coal-based) power plant involves systematic evaluation of the following factors:

**1. Availability of Fuel (Coal):**
The plant's annual coal requirement is huge. For a 250 MW plant at 80% PLF, annual coal consumption ≈ 1.75 million tonnes. The site should be within economic transport distance from the coal mine, ideally via rail or MGR (Merry-Go-Round) system. *(1 mark)*

**2. Availability of Cooling Water:**
Cooling water is required at approximately **3.5–4.5 m³/MWh**. The source (river, lake, sea, or cooling tower makeup) must be reliable year-round. Quality matters — total dissolved solids (TDS) should be within condenser tube material tolerance. *(1 mark)*

**3. Geotechnical and Geological Conditions:**
The soil must have adequate bearing capacity (typically >15–20 t/m² for turbo-generator foundations). Site should be above the **highest flood level (HFL)** with a margin of 1–2 m. Seismic zone classification is mandatory. *(1 mark)*

**4. Distance from Load Center:**
Power transmission over long distances incurs losses and costs. CEA guidelines recommend plants within **500 km** of major load centers unless transmission infrastructure is robust. *(0.5 marks)*

**5. Transportation Facilities:**
Rail siding for coal delivery and ash disposal. Road connectivity for heavy equipment (boiler drums, turbine rotors weighing hundreds of tonnes). Proximity to a port (for imported coal). *(0.5 marks)*

**6. Land Availability:**
Approximately **200–400 acres per 1000 MW** is required for main plant, coal handling, ash pond, township, and switchyard. Land should be affordable and free from displacement issues. *(0.5 marks)*

**7. Environmental Considerations:**
Clearance from State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) and Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC). Buffer zone from habitations. Compliance with fly ash notification. *(0.5 marks)*

**8. Other Factors:** Climate (affects condenser back-pressure), manpower availability, expansion potential, and political/regulatory climate. *(1 mark for completeness)*

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Plant capacity: $P = 250$ MW $= 250 \times 10^6$ W
- Net efficiency: $\eta = 36\% = 0.36$
- Calorific value: $\text{GCV} = 3500$ kcal/kg $= 3500 \times 4186$ J/kg $= 14.651 \times 10^6$ J/kg
- Cooling water $\Delta T = 9°C = 9$ K
- $C_p = 4186$ J/kg·K

**Solution:**

**(i) Hourly coal consumption:**

Energy generated per hour:
$$E_h = 250 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 9.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ J/hr}$$

Heat input required per hour (using efficiency):
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{E_h}{\eta} = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{11}}{0.36} = 2.5 \times 10^{12} \text{ J/hr}$$

Coal mass per hour:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}{\text{GCV}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{12}}{14.651 \times 10^6}$$
$$\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = 170{,}640 \text{ kg/hr} = 170.64 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Cooling water mass flow rate:**

Heat rejected to condenser:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - E_h = 2.5 \times 10^{12} - 9.0 \times 10^{11}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = 1.6 \times 10^{12} \text{ J/hr} = 4.444 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$$

Converting to per-second basis:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{12}}{3600} = 4.444 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$$

Cooling water mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}}}{C_p \times \Delta T} = \dfrac{4.444 \times 10^8}{4186 \times 9}$$
$$\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{4.444 \times 10^8}{37{,}674} = 11{,}798 \text{ kg/s}$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) River suitability:**

Convert river flow to mass flow (assuming $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³):
$$\dot{m}_{\text{river}} = 15{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{3600} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 4.167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\dot{m}_{\text{river}} = 4167 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Comparison:**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{required}} = 11{,}798 \text{ kg/s} \gg \dot{m}_{\text{river}} = 4167 \text{ kg/s}$$

The river can supply only **~35%** of the required cooling water.

**Conclusion:** The river is **NOT suitable** as a sole once-through cooling water source. The site would require either:
- A **cooling tower** with recirculating mode (using the river as makeup water only — typically ~5% of oncethrough requirement)
- **Multiple intake points** along the river
- Rejection of the site

*(3 marks for calculation + comment)*

**Total Part (b): 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks**

---

### **Option B (14 Marks) — Alternative Conceptual Question**

**Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]:**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **site selection criteria for a hydroelectric power plant**. Compare them with those of a steam power plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **environmental and safety considerations** that make nuclear power plant site selection uniquely stringent. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Hydroelectric vs. Steam Plant Site Selection (7 marks)**

**Site Selection Factors for Hydroelectric Plants:**

**1. Water Availability (Hydrology):**
- **Runoff data:** Minimum 30–50 years of river gauging records
- **Annual rainfall** in catchment area (>1000 mm preferred)
- **Minimum dry-season flow** must sustain generation
- **Storage capacity:** Reservoir volume vs. annual inflow ratio determines firm power *(1 mark)*

**2. Head (Hydraulic):**
- **Gross head** = Difference between reservoir water level and tailrace level
- **Net head** = Gross head − friction losses in water conductor system
- Higher head = smaller water flow needed for same power *(0.5 marks)*

**3. Topography and Geology:**
- **Dam site** should have narrow, V-shaped valley for dam length efficiency
- **Foundation rock** must be sound bedrock (granite/gneiss preferred); avoid karstic or fractured zones
- **Reservoir area** should have minimum leakage-prone geology
- Avoid high sediment-load rivers *(1 mark)*

**4. Submergence and Rehabilitation:**
- Land submergence area should have minimum habitation/forest
- R&R (Resettlement and Rehabilitation) costs must be socially and economically feasible
- Environmental impact on flora/fauna *(0.5 marks)*

**5. Distance from Load Center:**
- Unlike thermal, hydro plants are usually **remote** but their **low marginal cost** justifies long transmission lines
- Pumped-storage hydro plants are intentionally near load centers *(0.5 marks)*

**Comparison with Steam Power Plant:**

| Aspect | Hydro Plant | Steam Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel | Water (renewable) | Coal (depleting) |
| Water Use | Power generation | Cooling only |
| Land Submergence | **Huge** (reservoir) | Limited |
| Time to Construct | 8–15 years | 4–6 years |
| Geological Demand | **Most stringent** | Moderate |
| Capacity Factor | 40%–60% | 60%–85% |
| Pollution | Minimal | **High** (ash, SO₂, CO₂) |

*(3 marks for detailed comparison)*

**Total Part (a): 7 marks**

---

#### **Part (b) — Nuclear Power Plant Site Selection Uniqueness (7 marks)**

The site selection for nuclear power plants is governed by **stringent safety and environmental requirements** dictated by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** and the **Atomic Energy Commission**.

**1. Exclusion Zone (Population Criterion):**
A circular zone of **1.6 km radius** around the plant must be **completely unpopulated** by permanent residents. An additional **5 km sterilized zone** around that allows limited population with restrictions. Beyond that, a **25 km emergency planning zone** exists. *(1.5 marks)*

**2. Geological and Seismic Stability:**
- The site must lie in a **low-seismicity zone** (preferably Zone II or low III per IS 1893)
- **No active faults** within 5–8 km radius
- Stable bedrock for foundation
- No history of liquefaction or landslides *(1 mark)*

**3. Distance from Water Body (for emergency cooling):**
- **Ultimate Heat Sink (UHS):** A large, reliable water body must be within proximity for emergency core cooling in case of station blackout
- Sea, large lake, or cooling pond — minimum sustainable supply even under worst drought *(1 mark)*

**4. Meteorological Conditions:**
- Wind direction (prevailing wind should **not** blow toward major population centers
- Atmospheric dispersion characteristics (Pasquill stability classes) determine accident dose calculations
- Tornado and cyclone frequency *(0.5 marks)*

**5. Aircraft Crash Probability:**
- Site should not be directly under flight paths
- Far from airports (typically >5 km for major airports) *(0.5 marks)*

**6. Hydrological Considerations:**
- Site must be above **probable maximum flood (PMF)** level
- Tsunami protection (for coastal sites) — beyond design basis flood
- No upstream dam whose failure could endanger plant *(1 mark)*

**7. Security and Access Control:**
- Secure perimeter fencing
- Restricted access roads
- Hidden from public gaze (typically green cover)
- Distance from international borders *(0.5 marks)*

**8. Environmental Considerations:**
- Low population density zone (typically <1000 persons/km² within 5 km)
- Marine ecology (if coastal) — thermal discharge impact on aquatic life
- Radioactive effluent management — liquid and gaseous pathways controlled

*(1 mark for environmental + summary)*

**Total Part (b): 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Memorize the comparison table** — many students write paragraphs instead of crisp points. KTU examiners reward **structured answers** (tables, bullet points, diagrams).
> 2. **Always state numerical values with units.** Writing "high water requirement" loses marks; writing "4 m³/MWh" gains full credit.
> 3. **For nuclear sites, mention "AERB"** by name — it's a free half-mark.
> 4. **Don't confuse hydro "head" with "pressure"** — they are related but distinct.
> 5. In numericals, **show all steps** — even if the final answer is correct, missing intermediate steps costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Site selection is irreversible** — must be done before plant commissioning; mistakes cost crores.
- [ ] **Primary factors** (must-haves): Fuel, Water, Geotech, Load center, Environmental clearance.
- [ ] **Secondary factors** (should-haves): Transport, Land, Manpower, Climate, Expansion.
- [ ] **Steam plant priority list:** Coal → Cooling Water → Ash disposal → Load proximity → Land.
- [ ] **Hydro plant priority list:** Catchment hydrology → Head → Dam geology → Reservoir R&R → Load distance.
- [ ] **Nuclear plant priority list:** Seismic stability → Exclusion zone → UHS → Wind direction → Security.
- [ ] **Diesel plant priority list:** Load proximity → Fuel logistics → Modular distributed deployment.
- [ ] **Cooling water rule of thumb:** **~4 m³ per MWh** generated.
- [ ] **Land rule of thumb:** **~200–400 acres per 1000 MW** thermal.
- [ ] **Pit-head plants preferred** when coal source >500 km away.
- [ ] **Nuclear exclusion zone:** **1.6 km** (sterile) + **5 km** (sterilized) + **25 km** (emergency planning).
- [ ] **Typical thermal efficiency:** **35%–45%**; **Typical hydro η:** **85%–92%**.
- [ ] **Governing bodies to mention:** AERB (nuclear), CEA (planning), MoEFCC (environment), SPCB (state clearance).
- [ ] **Key examples:** Pit-head (Vindhyachal, Rihand); Coastal (Mundra, Krishnapatnam); Hydro (Bhakra, Tehri); Nuclear (Tarapur, Kudankulam).
- [ ] **Break-even distance** for coal transport vs. transmission: typically **~500 km** in modern EHV systems.
- [ ] **Once-through cooling** requires huge water; **cooling towers** reduce demand to ~5% of oncethrough.
- [ ] **Pump-back / pumped storage** hydro plants are intentionally near load centers for peak shaving.
- [ ] **Always state plant type first** before listing factors — site criteria change drastically with technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Handling, Storage and Burning Systems; Dust and Ash Handling System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Handling, Storage and Burning Systems; Dust and Ash Handling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Fuel Handling, Storage & Burning" in a Thermal Power Station?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Fuel Handling, Storage and Burning System** of a thermal power plant is the integrated set of mechanical equipment used to receive, unload, store, prepare, convey, pulverize, and combust coal (or alternative solid/liquid/gaseous fuels) in a controlled and continuous manner to deliver heat energy at the required rate for steam generation. It ensures that **fuel of the correct size, moisture, and fineness** is delivered to the furnace at the precise firing rate demanded by the boiler load.

The system is conventionally split into **three sub-stages**:

| Sub-stage | Primary Function | Key Equipment |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| **Fuel Handling** | Receipt, unloading, weighing, transfer | Wagon tipplers, grab buckets, conveyors, feeders |
| **Fuel Storage** | Buffer stock for 30–90 days | Stockyard, bunkers, silos |
| **Fuel Burning** | Combustion of prepared fuel | Stokers, pulverizers + burners, cyclone furnace |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must be able to (i) draw and explain the **coal handling plant layout**, (ii) compare **overfeed and underfeed stokers**, (iii) describe **pulverized fuel firing** with all its ancillaries, (iv) explain the operation of **dust collectors (cyclone, bag filter, ESP)** and (v) describe **ash handling systems** (wet, dry, semi-dry). Numerical problems based on the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** and **conveyor capacity** are routinely asked.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plant as a Kitchen"

Imagine running a massive industrial kitchen that must feed **steam-hungry boilers** 24 × 7.

- **Fuel Handling** = The supply chain that brings raw grain (coal) from the farm gate (wagon) to the kitchen (bunker).
- **Fuel Storage** = The warehouse/godown where grain is kept safe from rain (moisture) and pests.
- **Fuel Burning (Pulverization + Combustion)** = The grinder + the gas stove. You cannot cook whole grains — you must **grind to fine flour** (pulverize) before you can **burn it efficiently** in a controlled flame (burner).
- **Dust Handling** = The chimney filter and exhaust hood that trap fine soot (PM₂.₅/PM₁₀) before the kitchen exhaust pollutes the neighbourhood.
- **Ash Handling** = The dustbin + waste compactor that removes non-combustible residue (clinker + fly ash) so the stove stays clean and efficient.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Design Rule of Thumb:**
> Modern 500–800 MW units use **Pulverized Fuel (PF) firing**, where coal is ground to **< 75 μm (200 mesh)** so that **~70 % passes through a 200-mesh sieve** (the "70 % through 200 mesh" rule). Older units (≤ 100 MW) used **stokers** because coal was simply crushed to **10–25 mm** before being pushed onto a grate.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Standard storage period for thermal power plant coal:** **30 days (mandatory minimum), 60–90 days (recommended).**
- **Coal grindability index (Hardgrove):** typical Indian coal = **50–60 HGI** (lower = harder).
- **Pulverized fuel fineness:** **70 % through 200 mesh (75 μm)**.
- **Combustion air excess:** **15–25 %** over stoichiometric for PF firing.
- **ESP typical efficiency:** **> 99.5 %** for modern utility ESPs.
- **Bottom ash fraction:** **~15–20 %** of total ash; **Fly ash:** **~80–85 %**.
- **Calorific value of Indian coal (bituminous):** **3500–5500 kcal/kg**.
- **Drift velocity (w) in ESP:** typically **0.03–0.15 m/s**.
- **Specific gas volume from coal combustion:** ~**5–6 Nm³/kg of coal** at stoichiometric air.

## 1.4 Subsystem Boundary Diagram

The full "fuel-to-flue-gas" boundary encompasses:

$$
\text{Coal Wagon} \rightarrow \text{Tippler} \rightarrow \text{Stockyard} \rightarrow \text{Crusher House} \rightarrow \text{Bunker} \rightarrow \text{Pulverizer} \rightarrow \text{Burner} \rightarrow \text{Furnace} \rightarrow \text{Ash Hopper} \rightarrow \text{Ash Handling}
$$

with the **dust collector (ESP/Bag filter)** acting in parallel on the flue gas side after the **air-preheater (APH)** and **economizer**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coal Handling Plant (CHP) — Operational Logic

### Step-by-Step Flow Logic

1. **Receipt of Coal:** Coal arrives via **rail wagons (BOXN/BOXNHL)**, **trucks**, or **barges/inland waterways**. A weighbridge records the gross/tare mass.
2. **Unloading:**
   - *Wagon Tippler:* Rotates the wagon by 145°–160° to dump the entire load. Capacity: **4000–6000 TPH** per tippler.
   - *Grab Bucket (Bridge Crane):* Used when wagon tippler is unavailable; capacity **5–15 T per grab**.
   - *Barge Unloader:* Clamshell on portal crane, used at coastal stations.
3. **Primary Crushing:** Jaw or gyratory crusher reduces **run-of-mine (ROM)** coal from **≤ 300 mm to ≤ 100 mm**.
4. **Conveying:** **Belt conveyors** (800–2000 mm wide) carry coal to the stockyard at **1.5–3.5 m/s** with a 16°–18° maximum incline.
5. **Stockyard Storage:** Coal is stacked via **stacker** and reclaimed via **reclaimer (bucket-wheel)** or **dozers + front-end loaders**. Stack height = **10–15 m**.
6. **Secondary Crushing & Screening (if any):** For stoker-fired units, crushing to **10–25 mm**.
7. **Transfer to Bunker:** Tripper car distributes coal into the **bunker/coal silo** above the pulverizer.
8. **Pulverizing (only for PF units):** Ball/Bowl/Hammer mill grinds coal to **< 75 μm**.
9. **Pneumatic/Mechanical Conveying to Burner:** Primary air carries pulverized coal (PC) through **coal pipes** to the **burner nozzles**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three-stage conveying (Tippler → Conveyor → Bunker → Pulverizer)?**
> Because the plant needs **continuous, surge-free supply**. The stockyard is a 30-day buffer, the bunker is a 6–12 hour buffer, and the pulverizer is a "just-in-time" feeder. This nested buffering eliminates downtime from supply disruptions.

### 2.1.1 Coal Bunker Sizing (KTU Calculation)

The bunker must store coal for at least **6–8 hours of full boiler load (BMCR)**.

$$
\boxed{\; V_{\text{bunker}} = \frac{P_{\text{boiler}} \times \text{SFC}}{3600 \times \rho_{\text{coal}} \times \eta_{\text{filling}}} \;}
$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{bunker}}$ = bunker volume (m³)
- $P_{\text{boiler}}$ = boiler steam output (kg/h)
- $\text{SFC}$ = specific fuel consumption (kg coal/kg steam)
- $\rho_{\text{coal}}$ = bulk density of coal (≈ **900 kg/m³** for crushed coal)
- $\eta_{\text{filling}}$ = filling efficiency (**0.8** for conical hopper)

## 2.2 Belt Conveyor Capacity Formula

The mass flow rate of coal through a belt conveyor is given by:

$$
\boxed{\; Q_{\text{coal}} = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho_{\text{bulk}} \cdot k \;}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $Q_{\text{coal}}$ | Coal throughput | TPH (Tonnes Per Hour) |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of load on belt | m² |
| $v$ | Belt linear velocity | 1.5–3.5 m/s |
| $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ | Bulk density of coal | **900 kg/m³ = 0.9 t/m³** |
| $k$ | Inclination factor | 1.0 (flat) to 0.5 (steep) |

For a **troughed belt** with idler angle of **35°**, the cross-sectional area of the loaded material is empirically:

$$
A \approx 0.19 \cdot B^2 \quad \text{(B in metres, A in m²)}
$$

Hence the **rule-of-thumb thumb rule** widely used:

$$
\boxed{\; Q_{\text{coal}} \, (\text{TPH}) \approx 0.32 \cdot B^2 \cdot v \cdot \rho_{\text{bulk}} \;}
$$

## 2.3 Stoker Firing Systems — Comparative Analysis

A **stoker** is a mechanical grate that feeds, distributes, and burns coal on a moving surface, while primary air passes upward through the bed. Classified by the direction of coal feed relative to air:

### (A) Overfeed Stokers (Coal and air move in same direction)

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Chain Grate** | Endless chain of cast-iron links with tuyeres; moves coal forward at **1.5–4 m/h** | Medium-size boilers **2–25 TPH** |
| **Traveling Grate** | Continuous metal belt with perforated plates; coal fed at front, ash discharged at rear | **25–100 TPH** boilers |
| **Spreader Stoker** | Coal thrown into furnace by rotating paddles, burns in suspension + on grate | Coal with **high moisture & clinkering ash** |

### (B) Underfeed Stokers (Coal fed from below against air)

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single-Ram** | One ram pushes coal up into retort | Small boilers **< 4 TPH** |
| **Multiple-Ram** | Several staggered rams for even distribution | **4–25 TPH** |
| **Travelling Spreader** | Hybrid: spreads + overfeed | Up to **100 TPH** |

### Combustion Rate on Grate (KTU numerical)

$$
\boxed{\; \dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = R_g \cdot A_g \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; A_g = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \cdot \text{GCV}}{Q_{\text{release}}} \;}
$$

Where:
- $R_g$ = grate combustion rate release intensity (kg/m²·h)
- $A_g$ = effective grate area (m²)
- $Q_{\text{release}}$ = heat release rate per unit grate area (**≈ 1.4–2.5 GJ/m²·h** for chain-grate, **2.5–4.0 GJ/m²·h** for traveling grate, **4–6 GJ/m²·h** for spreader)

## 2.4 Pulverized Fuel (PF) Firing System — The Workhorse of Modern Plants

### Pulverizer Types

| Pulverizer | Operating Principle | Capacity Range | Best Suited For |
|------------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Ball Mill (Tube Mill)** | Steel balls (30–60 mm) cascading inside rotating drum | **10–70 T/h** per mill | Soft to medium-hard coal (HGI > 50) |
| **Bowl Mill (Roller Mill)** | 2–3 rollers pressed against rotating bowl by springs/hydraulics | **3–60 T/h** per mill | Most bituminous coals |
| **Hammer Mill** | High-speed swinging hammers impact coal | **5–50 T/h** | High-moisture, low-abrasion coal |
| **Attrition Mill** | Coal particles grind each other in turbulent air stream | **5–25 T/h** | Drying + grinding combined |

### PF System Components

1. **Raw Coal Feeder** (gravimetric / volumetric) → 2. **Pulverizer** → 3. **Classifier (static/dynamic)** rejects oversize → 4. **Exhauster/Primary Air Fan** → 5. **Coal Pipes** → 6. **Burner Nozzles**.

### Primary Air Functions

> [!NOTE]
> Primary air performs **four simultaneous jobs**: (i) dries the coal, (ii) transports pulverized coal, (iii) pre-heats it for ignition, (iv) supplies ~20–25 % of stoichiometric air for combustion. Primary air temperature for bituminous coal ≈ **150–250 °C**.

### Burner Classification

| Burner Type | Orientation | Used In |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **Tangential (Corner-fired)** | Firing from 4 corners forming a virtual fireball | Most Indian 500/660 MW units |
| **Wall-fired (Opposed-fired)** | Burners on opposite walls (front-back) | 200–500 MW |
| **Cyclone Burner** | Horizontal, tangential, with cyclone combustor | Coal with high ash fusion temp |

## 2.5 Cyclone Furnace

A **cyclone furnace** is a horizontal, water-cooled cylindrical combustor in which crushed coal (≤ 6 mm) is injected tangentially with primary air along with secondary air, creating a **high-g cyclone vortex** (G = centrifugal/gravity ratio). Combustion is completed inside the cyclone; molten ash forms a slag layer on the walls and drains into the bottom ash hopper.

**Advantages:**
- Can fire **high-ash, low-grade coal** (up to **45 % ash**).
- High heat release rate: **18–25 GJ/m³·h** (10× higher than PF furnace).
- Smaller furnace envelope.

**Disadvantages:**
- High NOx formation (~1200–1500 ppm).
- Slag handling complexity.

## 2.6 Dust Handling — Particulate Emission Control

Pollutants of concern: **Particulate Matter (PM)**, **SO₂**, **NOx**. For dust, three principal devices:

### (A) Mechanical (Cyclone) Separator

Centrifugal separation: gas enters **tangentially**, particles thrown to wall by **centrifugal force**, slide down to dust hopper; clean gas exits through the central vortex finder.

**Critical (cut) particle diameter:**

$$
\boxed{\; d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu_b \cdot B}{\pi N_e \cdot \rho_p \cdot v_i}} \;}
$$

Where:
- $\mu_b$ = gas viscosity
- $B$ = inlet width
- $N_e$ = effective number of turns (≈ **5–10** for reverse-flow cyclone)
- $\rho_p$ = particle density
- $v_i$ = inlet velocity (typically **15–25 m/s**)

**Efficiency for individual particle diameter:**

$$
\boxed{\; \eta_d = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{d_{50}}{d}\right)^2} \;}
$$

### (B) Fabric Filter (Bag House)

Filtration through fabric bags. Efficiency: **> 99.9 %**, even for sub-micron particles. Bag life: **2–4 years**. Operating temperature limit depends on fabric: **Polyester ≤ 130 °C**, **Nomex ≤ 200 °C**, **PTFE ≤ 260 °C**.

> [!TIP]
> Bag filters are **must-use** for **Indian coal** with high **limestone / Ca-content**, where ESP resistivity becomes very high (>10¹² Ω·cm).

### (C) Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) — The Deutsch–Anderson Equation

ESP charges dust particles via a discharge electrode (**–30 to –70 kV DC**) and collects them on grounded collecting plates. Migration velocity $w$ (drift velocity) is the **terminal velocity of a charged particle towards the collection plate**.

$$
\boxed{\; \eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w \, A}{Q}\right) \;}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|--------|---------|-------|
| $\eta$ | Fractional collection efficiency (e.g., 0.995 for 99.5 %) | — |
| $w$ | Effective drift (migration) velocity | m/s |
| $A$ | Total collection plate area | m² |
| $Q$ | Volumetric gas flow rate | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relation:** Doubling $w$ **halves** the plate area $A$ needed for the same efficiency. Doubling $A$ **does not double** $\eta$ — efficiency rises asymptotically. To go from 99 % to 99.9 % you need **> 6× the area**. This is why **retrofit ESPs are expensive**.

### ESP Specific Collection Area (SCA) — KTU Important

$$
\boxed{\; \text{SCA} = \dfrac{A}{Q} = -\dfrac{1}{w}\ln(1 - \eta) \quad [\text{m}^2/(\text{1000 m}^3/\text{h})] \;}
$$

Typical values: **30–80 m²/(1000 m³/h)** for utility boilers.

## 2.7 Ash Handling System

Ash is generated in two streams:

- **Bottom Ash (~15–20 %):** Coarse, falls to furnace hopper, quenched in water.
- **Fly Ash (~80–85 %):** Fine, carried with flue gas, captured in ESP/bag filter hoppers.

### Classification of Ash Handling Systems

| System | Medium | Method | Application |
|--------|--------|--------|-------------|
| **Wet (Hydraulic)** | Water under high pressure (10–60 bar) | Sluiceways, ash slurry pumps, ash ponds | Legacy plants |
| **Dry (Mechanical/Pneumatic)** | Compressed air or screw conveyors / belt conveyors | Vacuum/pressure pneumatic or drag chain | Modern plants (recommended) |
| **Semi-Dry** | Pre-wetting to ~20 % moisture | Conditioning + conveying | High-ash Indian coal |
| **High-concentration slurry (HCS)** | Slurry at 70–80 % solids | Pipeline transport | Long-distance disposal (>50 km) |

### Wet Ash Handling Capacity Formula

$$
\boxed{\; Q_{\text{ash slurry}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{ash}}}{C_w \cdot \rho_w} \;}
$$

Typical **water-to-ash ratio** in wet sluicing: **10:1 to 20:1** by mass.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{coal}} = 3600 A v \rho_{\text{bulk}} k$ | Belt conveyor TPH |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{coal}} \approx 0.32 B^2 v \rho$ | Belt conveyor thumb rule |
| 3 | $V_{\text{bunker}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \text{SFC}}{3600 \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_f}$ | Bunker sizing |
| 4 | $A_g = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \cdot \text{GCV}}{Q_{\text{release}}}$ | Grate area |
| 5 | $R_g = \dot{m}_{\text{coal}} / A_g$ | Grate firing rate |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{ESP}} = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ | ESP efficiency |
| 7 | $\text{SCA} = -\dfrac{\ln(1-\eta)}{w}$ | ESP plate area sizing |
| 8 | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu_b B}{\pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$ | Cyclone cut size |
| 9 | $\eta_d = 1 / [1 + (d_{50}/d)^2]$ | Cyclone grade efficiency |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{slurry}} = m_{\text{ash}} / (C_w \rho_w)$ | Wet ash flow |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every coal-fired plant in India (NTPC, Tata Power, Adani) must design these systems per **MoEFCC emission norms** (PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³ for new plants) and **Fly Ash Notification 2021** (100 % ash utilization mandatory). The ESP is the largest single equipment cost item after the boiler itself.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of ESP Collection Efficiency (Deutsch–Anderson Equation)

The **Deutsch equation** is derived by considering a differential slice of plate through which gas flows at uniform velocity $u = Q/A$. A dust particle entering at a distance $y$ from the plate moves toward the plate at velocity $w$. After time $t = dx/u$, it travels a horizontal distance $dx$ and vertical distance $w \cdot dt = w \cdot dx / u$.

Consider a particle entering at height $y$ from the plate. It is collected if it can travel the full vertical distance $y$ by the time the gas reaches the end of the plate. The time to traverse plate length $L$ is $L/u$. The vertical distance covered in that time is:

$$
\Delta y = w \cdot \frac{L}{u} = \frac{w \, L \, A}{Q \, L} = \frac{w A_{\text{slice}}}{Q_{\text{slice}}}
$$

The fraction of particles NOT collected (passing through) is the ratio of uncollected height to total inlet height. Using a first-order differential equation with the inlet concentration $n_0$ and after-plate concentration $n$:

$$
\frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{w \, dx \, B}{Q} = -\frac{w \, dA}{Q}
$$

Integrating from $A = 0$ to $A$ (total plate area):

$$
\int_{n_0}^{n} \frac{dn}{n} = -\frac{w}{Q} \int_{0}^{A} dA
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{n}{n_0}\right) = -\frac{wA}{Q}
$$

Exponentiating:

$$
\frac{n}{n_0} = e^{-wA/Q}
$$

Since collection efficiency $\eta$ is the fraction removed:

$$
\boxed{\; \eta = 1 - \frac{n}{n_0} = 1 - e^{-wA/Q} \;}
$$

This is the celebrated **Deutsch–Anderson equation**, foundational to all ESP design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of Deutsch Equation:** It assumes (i) uniform gas flow, (ii) uniform particle charge, (iii) uniform drift velocity, (iv) no re-entrainment. In real ESPs, **dust re-entrainment** and **back corona** reduce efficiency by **5–15 %** from the ideal value.

## 3.2 Numerical Problem — ESP Plate Area Sizing (KTU Pattern)

### **Problem:**
A 500 MW boiler burns **150 TPH of coal** with **35 % ash** content. Ash split: **80 % fly ash, 20 % bottom ash**. Flue gas flow rate through ESP = **2.5 × 10⁶ Nm³/h**. Drift velocity for the coal ash = **0.08 m/s**. Required collection efficiency = **99.5 %**.
**Find:** (a) Specific Collection Area (SCA), (b) Total plate area, (c) Number of fields if each field is **4 m high × 12 m long**, with **30 plates per field**.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert gas flow to m³/s.

$$
Q = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{3600} = 694.44 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Required SCA in SI units.

$$
\text{SCA}_{\text{SI}} = -\frac{1}{w}\ln(1 - \eta) = -\frac{1}{0.08}\ln(1 - 0.995)
$$

Compute inside:

$$
\ln(1 - 0.995) = \ln(0.005) = -5.298
$$

Hence:

$$
\text{SCA}_{\text{SI}} = -\frac{-5.298}{0.08} = 66.23 \;\text{m}^2/(\text{m}^3/\text{s})
$$

Convert to standard units (m² per 1000 m³/h):

$$
\text{SCA} = 66.23 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 238.4 \;\text{m}^2/(1000\;\text{m}^3/\text{h})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **higher than the typical range 30–80 m²/(1000 m³/h)** quoted in textbooks because we are demanding **99.5 %** efficiency — a logarithmic penalty! KTU students often lose 2 marks for missing the unit conversion.

**Step 3:** Total plate area.

$$
A = Q \times \text{SCA}_{\text{SI}} = 694.44 \times 66.23 = 45989 \;\text{m}^2
$$

Or in standard form:

$$
A = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6}{1000} \times 238.4 \approx 5.96 \times 10^5 \;\text{m}^2 \quad \text{(same after correction)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: Different unit combinations give the same physical area; the **board examiner expects consistent units**. Always state unit conversions explicitly.

**Step 4:** Area per plate (one collecting plate + one gas channel = 2 sides).

$$
A_{\text{plate side}} = 4 \times 12 = 48 \;\text{m}^2
$$

Effective area per plate (both sides collecting):

$$
A_{\text{plate}} = 2 \times 48 = 96 \;\text{m}^2
$$

Total plates needed:

$$
N = \frac{A}{A_{\text{plate}}} = \frac{45989}{96} \approx 479 \;\text{plates}
$$

**Step 5:** Plates per field (assume 30 plates/field in parallel).

$$
N_{\text{fields}} = \frac{479}{30} \approx 16 \;\text{fields}
$$

ESP is configured as **4 sections × 4 passes = 16 fields**.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — Belt Conveyor Sizing

### **Problem:**
A belt conveyor must deliver **1200 TPH of coal (bulk density 0.9 t/m³)** at **2.0 m/s** on a flat belt. The conveyor has **35° troughing idlers**. Find the **minimum belt width**.

### **Solution:**

Using thumb rule:

$$
Q = 0.32 \cdot B^2 \cdot v \cdot \rho
$$

Substitute:

$$
1200 = 0.32 \cdot B^2 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.9
$$

$$
1200 = 0.576 \, B^2
$$

$$
B^2 = 2083.3 \Rightarrow B = 45.6 \;\text{m} \; \text{(unrealistic!)}
$$

This indicates the **thumb rule has limitations for very high capacities**. Use the **detailed cross-section formula**:

For 35° troughed belt, load cross-section:

$$
A = 0.1036 \, B^2 + 0.1227 \, B \cdot h \quad (\text{where } h \text{ = surcharge height})
$$

Solving iteratively with $Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$:

Try **B = 1.6 m, A ≈ 0.49 m²**:

$$
Q = 3600 \times 0.49 \times 2.0 \times 0.9 = 3175 \;\text{TPH}
$$

Oversized. Try **B = 1.2 m, A ≈ 0.275 m²**:

$$
Q = 3600 \times 0.275 \times 2.0 \times 0.9 = 1782 \;\text{TPH}
$$

Still high. Try **B = 1.0 m, A ≈ 0.19 m²**:

$$
Q = 3600 \times 0.19 \times 2.0 \times 0.9 = 1231 \;\text{TPH} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Select standard belt width = 1000 mm (Belt class = 1000)**.

## 3.4 Numerical — Bunker Sizing

### **Problem:**
A 210 MW boiler has **steam output = 770 TPH**, SFC = **0.55 kg coal/kg steam**. Bulk density of crushed coal = **900 kg/m³**, filling factor = **0.8**. Find bunker volume for **8-hour storage**.

### **Solution:**

$$
V_{\text{bunker}} = \frac{P \times \text{SFC} \times t}{3600 \times \rho \times \eta_f}
$$

Where $t$ = storage time = 8 h = 8 × 3600 s.

Hourly coal rate:

$$
\dot{m} = 770 \times 1000 \times 0.55 = 423500 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

Total coal for 8 hours:

$$
m_{\text{total}} = 423500 \times 8 = 3388000 \;\text{kg} = 3388 \;\text{T}
$$

Bunker volume:

$$
V = \frac{3388000}{900 \times 0.8} = 4706 \;\text{m}^3
$$

This is typically split into **2–4 silos** of ~1200 m³ each.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stoker vs PF vs Cyclone

| Parameter | Stoker (Chain/Traveling) | Pulverized Fuel | Cyclone Furnace |
|-----------|--------------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Coal size** | 10–25 mm | < 75 μm (70 % thru 200 mesh) | < 6 mm |
| **Heat release (GJ/m²·h)** | 1.4–6.0 | 1.5–2.5 | 18–25 |
| **Excess air (%)** | 30–60 | 15–25 | 10–15 |
| **Boiler efficiency (%)** | 80–85 | 88–92 | 88–90 |
| **Ash in coal tolerated (%)** | Up to 25 | Up to 40 | Up to 45 |
| **Capacity range (TPH)** | < 100 | 100–2000 | 50–500 |
| **NOx level (ppm)** | 200–400 | 400–600 | 1200–1500 |
| **Capital cost (rel.)** | Low | High | Very high |
| **Maintenance** | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Indian coal suitability** | Excellent | Good (preferred) | Excellent |

## 3.6 Coal Moisture Drying in Pulverizer — Energy Balance

Moisture removal in primary air:

$$
m_{\text{dry air}} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = m_{\text{water}} \cdot (h_{g} - h_{f,\text{coal}})
$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = primary air enthalpy (inlet)
- $h_2$ = primary air enthalpy (outlet, ~**70 °C** for safety against ignition)
- $h_g$ = enthalpy of vapour leaving coal surface (~**100 °C saturated**)

Re-arranging for required air mass per kg coal:

$$
\boxed{\; m_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{m_w (h_g - h_{f,\text{coal}})}{(h_2 - h_1)} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For high-moisture Indian coal (10–15 % inherent moisture), this limits the minimum primary air temperature to **250–300 °C** to avoid mill plugging.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any **six advantages** of pulverized fuel firing over stoker firing.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher combustion efficiency** (88–92 %) due to finer particle size and uniform mixing.
2. **Faster response to load changes** — pulverized coal can be cut off instantly by stopping feeders.
3. **Higher heat release rate** — no need for large grate area.
4. **Suitable for low-grade, high-ash coal** (up to 40 % ash content).
5. **Lower excess air** requirement (15–25 %) → higher boiler efficiency.
6. **Smaller furnace volume** per MW capacity → reduced boiler capital cost.
7. **Better flame control** and ability to use low-grade fuel oils as auxiliary support.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Write a short note on **Deutsch–Anderson equation** and state the effect of doubling drift velocity on collection efficiency.*

**Model Answer:**

The Deutsch–Anderson equation gives the fractional collection efficiency $\eta$ of an electrostatic precipitator:

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right)
$$

where $w$ is the drift velocity (m/s), $A$ is the collection plate area (m²), and $Q$ is the gas flow rate (m³/s).

**Effect of doubling $w$:** Since $\eta$ depends on the **exponential of $w$**, doubling $w$ (with $A$ and $Q$ fixed) **increases $\eta$ significantly but never beyond 100 %**. Numerically, if original $wA/Q = 1$, then $\eta = 63.2$ %. After doubling, $wA/Q = 2$, then $\eta = 86.5$ %. The gain is most dramatic at low $wA/Q$ values.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a **neat schematic**, explain the **complete coal handling plant** of a modern thermal power station. List the function of each major equipment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 500 MW thermal power station uses **coal containing 30 % ash**. The plant operates at **85 % capacity factor** with **plant efficiency 38 %** and **coal GCV = 4500 kcal/kg**. Calculate: **(i)** Annual coal requirement in tonnes, **(ii)** Annual fly ash generation in tonnes, **(iii)** Number of wagons required annually if each wagon carries **58 tonnes**.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Coal Handling Plant — Sequence of Operations:**

1. **Coal Receipt:** Coal arrives by rail in BOXN/BOXNHL wagons, by road trucks, or by inland waterways/barges. Each wagon has a tare mark; weighbridges record gross mass.

2. **Wagon Tippler:** A rotary tippler clamps the wagon and rotates it by 145°–160°, dumping the entire load into a receiving hopper below. Modern **rotary side-tipplers** handle **4000–6000 TPH** per unit. A **vibro-feeder** below the hopper ensures steady discharge onto the conveyor.

3. **Vibrating Screen + Primary Crusher:** Oversized lumps (≥ 300 mm) are broken by a jaw or gyratory crusher to ≤ 100 mm. Magnetic separators remove tramp iron (bolts, nuts, scrap).

4. **Belt Conveyors:** The crushed coal travels via **troughed belt conveyors** (1000–2000 mm wide) at **1.5–3.5 m/s**. Belt conveyors can span distances over **5 km**. Trippers divert coal to bunkers.

5. **Stacker & Reclaimer (Stockyard):** The **stacker** distributes coal into longitudinal piles. The **bucket-wheel reclaimer** retrieves coal at **1500–4000 TPH**. The stockyard holds **30–60 days** of coal.

6. **Coal Bunker / Silo:** Located above the pulverizer, the bunker provides **6–8 hours** of surge capacity. It has a conical bottom with vibratory feeders or table feeders.

7. **Pulverizer Mill:** For PF firing, ball mill or bowl mill pulverizes coal to **< 75 μm (70 % through 200 mesh)**. Hot primary air at **150–250 °C** dries coal during grinding.

8. **Coal Piping & Burners:** Pulverized coal is pneumatically conveyed through **coal pipes** to the **burner nozzles**, mounted at the four corners (tangential firing).

9. **Auxiliaries:** Dust suppression sprays, fire hydrants (for coal pile fires), and magnetic separators.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Capacity = 500 MW
- Plant efficiency $\eta_p = 0.38$
- Coal GCV = 4500 kcal/kg
- Coal ash content = 30 %
- Capacity factor = 85 %
- Wagon capacity = 58 T
- Plant hours/year = 8760

**Step 1: Coal consumption rate (kg/h) at full load.**

Heat input rate at full load:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \frac{500 \times 10^3 \times 860}{\eta_p} = \frac{500000 \times 860}{0.38} = 1.1316 \times 10^9 \;\text{kcal/h}
$$

(using 1 kWh = 860 kcal)

Coal rate at full load:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = \frac{1.1316 \times 10^9}{4500} = 251467 \;\text{kg/h} = 251.47 \;\text{TPH}
$$

**Step 2: Annual coal at 85 % load factor.**

$$
m_{\text{annual}} = 251.47 \times 8760 \times 0.85 = 1872205 \;\text{tonnes/year} \approx 1.87 \;\text{MT/year}
$$

**Step 3: Annual fly ash generation.**

$$
m_{\text{ash}} = 1872205 \times 0.30 = 561661 \;\text{tonnes/year}
$$

Fly ash (80 %):

$$
m_{\text{fly ash}} = 561661 \times 0.80 = 449329 \;\text{tonnes/year}
$$

Bottom ash (20 %):

$$
m_{\text{bottom ash}} = 561661 \times 0.20 = 112332 \;\text{tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 4: Wagons per year.**

$$
N_{\text{wagons}} = \frac{1872205}{58} = 32280 \;\text{wagons/year} \approx 88 \;\text{wagons/day}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a **neat sketch the working of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**. Derive the **Deutsch–Anderson equation** for collection efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coal-fired boiler has **flue gas flow of 4 × 10⁵ Nm³/h** and ESP with **effective drift velocity = 0.12 m/s**. Determine the **plate area required for 99 % collection efficiency**. If the required efficiency is raised to **99.5 %**, by what percentage must the plate area increase?

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Working of ESP — Step-by-Step:**

1. **Flue gas** laden with dust enters the ESP through a **gas distribution system** (perforated plates / baffles) that ensures **uniform velocity profile** across the entire cross-section. Non-uniform flow causes short-circuiting and reduced efficiency.

2. The ESP consists of **alternating vertical plates** spaced **200–400 mm apart**, forming **gas passages**. Through each passage, a **discharge electrode** (thin tungsten wire or mast electrode) hangs centrally, held at **–30 to –70 kV DC** (negative polarity preferred for stable corona).

3. **Corona Discharge:** The high negative voltage ionizes gas molecules around the discharge electrode. **Negative gas ions** (mostly $\text{O}_2^-$ and $\text{O}^-$) migrate outwards toward the **collecting plates** (grounded, positive).

4. **Particle Charging:** As ions strike dust particles in the gas stream, particles acquire negative charge by two mechanisms:
   - **Field charging** (dominant for $d > 1\;\mu\text{m}$): ions follow field lines to particles.
   - **Diffusion charging** (dominant for $d < 1\;\mu\text{m}$): random thermal motion of ions.

5. **Migration:** Charged particles, under the influence of the **electric field** $E = V/d$, migrate to the collecting plate at **drift velocity** $w$ given by:

$$
w = \frac{q E C_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}
$$

where $C_c$ is the Cunningham slip correction factor (significant for $d < 5\;\mu\text{m}$), $q$ is the charge on particle, $\mu$ is gas viscosity.

6. **Collection:** Particles deposit as a **dust layer** on the plate.

7. **Rapping:** Periodically (every **1–10 minutes**), the plates are struck by **electromagnetic or pneumatic rappers**, dislodging the dust layer into **hoppers** below. Discharge electrodes are rapped independently.

8. **Ash Disposal:** Fly ash from hoppers is discharged via **screw conveyors, air slides, or pneumatic systems** to silos and ultimately to cement plants or ash ponds.

**Derivation of Deutsch–Anderson Equation:**
*(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)*

Final form:

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{wA}{Q}\right)
$$

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Convert gas flow to m³/s.

$$
Q = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{3600} = 111.11 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** For 99 % efficiency:

$$
\eta_1 = 0.99, \quad w = 0.12 \;\text{m/s}
$$

$$
A_1 = -\frac{Q}{w} \ln(1 - \eta_1) = -\frac{111.11}{0.12}\ln(0.01)
$$

$$
A_1 = -925.93 \times (-4.6052) = 4263 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** For 99.5 % efficiency:

$$
\eta_2 = 0.995
$$

$$
A_2 = -\frac{111.11}{0.12}\ln(0.005) = -925.93 \times (-5.2983) = 4905 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Percentage increase in plate area.

$$
\Delta\% = \frac{A_2 - A_1}{A_1} \times 100 = \frac{4905 - 4263}{4263} \times 100 = 15.06\;\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A marginal 0.5 % rise in efficiency (from 99 % → 99.5 %) requires a **15 % larger ESP** — confirming the **logarithmic penalty** discussed in Section 2.6.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Avoid These!**
> 
> 1. **Belt Conveyor Formula:** Students often forget the **inclination factor** $k$ and lose 2 marks. Use **$Q = 3600 A v \rho k$**, not just $Q = 3600 A v \rho$.
> 
> 2. **ESP Plate Area Units:** Many students compute $A$ in inconsistent units (m² vs m²/1000 m³/h) and report wrong numbers. **Always state units explicitly**.
> 
> 3. **Drift Velocity Values:** Don't write a generic $w$ value. For **Indian coal ash**, $w$ ranges from **0.04–0.10 m/s**; for **low-resistivity imported coal**, it can be **0.12–0.18 m/s**.
> 
> 4. **Coal Handling Diagram:** Examiners expect the **sequence** Tippler → Crusher → Conveyor → Stockyard → Bunker → Pulverizer → Burner. Drawing random boxes = -3 marks.
> 
> 5. **Ash Handling:** Distinguish clearly between **bottom ash** (water-granulated/slurry) and **fly ash** (dry mechanical/pneumatic). Mixing them up = -2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Pulverizer Discharge Temperature:** Below **70 °C** → condensation, plugging; Above **80 °C** → fire risk. Mention this for **full marks**.
> 
> 7. **Cyclone vs ESP:** ESP is preferred for fine particles; cyclone only for **> 10 μm**. Confusing the two loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Rapid-Fire Revision Notes**
> 
> - **Coal Handling:** Tippler → Crusher → Belt Conveyor → Stockyard → Tripper → Bunker → Pulverizer → Burner.
> - **Stockyard Capacity:** 30 days minimum (Indian regulation).
> - **Bunker Capacity:** 6–8 hours of BMCR coal consumption.
> - **Conveyor Speed:** 1.5–3.5 m/s, max incline 16°–18°.
> - **Pulverizer Fineness:** 70 % through 200 mesh (75 μm).
> - **Primary Air:** Dries + transports + partially combusts; **150–250 °C** for bituminous coal.
> - **Stokers** for small units (<100 TPH); **PF Firing** for large units.
> - **PF Firing Advantages:** High efficiency, fast response, low-grade coal tolerance.
> - **PF Firing Disadvantages:** High capital, fire/explosion hazard in mills, high power consumption (~**30 kWh/T**).
> - **Cyclone Furnace:** Heat release **18–25 GJ/m³·h**; tolerates 45 % ash; high NOx.
> - **ESP Drift Velocity:** 0.04–0.18 m/s.
> - **Deutsch–Anderson:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$.
> - **SCA Range:** 30–80 m²/(1000 m³/h) for normal utility boilers.
> - **Bag Filter:** Used for high-resistivity ash (>10¹² Ω·cm).
> - **Ash Split:** 80 % fly ash, 20 % bottom ash.
> - **Ash Handling Methods:** Wet (slurry), Dry (pneumatic), Semi-dry.
> - **Indian Regulation:** MoEFCC PM limit **30 mg/Nm³** (new plants, 2024 norm).
> - **Fly Ash Notification 2021:** 100 % utilization mandated within 5 years.
> - **Cyclone Cut Size:** $d_{50} \propto \sqrt{\mu_b / (v_i \rho_p)}$.
> - **High-moisture Indian Coal:** Needs primary air **≥ 250 °C** for safe drying.
> - **Mill Outlet Temp Limit:** **70–80 °C** for bituminous coal.
> - **Pulverizer Types:** Ball (soft coal), Bowl (versatile), Hammer (wet coal), Attrition (drying).
> - **Wagon Tippler Throughput:** 4000–6000 TPH per tippler.
> - **Bottom Ash Disposal Modern:** **Clinker grinder + slurry pump** OR **dry drag chain conveyor**.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Fuel Handling, Storage & Burning Systems; Dust and Ash Handling System**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — Course OEMET727*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Power Plant Components

In a modern thermal power plant, the boiler–turbine–generator trio is supported by an extensive network of **auxiliary components and auxiliaries**. These components are not optional add-ons; they are essential to ensure plant efficiency, safety, reliability, and environmental compliance. Module 3 of KTU's OEMET727 Power Plant Engineering syllabus (2024 Scheme) focuses on these critical components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Official KTU 2024 Definition (Module 3):**
> The auxiliary components of a thermal power plant include condensers, cooling towers, feedwater heaters, deaerators, pulverizers, ash handling systems, dust collectors, economizers, and superheaters. These components collectively improve cycle efficiency (η), ensure feedwater quality, handle fuel preparation, manage waste, and comply with environmental norms.

---

## 1.2 Why These Components Matter — The Intuitive Picture

Imagine you have just brewed hot tea in a kettle. You pour the hot liquid out, and the empty kettle still contains steam and is hot to touch. In a steam power plant, after the steam does its job in the turbine, we face the same challenge:
- **Where does the "used" steam go?** → **Condenser**
- **How do we convert the low-pressure exhaust steam back to water?** → **Condenser + Cooling system (cooling tower)**
- **How do we make the feedwater even hotter before sending it back to the boiler?** → **Feedwater heaters + Economizer**
- **How do we remove dissolved oxygen to prevent boiler tube corrosion?** → **Deaerator**
- **How do we grind the coal into fine powder for efficient combustion?** → **Pulverizer**
- **How do we handle the leftover ash?** → **Ash handling system**
- **How do we prevent pollution from fly ash?** → **Dust collector (ESP)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Circulatory System:**
> A power plant is like the human body. The **boiler** is the heart (pumps energy), **turbine** is the muscle that performs work, **condenser** is the lungs (releases waste heat), **cooling tower** is the skin (radiates excess heat), **feedwater heater** is the small intestine (prepares "food" for the heart), and **deaerator** is the kidney (purifies the "blood"). Just as the human body cannot function without any one organ, a power plant cannot operate without its auxiliary components.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Power Plant Components (Module 3 Scope)

| Category | Components | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Steam Cycle Components** | Condenser, Cooling Tower | Condense exhaust steam & reject latent heat |
| **Feedwater Treatment** | Feedwater Heater, Deaerator | Heat & purify boiler feedwater |
| **Fuel Handling** | Pulverizer, Coal Handling | Size reduction of coal |
| **Waste Management** | Ash Handling System, Dust Collector | Ash disposal & pollution control |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiner's commonly frame questions as "Explain any four components of a thermal power plant" (14 marks) — covering **purpose, working principle, types, and sketch**. Memorize at least 5 components thoroughly.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Used Across Module 3)

The following metrics are universally applied in Module 3 and are **mandatory** for solving numerical problems in KTU exams:

- **Cooling Tower Range** = Hot water temp − Cold water temp (°C)
- **Cooling Tower Approach** = Cold water temp − Wet bulb temp (°C)
- **Cooling Tower Effectiveness** = Range ÷ (Range + Approach)
- **Feedwater Heater Terminal Temperature Difference (TTD)** = Saturation temp of extraction steam − Feedwater outlet temp
- **Drain Cooler Approach (DCA)** = Saturation temp of higher-pressure heater − Drain outlet temp
- **Heater Effectiveness (η_fwh)** = Actual rise in feedwater temp ÷ Maximum possible rise
- **Condenser Vacuum Efficiency** = (P_sat at T_hot well − P_abs condenser) ÷ P_sat at T_hot well

> [!WARNING]
> Always express temperatures in **°C** and pressures in **bar** (or kPa) consistently. KTU valuators deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — STEAM CONDENSER

### Definition & Working
A **steam condenser** is a closed vessel (heat exchanger) that condenses the exhaust steam from the turbine by rejecting its latent heat to a cooling medium (water or air), thereby creating a low back-pressure at the turbine exhaust.

**Why we need a condenser:**
- Steam exhausting to atmosphere wastes latent heat (~540 kcal/kg).
- Condensing creates vacuum (≤ 0.1 bar abs), increasing turbine work output.
- Condensate is reused as feedwater — saves water treatment cost.

### Types of Condensers

**A. Surface Condenser** (most common in large plants)
- Steam and cooling water separated by tubes.
- Cooling water flows through tubes; steam condenses on outer shell side.
- **Vacuum maintained:** 0.05 to 0.10 bar abs.

**B. Jet Condenser** (mixing type)
- Steam and cooling water mix directly.
- Used in small plants; condensate cannot be recovered.

### Condenser Performance Equations

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_{\text{exhaust}} - h_{\text{condensate}})
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{pc} \cdot (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{\dot{m}_s \cdot (h_{\text{exhaust}} - h_{\text{condensate}})}{c_{pc} \cdot \Delta T_c}
$$

**Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for condenser:**

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

where:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{s,\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{s,\text{sat}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$
- $T_{s,\text{sat}}$ is the saturation temperature at condenser pressure

**Heat Transfer Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}}}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}
$$

where **U** = Overall heat transfer coefficient (typically **1500–3500 W/m²·K** for clean surface condensers).

---

## 2.2 Component 2 — COOLING TOWER

### Definition & Purpose
A cooling tower is a **heat rejection device** that transfers waste heat from circulating cooling water to the atmosphere through evaporative cooling. It is the "radiator" of a power plant.

### Types of Cooling Towers
1. **Natural Draft** (hyperboloid chimney) — used in large thermal plants (NTPC stations).
2. **Forced Draft** — fan-driven, smaller capacity.
3. **Induced Draft** — fan at top, more common.
4. **Counter-Flow** — air flows upward opposite to water downward.
5. **Cross-Flow** — air flows horizontally across falling water.

### Key Parameters

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $T_{hw} - T_{cw}$ | 5–15 °C |
| **Approach** | $T_{cw} - T_{wb}$ | 4–10 °C |
| **Effectiveness (ε)** | Range / (Range + Approach) | 0.5–0.75 |
| **Drift Loss** | Water lost as droplets | 0.02–0.05% of circulation |
| **Evaporation Loss** | Water lost by evaporation | 0.85% per 10 °C range |
| **Blowdown Loss** | To control TDS | 0.5–2% |

### Cooling Tower Mass Balance

$$
\dot{m}_{w,\text{makeup}} = \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} + \dot{m}_{\text{drift}} + \dot{m}_{\text{blowdown}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporation loss** = 0.00085 × $\dot{m}_w$ × Range (in °C) — this is a KTU favorite formula.

---

## 2.3 Component 3 — FEEDWATER HEATER (FWH)

### Purpose
To heat the condensate/feedwater using **extracted steam** from turbine intermediate stages before it enters the boiler, thereby **improving cycle efficiency**.

### Types
- **Open (Deaerating) FWH** — direct contact; steam and water mix.
- **Closed (Non-deaerating) FWH** — indirect contact via tubes.

### Key Performance Metrics

**1. Terminal Temperature Difference (TTD):**

$$
\text{TTD} = T_{\text{sat}} \, (\text{extraction steam}) - T_{\text{feedwater,out}}
$$

Ideal TTD ≈ **0 °C** (perfect heat transfer). Actual: 3–8 °C.

**2. Drain Cooler Approach (DCA):**

$$
\text{DCA} = T_{\text{sat}} \, (\text{HPH}) - T_{\text{drain,out}}
$$

Typical DCA ≈ 5–15 °C.

**3. Heater Effectiveness (η_fwh):**

$$
\eta_{\text{FWH}} = \frac{T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}}{T_{\text{sat}} - T_{\text{in}}}
$$

**4. Energy Balance (Open FWH):**

$$
\dot{m}_1 \, h_1 + \dot{m}_{\text{ext}} \, h_{\text{ext}} = (\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_{\text{ext}}) \, h_{\text{mix}}
$$

**5. Closed FWH (one-side fluid):**

$$
\dot{m}_f \, c_{pf} \, (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) = \dot{m}_{\text{ext}} \, (h_{\text{ext}} - h_{\text{drain}})
$$

---

## 2.4 Component 4 — DEAERATOR

### Purpose
To remove **dissolved oxygen (O₂)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** from feedwater using:
- **Henry's Law** (low pressure → low gas solubility)
- **Steam heating** (to ~105 °C saturation at 1.1 bar abs)

> Without deaeration, dissolved O₂ causes severe **pitting corrosion** of boiler tubes.

### Types
1. **Tray-type (Spray-cum-tray)** — most common.
2. **Spray-type** — water sprayed through steam.
3. **Vacuum deaerator** — used in low-pressure systems.

### Henry's Law

$$
P_{\text{gas}} = H \cdot x_{\text{gas}}
$$

At higher temperature, **H increases** → less gas dissolved. Heating feedwater to **saturation temp + a few degrees** drives out O₂ and CO₂.

### Vented Steam Mass Fraction (m_v / m_w)

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}_w} = 0.002 \, T_{da} - 0.15 \quad \text{(empirical)}
$$

---

## 2.5 Component 5 — PULVERIZER (Coal Mill)

### Purpose
To grind coal to fine powder (≈ **70% through 200 mesh**, 75 microns) for efficient combustion in pulverized coal-fired boilers.

### Types
1. **Ball Mill** (low-speed, 15–20 rpm)
2. **Race Mill** (medium-speed)
3. **Bowl Mill** (medium-speed, common in Indian plants)
4. **Impact Mill** (high-speed)
5. **Ball-and-Race Mill** (medium-speed)

### Power Consumption

$$
P_{\text{mill}} = K \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{coal}}^{1.2} \quad \text{(approx.)}
$$

**Specific power consumption** ≈ **15–30 kWh/ton** of coal.

### Capacity Equation

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{0.0105 \, D^2 \, L \, n \, \rho_b \, f}{(\text{HGI})}
$$

where:
- D = mill diameter (m)
- L = mill length (m)
- n = rpm
- ρ_b = bulk density of coal
- HGI = Hardgrove Grindability Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardgrove Index:** Higher HGI = softer coal = easier to grind. Indian coal typically HGI = 45–60.

---

## 2.6 Component 6 — ASH HANDLING SYSTEM

### Purpose
To collect, convey, and dispose of ash produced from coal combustion (~10–30% of coal weight).

### Two Categories of Ash
| Type | Description | Disposal |
|---|---|---|
| **Bottom Ash** | Heavy ash from boiler furnace bottom | Wet slurry to ash pond |
| **Fly Ash** | Light ash carried by flue gases | Collected by ESP, dry disposal |

### Systems

**A. Hydraulic (Wet) System**
- Bottom ash sluiced with water.
- Fly ash from hoppers mixed with water → slurry.
- Uses **clinker grinder, slurry pump, ash pond**.

**B. Pneumatic (Dry) System**
- Fly ash transported in air suspension through pipes.
- Used for **cement industry** feedstock.
- Higher capital cost, lower water consumption.

**C. Mechanical System**
- Belt conveyors, bucket elevators.
- Used for **bottom ash** in small plants.

### Ash Disposal Quantity

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{ash}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \times \text{Ash\%} \times \eta_{\text{collection}}
$$

---

## 2.7 Component 7 — DUST COLLECTOR / ESP

### Purpose
To remove **fly ash particulates** from flue gases before discharge through the chimney, meeting **MoEFCC emission norms** (PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³ for new plants).

### Types
1. **Mechanical Dust Collectors** (Cyclone, Multicyclone)
2. **Fabric Filters** (Baghouse)
3. **Wet Scrubbers**
4. **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** — most common in large plants

### ESP Working Principle

- **Discharge electrodes** (negatively charged) emit electrons → gas ions.
- Fly ash particles get negatively charged → migrate to **collecting plates** (positively charged).
- Plates are rapped periodically → ash falls into hoppers.

### Collection Efficiency (Deutsch-Anderson Equation)

$$
\eta_{\text{ESP}} = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A}{Q} \cdot w_k\right)
$$

where:
- A = total collecting plate area (m²)
- Q = gas flow rate (m³/s)
- w_k = effective migration velocity (m/s), typically **0.05–0.20 m/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Doubling the plate area **A** increases efficiency dramatically. For η = 99%, A/Q ≈ 40 m²/(m³/s).

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| # | Component | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Condenser heat duty | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_g - h_f)$ | Heat rejected calculation |
| 2 | Cooling water mass | $\dot{m}_c = \dot{Q} / [c_{pc}(T_{co}-T_{ci})]$ | Sizing CW pumps |
| 3 | Condenser area | $A = \dot{Q}/(U \cdot \text{LMTD})$ | Design |
| 4 | CT Range | $T_{hw} - T_{cw}$ | Cooling tower selection |
| 5 | CT Approach | $T_{cw} - T_{wb}$ | Performance check |
| 6 | CT Effectiveness | Range/(Range+Approach) | Rating |
| 7 | Evaporation loss | $0.00085 \cdot \dot{m}_w \cdot \text{Range}$ | Water makeup |
| 8 | FWH TTD | $T_{\text{sat}} - T_{\text{fw,out}}$ | Heater quality |
| 9 | FWH Effectiveness | $(T_o - T_i)/(T_{\text{sat}} - T_i)$ | Energy performance |
| 10 | Deaerator vent | $0.002 T_{da} - 0.15$ (per unit mass feedwater) | Steam venting |
| 11 | ESP Efficiency | $1 - \exp(-w_k A / Q)$ | Pollution control |
| 12 | Mill power | $K \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{coal}}^{1.2}$ | Energy audit |
| 13 | Ash quantity | $\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \cdot \text{Ash\%} \cdot \eta$ | Ash handling design |
| 14 | Condenser vacuum eff. | $(P_s - P_a)/P_s$ | Vacuum quality |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plant operators** use these metrics for daily performance monitoring.
- **NTPC, Tata Power, Adani Power** use ESP efficiency equations for environmental compliance.
- **IS 4919 (1982)** and **MoEFCC 2015 norms** govern Indian cooling tower and ESP designs.
- **Design engineers** apply LMTD and effectiveness-NTU methods for heater/condenser selection.
- **Maintenance crews** use TTD and approach to detect fouling (rising TTD = dirty tubes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Condenser LMTD and Heat Transfer Area

### Given Problem Setup
- Steam flow rate: $\dot{m}_s = 50$ kg/s
- Exhaust steam enthalpy: $h_e = 2300$ kJ/kg
- Condensate enthalpy: $h_c = 145$ kJ/kg
- Cooling water: $T_{ci} = 25°\text{C}$, $T_{co} = 38°\text{C}$
- Condenser pressure: 0.10 bar abs → $T_{\text{sat}} = 45.8°\text{C}$
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U = 2500$ W/m²·K
- $c_{pc} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K

### Step 1: Heat Duty of Condenser

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{m}_s (h_e - h_c)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = 50 \times (2300 - 145)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = 50 \times 2155 = 107{,}750 \text{ kW} = 107.75 \text{ MW}
$$

**Interpretation:** This enormous heat must be rejected by cooling water.

### Step 2: Cooling Water Mass Flow Rate

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}}}{c_{pc}(T_{co} - T_{ci})}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{107{,}750}{4.18 \times (38 - 25)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{107{,}750}{54.34} = 1982.9 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 3: Temperature Differences at Two Ends of Condenser

At inlet (steam side, water at $T_{ci}$):
$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{\text{sat}} - T_{ci} = 45.8 - 25 = 20.8°\text{C}
$$

At outlet (steam side, water at $T_{co}$):
$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{\text{sat}} - T_{co} = 45.8 - 38 = 7.8°\text{C}
$$

### Step 4: LMTD Calculation

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{7.8 - 20.8}{\ln(7.8 / 20.8)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{-13}{\ln(0.375)} = \frac{-13}{-0.981} = 13.25°\text{C}
$$

### Step 5: Heat Transfer Area

$$
A = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}}}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}
$$

$$
A = \frac{107{,}750 \times 10^3}{2500 \times 13.25}
$$

$$
A = \frac{1.0775 \times 10^8}{33{,}125} = 3253 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Result:** A 3253 m² condenser is needed — typical for a ~120 MW unit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Cooling Tower Effectiveness and Evaporation Loss

### Given
- $T_{hw} = 42°\text{C}$, $T_{cw} = 32°\text{C}$, $T_{wb} = 28°\text{C}$
- Circulation rate $\dot{m}_w = 5000$ kg/s

### Step 1: Range

$$
\text{Range} = T_{hw} - T_{cw} = 42 - 32 = 10°\text{C}
$$

### Step 2: Approach

$$
\text{Approach} = T_{cw} - T_{wb} = 32 - 28 = 4°\text{C}
$$

### Step 3: Effectiveness

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Range} + \text{Approach}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{10}{10 + 4} = \frac{10}{14} = 0.714 \; (71.4\%)
$$

### Step 4: Evaporation Loss

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = 0.00085 \times \dot{m}_w \times \text{Range}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = 0.00085 \times 5000 \times 10 = 42.5 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 5: Daily Water Loss (sanity check)

$$
42.5 \text{ kg/s} \times 86400 \text{ s/day} = 3.672 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/day} \approx 3672 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Result:** A 5000 kg/s cooling tower loses ~3700 m³/day through evaporation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — ESP Collection Efficiency by Deutsch Equation

### Given
- Flue gas flow: $Q = 200$ m³/s
- Plate area: $A = 12{,}000$ m²
- Migration velocity: $w_k = 0.10$ m/s

### Step 1: Substitute

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{w_k \cdot A}{Q}\right)
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{0.10 \times 12{,}000}{200}\right)
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp(-6)
$$

### Step 2: Evaluate

$$
\exp(-6) = 0.00248
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - 0.00248 = 0.9975 \;\; (99.75\%)
$$

**Result:** 99.75% of fly ash is collected — well above the **MoEFCC 30 mg/Nm³** standard.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Open Feedwater Heater Energy Balance

### Given
- Condensate flow: $\dot{m}_1 = 100$ kg/s at $T_1 = 95°\text{C}$, $h_1 = 398$ kJ/kg
- Extraction steam: $\dot{m}_{\text{ext}} = 20$ kg/s at $P_{\text{ext}} = 4$ bar abs
- Saturation temp at 4 bar: $T_{\text{sat}} = 143.6°\text{C}$
- $h_{\text{ext}} = h_g$ at 4 bar = **2738.6 kJ/kg**

### Step 1: Mass Balance

$$
\dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_{\text{ext}} = 100 + 20 = 120 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance

$$
\dot{m}_1 h_1 + \dot{m}_{\text{ext}} h_{\text{ext}} = \dot{m}_2 h_2
$$

$$
100 \times 398 + 20 \times 2738.6 = 120 \times h_2
$$

$$
39{,}800 + 54{,}772 = 120 \, h_2
$$

$$
94{,}572 = 120 \, h_2
$$

### Step 3: Outlet Enthalpy

$$
h_2 = \frac{94{,}572}{120} = 788.1 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 4: Outlet Temperature (from steam tables, subcooled)

For water at $h \approx 788$ kJ/kg: $T_2 \approx 184°\text{C}$ (close to saturation due to mixing).

### Step 5: Heater Effectiveness

$$
\eta_{\text{FWH}} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{\text{sat}} - T_1} = \frac{184 - 95}{143.6 - 95} = \frac{89}{48.6} = 1.83
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Effectiveness > 1 indicates **open FWH** (direct contact heater can reach saturation). For **closed FWH**, η ≤ 1.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Problem Keyword | Formula to Use |
|---|---|
| "cooling tower, hot water, cold water, wet bulb" | Range, Approach, Effectiveness |
| "condenser, vacuum, exhaust steam" | LMTD, Area, Vacuum efficiency |
| "ESP, dust, particulate, collection" | Deutsch equation |
| "feedwater heater, TTD, drainage" | TTD = $T_{\text{sat}} - T_{fw,o}$ |
| "open FWH, extraction, mixing" | Energy balance: $\sum \dot{m}_i h_i = \dot{m}_o h_o$ |
| "deaerator, dissolved oxygen" | Henry's law, venting rate |
| "pulverizer, HGI, ball mill" | $P = K \dot{m}^{1.2}$ |
| "ash handling, ESP, fly ash, bottom ash" | $\dot{m}_{\text{ash}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \cdot \text{Ash\%} \cdot \eta$ |
