| Air mass stored | $m = \dfrac{P V}{R T} = \dfrac{56\times 10^5 \times 3.1\times 10^5}{287 \times 320}$ | $m \approx 1.89 \times 10^6\,\text{kg}$ |
| Specific compression work | $w_{comp} \approx 463\,\text{kJ/kg}$ | 463 kJ/kg |
| Total stored electric energy | $E = m \times w_{comp} = 1.89\times 10^6 \times 463 \times 10^3\,\text{J}$ | $E \approx 8.75 \times 10^{11}\,\text{J} = 243\,\text{MWh}$ |
| Discharge duration at 290 MW | $t = E / P_{out} = 243/290$ | $\approx 0.84\,\text{h}$ ≈ matches Huntorf 2-hr spec when losses included |

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — CAES vs Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)

| Parameter | **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** | **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy storage medium | Water in upper reservoir | Compressed air in underground cavern |
| Energy density | 1 kWh/m³ (per 100 m head) | 3 – 6 kWh/m³ (in cavern) |
| Round-trip efficiency | 75% – 85% | 42% – 70% (technology dependent) |
| Geographical constraint | Needs hills & water (severe) | Needs salt cavern or aquifer geology |
| Typical capacity | 500 MW – 3000 MW | 50 MW – 400 MW |
| Construction time | 8 – 15 years | 4 – 7 years |
| Capital cost (per kWh) | $100 – $300 | $50 – $150 |
| Environmental impact | Reservoir flooding, fish migration | Lower (uses existing geological voids) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Diabatic CAES Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Because physical drawings of caverns, wellheads, and multi-stage turbomachinery are difficult to render in Mermaid, the figure below is a **functional block-flow architecture** that traces every energy and material stream — a representation examiners in KTU often use to assess whether a student understands the *system topology* and not just a single component.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    ic1[INTERCOOLER 1]:::hx
    hpc[HIGH PRESSURE COMPRESSOR 4 STAGE]:::comp
    ic2[INTERCOOLER 2]:::hx
    afterc[AFTERCOOLER TO CAVERN TEMP]:::hx
    cavern[SALT CAVERN 310000 m3 46 TO 66 bar]:::store
    well[PRODUCTION WELL AND THROTTLE VALVE]:::flow
    regen[RECUPERATIVE REGENERATOR]:::hx
    combustor[COMBUSTION CHAMBER NATURAL GAS FIRED]:::fuel
    hpt[HIGH PRESSURE TURBINE]:::turb
    lpt[LOW PRESSURE TURBINE]:::turb
    gen[SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR 290 MW]:::elec
    stack[EXHAUST STACK]:::sink
    gridOut[AC GRID PEAK DEMAND]:::src

    gridAC --> rect1 --> motor
    motor --> lpc --> ic1 --> hpc --> ic2 --> afterc --> cavern

    combustor -.exhaust.-> hpt

    combustor -- fuel --> gasSupply[NATURAL GAS SUPPLY]:::fuel
```

## 4.2 System Topology — Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    power[GRID OFF PEAK POWER]:::src
    comp[MULTI STAGE COMPRESSOR WITH INTERCOOLING]:::comp
    TES1[THERMAL ENERGY STORE COMPRESSION HEAT]:::store
    storr[UNDERGROUND CAVERN]:::store

    turbine[HP AND LP EXPANSION TURBINE]:::turb

    power --> comp
    comp -- hot compressed air heat --> TES1
    comp -- cooled compressed air --> storr
    storr -- released air --> TES1swap
    TES1swap -- hot air from TES heat --> turbine
    TES1swap -. heat return .- TES1
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Discharging: Compressor OFF, Turbine ON, Natural gas ON

    Cooldown --> Standby
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Compressed Air Energy Storage and list its two main commercial variants. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**CAES (3 lines, ~30 words):** Compressed Air Energy Storage is a large-scale energy storage system in which off-peak electrical energy is used to compress air and store it in an underground cavern; during peak demand, the air is released, reheated, and expanded through a turbine to regenerate electricity.

**Two commercial variants (1 mark each):**
1. **Diabatic CAES** — Heat of compression is rejected to the atmosphere; natural gas is burned to reheat air before expansion. (Examples: Huntorf, McIntosh.)
2. **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)** — Heat of compression is captured in a Thermal Energy Storage (TES) unit and reused during expansion, eliminating the need for fossil-fuel reheat. (Example: ADELE pilot, Germany.)

---

### Question 2: State any three advantages of CAES over Pumped Hydro Storage. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (1 mark per advantage, 3 total):**

1. **Lower geographical dependency** — CAES only requires a suitable salt cavern or porous rock formation, whereas PHS needs specific topography with two reservoirs at different elevations.
2. **Lower capital cost per kWh** — typically $50–$150/kWh versus $100–$300/kWh for PHS.
3. **Shorter construction time** — 4–7 years versus 8–15 years for PHS; modular, less environmental disruption.
4. *(Bonus alternative)* Higher energy density per unit volume of storage medium.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following**

---

### Question A: Design and Thermodynamic Analysis of a Diabatic CAES Plant

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a **diabatic Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** system. Clearly differentiate it from a conventional gas turbine (Brayton) cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diabatic CAES plant compresses ambient air at $1.013\,\text{bar}$, $300\,\text{K}$ to a cavern pressure of $70\,\text{bar}$ using a multi-stage intercooled compressor. Air leaves the cavern at $320\,\text{K}$, $70\,\text{bar}$ and is fired with natural gas to a turbine inlet temperature of $1200\,\text{K}$. The turbine expands to atmospheric pressure. Assume $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $c_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, compressor isentropic efficiency $\eta_c = 0.85$, turbine isentropic efficiency $\eta_t = 0.88$, and effective multi-stage intercooled compressor work factor = **0.65 × isentropic work**.
Calculate:
1. Specific compression work (kJ/kg of air).
2. Specific expansion work (kJ/kg of air).
3. Heat added by fuel (kJ/kg of air).
4. Round-trip efficiency of the plant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation — 7 marks**

Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. The functional blocks and operating sequence:

1. **Charging Mode (3 marks):** During off-peak hours, surplus grid electricity drives the **Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC)** followed by the **High-Pressure Compressor (HPC)**. Inter-stage and after-coolers remove the heat of compression, dumping it to the atmosphere (this is what makes it "diabatic"). The cooled, high-pressure air is stored in an underground salt cavern at 40–80 bar.

2. **Discharging Mode (3 marks):** During peak demand, high-pressure air is withdrawn from the cavern, mixed with natural gas in a combustion chamber, and fired to a turbine inlet temperature of ~1200 K. Hot, high-pressure air expands through HP and LP turbines coupled to a generator. Exhaust heat is partially recovered in a regenerator and dumped through the stack.

3. **Differentiation from Brayton (1 mark):** Unlike a Brayton gas turbine where compression, combustion, and expansion occur continuously in a closed loop on the same air mass, **CAES decouples compression and expansion in time**. The cavern acts as a "time buffer" between the two halves of the cycle.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Isentropic compression work (per kg of air):** (1 mark)

Pressure ratio: $r = 70/1.013 \approx 69.1$

$$w_{c,s} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}RT_1\left[r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$

$$r^{0.2857} = 69.1^{0.2857} \approx 3.354$$

$$w_{c,s} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times (3.354 - 1) = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 300 \times 2.354$$

$$w_{c,s} = 709.3\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Actual compression work with intercooling and mechanical losses:** (1 mark)

$$w_{c,actual} = 0.65 \times 709.3 = 461.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Heat added by fuel:** (1 mark)

$$q_{fuel} = c_p(T_3' - T_3) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 320) = 884.4\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Isentropic expansion work:** (1 mark)

$$w_{t,s} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}RT_3'\left[1 - \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$$

$$w_{t,s} = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 1200 \times (1 - 1/3.354) = 1205.4 \times 0.7018 = 845.9\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Actual expansion work:** (1 mark)

$$w_{t,actual} = \eta_t \times w_{t,s} = 0.88 \times 845.9 = 744.4\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Round-trip efficiency:** (2 marks)

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{w_{t,actual}}{w_{c,actual} + q_{fuel}} = \frac{744.4}{461.0 + 884.4} = \frac{744.4}{1345.4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{RT} \approx 55.3\%}$$

---

### Question B: Adiabatic CAES, Geological Storage, and CAES vs PHS Comparison

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)**. How does the integration of a **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** unit improve the round-trip efficiency compared to diabatic CAES? State any two TES media used in A-CAES pilots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **geological storage options** for CAES, with emphasis on **salt caverns, depleted gas fields, and aquifers**. What is the role of **underground air pressure** and **depth of the cavern** in determining the feasibility of a CAES installation? Compare the **round-trip efficiency, geographical flexibility, and capital cost** of CAES with that of **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Adiabatic CAES Concept — 7 marks**

1. **Working Principle (3 marks):** In **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)**, the heat generated during the compression phase is **not rejected to the atmosphere**. Instead, it is captured by an intermediate fluid and stored in a **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** unit. When the compressed air is later released from the cavern during the discharge phase, the **stored heat is transferred back** to the air via a heat exchanger, raising it to a temperature high enough to drive the turbine without burning any fossil fuel. This makes A-CAES a **fully renewable / decarbonized** storage technology.

2. **Efficiency Improvement (2 marks):** By recycling the compression heat, A-CAES eliminates the largest thermodynamic loss in diabatic CAES — the **rejection of high-grade thermal energy** that was then re-supplied by burning natural gas. Round-trip efficiency rises from ≈ 45% (diabatic) to a target of **60–70% (adiabatic)**. There is no fuel cost, so the operating cost of stored electricity is also lower.

3. **TES Media (2 marks):**
   - **Packed-bed of ceramic stones / firebricks** (used in the ADELE pilot, Germany).
   - **Molten-salt mixtures** such as the binary eutectic of KNO₃ / NaNO₃ (used in concentrated solar power TES, proposed for A-CAES).
   - *(Other valid option: latent-heat PCM, or high-temperature oil as a sensible-heat medium.)*

**(b) Geological Storage & Comparison — 7 marks**

1. **Geological Storage Options (3 marks):**
   - **Salt Caverns** — Created by solution-mining (pumping water into a salt dome to dissolve the salt, then extracting brine). They are **leak-tight, mechanically strong, low-deformation, and chemically inert** — making them the *gold standard* for CAES. Huntorf and McIntosh both use salt caverns.
   - **Depleted Gas/Oil Fields** — Repurposed reservoirs with existing wells and infrastructure. Cheaper to develop but require careful pressure management to avoid reservoir collapse or leakage.
   - **Aquifers** — Porous, water-saturated rock formations capped by an impermeable layer. Lower cost but more challenging to maintain air-tightness and to manage the air–water interface (the "cushion gas" problem).

2. **Role of Pressure & Depth (2 marks):**
   - The **storage pressure** is determined by the depth of the cavern and the hydrostatic head of the overlying rock. For every 10 m of depth, the lithostatic pressure increases by approximately **1 bar**. A cavern at 1000 m depth typically operates at **80–100 bar**.
   - **Higher depth = higher pressure = greater energy density** (since $E \propto P$), but also higher drilling cost and greater risk of micro-fractures causing air leakage.
   - A minimum depth of **~500 m** is generally required for CAES to deliver useful energy density.

3. **CAES vs PHS Comparison (2 marks):**
   - **Round-trip efficiency:** PHS (75–85%) > A-CAES (60–70%) > Diabatic CAES (45%).
   - **Geographical flexibility:** CAES is far more flexible — only needs a suitable geology, while PHS requires specific topography (two reservoirs at different elevations, large water body).
   - **Capital cost:** CAES is cheaper per kWh stored ($50–$150/kWh) than PHS ($100–$300/kWh) and is faster to build.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on CAES Questions**
> 1. **Confusing CAES with a Brayton cycle on PV/Ts diagrams.** CAES has *two* separate thermodynamic paths: the compression path and the expansion path. Do NOT draw a single closed Brayton loop. The cavern breaks the loop in time.
> 2. **Forgetting to include fuel energy in diabatic efficiency.** A common error is to compute $\eta = w_{t}/w_{c}$ only. The correct denominator for a diabatic plant includes **both** the electrical energy input and the natural-gas chemical energy input. The result is always lower (typically 40–55%) than adiabatic efficiency.
> 3. **Mixing up isentropic vs isothermal compression work.** Without intercooling, use isentropic. **With** multi-stage intercooling, the actual work is approximately **60–70%** of the isentropic value. State your assumption clearly.
> 4. **Ignoring the cavern as an isothermal storage.** During long storage, the air in the cavern equilibrates to the rock temperature (~ 320 K), not the compressor exit temperature. The discharge air starts at $T_3 \approx 320\,\text{K}$, not $T_2 \approx 1000\,\text{K}$.
> 5. **Unit errors:** Pressures in CAES problems must be **consistently in bar or Pa** (NOT mixing). The gas constant $R$ in SI is $287\,\text{J/kg·K}$, but in kJ it is $0.287$. State and stick to one system.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CAES = Compression (off-peak) + Underground Storage + Expansion (peak)**. It is the **second-most mature** large-scale energy storage technology after PHS.
- **Three variants:** Diabatic (uses natural gas), Adiabatic (uses TES, no fuel), Isothermal (theoretical, R&D).
- **Two real-world plants:** **Huntorf, Germany (290 MW, 1978)** and **McIntosh, USA (110 MW, 1991)** — both diabatic with salt caverns.
- **Storage pressure range:** 40 – 80 bar; **Cavern volume:** 300,000 – 500,000 m³.
- **Round-trip efficiency:** Diabatic ≈ 45%, A-CAES target ≈ 65%.
- **Key formulas:** isentropic compression work $w_c = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}RT_1\left[r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$, isentropic expansion work $w_t = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}RT_3\left[1 - r^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$, and $\eta_{RT} = w_{t,actual} / (w_{c,actual} + q_{fuel})$ for diabatic systems.
- **Storage medium options:** Salt caverns (best), depleted gas fields, aquifers, lined rock caverns.
- **Energy density:** ≈ 3–6 kWh/m³ (in cavern) — much higher than PHS at shallow heads.
- **CAES advantage over PHS:** Lower geographical constraint, lower capex per kWh, faster construction.
- **CAES disadvantage vs PHS:** Lower round-trip efficiency; still requires fuel in diabatic mode; dependent on suitable geology.
- **Adiabatic CAES uses TES** to recycle compression heat, removing the natural gas dependency and pushing efficiency toward 70%.
- **Air standard assumptions:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $c_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$.
- **Brayton vs CAES:** Do not confuse; in CAES, compression and expansion are decoupled in time, with the cavern acting as the buffer.
- **For KTU numericals:** Always state assumptions; use intercooled-multi-stage work factor (0.65 × isentropic); account for turbine isentropic efficiency; include fuel heat in denominator of diabatic round-trip efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Flywheel Energy Storage # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)** is a kinetic energy storage device that stores electrical energy in the form of rotational mechanical energy by accelerating a rotor (flywheel) to a very high speed and maintaining that energy in the form of rotational inertia. When energy is required, the rotational speed is reduced through a generator, converting the kinetic energy back into electrical energy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A flywheel is a mechanical device that stores rotational energy in the form of kinetic energy. The amount of stored energy depends on the **moment of inertia** of the rotor and the **square of its angular velocity**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine spinning a heavy **bicycle wheel** by hand and then trying to stop it — you feel a strong resistance. That "resistance to stop" is the stored kinetic energy. A flywheel works on the same principle, but it is: - **Spun at extremely high speeds** (up to 60,000 RPM in modern composite flywheels) - **Enclosed in a vacuum** to eliminate air friction - **Suspended on magnetic bearings** to eliminate mechanical friction A simpler analogy: Think of a **potter's wheel**. Once spinning, it keeps going for a long time. The energy used to spin it is "stored" and is available to do work (shaping clay). A flywheel is essentially a potter's wheel scaled up, sealed in a vacuum chamber, and connected to a motor-generator. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Flywheels do not store energy chemically (like batteries) or by lifting mass (like pumped hydro). They store energy as **pure rotational motion**, making them extremely fast to charge and discharge. ## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics - **Energy stored (E):** Measured in **Joules (J)** or **kWh** (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J) - **Specific energy:** Energy stored per unit mass (Wh/kg) - **Power density:** Power delivered per unit mass (W/kg) - **Efficiency:** Typically **85% – 95%** round-trip - **Cycle life:** Effectively **infinite** (no chemical degradation) - **Self-discharge rate:** ~**20% per hour** for high-speed flywheels due to bearing and windage losses # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The operational cycle of a flywheel energy storage system has three distinct phases: **Step 1 — Charging (Motor Mode):** - Electrical energy from the grid (or a power plant) is fed to a **motor-generator** (typically a permanent magnet synchronous machine). - The motor accelerates the flywheel rotor to a high angular velocity (ω). - Energy is stored in the rotating mass as **kinetic energy**. **Step 2 — Storage (Holding Mode):** - The rotor continues to spin at (or near) the charging speed. - Modern systems use **magnetic levitation bearings** and a **vacuum-sealed housing** to minimize frictional losses. - Energy is held in the rotational inertia until needed. **Step 3 — Discharging (Generator Mode):** - The motor-generator switches to generator mode, driven by the inertia of the spinning rotor. - As the rotor decelerates, electrical energy is delivered to the load. - The output frequency is maintained by **power electronics** (AC-DC-AC conversion). > [!NOTE] > **Why Flywheels are Used in Power Plants:** > 1. **Load leveling** — store energy during off-peak hours, release during peak demand > 2. **Frequency regulation** — rapidly inject/absorb small amounts of power > 3. **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** — bridge short power outages > 4. **Spinning reserve** — provide immediate backup power ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table contains all the **high-yield formulas** required for KTU examinations on Flywheel Energy Storage. These are the exact equations the examiner expects you to write. | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |--------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------| | Kinetic energy stored | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | Energy in rotating mass | Joules (J) | | Moment of inertia (solid disc) | $I = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | Disc-type rotor | kg·m² | | Moment of inertia (thin rim) | $I = m r^{2}$ | Rim-type rotor | kg·m² | | Moment of inertia (general) | $I = m k^{2}$ | Using radius of gyration $k$ | kg·m² | | Energy (radius of gyration form) | $E = \frac{1}{2} m k^{2} \omega^{2}$ | Generalized expression | Joules (J) | | Specific energy | $e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2} k^{2} \omega^{2}$ | Energy per unit mass | J/kg | | Power delivered | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Where $T$ is torque | Watts (W) | | Angular velocity relation | $\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $N$ = RPM | rad/s | | Rim stress (centrifugal) | $\sigma = \rho v^{2}$ | $v = r\omega$, $\rho$ = density | N/m² (Pa) | | Maximum rim velocity | $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{ult}/\rho}$ | Limited by material strength | m/s | | Energy density limit | $e_{max} = K \cdot \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{\rho}$ | $K$ = geometry factor | J/kg | | Discharge time | $t_{dis} = \frac{J(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})}{2 P_{avg}}$ | Discharge from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$ | seconds (s) | | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Output energy / Input energy | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Conversion:** When RPM is given, **always** convert to rad/s using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ before substituting into energy equations. This is a common KTU mark-deduction point. ## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility Flywheels bridge the gap between the **slow response** of chemical batteries and the **instant response** required in power plants. They are used in: - **National Grid UK** — Beacon Power flywheel farms for frequency regulation - **Renewable integration** — smoothing wind turbine output - **Hybrid vehicles** — Formula 1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) - **Space applications** — International Space Station attitude control # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Kinetic Energy Stored in a Flywheel **Starting Point:** Consider a small mass element $dm$ on the flywheel rim at a distance $r$ from the axis of rotation, moving with tangential velocity $v = r\omega$. **Step 1 — Kinetic energy of a single mass element:** The kinetic energy of a particle of mass $dm$ moving with velocity $v$ is: $$dE = \frac{1}{2} (dm) \cdot v^{2}$$ Substitute $v = r\omega$: $$dE = \frac{1}{2} (dm) \cdot (r\omega)^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \omega^{2} \cdot r^{2} \cdot dm$$ **Step 2 — Integrate over the entire mass of the flywheel:** The total kinetic energy of the rotating body is obtained by integrating over the entire mass: $$E = \int dE = \int \frac{1}{2} \omega^{2} r^{2} \, dm$$ **Step 3 — Recognize the moment of inertia:** By definition, the moment of inertia about the axis of rotation is: $$I = \int r^{2} \, dm$$ Since $\omega$ is the same for all mass elements of a rigid body, we can take $\omega^2$ outside the integral: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \omega^{2} \int r^{2} \, dm = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **fundamental equation** of flywheel energy storage. Examiners often ask to derive this. Note that energy is proportional to the **square of angular velocity**, which is why modern flywheels spin so fast. ## 3.2 Derivation II: Energy Stored in Terms of Mass and Radius of Gyration **Step 1 — Start with the moment of inertia in terms of radius of gyration:** By definition of radius of gyration $k$: $$I = m k^{2}$$ where $m$ is the total mass of the rotor and $k$ is the radius of gyration (a geometric property). **Step 2 — Substitute into the energy equation:** $$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (m k^{2}) \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} m k^{2} \omega^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for specific energy (energy per unit mass):** $$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2} k^{2} \omega^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to RPM form (KTU board style):** Since $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$: $$E = \frac{1}{2} m k^{2} \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^{2} = \frac{m k^{2} \pi^{2} N^{2}}{1800}$$ $$\boxed{E = \frac{\pi^{2} m k^{2} N^{2}}{1800} \text{ Joules}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This **RPM-based formula** is the version most commonly tested in KTU board exams. Memorize: $E = \dfrac{\pi^{2} m k^{2} N^{2}}{1800}$ J. ## 3.3 Derivation III: Centrifugal Stress in a Flywheel Rim This derivation establishes the **maximum safe operating speed** of a flywheel. **Step 1 — Consider a small element of the rim:** Consider a small arc element of the rim of length $dl$ and cross-sectional area $A$. Let the rim radius be $r$. The element has mass $dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dl$ where $\rho$ is the material density. **Step 2 — The element is subjected to centrifugal force:** The element moving in a circle of radius $r$ at velocity $v = r\omega$ experiences an outward centrifugal force: $$dF = (dm) \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{r} = (\rho A \, dl) \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{r}$$ **Step 3 — Stress in the rim cross-section:** The tensile stress (force per unit area) in the rim is obtained by recognizing that the two halves of the rim pull on the element: $$\sigma = \frac{\text{Centrifugal force}}{A} = \rho v^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum safe velocity:** To prevent failure, the stress must remain below the material's ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ult}$ (with a safety factor applied): $$\sigma_{max} = \rho v_{max}^{2} \leq \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{n}$$ where $n$ is the factor of safety. Therefore: $$\boxed{v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{ult}}{n \cdot \rho}}}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum specific energy:** The specific energy is related to the rim velocity: $$e_{max} = \frac{1}{2} v_{max}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{2 n \rho}$$ $$\boxed{e_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{2 n \rho} \text{ J/kg}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Engineering Insight:** This derivation shows why **carbon-fiber composite flywheels** (high $\sigma_{ult}/\rho$ ratio) can store far more energy than **steel flywheels**. The $\sigma_{ult}/\rho$ ratio is roughly **20 times higher** for advanced composites than for steel. ## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A flywheel has a mass of **500 kg** and a radius of gyration of **0.4 m**. It is accelerated from rest to **3000 RPM**. Calculate: - (a) The kinetic energy stored at full speed - (b) The energy delivered if it slows down to 1500 RPM - (c) The maximum power if the discharge occurs in 30 seconds **Solution:** **Part (a):** Convert RPM to rad/s: $$\omega_{1} = \frac{2 \pi (3000)}{60} = 100 \pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$ Compute moment of inertia: $$I = m k^{2} = 500 \times (0.4)^{2} = 500 \times 0.16 = 80 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$ Compute kinetic energy: $$E_{1} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{1}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times (314.16)^{2}$$ $$E_{1} = 40 \times 98,696 = 3,947,841 \text{ J} \approx 3.95 \text{ MJ}$$ **Part (b):** Compute $\omega_2$ at 1500 RPM: $$\omega_{2} = \frac{2 \pi (1500)}{60} = 50 \pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$ Energy remaining: $$E_{2} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{2}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 80 \times (157.08)^{2} = 40 \times 24,674 = 986,960 \text{ J}$$ Energy delivered: $$\Delta E = E_{1} - E_{2} = 3,947,841 - 986,960 = 2,960,881 \text{ J} \approx 2.96 \text{ MJ}$$ **Part (c):** Maximum power for 30-second discharge: $$P_{avg} = \frac{\Delta E}{t} = \frac{2,960,881}{30} \approx 98,696 \text{ W} \approx 98.7 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to convert RPM to rad/s and write $\omega = N$ directly. This leads to answers off by a factor of ~$(2\pi/60)^2 \approx 0.01093$, costing **3 full marks**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flywheel Energy Storage System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of a FESS as deployed in a power plant context. Each block represents a physical subsystem. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[GRID OR POWER PLANT
Electrical Input] --> B[POWER ELECTRONICS
AC-DC Converter] B --> C[MOTOR GENERATOR
PM Synchronous Machine] C --> D[ROTOR ASSEMBLY
Flywheel Mass] D --> E[BEARING SYSTEM
Magnetic Levitation] D --> F[VACUUM HOUSING
Sealed Chamber] C --> G[POWER ELECTRONICS
DC-AC Inverter] G --> H[OUTPUT BUS
Load or Grid] I[CONTROL SYSTEM
PLCs and Sensors] --> B I --> C I --> G style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff style H fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style I fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff style D fill:#d62728,color:#fff style E fill:#9467bd,color:#fff style F fill:#8c564b,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Charging and Discharging States ```mermaid flowchart TB S1((START)) --> Q{Operating Mode?} Q -->|CHARGE| C1[Receive electrical energy] C1 --> C2[AC-DC conversion] C2 --> C3[Motor accelerates rotor] C3 --> C4[Kinetic energy stored in spinning mass] C4 --> C5[Vacuum reduces air drag] C5 --> C6[Speed reaches target RPM] C6 --> H{Hold mode?} H -->|Yes| H1[Maintain rotation
Minimal losses] H1 --> H2{Maintain or release?} H2 -->|Hold| H1 H2 -->|Release| Q Q -->|DISCHARGE| D1[Switch to generator mode] D1 --> D2[Rotor decelerates] D2 --> D3[DC-AC conversion] D3 --> D4[Electrical energy to load] D4 --> D5[Speed drops to minimum] D5 --> S2((END)) style C4 fill:#ff9999 style D2 fill:#99ccff style H1 fill:#ffff99 ``` ## 4.3 Component Interaction Matrix | **Component** | **Primary Function** | **Energy Role** | **Loss Mechanism** | |---------------|---------------------|-----------------|--------------------| | Rotor (flywheel) | Stores kinetic energy | Energy reservoir | Bearing friction, windage | | Motor-Generator | Energy conversion | Interface | Copper loss, iron loss | | Power Electronics | Frequency/voltage control | Conditioning | Switching loss | | Magnetic Bearings | Support rotor | Position hold | Eddy current loss | | Vacuum Housing | Reduce air drag | Insulator | Leakage (pumps needed) | | Control System | Supervise operation | Coordination | Sensor noise | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a flywheel. State the equation for the energy stored in a flywheel.** **Model Answer:** A flywheel is a rotating mechanical device that stores energy in the form of **kinetic energy of rotation**. It consists of a heavy disc or rim mounted on a shaft, supported by low-friction bearings. The energy stored in a flywheel is given by: $$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$$ where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s). In terms of mass $m$ and radius of gyration $k$: $$E = \frac{1}{2} m k^{2} \omega^{2}$$ This energy is directly proportional to the moment of inertia and the **square of the angular velocity**, which is why modern flywheels operate at very high speeds. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List any four advantages of a flywheel energy storage system over conventional battery storage.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Long cycle life:** Flywheels can undergo **>100,000 charge-discharge cycles** without degradation, far exceeding battery life. 2. **High power density:** They can deliver very high power for short durations (MW-class pulses). 3. **Rapid response:** Charge and discharge times are in the order of **seconds to minutes**, ideal for frequency regulation. 4. **Environmentally friendly:** No toxic chemicals, no thermal runaway risk, and materials are recyclable. 5. **Wide operating temperature range:** Not affected by ambient temperature like batteries. 6. **Low self-discharge when properly sealed:** Modern vacuum-sealed designs lose only ~1–2% per hour. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Q3. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Question A (14 Marks):** (a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the kinetic energy stored in a flywheel in terms of its mass, radius of gyration, and angular velocity. State the assumptions clearly. (b) **[7 Marks]** A flywheel has a moment of inertia of 1000 kg·m² and rotates at 600 RPM. Calculate: (i) The energy stored in kWh (ii) The energy released if it slows down to 300 RPM (iii) The average power output if the discharge occurs uniformly over 5 minutes **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Consider a small mass element $dm$ on the flywheel located at a distance $r$ from the axis of rotation. As the flywheel rotates with angular velocity $\omega$, this element moves with a tangential velocity $v = r\omega$. The kinetic energy of this small element is: $$dE = \frac{1}{2} (dm) \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (dm) \cdot r^{2} \omega^{2}$$ **Assumptions:** - The flywheel is a **rigid body** rotating about a fixed axis. - All mass elements rotate with the **same angular velocity** $\omega$. - The shaft is massless and the bearings are frictionless (ideal case). For the entire flywheel, integrate over the whole mass: $$E = \int dE = \frac{1}{2} \omega^{2} \int r^{2} \, dm$$ By the definition of moment of inertia: $$I = \int r^{2} \, dm$$ Therefore: $$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$$ Using $I = m k^{2}$ (where $k$ is the radius of gyration): $$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} m k^{2} \omega^{2}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $I = 1000$ kg·m², $N_1 = 600$ RPM, $N_2 = 300$ RPM, $t = 5$ min $= 300$ s. **(i) Energy stored at 600 RPM:** Convert to rad/s: $$\omega_{1} = \frac{2\pi (600)}{60} = 20\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$E_{1} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{1}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (62.832)^{2}$$ $$E_{1} = 500 \times 3,947.84 = 1,973,920 \text{ J} \approx 1.974 \text{ MJ}$$ Converting to kWh: $$E_{1} = \frac{1,973,920}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.548 \text{ kWh}$$ **(ii) Energy released on slowing to 300 RPM:** $$\omega_{2} = \frac{2\pi (300)}{60} = 10\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$E_{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (31.416)^{2} = 500 \times 986.96 = 493,480 \text{ J}$$ Energy released: $$\Delta E = E_{1} - E_{2} = 1,973,920 - 493,480 = 1,480,440 \text{ J} \approx 1.48 \text{ MJ}$$ **(iii) Average power output:** $$P_{avg} = \frac{\Delta E}{t} = \frac{1,480,440}{300} \approx 4,935 \text{ W} \approx 4.93 \text{ kW}$$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a flywheel energy storage system used in power plants, with a neat block diagram. (b) **[7 Marks]** A steel flywheel has a rim mean radius of 1.5 m and a rim cross-sectional area of $0.02$ m². The ultimate tensile strength of steel is 500 MPa and the density is 7850 kg/m³. Find: (i) The maximum safe peripheral speed (ii) The maximum kinetic energy stored in the rim (iii) The maximum specific energy **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working:** A flywheel energy storage system (FESS) consists of the following main components: 1. **Rotor (Flywheel):** A heavy rotating mass, made of either steel (low-speed FESS) or carbon-fiber composite (high-speed FESS). It stores the kinetic energy. 2. **Shaft:** Connects the rotor to the motor-generator assembly. Made of high-strength alloy steel. 3. **Motor-Generator:** A reversible electrical machine that operates as a motor during charging and as a generator during discharging. Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines (PMSM) are commonly used. 4. **Bearings:** Modern FESS uses **active magnetic bearings** (AMB) to levitate the rotor, eliminating mechanical contact and friction. 5. **Vacuum Housing:** The rotor is enclosed in a sealed chamber where air is evacuated to a very low pressure (typically $< 10^{-3}$ mbar) to eliminate aerodynamic drag. 6. **Power Electronics:** Includes AC-DC rectifier, DC-AC inverter, and associated filters to condition the power to and from the grid. 7. **Control System:** PLCs and sensors monitor rotor speed, temperature, vibration, and vacuum level. **Working Principle:** During charging, the motor-generator (acting as a motor) draws electrical energy from the grid and accelerates the rotor to a high speed. The energy is stored as rotational kinetic energy. During discharging, the motor-generator (acting as a generator) is driven by the inertia of the decelerating rotor, producing electrical energy that is fed back to the load through the inverter. **Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1) **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $r = 1.5$ m, $A = 0.02$ m², $\sigma_{ult} = 500 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, safety factor $n = 2$ (assumed standard). **(i) Maximum safe peripheral speed:** Allowable stress with safety factor: $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{n} = \frac{500 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 250 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$ From the stress equation $\sigma = \rho v^{2}$: $$v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{250 \times 10^{6}}{7850}}$$ $$v_{max} = \sqrt{31,847} \approx 178.5 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Maximum kinetic energy stored:** Mass of the rim: $$m = \rho \times A \times (2\pi r) = 7850 \times 0.02 \times (2\pi \times 1.5)$$ $$m = 7850 \times 0.02 \times 9.425 = 1,479.7 \text{ kg}$$ Kinetic energy of the rim (thin rim approximation, $I = mr^{2}$): $$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} m v_{max}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1479.7 \times (178.5)^{2}$$ $$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1479.7 \times 31,862 \approx 2.357 \times 10^{7} \text{ J} \approx 23.57 \text{ MJ}$$ **(iii) Maximum specific energy:** $$e_{max} = \frac{E_{max}}{m} = \frac{1}{2} v_{max}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times (178.5)^{2} \approx 15,931 \text{ J/kg}$$ Or in Wh/kg: $$e_{max} = \frac{15,931}{3600} \approx 4.43 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Flywheel Questions:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Using $N$ (RPM) directly in formulas instead of converting to $\omega$ (rad/s). Always apply $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. > 2. **Confusing moment of inertia formulas:** A solid disc is $I = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$, but a thin rim is $I = mr^2$. Read the problem carefully. > 3. **Missing units in final answer:** Always state units (J, kWh, Wh/kg). Examiner will deduct **½ mark** for missing units. > 4. **No diagram in Part B questions:** For 14-mark questions, a **neat block diagram** is mandatory and carries **2 marks**. Never skip it. > 5. **Sign errors in energy released:** Energy delivered is $E_1 - E_2$ (not $E_2 - E_1$). Note that $\omega_1 > \omega_2$ always for discharge. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core equation:** $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ — energy is proportional to **square** of speed - **Energy per unit mass (specific energy):** $e = \frac{1}{2} k^{2} \omega^{2}$ - **RPM-based formula:** $E = \dfrac{\pi^{2} m k^{2} N^{2}}{1800}$ J - **Centrifugal stress limit:** $\sigma = \rho v^{2}$ — sets the **maximum safe speed** - **Maximum specific energy of a flywheel:** $e_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{2n\rho}$ - **Conversion factor:** $1$ kWh $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ J - **Conversion factor:** $\omega$ (rad/s) $= \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is in RPM - **Key components:** Rotor, motor-generator, magnetic bearings, vacuum housing, power electronics, control system - **High-speed FESS:** Use carbon-fiber composite rotors, magnetic bearings, vacuum — speeds up to 60,000 RPM - **Low-speed FESS:** Use steel rotors, mechanical bearings — speeds up to 10,000 RPM - **Applications:** Load leveling, frequency regulation, UPS, peak shaving, renewable integration, spinning reserve - **Advantages:** Long life, high power density, fast response, eco-friendly, no chemical degradation - **Disadvantages:** High self-discharge (20%/hr for high-speed), gyroscopic effects, safety concerns on rotor failure - **Efficiency range:** 85% – 95% round-trip - **Comparison with batteries:** Flywheels are **superior in power density and cycle life**; batteries are **superior in energy density and self-discharge** > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** Memorize the three "magic" equations: > 1. $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ > 2. $E = \frac{\pi^{2} m k^{2} N^{2}}{1800}$ > 3. $\sigma = \rho v^{2}$ > > These three equations, combined with $\omega = 2\pi N/60$, can solve **every** KTU flywheel problem.

topics.title: Electrochemical Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electrochemical Energy Storage # Electrochemical Energy Storage – Module 4 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Formal):** > *Electrochemical Energy Storage (EES) refers to the conversion of chemical energy into electrical energy (galvanic mode) or the use of electrical energy to drive non-spontaneous chemical reactions (electrolytic mode) through redox (reduction–oxidation) reactions occurring at the interface of an electrode and an electrolyte. The governing principle is the **separation of half-reactions** (oxidation at the anode, reduction at the cathode) with charge transfer mediated by ionic conduction through the electrolyte and electronic conduction through the external circuit.* In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** Power Plant Engineering syllabus, electrochemical storage is studied as the *central bankable technology* for compensating the **intermittency** of solar PV and wind power plants, providing ancillary services (frequency regulation, spinning reserve, black-start), and enabling off-grid/ micro-grid architectures. ### Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of an electrochemical cell as a **"two-water-tank system connected by a pipe at the bottom"**: - The **anode** is Tank-A filled with electrons at high "pressure" (chemical potential). - The **cathode** is Tank-B with low electron pressure. - The **electrolyte** is the connecting pipe — it allows *ions* (not electrons) to flow, forcing electrons to take the **external circuit** (the wire), where they do useful work (light a bulb). - The **separator** prevents the two fluids from directly mixing, just as the pipe-end valve prevents turbulent mixing. - A **battery** is many such tanks stacked; a **fuel cell** is a tank where fresh fuel is continuously poured in from the top (and waste removed). > [!NOTE] > **Standard reference metrics used by KTU examiners:** > - **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ ≈ 26.8 Ah mol⁻¹** > - **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** > - **Standard Temperature, T = 298.15 K (25 °C)** > - **Thermal Voltage, RT/F = 0.02569 V at 25 °C** --- ### Classification of Electrochemical Storage Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite – The 4-Family Classification** | Family | Energy Storage Mechanism | Typical Device | Rechargeable? | |---|---|---|---| | **Primary Cells** | Irreversible redox | Zinc–Carbon, Alkaline | **No** (single-use) | | **Secondary Cells** | Reversible redox (Batteries) | Lead–Acid, Li-ion, Ni–Cd, NaS | **Yes** (cyclic) | | **Fuel Cells** | Continuous external fuel feed | PEMFC, SOFC, AFC | Yes (by refuelling) | | **Supercapacitors** | Electrical Double-Layer + pseudo-capacitance | EDLC, Hybrid capacitors | **Yes** (100k–1M cycles) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Anchor:** "Electrochemical Energy Storage" *encompasses* batteries, fuel cells, and supercapacitors. Treat them as a **family** of devices — examiners love comparative questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Electrochemical Cell Every galvanic cell consists of four essential components: 1. **Anode** (negative electrode during discharge) — site of **oxidation** (loss of electrons). 2. **Cathode** (positive electrode during discharge) — site of **reduction** (gain of electrons). 3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conductor (aqueous, molten salt, solid ceramic, or polymer). 4. **Separator** — porous insulator preventing electronic short-circuit but allowing ion flow. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic — "An Ox, Red Cat":** **AN**ode = **OX**idation ; **RED**uction happens at **CAT**hode. ## 2.2 Theoretical Framework: The Three Pillars ### Pillar 1 — Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (Quantitative Mass–Charge Relation) **Faraday's First Law:** *The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed.* $$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{F \cdot z} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{F \cdot z}$$ **Faraday's Second Law:** *The masses of different substances liberated by the same charge are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.* $$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} \quad \text{where} \quad E = \frac{M}{z}$$ ### Pillar 2 — Thermodynamic Cell Voltage (Nernst Equation) For a generic cell reaction: $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ The **equilibrium cell potential** is: $$E_{cell} = E^{0}_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \right)$$ At the KTU-preferred standard temperature **T = 298.15 K**, with the natural log converted to base-10 logarithm: $$E_{cell} = E^{0}_{cell} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \right)$$ ### Pillar 3 — Butler–Volmer Kinetics (Why Voltage Drops Under Load) When current I is drawn, the cell deviates from $E_{eq}$ due to: $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln \left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right)$$ The terminal voltage during discharge becomes: $$V_{terminal} = E_{eq} - \vert \eta_{act} \vert - \vert \eta_{conc} \vert - iR_{internal}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print This!) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Equations for KTU ESE 2024 — Module 4** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{F \cdot z}$ | Faraday's 1st Law (mass deposited) | kg, A, s | | 2 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Faraday's 2nd Law (equivalent weight) | Dimensionless | | 3 | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$ | Nernst Equation (at 25 °C) | Volts | | 4 | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst Equation (general) | Volts | | 5 | $\Delta G^{0} = -nFE^{0}$ | Gibbs Free Energy ↔ Cell Potential | J, V | | 6 | $Q_{theory} = 26.8 \cdot n \cdot m$ | Theoretical capacity (from mass m) | Ah | | 7 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{V \cdot Ah}{kg}$ | Specific Energy | Wh kg⁻¹ | | 8 | $P_{specific} = \dfrac{V \cdot A}{kg}$ | Specific Power | W kg⁻¹ | | 9 | $\eta_{coulombic} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100$ | Coulombic (Ah) Efficiency | % | | 10 | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | Energy (Wh) Efficiency | % | | 11 | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of Charge | % | | 12 | $DOD = 100 - SOC$ | Depth of Discharge | % | | 13 | $V_{terminal} = E_{oc} - iR_{int}$ | Loaded terminal voltage | V | | 14 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | C-rate (charge/discharge) | h⁻¹ | | 15 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ | Capacitor/EDLC energy | Joules | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TYPING RULE:** When denoting absolute value or determiners inside tables, always use `\vert` (e.g., $V \vert_{loaded}$) to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe `|`. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Power Plants | Storage System | Power-Plant Application | Why it Fits | |---|---|---| | **Lead–Acid Battery** | Diesel-genset starting, UPS, small island microgrids | Lowest ₹/kWh, proven reliability, recyclable | | **Lithium-Ion (LFP/NMC)** | Solar PV smoothing, EV charging buffers, BESS (Battery Energy Storage Systems) | High energy density, 90–95 % round-trip η | | **Sodium–Sulfur (NaS)** | Grid-scale load levelling (up to 8 h discharge) | High temperature operation, 80 % η, long cycle life | | **Vanadium Redox Flow (VRFB)** | Long-duration storage (4–12 h) for wind integration | Decoupled power/energy ratings, infinite cycle | | **PEM Fuel Cell** | Backup power, hydrogen-based peaking plants | Zero local emissions, quiet | | **Supercapacitors** | Frequency regulation, ride-through, hybrid with batteries | 10⁵–10⁶ cycles, ms response | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote **specific energy (Wh/kg)** and **cycle life** when comparing technologies in 14-mark answers — these are the two metrics that *always* appear in the marking scheme. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Capacity from Faraday's Law > **Statement:** *Derive an expression for the theoretical specific capacity of a metal–air cell using Faraday's First Law.* **Given:** A metal of atomic mass $M$ and valency $z$ is oxidised completely at the anode. **Find:** Theoretical capacity $Q$ in Ah kg⁻¹. ### Step 1 — Mass of active material considered Take $m = 1$ kg of the metal. ### Step 2 — Number of moles of metal $$n_{moles} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{1}{M} \text{ mol (for m = 1 kg)}$$ ### Step 3 — Moles of electrons released Each atom releases $z$ electrons on oxidation: $$n_{e^{-}} = \frac{z}{M} \text{ mol of electrons (per kg)}$$ ### Step 4 — Total charge from Faraday's First Law Using $Q = n \cdot F$: $$Q = \frac{z}{M} \cdot F \text{ Coulombs per kg}$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Ampere-hours Since $1 \text{ Ah} = 3600 \text{ C}$: $$Q = \frac{z \cdot F}{M \cdot 3600} \text{ Ah/kg}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ $$Q = \frac{z \cdot 96485}{M \cdot 3600} = \frac{26.8 \cdot z}{M} \text{ Ah/kg}$$ > **Final Result:** > $$\boxed{Q_{theory} = \frac{26.8 \cdot z}{M} \text{ Ah kg}^{-1}}$$ ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A Zinc–Air cell uses Zn ($M = 65.38$ g mol⁻¹, $z = 2$). Calculate the theoretical specific capacity of zinc and the energy density if average discharge voltage is 1.35 V. **Solution — Step 1: Capacity** $$Q = \frac{26.8 \times 2}{65.38} = \frac{53.6}{65.38} = 0.820 \text{ Ah g}^{-1} = 820 \text{ Ah kg}^{-1}$$ **Solution — Step 2: Energy Density** $$E = V \times Q = 1.35 \times 820 = 1107 \text{ Wh kg}^{-1}$$ > **Answer: 1107 Wh kg⁻¹** (comparable to Li-ion, which is why Zn–air is researched as a next-gen chemistry.) --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic First Principles > **Statement:** *Derive the Nernst equation starting from the change in Gibbs free energy.* ### Step 1 — Relation between Free Energy and Cell Potential For a reversible electrochemical cell, the maximum non-PV work obtainable equals the change in Gibbs free energy: $$\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E_{cell}$$ > *(The negative sign indicates that a *spontaneous* reaction (ΔG < 0) yields a *positive* cell potential.)* ### Step 2 — General Free Energy of a Non-Standard Reaction For reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \right)$$ ### Step 3 — Equate both expressions of ΔG $$-nFE = -nFE^{0} + RT \ln \left( \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \right)$$ ### Step 4 — Divide throughout by $-nF$ $$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \right)$$ ### Step 5 — Convert to $\log_{10}$ at 298.15 K Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$ and substituting $R = 8.314$, $T = 298.15$, $F = 96485$: $$\frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.05916 \approx 0.0592$$ > **Final Result:** > $$\boxed{E = E^{0} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} \left( \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}} \right) \text{ at } 25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discharge Voltage Under Load (Polarisation) The **terminal voltage** observed when a battery delivers current $I$ is: $$V_{t} = E_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{conc}$$ - $E_{oc}$ = Open-circuit voltage (Nernst potential) - $IR_{int}$ = Ohmic drop (electrolyte + electrode resistance) - $\eta_{act}$ = Activation overpotential (charge-transfer barrier) - $\eta_{conc}$ = Concentration overpotential (mass-transport limit) > **KTU Trick:** If only $IR$-drop is considered (ideal scenario), $V_t = E_{oc} - IR_{int}$. This appears in 7/14-mark numerical problems. --- ## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Chemistry Equations of Major Batteries | Battery | Anode (Oxidation) | Cathode (Reduction) | Overall Cell Reaction | $E^{0}_{cell}$ (V) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Lead–Acid** | $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-}$ | $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | **2.05** | | **Li-ion (LFP)** | $\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \rightarrow x\text{Li}^{+} + xe^{-} + 6\text{C}$ | $\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{FePO}_4 + x\text{Li}^{+} + xe^{-} \rightarrow \text{LiFePO}_4$ | $\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 + \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{FePO}_4 \rightleftharpoons 6\text{C} + \text{LiFePO}_4$ | **3.2 – 3.3** | | **Ni–Cd** | $\text{Cd} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2e^{-}$ | $\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^{-}$ | $\text{Cd} + 2\text{NiOOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cd(OH)}_2 + 2\text{Ni(OH)}_2$ | **1.35** | | **NaS** | $2\text{Na} \rightarrow 2\text{Na}^{+} + 2e^{-}$ | $\text{S} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{S}^{2-}$ | $2\text{Na} + x\text{S} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S}_x$ (300–350 °C) | **2.0 – 2.1** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** The **Lead–Acid** overall reaction consumes sulfuric acid → its specific gravity (1.28 → 1.10) is the *state-of-charge indicator* — a 14-mark favourite in KTU viva. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem:** A 12 V, 100 Ah lead–acid battery delivers a constant current of 20 A for 4 hours. Calculate: (a) charge delivered in coulombs, (b) energy delivered in Wh, (c) specific energy if battery mass is 30 kg, (d) C-rate during discharge. **Solution:** (a) Charge $Q = I \times t = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ Ah} = 80 \times 3600 = 288{,}000 \text{ C}$ (b) Energy $E = V \times Q = 12 \times 80 = 960 \text{ Wh}$ (c) Specific energy $= 960/30 = 32 \text{ Wh kg}^{-1}$ (d) C-rate $= 20/100 = 0.2\text{C}$ (≈ 5-hour rate) # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy & Operation of a Galvanic Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Anode
Negative Electrode
Site of Oxidation] -- Electrons leave
via external wire --> L1[External Load
Bulb, Motor, Grid] L1 -- Electrons return --> C1[Cathode
Positive Electrode
Site of Reduction] A1 -- Ions migrate
through electrolyte --> E1[Electrolyte
Ionic Conductor
H2SO4 / KOH / Li-salt] E1 -- Completes internal
ionic circuit --> C1 S1[Separator
Porous Insulator] -. Blocks electron flow .-> A1 S1 -. Allows ion passage .-> C1 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Classification of Electrochemical Storage ```mermaid flowchart TB R1[Electrochemical
Energy Storage] --> R2[Batteries
Closed System] R1 --> R3[Fuel Cells
Open System] R1 --> R4[Supercapacitors
EDLC Hybrid] R2 --> R2A[Primary
Non-rechargeable] R2 --> R2B[Secondary
Rechargeable] R2A --> R2A1[Zinc Carbon] R2A --> R2A2[Alkaline MnO2] R2B --> R2B1[Lead Acid
SLI BESS] R2B --> R2B2[Lithium Ion
LFP NMC LTO] R2B --> R2B3[Nickel Cadmium] R2B --> R2B4[Sodium Sulfur
High T 350C] R2B --> R2B5[Vanadium Redox
Flow Battery] R3 --> R3A[PEMFC
Low T 80C] R3 --> R3B[SOFC
High T 800C] R3 --> R3C[MCFC
Molten Salt] R3 --> R3D[AFC
Alkaline] R4 --> R4A[EDLC
Carbon Carbon] R4 --> R4B[Hybrid
Li-ion Capacitor] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — Charging & Discharging of Lead–Acid Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISCHARGE D1[Pb anode
oxidises to PbSO4] -- releases
2e minus --> D2[External Load
Electrons flow] D2 -- electrons reach --> D3[PbO2 cathode
reduces to PbSO4] D1 -- SO4 2- ions
migrate --> D3 end subgraph CHARGE C3[External DC Source
Forces current] -- pushes electrons
back --> C1[PbSO4 anode
reduces to Pb] C1 -- SO4 2- returns
to electrolyte --> C2[PbSO4 cathode
oxidises to PbO2] end ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Performance — Ragone Plot Description (Radar) ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Specific Energy
Wh per kg] --> Q2[Specific Power
W per kg] Q2 --> Q3[Cycle Life
cycles] Q3 --> Q4[Round Trip
Efficiency percent] Q4 --> Q5[Cost
Rupees per kWh] Q5 --> Q1 Q1 -. Lead Acid 35 .-> N1 Q1 -. Li ion 200 .-> N2 Q1 -. NaS 220 .-> N3 Q1 -. VRFB 35 .-> N4 Q1 -. Supercap 8 .-> N5 Q1 -. Fuel Cell 800 .-> N6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this carefully:** A **Ragone plot** (energy vs power, log–log) is the gold-standard way to compare storage devices. **Supercapacitors** sit in the *high-power, low-energy* corner; **Fuel cells** sit in the *high-energy, low-power* corner; **Li-ion** sits in the middle. ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB) ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[Tank A
V2 plus / VO2 plus
Positive Electrolyte] -- Pump --> C1[Cathode Side
Electrochemical Stack] C1 -- Pumps back --> T1 T2[Tank B
V3 plus / V2 plus
Negative Electrolyte] -- Pump --> A1[Anode Side
Electrochemical Stack] A1 -- Pumps back --> T2 M1[Proton Exchange
Membrane Separator] -. Crosses between
stacks only H plus ions .-> C1 A1 --> M1 M1 --> C1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define the term "State of Charge (SOC)" of a battery. How is it related to Depth of Discharge (DOD)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** State of Charge (SOC) of a battery is defined as the ratio of the *remaining capacity* to the *rated (nominal) capacity*, expressed as a percentage. $$SOC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100 \, \%$$ Depth of Discharge (DOD) is the *complementary* percentage of charge that has been removed: $$DOD = 100 - SOC$$ Together, $SOC + DOD = 100 \, \%$. SOC is the most critical real-time parameter monitored by a **Battery Management System (BMS)** in BESS installations. **(3 marks: def. 1 + relation 1 + BMS relevance 1)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Faraday's First Law:** *The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.* $$m \propto Q \implies m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{F \cdot z} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{F \cdot z}$$ **Faraday's Second Law:** *When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of the substances liberated are proportional to their respective chemical equivalent weights.* $$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{M_1/z_1}{M_2/z_2}$$ These laws are the *foundation for sizing* the active material mass in any battery design. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question in a Module has an internal choice — *either* (a) OR (b) of 14 marks, usually split as (7 + 7) sub-parts. ### ⭐ QUESTION A — **(14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working principle of a Lead–Acid battery** during charging and discharging. Write the half-cell and overall cell reactions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **Sodium–Sulfur (NaS) battery** operates at 350 °C with molten electrodes and a solid $\beta$-alumina electrolyte. Explain its working, materials, and state **three reasons** why it is suitable for **grid-scale load-levelling** in a thermal power plant. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A ### Part (a) — Lead–Acid Battery (7 marks) **Construction:** - **Anode:** Spongy lead (Pb) plates. - **Cathode:** Lead dioxide ($\text{PbO}_2$) plates. - **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, specific gravity 1.28 when fully charged). - **Separator:** Microporous rubber/PVC sheets between plates to prevent shorting. **Discharge Reactions (Galvanic Mode):** Anode (oxidation): $$\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^{-}$$ Cathode (reduction): $$\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Overall Discharge:** $$\boxed{\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}}$$ During discharge, both electrodes convert to $\text{PbSO}_4$ and water dilutes the acid → **specific gravity falls** → used as SOC indicator. **Charge Reactions (Electrolytic Mode):** Reverse of above, driven by an external DC source (rectifier in substations). **Open-circuit voltage ≈ 2.05 V per cell**; six cells in series give the standard **12 V automotive battery**. **(7 marks: construction 2 + discharge eq 2 + charge eq 1 + voltage 1 + indicator 1)** --- ### Part (b) — Sodium–Sulfur Battery (7 marks) **Construction & Working:** - **Anode:** Liquid sodium (Na), molten at 350 °C. - **Cathode:** Liquid sulfur (S) mixed with a graphite felt current collector. - **Electrolyte:** Solid $\beta$-alumina ($\beta$-$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) ceramic — conducts only **Na⁺ ions**. **Discharge Reactions:** Anode: $2\text{Na} \rightarrow 2\text{Na}^{+} + 2e^{-}$ Cathode: $x\text{S} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{S}_x^{2-}$ Overall: $2\text{Na} + x\text{S} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{S}_x$ The cell delivers **~ 2.0 V**, with energy density around **220 Wh/kg**. **Three Reasons for Grid-Scale Suitability:** 1. **High Energy Density (220 Wh/kg):** Compact footprint for MW-scale BESS in thermal power stations. 2. **Long Cycle Life (2500+ cycles):** Suitable for daily deep discharge in load-levelling duty. 3. **High Round-Trip Efficiency (~ 85 %):** Stored energy is recovered economically — payback within 5–7 years for a 1 MW unit. 4. **Pulse Power Capability:** Can deliver 6× rated power for 30 s, useful for **spinning reserve**. **(7 marks: working 3 + chemistry 1 + reasons 3)** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — **(14 Marks)** [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the **Nernst equation** from thermodynamic principles. Hence, calculate the EMF of the cell $\text{Zn} \vert \text{Zn}^{2+}(0.01\,M) \Vert \text{Cu}^{2+}(1.0\,M) \vert \text{Cu}$ at 25 °C, given $E^0_{cell} = 1.10$ V. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB)** with the **Lithium-Ion battery** in terms of: energy/power decoupling, cycle life, energy density, suitable duration, and electrolyte cost. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B ### Part (a) — Nernst Derivation & EMF Calculation (7 marks) **Derivation:** (see Section 3.2 for the full derivation — write 4 key steps) Step 1: $\Delta G = -nFE$ Step 2: $\Delta G = \Delta G^{0} + RT \ln Q$ Step 3: Equate and divide by $-nF$: $$E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ Step 4: At 25 °C, convert to $\log_{10}$: $$E = E^{0} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q$$ **EMF Calculation:** Cell reaction: $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$, $n = 2$. Reaction quotient: $$Q = \frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 10^{-2}$$ Substituting: $$E = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log_{10}(10^{-2})$$ $$E = 1.10 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \times (-2)$$ $$E = 1.10 + 0.0592 = 1.1592 \text{ V}$$ > **Answer: $E_{cell} = 1.1592$ V** (5 marks for derivation + 2 marks for calculation) --- ### Part (b) — VRFB vs Li-ion Comparison (7 marks) | Parameter | Vanadium Redox Flow (VRFB) | Lithium-Ion (LFP) | |---|---|---| | **Energy/Power Decoupling** | ✅ Tank size ↑ → energy ↑; stack size ↑ → power ↑ | ❌ Coupled (single pack) | | **Cycle Life** | 15,000 – 20,000 cycles (vanadium electrolyte never degrades) | 3,000 – 6,000 cycles (LFP) | | **Energy Density** | 25 – 35 Wh/kg (low — heavy tanks) | 90 – 160 Wh/kg | | **Suitable Duration** | 4 – 12 hours (long duration) | 1 – 4 hours (short–medium) | | **Electrolyte Cost** | High initial (vanadium pentoxide ~ ₹3500/kg) but 100 % reusable | Lower per kWh, but no recovery after EOL | | **Round-Trip Efficiency** | 65 – 80 % | 90 – 95 % | | **Best Fit** | Wind/solar firming, microgrids | EV, BESS, frequency regulation | > **Conclusion (1 mark):** For a *long-duration, deep-cycle* duty in a power plant (e.g., overnight storage), **VRFB is superior**; for *high-efficiency, short-duration* grid services, **Li-ion wins**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in this topic:** > 1. **Sign error in Nernst equation** — confusing products/reactants placement. *Always* keep products in numerator. Marks lost: **2 of 7**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert $F$ to Ah** — using 96,485 C gives *Coulombs*, but capacity is asked in *Ah*; divide by 3600. Marks lost: **1 of 7**. > 3. **Confusing Anode/Cathode during charging vs discharging** — they SWAP roles. Marks lost: **2 of 14**. > 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — strict *KTU 2024* rule. Marks lost: **½ to 1 per sub-question**. > 5. **Calling Lead–Acid "Lithium"** in descriptive answers — read the question TWICE. > 6. **Skipping the cell-reaction equation** in battery questions — the *equation is worth 30 %* of the marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Module 4: Electrochemical Energy Storage** - ✅ **Electrochemical cell = anode (ox) + cathode (red) + electrolyte + separator**; ions move inside, electrons move outside. - ✅ **Faraday's Laws:** $m = \frac{I t M}{F z}$ ; $Q_{theory} = \frac{26.8 z}{M}$ Ah kg⁻¹. - ✅ **Nernst:** $E = E^0 - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$ at 25 °C; remember products in numerator. - ✅ **Gibbs–Nernst link:** $\Delta G^0 = -n F E^0$ — the bridge between chemistry and electricity. - ✅ **Lead–Acid overall:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$; $E^0 = 2.05$ V; 6 cells → 12 V. - ✅ **Li-ion (LFP):** $E^0 \approx 3.2$ V; 90–95 % η; 3000+ cycles; cobalt-free, safest Li-chemistry. - ✅ **NaS:** operates 300–350 °C; $\beta$-alumina solid electrolyte; 220 Wh/kg; 2500 cycles; **load-levelling**. - ✅ **VRFB:** V²⁺/V³⁺ and V⁴⁺/V⁵⁺ couples; decoupled power/energy; long-duration; 65–80 % η. - ✅ **PEMFC:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ + electricity; 50–60 % η; low-T (80 °C). - ✅ **Supercapacitor:** energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; 10⁵–10⁶ cycles; high power, low energy. - ✅ **SOC + DOD = 100 %**; **C-rate = I/Q_rated** (h⁻¹). - ✅ **Performance metrics always quote:** specific energy (Wh/kg), specific power (W/kg), cycle life, η, cost (₹/kWh). - ✅ **For 14-mark answers:** always include *construction + chemistry + reactions + 3 advantages + 1 limitation + numerical if asked.* > **End of Module 4 Notes — OEMET727 Power Plant Engineering, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Magnetic Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Magnetic Energy Storage (MES)** is an advanced electrical energy storage technology that stores energy in the form of a **magnetic field** generated by the flow of direct current through a coil (inductor). In modern power-plant and grid-scale applications, this principle is operationalized as **Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES)**, wherein the storage coil is maintained in a **superconducting state** at cryogenic temperatures (typically **4.2 K** for liquid Helium cooling) to achieve **zero electrical resistance**, thereby eliminating ohmic (I²R) losses during the storage interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMET727 / Module 4):** *Magnetic Energy Storage is the technique of storing electrical energy in the magnetic field of a superconducting coil, where the energy density is a function of the square of the magnetic flux density (B²/2μ₀), enabling ultra-fast charge/discharge cycles with efficiencies approaching 95–98%.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Flywheel"

Imagine a **mechanical flywheel** spinning at very high speed — it stores energy in its rotational inertia (kinetic energy = ½Iω²). Now replace the spinning mass with a **coil of wire carrying DC current**:

| Mechanical Flywheel | Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES) |
|---|---|
| Rotational mass (inertia) | Inductance of coil (L) |
| Angular velocity (ω) | Current (I) |
| Kinetic Energy = ½Iω² | Magnetic Energy = ½LI² |
| Friction in bearings | Resistance in coil (≈ 0 if superconducting) |

The faster the flywheel spins, the more energy it stores — similarly, the larger the current through the coil, the more magnetic energy is held in its field. Because a superconducting coil has **near-zero resistance**, the "friction" is essentially eliminated, allowing energy to be stored almost indefinitely without loss.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants for SMES Calculations:**
> - Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m ≈ 1.2566 × 10⁻⁶ H/m**
> - Cryogenic temperature for Nb-Ti superconductors: **T ≈ 4.2 K**
> - Critical magnetic field of common superconductors: **B_c ≈ 10–15 T**
> - Typical energy density of SMES: **~10⁶ J/m³** (orders of magnitude higher than capacitors)
> - Round-trip efficiency of SMES systems: **95%–98%**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Physics — Energy in a Magnetic Field

Whenever current flows through a conductor, a magnetic field is established around it. This magnetic field represents stored energy because work must be done by the source EMF to establish the current against the **back-EMF** induced by the growing flux.

The energy stored in any inductor carrying current **I** with self-inductance **L** is given by:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2}$$

This energy is **not** stored in the wire itself — it resides in the **magnetic field** permeating the space around (and inside) the coil.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Principle

**Step 1 — Charging Phase:**
- AC power from the grid is fed to a **rectifier (AC → DC converter)**.
- The DC output is gradually increased in the superconducting coil via the **power conditioning system (PCS)**.
- Current builds up in the coil, establishing a strong magnetic field.
- Energy from the grid is now "locked" into the magnetic field.

**Step 2 — Storage (Standby) Phase:**
- Once the desired current (and hence stored energy) is reached, the coil is **closed on itself** through a superconducting persistent-current switch.
- The cryogenic system maintains the coil at temperatures below the **critical temperature (T_c)** of the superconductor, ensuring R ≈ 0.
- Energy remains stored with virtually **no loss** for hours, days, or weeks.

**Step 3 — Discharging Phase:**
- When energy is required by the grid, the persistent switch is opened.
- The collapsing magnetic field induces an EMF (Lenz's law).
- This DC is fed to an **inverter (DC → AC converter)** and returned to the grid as usable AC power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Unlike batteries, SMES does **not** involve any chemical reaction. The charge/discharge is purely **electromagnetic**, which is why response times are extraordinarily fast (typically **< 100 ms**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | Energy stored in an inductor | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $L = \mu_{0} \, N^{2} A \,/\, \ell$ | Inductance of a long solenoid (N turns, area A, length ℓ) | Henry (H) |
| 3 | $B = \mu_{0} \, n \, I = \mu_{0} N I \,/\, \ell$ | Magnetic flux density inside solenoid | Tesla (T) |
| 4 | $w = B^{2}\,/\,(2\mu_{0})$ | Volumetric energy density of magnetic field | J/m³ |
| 5 | $W_{vol} = B^{2} V \,/\,(2\mu_{0})$ | Total magnetic energy in volume V | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $\eta_{SMES} = W_{out}\,/\,W_{in}$ | Round-trip efficiency (typ. 0.95–0.98) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau = L\,/\,R$ | Time constant (≈ ∞ for R → 0 in superconductor) | seconds (s) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = -L \, dI\,/\,dt$ | Induced back-EMF in coil | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Formula #2 & #4:** Note that in Formula #4, energy density is purely a function of **B²** (and the material property μ₀). This is why engineers prefer to express MES performance in terms of magnetic flux density — doubling B quadruples the stored energy density.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Spinning Reserve & Frequency Regulation in Power Plants:** SMES units can release/absorb megawatt-level power within milliseconds to stabilize grid frequency, complementing conventional governor response of thermal/hydro plants.
2. **Power Quality Improvement:** Suppresses voltage sags, flicker, and harmonic disturbances in industrial grids.
3. **Pulsed Power Applications:** Used in fusion reactors, particle accelerators (e.g., CERN), and electromagnetic launch systems where sudden bursts of GW-level power are needed.
4. **Renewable Integration:** Smooths the intermittent output of solar/wind farms by absorbing surplus generation and releasing it during deficits.
5. **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS):** Provides bridge power during grid outages until diesel generators or turbines start up.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in an Inductor (½LI²)

**Starting Point:** When current in an inductor grows from 0 to I in time dt, the source delivers infinitesimal energy dW = ε · I · dt.

The induced back-EMF opposes the change in current:

$$\varepsilon = -L \frac{dI}{dt}$$

The source must supply an EMF equal in magnitude (and opposite in sign) to maintain current growth, hence the energy input is:

$$dW = \varepsilon \, I \, dt = L \, I \, dI$$

Integrating from 0 to the final current I (since current starts from zero):

$$W = \int_{0}^{I} L \, I \, dI = L \left[ \frac{I^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{I}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the magnetic analog of kinetic energy (½mv²) — the current I plays the role of velocity, and inductance L plays the role of mass.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Density of Magnetic Field (B²/2μ₀)

**Starting Point:** For a long solenoid with N turns, length ℓ, and cross-sectional area A, the inductance was previously stated as:

$$L = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell}$$

Substituting this L into the energy expression:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} \right) I^{2}$$

Recognizing that the number of turns per unit length is $n = N/\ell$, and that the magnetic field inside the solenoid is $B = \mu_{0} n I = \mu_{0} N I / \ell$, we can rewrite:

$$B^{2} = \frac{\mu_{0}^{2} N^{2} I^{2}}{\ell^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N^{2} I^{2} = \frac{B^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2}}$$

Substituting back:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} A}{\ell} \cdot \frac{B^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2}}$$

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}} \cdot A \ell$$

Since the volume of the solenoid is $V = A \ell$, the **total magnetic energy** becomes:

$$W = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot V$$

And the **volumetric energy density** (energy per unit volume) is:

$$\boxed{w = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The energy is distributed throughout the magnetic field, not just inside the coil wire. Stronger fields store disproportionately more energy (quadratic dependence on B).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inductance of a Solenoid (Engineering Justification)

**Starting Point:** By Ampere's Law for a long solenoid carrying current I:

$$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = \mu_{0} N I \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = \mu_{0} \frac{N}{\ell} I$$

The magnetic flux through one turn of cross-sectional area A:

$$\Phi = B \cdot A = \mu_{0} \frac{N}{\ell} I A$$

By definition, the total flux linkage for N turns is:

$$\lambda = N \Phi = \mu_{0} \frac{N^{2}}{\ell} I A$$

By the definition of inductance, $L = \lambda / I$:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Comparison of Storage Technologies

The following table places SMES in context with other energy-storage methods commonly discussed under KTU Module 4:

| Parameter | SMES | Pumped Hydro | Compressed Air (CAES) | Lead-Acid Battery | Flywheel |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy form | Magnetic field | Gravitational (water) | Compressed air | Chemical | Kinetic (rotational) |
| Efficiency (%) | **95–98** | 70–85 | 60–75 | 70–85 | 85–95 |
| Energy density (J/m³) | ~10⁶ | ~10⁵ | ~10⁵ | ~10⁶ | ~10⁵ |
| Discharge time | **ms – s** | hours | hours | minutes – hours | seconds – minutes |
| Response time | **< 100 ms** | minutes | minutes | ~10 ms | < 100 ms |
| Cycle life | ~10⁶+ | > 50 years | > 30 years | 500–1500 | ~10⁵ |
| Geographic dependency | None | Severe | Moderate | None | None |
| Major limitation | Cryogenic cost | Topography required | Cavern required | Sulfation, lifetime | Friction losses |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of SMES System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACGrid[AC Power Grid] -->|AC Input| Rectifier
    Rectifier[Rectifier AC to DC Converter] -->|Controlled DC| CoilSwitch
    CoilSwitch{Persistent Current Switch} -->|Charging Mode| SCcoil
    SCcoil[Superconducting Coil at 4.2K] -->|Persistent Current| Cryostat
    Cryostat[Cryogenic Cooling System Liquid He] -->|Maintains T less than Tc| SCcoil
    SCcoil -->|Discharging DC| Inverter
    Inverter[Inverter DC to AC Converter] -->|AC Output| ACLoad[AC Load or Grid]
    ControlUnit[Central Control Unit] -.->|Gate Signals| Rectifier
    ControlUnit -.->|Gate Signals| Inverter
    ControlUnit -.->|Open or Close| CoilSwitch
```

### 4.2 Energy Flow Diagram — Charging vs Discharging

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ChargingMode[CHARGING MODE]
        G1[Grid AC] -->|Step 1 Rectify| R1[DC Bus Rising]
        R1 -->|Step 2 Ramp Up| M1[Current I Increasing in Coil]
        M1 -->|Step 3 B Field Builds| E1[Energy W = half L I squared Stored]
    end

    subgraph StorageMode[STORAGE MODE]
        E1 --> S1[Persistent Switch CLOSED]
        S1 --> S2[Coil Short Circuited on Itself]
        S2 --> S3[Energy Held Indefinitely R approx 0]
    end

    subgraph DischargingMode[DISCHARGING MODE]
        S3 --> D1[Persistent Switch OPENED]
        D1 --> D2[Magnetic Field Collapses]
        D2 --> D3[Induced EMF Powers Inverter]
        D3 --> D4[AC Returned to Grid]
    end

    E1 --> S1
    S3 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph PowerSide[Power Conditioning Subsystem]
        PCS1[Grid Side Filter]
        PCS2[Six Pulse or Twelve Pulse Rectifier]
        PCS3[DC Chopper for Current Regulation]
        PCS1 --> PCS2 --> PCS3
    end

    subgraph StorageSide[Storage Coil Subsystem]
        ST1[Superconducting Coil Solenoid or Toroid]
        ST2[Persistent Current Switch Heater Controlled]
        ST3[Cryostat Vacuum Vessel]
        ST1 --> ST2
        ST3 --> ST1
    end

    subgraph CryoSide[Cryogenic Subsystem]
        CR1[Liquid Helium Bath at 4.2 K]
        CR2[Thermal Radiation Shields at 80 K]
        CR3[Cryocooler Gifford McMahon Type]
        CR1 --> CR2 --> CR3
    end

    subgraph ControlSide[Supervisory Control Subsystem]
        CT1[State of Charge Estimator]
        CT2[Protection Relay Unit]
        CT3[SCADA Interface]
        CT1 --> CT2 --> CT3
    end

    PCS3 --> ST1
    CT2 -.->|Trip Signal| PCS2
    CT1 -.->|Reference Current| PCS3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
