> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** When asked to "explain the working of SMES", always draw (or describe) **three distinct functional blocks** — *Power Conditioning*, *Storage Coil*, and *Cryogenic Cooling* — and explicitly mention the role of the **persistent-current switch** in enabling lossless storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage (SMES). State any two advantages of SMES over conventional battery storage.**

**Model Answer:**
SMES is an energy storage technology in which electrical energy is stored in the magnetic field of a coil wound from superconducting wire, maintained below its critical temperature so that it exhibits zero electrical resistance.

**Advantages over batteries:**
1. **Very high round-trip efficiency (95–98%)** because no chemical reactions or resistive losses are involved during storage.
2. **Extremely fast response time (< 100 ms)** — suitable for frequency regulation and power-quality applications where batteries are too slow.
3. (Bonus) Almost unlimited cycle life (~10⁶ cycles) without degradation.

*(Examiner allocates ~1 mark for definition + ~2 marks for the two advantages.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is cryogenic cooling necessary for a SMES system? What is the typical operating temperature?**

**Model Answer:**
Cryogenic cooling is essential to keep the storage coil **below its critical temperature (T_c)**, the threshold below which the material transitions to the superconducting state (R → 0).

For conventional **Nb-Ti (Niobium-Titanium)** superconductors used in commercial SMES systems, the critical temperature is approximately **9.2 K**, and the operating temperature maintained by liquid helium is **4.2 K** — well below T_c to provide a thermal safety margin and accommodate heat leaks from current leads and radiation.

Without cryogenic cooling, the coil would revert to its normal (resistive) state, and the magnetic energy would dissipate as I²R heat within seconds, defeating the purpose of storage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the energy stored per unit volume in a magnetic field and show that it equals **B² / (2μ₀)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SMES solenoid has **N = 5000 turns**, length **ℓ = 2 m**, mean radius **r = 0.5 m**, and carries a current **I = 1000 A**. Calculate: (i) the magnetic flux density B inside the coil, (ii) the total inductance L, and (iii) the total magnetic energy stored. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

The self-inductance of a long solenoid of N turns, length ℓ, and cross-sectional area A is derived from Faraday's law:

$$L = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell}$$

The energy stored in an inductor carrying current I is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} \cdot I^{2}$$

Using the magnetic flux density inside a solenoid, $B = \mu_{0} N I / \ell$, we square both sides:

$$B^{2} = \frac{\mu_{0}^{2} N^{2} I^{2}}{\ell^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N^{2} I^{2} = \frac{B^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2}}$$

Substituting back into the energy equation:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} A}{\ell} \cdot \frac{B^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mu_{0}^{2}} = \frac{B^{2}}{2\mu_{0}} \cdot (A \ell)$$

Since volume V = A ℓ, dividing W by V gives the volumetric energy density:

$$\boxed{w = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}}$$

*(Full marks: 2 for stating L formula, 2 for energy expression, 2 for substitution, 1 for final boxed result.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** N = 5000, ℓ = 2 m, r = 0.5 m, I = 1000 A, μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m.

**(i) Magnetic flux density B:**

$$B = \mu_{0} \cdot \frac{N}{\ell} \cdot I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{5000}{2} \cdot 1000$$

$$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (2500) \cdot (1000)$$

$$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{6})$$

$$B = 10\pi \times 10^{-1} = \pi \approx \boxed{3.142 \text{ T}}$$

**(ii) Total inductance L:**

Cross-sectional area: $A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \cdot (0.5)^{2} = 0.25\pi \text{ m}^{2}$

$$L = \frac{\mu_{0} N^{2} A}{\ell} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (5000)^{2} \cdot (0.25\pi)}{2}$$

$$L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (0.25\pi)}{2}$$

$$L = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (6.25 \times 10^{6} \pi)}{2}$$

$$L = \frac{25 \pi^{2} \times 10^{-1}}{2} = 12.5 \pi^{2} \times 10^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 1.2337 \text{ H}}$$

**(iii) Total magnetic energy stored:**

$$W = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (1.2337) \cdot (1000)^{2}$$

$$W = 0.5 \cdot 1.2337 \cdot 10^{6}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 6.17 \times 10^{5} \text{ J} = 617 \text{ kJ}}$$

*(Verification via energy density:* $w = B^{2}/(2\mu_{0}) = (3.142)^{2}/(2 \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \approx 3.93 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m}^{3}$. Volume = A·ℓ = 0.25π · 2 = 0.5π ≈ 1.571 m³. Then W = w·V ≈ 3.93 × 10⁶ × 1.571 ≈ 6.17 × 10⁵ J. ✓ *)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle of a SMES system**. Clearly mention the role of the rectifier, inverter, superconducting coil, cryogenic system, and persistent-current switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare SMES with **Pumped Hydro Storage** and **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** under the following heads: efficiency, response time, geographical dependence, cycle life, and environmental impact. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (with verbal block-diagram description):**

The SMES system comprises four main sub-systems:

1. **Power Conditioning System (PCS):** Contains a **rectifier** (which converts grid AC into controlled DC during charging) and an **inverter** (which converts the coil's DC back into grid-synchronized AC during discharging). A DC chopper regulates the current ramp rate.

2. **Superconducting Coil:** Wound from Nb-Ti or Nb₃Sn superconductor. Stores energy in its magnetic field as $W = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^{2}$.

3. **Persistent-Current Switch (PCS-Switch):** A small section of the coil is heated above T_c using a small heater. When cooled (heater off), the switch becomes superconducting and "short-circuits" the coil, allowing current to circulate indefinitely with zero loss.

4. **Cryogenic System:** Maintains the coil at 4.2 K using liquid helium, with thermal radiation shields at 80 K cooled by a Gifford-McMahon cryocooler.

**Operating Sequence:**

- **Charge:** Grid AC → Rectifier → DC bus → coil current ramps up to I_design → magnetic field builds → PCS-switch closes → rectifier disconnected.
- **Store:** Coil current circulates persistently; R ≈ 0 so energy loss is negligible.
- **Discharge:** PCS-switch opens → induced EMF drives current through inverter → AC delivered to load/grid.

*(Mark split: 2 for diagram description, 2 for charging, 1 for storage, 2 for discharging.)*

---

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | SMES | Pumped Hydro | CAES |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | 95–98% | 70–85% | 60–75% |
| **Response time** | < 100 ms (very fast) | Minutes (slow) | Minutes (slow) |
| **Geographical dependence** | None (can be located anywhere) | Severe (needs two reservoirs at different elevations) | Moderate (needs cavern/salt-dome) |
| **Cycle life** | ~10⁶ cycles | 50+ years | 30+ years |
| **Environmental impact** | Minimal (no emissions); helium is a finite resource | Large land submergence; ecological disturbance | Moderate (subsidence risk, fossil fuel used in some CAES designs) |

**Conclusion:** SMES excels in **response time, efficiency, and siting flexibility** but is constrained by **high capital cost and cryogenic complexity**. Pumped hydro offers massive capacity but is slow and geographically constrained. CAES is a middle ground but still suffers from slow response.

*(Mark split: ~1.4 marks per criterion × 5 criteria = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SMES with conventional inductor storage:** A normal copper coil loses all its energy in milliseconds due to I²R dissipation. SMES is *specifically* about **superconducting** coils. Mentioning "superconducting" is mandatory — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if omitted.
> 2. **Forgetting the persistent-current switch:** Many students describe only the rectifier-inverter chain and miss the crucial role of the persistent switch that enables truly lossless storage. This costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up energy and energy density:** In numerical problems, students often confuse $W = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^{2}$ (total energy in joules) with $w = B^{2}/(2\mu_{0})$ (energy density in J/m³). Always check units before finalizing the answer.
> 4. **Ignoring units in numerical answers:** Final answer without units = **0.5 mark deduction** per KTU valuation norms.
> 5. **Skipping the cryogenic system explanation:** SMES without cryogenics is not SMES. Examiners look for mention of **T_c, liquid helium, and the role of cryocooler**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** SMES stores energy in the magnetic field of a **superconducting** coil maintained below its **critical temperature (T_c)**.
- ✅ **Core Equation:** $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ — analogous to kinetic energy ½mv².
- ✅ **Energy Density:** $w = B^{2}/(2\mu_{0})$ — energy scales **quadratically** with magnetic flux density.
- ✅ **Solenoid Inductance:** $L = \mu_{0} N^{2} A / \ell$ — increases with N² and A.
- ✅ **Three Functional Blocks:** Power Conditioning System (rectifier + inverter), Storage Coil (superconducting), Cryogenic Cooling (liquid He at 4.2 K).
- ✅ **Persistent-Current Switch:** Enables lossless storage by short-circuiting the coil when fully charged.
- ✅ **Key Advantages:** 95–98% efficiency, < 100 ms response, ~10⁶ cycle life, no geographic constraints.
- ✅ **Key Disadvantages:** High capital cost, cryogenic complexity, finite helium resource, strong stray magnetic fields requiring shielding.
- ✅ **Typical Applications:** Spinning reserve, frequency regulation, power quality, renewable integration, pulsed-power systems, UPS bridge power.
- ✅ **Comparison Anchor:** SMES = *fastest response*; Pumped Hydro = *largest capacity*; CAES = *middle ground*; Battery = *moderate capacity & speed*.
- ✅ **Numerical Constants to Memorize:** μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m; T_c of Nb-Ti ≈ 9.2 K; Operating T ≈ 4.2 K; B_c ≈ 10–15 T.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermal Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** is a technology that stocks thermal energy by heating or cooling a storage medium so that the stored energy can be used later for power generation, heating, or cooling applications. In the context of power plant engineering, TES bridges the temporal mismatch between **energy supply** (e.g., intermittent solar radiation, off-peak grid electricity) and **energy demand** (peak load hours), thereby improving plant capacity factor, efficiency, and dispatchability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim context):** *"Thermal Energy Storage — methods of storing thermal energy: sensible heat, latent heat and thermochemical storage; selection of storage media; applications in solar thermal power plants and conventional thermal stations."*

The three principal modes of thermal energy storage recognised in the KTU 2024 OEMET727 syllabus are:

1. **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)** — energy stored by a measurable temperature change of the medium.
2. **Latent Heat Storage (LHS)** — energy stored as the latent heat of a phase change (typically solid–liquid).
3. **Thermochemical Storage (TCS)** — energy stored in the form of reversible endothermic/exothermic chemical reactions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker and the Thermos Flask"

Think of Thermal Energy Storage as a **bank account for heat**. Just as you deposit money when you earn it and withdraw it when you need to spend:

- **Sensible Heat Storage** is like heating water in a thermos flask — you can *feel* the heat by touching the flask (the temperature has visibly risen). The hotter the water, the more "money" you have saved.
- **Latent Heat Storage** is like an ice cube in your drink — even when the ice is *melting* at exactly 0 °C, it is still absorbing huge amounts of heat from your drink without changing temperature. The "money" is hidden inside the phase change itself.
- **Thermochemical Storage** is like a rechargeable chemical battery — you "charge" it by triggering an endothermic reaction (heat absorbed), and you "discharge" it later by reversing the reaction (heat released). The energy density is enormous, but the equipment is sophisticated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TES matters in modern power engineering:** In a Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plant, sunshine is available only ~6–8 hours/day, but grid demand peaks at night. A well-designed TES system can extend plant dispatch by **6–15 hours**, transforming a solar plant from an intermittent resource into a firm, dispatchable generator.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

The three universally benchmarked parameters for any TES system are:

- **Energy Density (volumetric)** $\rho_v$ in **kWh/m³** — compactness of storage.
- **Energy Density (gravimetric)** $\rho_g$ in **kWh/kg** — lightness of storage.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency** $\eta_{RT}$ in **%** — ratio of energy recovered to energy stored.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A commonly used reference value is **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3600 kJ**. Always carry this conversion factor in TES calculations — examiners award marks for unit consistency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) — Operational Logic

In SHS, energy is stored by raising (or lowering) the temperature of a single-phase storage medium. The governing equation is the classical calorimetric relation:

$$Q_{SHS} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $Q_{SHS}$ = sensible heat stored (kJ or kWh)
- $m$ = mass of storage medium (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity at constant pressure (kJ/kg·K)
- $\Delta T = (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ = temperature swing (K or °C)

### Engineering Steps in SHS System Design

1. **Choose the medium** (water, molten salt, thermal oil, rock-bed, concrete, sand).
2. **Decide operating temperature window** $[T_{cold}, T_{hot}]$ — must lie within the medium's thermal stability range.
3. **Calculate required mass** $m = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$.
4. **Size the storage tank/vessel** accounting for density $\rho$ and packing fraction $\phi$.
5. **Account for losses** — apply a round-trip efficiency factor $\eta_{RT} \approx 0.85 - 0.95$.

### Commonly Used SHS Media (KTU-favourite exam data)

| Medium | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Typical $T$ range (°C) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water (liquid) | **4.186** | 1000 | 0 – 95 | Low-temp, domestic |
| Thermal oil (e.g., Therminol VP-1) | **2.6** | ~870 | 12 – 400 | Process heat |
| Solar salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) | **1.55** | ~1900 (liq.) | 220 – 565 | CSP, KTU classic |
| Cast iron / Steel | 0.50 | 7200 | up to 800 | Packed-bed |
| Concrete / castable | ~0.88 | ~2200 | up to 600 | Modular storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-favourite trap:** Students often quote "specific heat of water = 1 kcal/kg·K" without converting. The SI value **4.186 kJ/kg·K** is the examiner-preferred form.

## 2.2 Latent Heat Storage (LHS) — Operational Logic

LHS exploits the **phase change enthalpy** of a material, most commonly the solid–liquid transition. The governing equation is:

$$Q_{LHS} = m \cdot h_{sl}$$

Where $h_{sl}$ is the specific latent heat of fusion (kJ/kg). During the phase change the temperature remains essentially constant at the **melting point** $T_m$.

### Phase Change Materials (PCM) — KTU Categories

- **Organic PCMs:** Paraffin waxes (e.g., n-eicosane, $T_m$ = 36–70 °C, $h_{sl}$ = 200–250 kJ/kg), fatty acids.
- **Inorganic PCMs:** Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt Na₂SO₄·10H₂O, $T_m$ ≈ 32 °C), molten salts (e.g., NaCl–MgCl₂ eutectics).
- **Eutectic PCMs:** Engineered mixtures with sharp melting points.

### Combined SHS + LHS Model (Real PCM Behaviour)

For a real PCM, energy storage is the sum of three contributions:

$$Q_{total} = m \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_i) + m \cdot h_{sl} + m \cdot c_{p,l} \cdot (T_f - T_m)$$

Where subscripts $s$, $l$, $i$, $f$ refer to solid, liquid, initial and final states respectively.

## 2.3 Thermochemical Storage (TCS)

TCS stores energy by driving an endothermic reversible reaction during charging and recovering it via the exothermic reverse reaction during discharging.

$$AB + \text{Heat} \rightleftharpoons A + B$$

Examples:
- CaO + Heat ⇌ Ca(OH)₂ (calcium oxide hydration/dehydration, $T$ ≈ 500 °C)
- Metal hydrides: $MH_x \rightleftharpoons M + \frac{x}{2}H_2$
- Ammonia synthesis/decomposition

Energy density of TCS can reach **500–3000 kJ/kg**, an order of magnitude higher than SHS.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{SHS} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat storage | kJ |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot \phi}$ | Tank/vessel volume | m³ |
| 3 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{c_p \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta T}{3600}$ | Volumetric energy density (SHS) | kWh/m³ |
| 4 | $Q_{LHS} = m \cdot h_{sl}$ | Latent heat storage | kJ |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{amb}$ | Heat loss from tank | kW |
| 6 | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % |
| 7 | $t_{discharge} = \dfrac{Q_{stored}}{\dot{Q}_{out}}$ | Discharge duration | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulae directly drive the design of TES systems for Concentrated Solar Power (CSP), Combined Heat and Power (CHP), district heating networks, and grid-scale thermal batteries (e.g., the Siemens Gamesa *ETES* molten-salt storage in Hamburg). TES is now treated as a *flexibility asset* in modern power system dispatch.

## 2.5 Comparative Analysis — KTU-style 7-Mark Favourite

| Parameter | SHS | LHS | TCS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy density | Low (50–200 kJ/kg) | Medium (150–400 kJ/kg) | Very high (500–3000 kJ/kg) |
| Temperature stability during storage | Varies | Near-constant ($T_m$) | Varies |
| System complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Cost per kWh stored | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical media | Water, salts, oils | Paraffin, salt hydrates | CaO/Ca(OH)₂, metal hydrides |
| Maturity | Commercial | Commercial | Pilot stage |
| Best use | Large-scale, CSP | Compact, building | Long-duration, R&D |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Mass of Storage Medium (SHS)

Starting from the fundamental calorimetric equation for a single-phase substance:

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

Rearranging to solve for the **mass** required to store a specified amount of thermal energy $Q_{req}$:

$$m = \frac{Q_{req}}{c_p \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})}$$

Since $Q_{req}$ is typically specified in kWh for a power plant application, we must convert to kJ using the canonical factor **1 kWh = 3600 kJ**:

$$m = \frac{Q_{req} \times 3600}{c_p \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})} \quad \text{[kg]}$$

The corresponding **tank volume** $V$ is found by dividing by the medium's bulk density $\rho$ and the packing/void factor $\phi$:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot \phi} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{]}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 10 MWe Concentrated Solar Power plant is to be equipped with a 6-hour molten-salt TES system. Solar salt properties: $c_p = 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho = 1900$ kg/m³ (liquid), $\phi = 0.98$ (tank fill fraction). Hot tank: 565 °C, Cold tank: 295 °C. Compute (a) energy to be stored, (b) mass of salt, (c) tank volume.

**Solution:**

**(a) Energy to be stored:**

$$Q_{req} = P \times t = 10{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 6 \text{ h} = 60{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

Converting:

$$Q_{req} = 60{,}000 \times 3600 = 2.16 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ}$$

**(b) Mass of salt:**

$$\Delta T = 565 - 295 = 270 \text{ K}$$

$$m = \frac{2.16 \times 10^8}{1.55 \times 270} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^8}{418.5}$$

$$m = 5.162 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} \approx 516.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(c) Tank volume:**

$$V = \frac{5.162 \times 10^5}{1900 \times 0.98} = \frac{5.162 \times 10^5}{1862}$$

$$V \approx 277.2 \text{ m}^3$$

If the tank is cylindrical with height equal to diameter ($H = D$), then $V = \frac{\pi}{4}D^3$, giving $D \approx 7.07$ m.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Combined SHS + LHS Energy Storage Equation

For a real PCM, the storage process consists of three sequential stages. The total heat $Q_{total}$ stored per unit mass is:

**Stage 1 — Sensible heating of solid (from $T_i$ to $T_m$):**

$$q_1 = c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_i)$$

**Stage 2 — Latent heat absorption at $T_m$:**

$$q_2 = h_{sl}$$

**Stage 3 — Sensible heating of liquid (from $T_m$ to $T_f$):**

$$q_3 = c_{p,l} \cdot (T_f - T_m)$$

Adding all three:

$$q_{total} = c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + h_{sl} + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m)$$

Multiplying by mass:

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + h_{sl} + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m) \right]}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (PCM)

**Problem:** A building-integrated TES uses paraffin PCM with $c_{p,s} = 1.8$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,l} = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{sl} = 200$ kJ/kg, $T_m = 60$ °C. The PCM is initially at 25 °C and heated to 80 °C. Compute the total energy stored per kg.

**Solution:**

$$q_{total} = 1.8 \times (60 - 25) + 200 + 2.1 \times (80 - 60)$$

$$q_{total} = 1.8 \times 35 + 200 + 2.1 \times 20$$

$$q_{total} = 63 + 200 + 42 = 305 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Converting to kWh/kg:

$$q_{total} = \frac{305}{3600} = 0.0847 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the latent component (200 kJ/kg) accounts for **65.6 %** of total stored energy — illustrating why PCMs are so attractive for compact thermal storage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Round-Trip Efficiency with Standby Losses

A realistic storage system loses heat to the surroundings during the dwell period. The total energy lost during charging $\rightarrow$ standby $\rightarrow$ discharging is:

$$Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{avg} - T_{amb}) \cdot \tau$$

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = external surface area of tank (m²)
- $T_{avg}$ = average fluid temperature (K)
- $T_{amb}$ = ambient temperature (K)
- $\tau$ = dwell/standby time (s)

The recovered energy is therefore:

$$Q_{out} = Q_{in} - Q_{loss}$$

And round-trip efficiency:

$$\eta_{RT} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{U \cdot A \cdot (T_{avg} - T_{amb}) \cdot \tau}{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{charge}}\right) \times 100\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Tool

| Application | Recommended TES | Why |
|---|---|---|
| CSP (400–600 °C) | Sensible (molten salt) | High $T$ stability, low cost |
| Building HVAC (20–40 °C) | Latent (paraffin PCM) | Compact, near-constant $T$ |
| District heating | Sensible (hot water) | Cheap, large volumes OK |
| Off-peak electricity → heat | Thermochemical / SHS | Long duration storage |
| Solar cooker / cooker storage | Latent (organic PCM) | Small ΔT window |
| Industrial waste-heat recovery | SHS (concrete/regenerator) | High $T$, cyclic |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — TES Modes and Their Energy Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Energy Source: Solar, Waste Heat, Off-peak Electricity] --> B{TES Selection}
    B --> C1[Sensible Heat Storage]
    B --> C2[Latent Heat Storage]
    B --> C3[Thermochemical Storage]

    C1 --> D1[Media: Water, Molten Salt, Thermal Oil, Rock Bed, Concrete]
    D1 --> E1[Energy: Q equals m times cp times Delta T]

    C2 --> D2[Media: Paraffin, Salt Hydrates, Eutectics]
    D2 --> E2[Energy: Q equals m times h_sl]

    C3 --> D3[Reaction: Metal Hydrides, CaO-Ca OH 2, Ammonia]
    D3 --> E3[Energy: Q equals m times Delta H reaction]

    E1 --> F[Heat Exchanger Network]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Power Block: Turbine, Engine, ORC]
    G --> H[Electrical Grid Output]

    F --> I[Standby Thermal Losses]
    I --> J[Round Trip Efficiency Reduction]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Tank Molten Salt TES in a CSP Plant (KTU-favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Solar Field: Parabolic Trough or Heliostats] -- 295 C salt pump --> HT[Hot Tank at 565 C]
    HT -- Hot salt via steam generator --> SG[Steam Generator / Heat Exchanger]
    SG --> TU[Turbine Generator Set]
    TU --> EL[Electricity to Grid]
    SG -- Cooled 565 C to 295 C salt --> CT[Cold Tank at 295 C]
    CT -- Pump returns salt --> S1
    HT -. Heat loss to environment .-> AMB[Ambient]
    CT -. Heat loss to ambient .-> AMB
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Phase Change Material (PCM) TES Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Heat Input from HTF] --> P1[Outer Encapsulation Shell]
    P1 --> P2[PCM Core: Paraffin or Salt Hydrate]
    P2 -- Solid to Liquid Phase Change --> P3[Latent Heat Absorbed Q equals m h_sl]
    P3 --> OUT[Heat Released to Load on Demand]
    OUT -. Heat exchanger to space heating or process .- OLOAD[Building or Process]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right TES for a Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What temperature range does the plant operate in?] --> Q2{Operating T below 100 C}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Use Water SHS or Organic PCM]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Operating T between 100 and 400 C}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R2[Use Thermal Oil SHS or Salt Hydrate LHS]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Operating T above 400 C}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R3[Use Molten Salt or Solid Particle SHS]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Consider Thermochemical Storage for R and D]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams emphasise the **functional architecture** rather than attempting to render physical 3-D schematics, ensuring compatibility with Mermaid's text-based rendering engine while preserving engineering fidelity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the three modes of thermal energy storage. Give one example medium for each.

**Model Answer:**
The three modes of thermal energy storage are:
1. **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):** Heat stored by raising the temperature of a medium. Example: hot water in a tank, molten salt.
2. **Latent Heat Storage (LHS):** Heat stored as the latent heat of phase change of a PCM. Example: paraffin wax undergoing solid–liquid transition at its melting point.
3. **Thermochemical Storage (TCS):** Heat stored by driving a reversible endothermic chemical reaction. Example: CaO + H₂O ⇌ Ca(OH)₂ system.
*(3 marks — 1 mark each for definition + 1 mark each for example, distributed across the three modes.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** A solar pond stores 200 m³ of water between 30 °C and 80 °C. Calculate the energy stored in kWh. Take $c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/kg·K and $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³.

**Model Answer:**
Mass of water:
$$m = \rho \times V = 1000 \times 200 = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ kg}$$
$\Delta T = 80 - 30 = 50$ K
$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 2 \times 10^5 \times 4.186 \times 50 = 4.186 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}$$
Converting to kWh:
$$Q = \frac{4.186 \times 10^7}{3600} = 11{,}627.8 \text{ kWh} \approx 11{,}628 \text{ kWh}$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for mass, 1 mark for Q in kJ, 1 mark for conversion to kWh.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model paper]** 
**(a)** Derive the governing energy equation for sensible heat storage in a single-phase medium. A CSP plant requires 8 hours of storage at a 5 MWe output. Compute the mass of solar salt needed. Assume hot tank = 565 °C, cold tank = 290 °C, $c_p = 1.55$ kJ/kg·K, and a round-trip efficiency of 90 %. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working principle of latent heat thermal energy storage with a neat block diagram. Discuss the advantages of PCM-based storage over sensible heat storage in building applications. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A

**(a)** *Derivation + numerical:*
Starting from the first law for a closed control mass (no mass flow, no work):
$$Q = \Delta U = m \cdot c_v \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(constant volume)}$$
For most TES systems the medium flows at near-constant pressure, so:
$$Q = \Delta H = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$
Energy required at output:
$$E_{out} = P \times t = 5000 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 40{,}000 \text{ kWh} = 1.44 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ}$$
Accounting for round-trip efficiency:
$$E_{in} = \frac{E_{out}}{\eta_{RT}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^8}{0.90} = 1.6 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ}$$
$\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275$ K
$$m = \frac{1.6 \times 10^8}{1.55 \times 275} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^8}{426.25} = 3.754 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} \approx 375.4 \text{ tonnes}$$
*(7 marks: 2 derivation + 3 numerical setup + 2 final answer with units.)*

**(b)** *LHS working + diagram + advantages:*
LHS works by exploiting the **latent heat of phase change** of a PCM, typically solid–liquid. During charging, the PCM melts at a near-constant temperature $T_m$, absorbing its latent heat $h_{sl}$. During discharging, it solidifies, releasing this heat.

**Block Diagram (text representation):**
> HTF In → Encapsulated PCM bed → Phase change solid→liquid → HTF Out → Cold storage → On demand → HTF flows back reversing phase → Heat released to load.

**Advantages over SHS in buildings:**
- High energy density in small volume → compactness.
- Near-isothermal storage → comfortable indoor temperatures.
- Smaller temperature swings → lower tank wall thickness and cost.
- Better integration with solar walls and underfloor heating.
*(7 marks: 3 working + 2 diagram + 2 advantages.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 model paper]**
**(a)** Explain the working of a two-tank molten salt thermal storage system used in a Concentrated Solar Power plant. State typical operating temperatures and salt composition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 m³ concrete thermal storage unit is used to store heat between 200 °C and 500 °C for an industrial process. Density of concrete = 2200 kg/m³, $c_p$ = 0.88 kJ/kg·K. Calculate the energy stored in kWh and the equivalent discharge duration at 500 kW load. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B

**(a)** *Two-tank molten salt TES description:*
The system uses **two tanks** — a **hot tank** storing solar salt at ~565 °C and a **cold tank** at ~290 °C. During sunshine, the cold salt is pumped through the solar field (or tower receiver), absorbs heat, and is stored in the hot tank. When electricity is required (day or night), hot salt is pumped through a **steam generator**, transferring heat to water to produce superheated steam that drives the turbine. The cooled salt returns to the cold tank.
- **Salt composition:** 60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃ (binary "solar salt").
- **Hot tank:** ~565 °C; **Cold tank:** ~290–295 °C.
- **ΔT:** ~270–290 K.
*(7 marks: 4 description + 2 composition + 1 temperatures.)*

**(b)** *Numerical:*
Mass of concrete:
$$m = \rho V = 2200 \times 50 = 1.1 \times 10^5 \text{ kg}$$
$\Delta T = 500 - 200 = 300$ K
$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1.1 \times 10^5 \times 0.88 \times 300 = 2.904 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ}$$
Converting to kWh:
$$Q = \frac{2.904 \times 10^7}{3600} = 8066.7 \text{ kWh}$$
Discharge duration at 500 kW:
$$t = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{8066.7}{500} = 16.13 \text{ h}$$
*(7 marks: 3 mass + 2 Q in kJ + 1 conversion + 1 time.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing kcal and kJ without conversion. The conversion **1 kWh = 3600 kJ = 860 kcal** is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Forgetting round-trip efficiency** — the stored energy must be inflated by $1/\eta_{RT}$ to deliver the required output energy. Many students solve for $m$ using the *output* energy directly and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Using density of water for molten salt** — solar salt density is ~1900 kg/m³ (not 1000). Tank sizing is highly sensitive to this value.
> 4. **Confusing $c_p$ and $c_v$** — for liquids and gases flowing in TES systems, always use $c_p$ at constant pressure.
> 5. **Not specifying state of PCM** — a real PCM stores energy via *three* terms (solid sensible + latent + liquid sensible). Quoting only $h_{sl}$ earns partial credit, not full marks.
> 6. **Missing packing fraction $\phi$** in tank sizing — examiners routinely test whether students apply the real-world bulk volume correction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TES has three modes:** Sensible (SHS), Latent (LHS), Thermochemical (TCS). Each trades energy density against complexity.
- **The governing equation** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ for SHS is the single most important formula in this module.
- **For PCMs**, always state all three terms: solid sensible heating + latent heat + liquid sensible heating.
- **Molten salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃)** is the workhorse medium of CSP plants operating between ~290 °C and ~565 °C.
- **1 kWh = 3600 kJ = 3.6 MJ** — internalise this conversion; it appears in nearly every KTU numerical.
- **Round-trip efficiency** for sensible systems is typically 85–95 %; for TCS it can be lower (60–80 %) due to reaction irreversibilities.
- **Round-trip efficiency formula** $\eta_{RT} = Q_{discharged}/Q_{charged}$ must always be expressed as a percentage.
- **Applications to remember:** CSP dispatchability, peak load shaving, CHP integration, building HVAC, industrial waste-heat recovery, solar cookers, district heating.
- **Comparative memory hook:** SHS = "temperature change", LHS = "phase change", TCS = "chemical change".
- **Common numerical traps:** missing $\eta_{RT}$, wrong $c_p$, ignoring density for molten salt, forgetting conversion from kJ to kWh.
- **Examiner-favourite media data to memorise:** $c_p$ (water) = 4.186 kJ/kg·K; $c_p$ (solar salt) = 1.55 kJ/kg·K; $c_p$ (thermal oil) ≈ 2.6 kJ/kg·K.
- **KTU 2024 weightage trend:** Part A questions on TES frequently ask for definitions + one example; Part B questions almost always include at least one full numerical on molten salt or PCM sizing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET727 - POWER PLANT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economics of Power Generation: Load Estimation, Load Curve, Load Factor, Diversity Factor, Capacity Factor, Use Factor, Plant Selection, Generation and Distribution Economics, Useful Life, and Tariff for Electrical Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — ECONOMICS OF POWER GENERATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Power Plant Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Economics of Power Generation* is the branch of engineering that deals with the **optimal utilization of capital, fuel, and human resources** to supply electrical energy to consumers at the **lowest possible cost per unit (kWh)** while ensuring **reliability, continuity, and quality** of supply.

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental aim of power plant economics is to answer two key questions:
> 1. **How much plant capacity should be installed?** (Load Estimation)
> 2. **How should the cost of electricity be recovered from consumers?** (Tariff Design)

---

### 1.2 Load and Load Estimation

**Load** refers to the electrical power demanded by consumers at any given instant, measured in **kW** or **MW**. **Load estimation** is the process of forecasting future power demand based on historical data, population growth, industrial expansion, and economic indicators.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket"**

Imagine a water pipeline system supplying a city. The water flow rate demanded by houses varies — mornings are heavy (showers, washing), evenings peak (cooking, cleaning), and nights drop. To size the pipeline, you must estimate the *peak* demand, not the average. Similarly, in electrical systems, the **maximum demand** dictates the size of generators, transformers, and transmission lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Electrical Loads:**
>
> | Load Type | Characteristics | Power Factor |
> |-----------|-----------------|--------------|
> | **Residential** | Lighting, fans, domestic appliances | 0.8 – 0.9 lagging |
> | **Commercial** | AC, lifts, escalators, lighting | 0.85 – 0.9 lagging |
> | **Industrial** | Motors, furnaces, heavy machinery | 0.6 – 0.8 lagging |
> | **Agricultural** | Pump sets, irrigation | 0.7 – 0.8 lagging |
> | **Traction** | Railways, electric vehicles | 0.7 – 0.85 lagging |

### 1.3 Connected Load vs. Maximum Demand vs. Average Load

- **Connected Load:** Sum of the rated capacities of all electrical equipment connected to the system. It is an *installed* value, not an *operating* value.
- **Maximum Demand:** The greatest demand of load that has occurred during a specified period (e.g., a day, month, or year).
- **Average Load:** The total energy consumed over a period divided by the number of hours in that period.

### 1.4 Load Curve

A **Load Curve** is a graphical plot showing the variation of electrical load on a power station with respect to time. It is the most fundamental tool in power plant economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Load Curves in KTU Syllabus:**
> - **Daily Load Curve** — Load variation over 24 hours.
> - **Monthly Load Curve** — Load variation over a month (constructed from daily curves).
> - **Yearly Load Curve** — Load variation over a year (also called *annual load duration curve*).

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Heartbeat Monitor"**

A load curve is like the heartbeat monitor of a power grid. Just as a doctor reads an ECG to assess heart health, an engineer reads a load curve to assess the health and utilization of a power station. The peaks indicate stress, the valleys indicate idle capacity, and the area under the curve represents *total energy sold*.

### 1.5 Key Economic Factors — Quick Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Load Factor (LF):** Ratio of average load to peak load during a given period.  
> **Diversity Factor (DF):** Ratio of the sum of individual maximum demands of consumers to the maximum demand on the power station.  
> **Capacity Factor (CF):** Ratio of actual energy generated to the energy that would have been generated if the plant ran at its rated capacity continuously.  
> **Use Factor (UF):** Ratio of energy generated to the energy that could have been generated if the plant ran at rated capacity during the hours it was actually in operation.  
> **Demand Factor (DFc):** Ratio of maximum demand of a consumer to its connected load.  
> **Plant Factor (PF):** Same as capacity factor in many textbooks.

### 1.6 Plant Selection

**Plant selection** is the decision-making process for choosing the type of power plant (thermal, hydro, nuclear, gas, renewable) based on technical, economic, environmental, and geographical factors. This is a high-weightage topic in KTU examinations.

### 1.7 Useful Life

The **Useful Life** of a power plant is the period during which the plant operates economically and efficiently before obsolescence, after which it should be replaced or overhauled. Typically:
- **Thermal Plant:** 20–30 years
- **Hydro Plant:** 50–100 years
- **Nuclear Plant:** 30–40 years

### 1.8 Tariff for Electrical Energy

A **Tariff** is the rate structure at which electrical energy is charged to consumers. It is designed to:
1. Recover the cost of generation, transmission, and distribution.
2. Provide a reasonable profit to the utility.
3. Encourage efficient use of electricity.
4. Penalize low power factor loads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Load Curve — Detailed Analysis

A load curve is constructed by recording the load at regular intervals (typically every half-hour or every hour) and plotting it against time.

**Key Areas under Load Curve:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Area under the Daily Load Curve = Total energy (kWh or MWh) generated in 24 hours.**

$$
\text{Average Load} = \frac{\text{Area under load curve (kWh)}}{\text{Number of hours (h)}}
$$

### 2.2 Load Duration Curve

When the daily load curve is re-arranged in **descending order of magnitude** (i.e., the highest load is placed first, then the next highest, and so on), the resulting curve is called a **Load Duration Curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between Load Curve and Load Duration Curve:**
>
> | Feature | Load Curve | Load Duration Curve |
> |---------|------------|--------------------|
> | Order | Chronological (time-based) | Descending (magnitude-based) |
> | Time information | Preserved | Lost |
> | Use | Operating decisions | Capacity planning |

### 2.3 Concept of Maximum Demand

The **Maximum Demand** of a system is the highest load sustained for a sufficient duration (usually 30 minutes) during the period under consideration. It is the most critical parameter for sizing:
- **Generating capacity** of the plant
- **Transformers**
- **Switchgear and protection equipment**
- **Transmission lines**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Maximum Demand** with **Connected Load**. The maximum demand is *always less* than the connected load, because not all connected devices operate simultaneously.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master formula sheet** for this module. Memorize it for the KTU ESE.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | **Average Load** | $\text{AL} = \dfrac{E}{t}$ | $E$ = Energy, $t$ = time | kW |
| 2 | **Load Factor** | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | — | dimensionless (or %) |
| 3 | **Demand Factor** | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{\text{Max Demand of Consumer}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Diversity Factor** | $\text{DivF} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Max Demand on Station}}$ | — | dimensionless (≥ 1) |
| 5 | **Coincidence Factor** | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Diversity Factor}}$ | — | dimensionless (≤ 1) |
| 6 | **Plant Capacity Factor** | $\text{PCF} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Actually Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours of Period}}$ | — | dimensionless (or %) |
| 7 | **Plant Use Factor** | $\text{PUF} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours of Use}}$ | — | dimensionless (or %) |
| 8 | **Utilization Factor** | $\text{UF} = \dfrac{\text{Max Demand}}{\text{Plant Capacity}}$ | — | dimensionless (or %) |
| 9 | **Reserve Capacity** | $\text{RC} = \text{Plant Capacity} - \text{Max Demand}$ | — | MW |
| 10 | **Diversity of Load** | $\text{DoL} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Demand Factor} \times \text{Connected Load})}{\text{Peak Demand}}$ | — | — |

---

## 2.5 Deep Dive — Plant Selection Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 emphasizes plant selection as a 14-mark question. The key factors are:**

### 2.5.1 Technical Factors
1. **Availability of fuel and water** at the site.
2. **Type of load demand** (base load, peak load, intermediate load).
3. **Reliability and availability** of the plant.
4. **Efficiency** at part-load and full-load operation.
5. **Starting time and rate of response** (critical for peak-load plants).

### 2.5.2 Economic Factors
1. **Initial capital cost** (₹/kW installed).
2. **Operating cost** (fuel, labor, maintenance).
3. **Cost of electricity generation** (₹/kWh).
4. **Useful life** of the plant.
5. **Depreciation and salvage value**.

### 2.5.3 Environmental & Social Factors
1. **Emission of CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ** (relevant for thermal).
2. **Land footprint** (large for hydro and solar).
3. **Displacement of population** (hydro projects).
4. **Noise and aesthetic impact**.

### 2.5.4 Plant Selection Table

| Plant Type | Capital Cost (₹/kW) | Running Cost | Efficiency | Starting Time | Best Suited For |
|------------|---------------------|--------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Steam (Coal) | High | High | 35–40% | Hours | Base load |
| Diesel | Low | High | 35–45% | Minutes | Standby / Peak |
| Gas Turbine | Medium | Medium | 30–35% | Minutes | Peak load |
| Hydro | Very High | Very Low | 85–90% | Minutes | Base / Peak |
| Nuclear | Very High | Low | 33% | Days | Base load |
| Solar PV | High | Nil | 15–20% | Instant | Distributed / Peaking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:**
> - **Base Load Plants:** High capital cost + Low running cost → Nuclear, Hydro, Coal.
> - **Peak Load Plants:** Low capital cost + Quick start → Gas Turbine, Diesel, Pumped Hydro.

---

## 2.6 Generation and Distribution Economics

### 2.6.1 Cost Components of Power Generation

> [!NOTE]
> The **total cost of electricity** has two principal components:
> 1. **Fixed Cost (FC):** Independent of energy generated. Includes interest on capital, depreciation, insurance, taxes, salaries of permanent staff.
> 2. **Variable / Running Cost (VC):** Depends on the energy generated. Includes fuel cost, water cost, lubricants, maintenance, and wages of operating staff.

**Total Cost Equation:**

$$
\text{TC} = \text{FC} + \text{VC} \quad [\text{₹/year or ₹}]
$$

**Cost per Unit (CPU):**

$$
\text{CPU} = \frac{\text{FC} + \text{VC} \times E}{\text{Energy Sold in Units}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{₹}}{\text{kWh}}\right]
$$

### 2.6.2 Depreciation

**Depreciation** is the annual reduction in the value of an asset due to wear, tear, and obsolescence.

**Straight-Line Method:**

$$
D = \frac{C - S}{n}
$$

where $C$ = initial cost, $S$ = salvage value, $n$ = useful life in years.

**Sinking Fund Method:**

$$
D = (C - S) \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

where $i$ = annual interest rate (as a decimal).

---

## 2.7 Useful Life — Economic Considerations

The **economic useful life** is the age at which the *average cost per unit* of energy produced is minimum. Beyond this age, the cost of maintenance rises steeply, making replacement economical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** The plant should be retired or replaced when the **average cost curve** begins to rise. This intersection point defines the **optimal economic life**.

## 2.8 Tariff for Electrical Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Objectives of a Good Tariff (KTU 2024):**
> 1. Recovery of cost of production, transmission, and distribution.
> 2. Reasonable return on the capital invested.
> 3. Fairness — different consumer classes pay proportionally.
> 4. Simplicity — easy to understand and apply.
> 5. Discouragement of wasteful use.
> 6. Promotion of off-peak consumption.

### 2.8.1 Types of Tariffs

| # | Tariff Type | Description |
|---|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Flat Tariff** | Single rate per unit (₹/kWh) regardless of consumption. |
| 2 | **Two-Part Tariff** | Fixed charge (₹/kW of max demand) + variable charge (₹/kWh). |
| 3 | **Three-Part Tariff / Block Rate** | Fixed charge + variable charge that varies in blocks. |
| 4 | **Block Rate Tariff** | Rate per unit decreases as consumption increases. |
| 5 | **Power Factor Tariff** | Includes a kVAh charge or PF penalty. |
| 6 | **Maximum Demand Tariff** | Charge based on kVA of maximum demand. |
| 7 | **Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariff** | Different rates for peak and off-peak hours. |

### 2.8.2 Most Common Tariff — Two-Part Tariff

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Two-Part Tariff** is the most popular in KTU problems.

$$
\text{Total Bill} = \text{Fixed Charge} + \text{Running Charge}
$$

$$
B = a \times \text{MD} + b \times E
$$

where:
- $a$ = fixed charge rate (₹ per kW of maximum demand per year)
- $b$ = running charge rate (₹ per kWh of energy consumed)
- $\text{MD}$ = maximum demand in kW
- $E$ = energy consumed in kWh

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Load Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** Average Load is defined as the total energy divided by the period of operation.

### Step 1 — Express Average Load

$$
\text{Average Load} = \frac{\text{Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Time (h)}}
$$

### Step 2 — Express Energy as Area under the Load Curve

The area under the daily load curve, when computed by integration (or by summing trapezoidal segments), gives the total energy supplied in 24 hours.

$$
E = \int_0^{24} P(t)\, dt \quad \text{[kWh]}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Load Factor

By definition, the Load Factor is the ratio of the Average Load to the Peak (Maximum) Load:

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Average Load

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{E \,/\, t}{P_{\max}} = \frac{E}{P_{\max} \cdot t}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Formula

$$
\boxed{\text{LF} = \frac{\text{Energy Generated}}{\text{Peak Load} \times \text{Time Period}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **operational formula** for load factor and the one used in nearly all KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Diversity Factor

### Step 1 — Conceptual Setup

Consider $n$ consumers, each with its own individual maximum demand: $D_1, D_2, D_3, \ldots, D_n$.

If all of these consumers were to draw their individual peak loads **simultaneously**, the load on the power station would be:

$$
D_{\text{sum}} = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + \cdots + D_n
$$

### Step 2 — Reality of Diversity

In practice, all consumers **do not** reach their individual peak demands at the same time. Different consumers have different usage patterns (residential peaks in the evening, industrial peaks during the day, etc.). Therefore, the actual **simultaneous maximum demand on the station** ($D_{\text{station}}$) is *less* than $D_{\text{sum}}$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Definition

$$
\text{Diversity Factor} = \frac{D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + \cdots + D_n}{D_{\text{station}}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Formula

$$
\boxed{\text{Diversity Factor} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i}{D_{\text{station}}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of Diversity Factor:**
> - Always **≥ 1** (numerator ≥ denominator in practice).
> - **Greater than 1** ⇒ Greater diversity ⇒ Lower station capacity needed.
> - A diversity factor close to 1 indicates that all consumers peak together — an undesirable situation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Plant Capacity Factor

### Step 1 — Energy Generated in Time $t$

If a plant of installed capacity $P_{\text{cap}}$ actually generates energy $E_{\text{actual}}$ over a period $t$:

$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \int_0^{t} P_{\text{gen}}(t)\, dt
$$

### Step 2 — Maximum Possible Energy

The maximum possible energy that could have been generated in the same period, had the plant run at its *full rated capacity* the entire time:

$$
E_{\max} = P_{\text{cap}} \times t
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Definition

$$
\text{Plant Capacity Factor} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\max}} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{cap}} \times t}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Formula

$$
\boxed{\text{PCF} = \frac{\text{Energy Actually Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Period (h)}}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Use Factor

### Step 1 — Distinguish "Hours of Period" from "Hours of Use"

The plant is *not* always in operation. Let:
- $t$ = total hours in the period (e.g., 8760 hours in a year)
- $h_u$ = hours the plant was *actually* in operation (typically $h_u \le t$)

### Step 2 — Energy Generated

$$
E = \int_0^{h_u} P_{\text{gen}}(t)\, dt
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Energy during Use

The maximum possible energy *during the hours of use*:

$$
E_{\text{max, use}} = P_{\text{cap}} \times h_u
$$

### Step 4 — Final Formula

$$
\boxed{\text{Use Factor} = \frac{E}{P_{\text{cap}} \times h_u}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference — Capacity Factor vs. Use Factor:**
>
> | Feature | Capacity Factor | Use Factor |
> |---------|-----------------|------------|
> | Denominator uses | Total period ($t$) | Hours of actual use ($h_u$) |
> | Value relative to Capacity Factor | Lower | Higher (since $h_u \le t$) |
> | Indicates | Overall plant utilization | Utilization during running hours |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 1 — Load Factor, Capacity Factor, Use Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
> A power station has an installed capacity of **100 MW** and supplies a load having:
> - **Annual maximum demand** = 80 MW
> - **Annual load factor** = 0.5
> - The plant is in operation for **6000 hours** in the year.
>
> Calculate:
> 1. Annual energy generated
> 2. Plant capacity factor
> 3. Plant use factor
> 4. Diversity factor if the sum of individual max demands = 120 MW

### Step 1 — Annual Energy Generated

$$
\text{Energy} = \text{Average Load} \times \text{Time}
$$

Average Load:
$$
\text{AL} = \text{LF} \times \text{Peak Load} = 0.5 \times 80 = 40 \text{ MW}
$$

Energy:
$$
E = 40 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 3{,}50{,}400 \text{ MWh} = 3.504 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh}
$$

### Step 2 — Plant Capacity Factor

$$
\text{PCF} = \frac{E}{P_{\text{cap}} \times t} = \frac{3{,}50{,}400}{100 \times 8760} = \frac{3{,}50{,}400}{8{,}76{,}000} = 0.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PCF} = 40\%}
$$

### Step 3 — Plant Use Factor

$$
\text{PUF} = \frac{E}{P_{\text{cap}} \times h_u} = \frac{3{,}50{,}400}{100 \times 6000} = \frac{3{,}50{,}400}{6{,}00{,}000} = 0.584
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PUF} = 58.4\%}
$$

### Step 4 — Diversity Factor

$$
\text{DivF} = \frac{\sum D_i}{D_{\text{station}}} = \frac{120}{80} = 1.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Diversity Factor} = 1.5}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 2 — Two-Part Tariff Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
> A consumer has a maximum demand of **50 kW** and consumes **30,000 kWh** per month. The tariff is:
> - Fixed charge: **₹200 per kW per year** of maximum demand
> - Running charge: **₹5 per kWh**
>
> Calculate the monthly bill, the average cost per kWh, and the bill if a power factor penalty of ₹0.50 per kVARh is imposed when PF < 0.85 (assume average PF = 0.8 lagging).

### Step 1 — Monthly Fixed Charge

The fixed charge is given *per year*, so we prorate to monthly:

$$
\text{Monthly Fixed} = \frac{200 \times 50}{12} = \frac{10{,}000}{12} = ₹833.33
$$

### Step 2 — Running Charge

$$
\text{Running Charge} = 5 \times 30{,}000 = ₹1{,}50{,}000
$$

### Step 3 — Total Bill (without PF penalty)

$$
\text{Total Bill} = 833.33 + 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹1{,}50{,}833.33
$$

### Step 4 — Average Cost per kWh

$$
\text{CPU} = \frac{1{,}50{,}833.33}{30{,}000} = ₹5.028 \text{ per kWh}
$$

### Step 5 — Power Factor Penalty

When PF = 0.8 lagging:
$$
\cos\phi = 0.8 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \tan\phi = 0.75
$$

Reactive energy:
$$
\text{kVARh} = \text{kWh} \times \tan\phi = 30{,}000 \times 0.75 = 22{,}500 \text{ kVARh}
$$

Penalty (PF < 0.85, so penalty applies):
$$
\text{Penalty} = 0.50 \times 22{,}500 = ₹11{,}250
$$

### Step 6 — Final Bill

$$
\text{Final Bill} = 1{,}50{,}833.33 + 11{,}250 = ₹1{,}62{,}083.33
$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 3 — Plant Selection Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
> A region has a daily load curve characterized by:
> - **6 AM to 6 PM:** Constant 50 MW
> - **6 PM to 10 PM:** Peak 80 MW
> - **10 PM to 6 AM:** Constant 20 MW
>
> Recommend an appropriate combination of base-load and peak-load plants, and justify.

### Step 1 — Compute Daily Energy

Base load (12 h at 50 MW):
$$
E_1 = 50 \times 12 = 600 \text{ MWh}
$$

Peak load (4 h at 80 MW):
$$
E_2 = 80 \times 4 = 320 \text{ MWh}
$$

Off-peak (8 h at 20 MW):
$$
E_3 = 20 \times 8 = 160 \text{ MWh}
$$

Total:
$$
E_{\text{total}} = 600 + 320 + 160 = 1080 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Load Categories

- **Base load (24 h, 0–50 MW):** ⇒ Steam/Nuclear/Hydro
- **Intermediate load (12 h, 50–80 MW):** ⇒ Gas turbine or older steam
- **Peak load (4 h, 50–80 MW):** ⇒ Diesel / Gas turbine / Pumped hydro

### Step 3 — Plant Mix Recommendation

| Plant | Capacity | Type | Justification |
|-------|----------|------|---------------|
| Coal Steam | 40 MW | Base | High CF, low running cost |
| Hydro | 10 MW | Base | Cheap, renewable |
| Gas Turbine | 30 MW | Peak | Quick start, low capital |

---

## 3.8 Cost of Power Generation — Analytical Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation of cost per kWh:**

Let:
- $C$ = capital cost of plant (₹)
- $i$ = annual interest rate
- $n$ = useful life (years)
- $S$ = salvage value (₹)
- $O$ = annual operating cost (₹) excluding fuel
- $F$ = annual fuel cost (₹)
- $E$ = annual energy generated (kWh)

**Annual Fixed Cost (Capital Recovery):**

$$
\text{FC} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} \cdot (C - S) + S \cdot i
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first term is the **capital recovery factor (CRF)** applied to the depreciable value. The second term accounts for the *opportunity cost* of the salvage value.

**Annual Variable Cost:**

$$
\text{VC} = O + F
$$

**Total Annual Cost:**

$$
\text{TC} = \text{FC} + \text{VC}
$$

**Cost per kWh:**

$$
\boxed{\text{CPU} = \frac{\text{FC} + \text{VC}}{E} \quad \left[\frac{\text{₹}}{\text{kWh}}\right]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Increasing the **load factor** (i.e., generating more energy $E$ from the same plant) reduces the CPU because the fixed cost is spread over more units. This is why utilities encourage off-peak consumption.
